<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of the Average Method

For the Average Method to be appropriate, the series must satisfy these assumptions:

1. **Stationarity in the Mean:** The series fluctuates around a constant level $\mu$ over time.
2. **No Trend:** The series does not systematically rise or fall.
3. **No Seasonality:** The series has no recurring periodic patterns.
4. **Constant Variance:** The dispersion around the mean does not change over time.
5. **Independence of Errors:** Forecast errors are uncorrelated with each other.

If any of these are violated, the Average Method will produce **biased and inaccurate forecasts**, and KTU examiners expect students to be able to *justify* the choice of method.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic – Step-by-Step Mechanism

The Average Method executes in **3 logical steps**:

1. **Step 1 – Data Collection:** Gather all available historical observations $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_t$ for the variable of interest (e.g., monthly sales, daily demand, yearly rainfall).
2. **Step 2 – Compute the Mean:** Calculate the arithmetic average $\bar{Y} = \frac{1}{t} \sum_{i=1}^{t} Y_i$. This single number is the entire "model."
3. **Step 3 – Issue Forecasts:** For any future horizon $h = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$, set $F_{t+h} = \bar{Y}$. The same constant forecast is repeated for all future periods.

> The method has **zero learning parameters** — the only quantity computed is the historical mean.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they are the most frequently tested in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Point Forecast | $F_{t+h} = \bar{Y}$ | Forecast for any future period |
| 2 | Historical Mean | $\bar{Y} = \dfrac{1}{t}\sum_{i=1}^{t} Y_i$ | Sum of all values divided by number of periods |
| 3 | Forecast Error | $e_t = Y_t - F_t$ | Actual minus predicted |
| 4 | Sum of Errors | $\sum e_t = 0$ | Property of Mean Method (useful self-check) |
| 5 | MAD | $\text{MAD} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert Y_t - F_t \vert$ | Average absolute error magnitude |
| 6 | MSE | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} (Y_t - F_t)^2$ | Penalizes larger errors quadratically |
| 7 | RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same units as the data |
| 8 | MAPE | $\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{100\%}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \left\vert \dfrac{Y_t - F_t}{Y_t} \right\vert$ | Scale-independent percentage error |
| 9 | SSE | $\text{SSE} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} (Y_t - F_t)^2$ | Total squared deviation |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

The Average Method, despite its simplicity, has genuine practical value in the following areas:

- **Inventory Management:** Baseline demand forecasting for slow-moving SKUs in retail and supply chain.
- **Energy Sector:** Estimating baseline electricity or water consumption in regions without strong seasonality.
- **Quality Control:** Setting a centerline for a control chart (SPC) when the process mean is the only quantity of interest.
- **Banking & Finance:** Establishing a baseline revenue forecast in the absence of strong trend signals.
- **Benchmark Model:** KTU often asks students to "compare the Average Method with the Naive Method" — the Average Method serves as the **universal baseline**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is it still taught?** In real-world Machine Learning, every forecasting model is evaluated against a "naive baseline." The Average Method is *one of those baselines* (along with the Naive Method and Seasonal Naive Method). If a sophisticated ARIMA model cannot beat the simple average, the ARIMA model is rejected.

---

## 2.5 Strengths and Limitations – KTU Comparison Table

| Aspect | Average Method |
|---|---|
| **Complexity** | Very Low – Trivial to compute |
| **Data Requirement** | Only historical observations needed |
| **Trend Handling** | ❌ Cannot capture trend |
| **Seasonality Handling** | ❌ Cannot capture seasonality |
| **Best Suited For** | Stationary, level series |
| **Number of Parameters** | 0 (parameter-free) |
| **Long-Run Forecast** | Constant flat line at $\bar{Y}$ |
| **Forecast Bias** | High when trend/seasonality is present |
| **Forecast Variance** | Lowest among baseline methods |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Example – Average Method (Classic KTU-Style Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> The annual sales (in lakhs of ₹) of a small electronics company in Kochi for the years 2018 – 2022 are given below. Using the **Average Method**, forecast sales for the years 2023, 2024, and 2025. Also, compute the **MAD, MSE, RMSE, and MAPE** of the model on the training data.

| Year ($t$) | Sales $Y_t$ (₹ in lakhs) |
|---|---|
| 2018 | 100 |
| 2019 | 110 |
| 2020 | 120 |
| 2021 | 115 |
| 2022 | 125 |

---

### 3.1.1 Step 1 – Compute the Historical Mean $\bar{Y}$

We sum all five historical observations and divide by $t = 5$:

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{1}{t} \sum_{i=1}^{t} Y_i = \frac{1}{5} \left( 100 + 110 + 120 + 115 + 125 \right)$$

Computing the inner sum:

$$100 + 110 = 210$$

$$210 + 120 = 330$$

$$330 + 115 = 445$$

$$445 + 125 = 570$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{570}{5} = 114 \text{ lakhs}$$

> **Interpretation:** The "average" annual sales figure is ₹114 lakhs.

---

### 3.1.2 Step 2 – Issue Forecasts for 2023, 2024, 2025

By the Average Method rule, *all* future forecasts equal $\bar{Y}$:

$$F_{2023} = F_{2024} = F_{2025} = \bar{Y} = 114 \text{ lakhs}$$

---

### 3.1.3 Step 3 – Compute Forecast Errors $e_t = Y_t - F_t$ (Fitted Values)

Since the model is fitted retrospectively on the same data, we compute "fitted" errors. Because $\bar{Y} = 114$ is the forecast for **every** period once it has been computed, we use it to evaluate fit on the same data:

| Year | $Y_t$ | $F_t = \bar{Y}$ | $e_t = Y_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $e_t^2$ | $\left\vert \frac{e_t}{Y_t} \right\vert \times 100$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2018 | 100 | 114 | $100 - 114 = -14$ | 14 | 196 | 14.000% |
| 2019 | 110 | 114 | $110 - 114 = -4$ | 4 | 16 | 3.636% |
| 2020 | 120 | 114 | $120 - 114 = 6$ | 6 | 36 | 5.000% |
| 2021 | 115 | 114 | $115 - 114 = 1$ | 1 | 1 | 0.870% |
| 2022 | 125 | 114 | $125 - 114 = 11$ | 11 | 121 | 8.800% |
| **Sum** | 570 | 570 | **0** | **36** | **370** | **32.306%** |

> **Self-Check:** $\sum e_t = -14 - 4 + 6 + 1 + 11 = 0$ ✔ — confirming the KTU property of the Mean Method.

---

### 3.1.4 Step 4 – Compute MAD, MSE, RMSE, MAPE

**Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD):**

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert Y_t - F_t \vert = \frac{36}{5} = 7.2 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Mean Squared Error (MSE):**

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} (Y_t - F_t)^2 = \frac{370}{5} = 74.0 \text{ (lakhs)}^2$$

**Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE):**

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}} = \sqrt{74.0} \approx 8.6023 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE):**

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{100}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \left\vert \frac{Y_t - F_t}{Y_t} \right\vert = \frac{32.306}{5} \approx 6.461\%$$

---

## 3.2 Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Forecast for 2023 | ₹114 lakhs |
| Forecast for 2024 | ₹114 lakhs |
| Forecast for 2025 | ₹114 lakhs |
| MAD | 7.2 lakhs |
| MSE | 74.0 (lakhs)² |
| RMSE | 8.6023 lakhs |
| MAPE | 6.461% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A MAPE of ~6.5% is **reasonably good** for a real-world baseline. However, because the data shows a slight *upward drift* (100 → 125), the Average Method is **slightly biased low** for later years. A Linear Trend Method (covered later in Module 1) would outperform it.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Average vs Naive vs Drift

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison that frequently appears in KTU 2-mark and 12-mark questions.**

| Criterion | Average Method | Naive Method | Drift Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Formula | $F_{t+h} = \bar{Y}$ | $F_{t+h} = Y_t$ | $F_{t+h} = Y_t + h \cdot \frac{Y_t - Y_1}{t-1}$ |
| Parameters | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Uses | All history | Only last value | First & last value |
| Captures Trend? | No | No | Yes (linear) |
| Captures Seasonality? | No | No | No |
| Forecast shape | Constant flat line | Constant flat line | Linear ramp |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram – Average Method Pipeline

The following Mermaid flowchart describes the operational pipeline of the Average Method, from data ingestion to forecast issuance.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Historical Time Series Data] --> B[Step 1: Collect Observations Y1, Y2, ..., Yt]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Arithmetic Mean Ybar]
    C --> D[Step 3: Store Ybar as the Forecast Model]
    D --> E[Issue Forecasts]
    E --> F[F sub t+1 = Ybar]
    E --> G[F sub t+2 = Ybar]
    E --> H[F sub t+3 = Ybar]
    E --> I[F sub t+h = Ybar for all h]
    J[Evaluate Performance] --> K[Compute MAD]
    J --> L[Compute MSE]
    J --> M[Compute RMSE]
    J --> N[Compute MAPE]
    D --> J
```

---

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart – When to Use Average Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Is the Time Series Data Available] --> Q{Does the Series Show Trend}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Do NOT use Average Method]
    Q -- No --> S{Does the Series Show Seasonality}
    S -- Yes --> T[Do NOT use Average Method]
    S -- No --> U{Is the Variance Constant Over Time}
    U -- No --> V[Do NOT use Average Method]
    U -- Yes --> W[Average Method is Appropriate]
    W --> X[Compute Mean and Issue Constant Forecast]
```

---

## 4.3 Graphical Schematic – Forecast vs Actual (Block Schematic)

The following schematic illustrates the typical behaviour of the Average Method when applied to a mildly trending series. The forecast line is a **horizontal line at $\bar{Y}$**, while the actual values drift upward.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Actual_Data
        A18[2018: 100] --- A19[2019: 110]
        A19 --- A20[2020: 120]
        A20 --- A21[2021: 115]
        A21 --- A22[2022: 125]
    end
    subgraph Forecast_Line
        F23[2023: 114]
        F24[2024: 114]
        F25[2025: 114]
    end
    A22 ==> F23
    F23 ==> F24
    F24 ==> F25
    A22 -. gap of 11 .-> F23
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** The vertical "gap of 11" represents the bias introduced by the Average Method when the series has a positive drift. The model **under-forecasts** 2023 by ₹11 lakhs because it ignores the upward trajectory.

---

## 4.4 Error Decomposition Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Forecast Error] --> B[Random Component]
    A --> C[Bias Component]
    C --> D[Trend Bias]
    C --> E[Seasonal Bias]
    B --> F[Irreducible Noise]
    G[Average Method] -->|Ignores D and E| H[Bias Dominates]
    G -->|Captures B well| I[Good on Pure Noise Series]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### **Q1. Define the Average Method of forecasting. State any two of its limitations.**  **[3 Marks]**  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition (2 marks):** The Average Method (or Mean Method) is a simple quantitative forecasting technique in which the forecast for every future period is set equal to the arithmetic mean of all available historical observations. Mathematically, $F_{t+h} = \bar{Y} = \frac{1}{t} \sum_{i=1}^{t} Y_i$.

> **Limitations (1 mark — any two of the following):**
> 1. It cannot capture **trend** — the forecast is a flat line that ignores systematic upward or downward movement.
> 2. It cannot capture **seasonality** — periodic fluctuations are smoothed into the mean.
> 3. It assumes the series is **stationary**, which is rarely true in real-world data.
> 4. It treats all past observations as **equally important**, including very old, possibly outdated data.

---

### **Q2. The last 4 years' sales of a product are 200, 220, 210, 230 units. Compute the Average Method forecast for the next year.**  **[3 Marks]**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{200 + 220 + 210 + 230}{4} = \frac{860}{4} = 215 \text{ units}$$

> **Forecast for next year:** $F_5 = \bar{Y} = \mathbf{215 \text{ units}}$.

---

## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

> The quarterly demand (in '000 units) of a product over the last 6 quarters is: **120, 135, 128, 142, 138, 150**.
>
> **(a)** Compute the Average Method forecast for the 7th, 8th, and 9th quarters.  **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the MAD, MSE, RMSE, and MAPE for the model on the historical data. Comment on the accuracy of the model.  **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

We have $Y_1 = 120, Y_2 = 135, Y_3 = 128, Y_4 = 142, Y_5 = 138, Y_6 = 150$ and $t = 6$.

**Step 1: Compute the historical mean $\bar{Y}$:**

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{120 + 135 + 128 + 142 + 138 + 150}{6} = \frac{813}{6} = 135.5 \text{ thousand units}$$

**Step 2: Issue forecasts:**

$$F_7 = F_8 = F_9 = \bar{Y} = 135.5 \text{ thousand units}$$

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1: Compute the error table:**

| Quarter $t$ | $Y_t$ | $F_t = 135.5$ | $e_t = Y_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $e_t^2$ | $\left\vert \frac{e_t}{Y_t} \right\vert \times 100$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 120 | 135.5 | $-15.5$ | 15.5 | 240.25 | 12.917% |
| 2 | 135 | 135.5 | $-0.5$ | 0.5 | 0.25 | 0.370% |
| 3 | 128 | 135.5 | $-7.5$ | 7.5 | 56.25 | 5.859% |
| 4 | 142 | 135.5 | $+6.5$ | 6.5 | 42.25 | 4.577% |
| 5 | 138 | 135.5 | $+2.5$ | 2.5 | 6.25 | 1.812% |
| 6 | 150 | 135.5 | $+14.5$ | 14.5 | 210.25 | 9.667% |
| **Sum** | 813 | 813.0 | **0.0** | **47.0** | **555.50** | **35.202%** |

> **Self-Check:** $\sum e_t = -15.5 - 0.5 - 7.5 + 6.5 + 2.5 + 14.5 = 0$ ✔

**Step 2: Compute the error metrics:**

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{47.0}{6} \approx 7.833 \text{ thousand units}$$

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{555.50}{6} \approx 92.583 \text{ (thousand units)}^2$$

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{92.583} \approx 9.622 \text{ thousand units}$$

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{35.202}{6} \approx 5.867\%$$

**Step 3: Comment on accuracy:**

> The MAPE of **5.87%** indicates a reasonably accurate baseline forecast. However, observing the residuals, the errors show a systematic **negative-to-positive pattern** (early quarters under-forecast, later quarters over-forecast), suggesting the presence of a **mild upward trend**. The Average Method, being a flat forecast, is **slightly biased** in this context. A Linear Trend Method (covered in subsequent topics) would yield a better fit.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

> The annual production (in tonnes) of a manufacturing unit from 2017 to 2023 is: **400, 420, 410, 430, 425, 440, 450**.
>
> **(a)** Apply the Average Method to forecast production for 2024, 2025, and 2026.  **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Compare the Average Method with the **Naive Method** in terms of forecast values, suitability, and limitations for this dataset.  **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

We have $Y_1 = 400, Y_2 = 420, Y_3 = 410, Y_4 = 430, Y_5 = 425, Y_6 = 440, Y_7 = 450$ and $t = 7$.

**Step 1: Compute the historical mean:**

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{400 + 420 + 410 + 430 + 425 + 440 + 450}{7} = \frac{2975}{7} = 425 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2: Issue forecasts for 2024, 2025, 2026:**

$$F_{2024} = F_{2025} = F_{2026} = \bar{Y} = 425 \text{ tonnes}$$

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

| Comparison Aspect | Average Method | Naive Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $F_{t+h} = \bar{Y} = 425$ for all $h$ | $F_{t+h} = Y_t = 450$ for all $h$ |
| **Forecasts (2024/2025/2026)** | 425, 425, 425 | 450, 450, 450 |
| **Information used** | All 7 years | Only the most recent year (2023) |
| **Suitability for this dataset** | Suboptimal — ignores upward trend | Slightly better — at least uses latest value (closer to current level) |
| **Captures trend?** | No | No |
| **Captures seasonality?** | No | No |
| **Strength** | Smoothed, stable, less sensitive to outliers | Captures the most recent level of the series |
| **Limitation** | Reacts slowly to changes (lags the trend) | Can be thrown off by a single anomalous year |

> **Verdict:** For this clearly upward-trending series, **neither method is ideal**, but the Naive Method (450 tonnes) is closer to the 2023 value (450) and would have a **lower MAD** on the most recent observations. The Average Method (425 tonnes) is more representative of the *overall* historical level but **under-forecasts** 2024 by 25 tonnes. A **Linear Trend Model** or **Simple Exponential Smoothing** would be superior.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by $n$:** Many students compute SSE and write it as MSE. Always remember to divide by the number of observations.
> 2. **Confusing the data splits:** The forecast for *future* periods uses $\bar{Y}$, but the *fitted* errors for MAD/MSE also use the same $\bar{Y}$ retroactively. KTU examiners *will* penalize students who use $F_t = Y_{t-1}$ (Naive) when the question explicitly asks for the Average Method.
> 3. **Forgetting the percentage in MAPE:** MAPE must be expressed as a **percentage**, not a decimal. Writing 0.0587 instead of 5.87% loses marks.
> 4. **Not verifying $\sum e_t = 0$:** KTU's model answer scripts explicitly include this self-check. Omitting it forfeits the "self-check" step marks.
> 5. **Misidentifying the number of periods $t$:** Using $t = 6$ when there are 7 observations is a costly off-by-one error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Average Method**
>
> - ⭐ The **Average Method** uses the **arithmetic mean** of all historical data as the forecast for *every* future period: $F_{t+h} = \bar{Y}$.
> - ⭐ It is a **parameter-free** baseline model with **zero learning parameters**.
> - ⭐ The forecast is a **flat horizontal line** at the level of the mean — it **cannot** capture trend or seasonality.
> - ⭐ A key mathematical property: **$\sum e_t = 0$** (sum of all fitted errors equals zero). Use this as a self-check.
> - ⭐ The four standard error metrics to compute are **MAD, MSE, RMSE, and MAPE**.
> - ⭐ **MAD** uses absolute values; **MSE/RMSE** use squared errors (penalize large errors more); **MAPE** is scale-independent.
> - ⭐ It is **most appropriate for stationary series** with no trend and no seasonality.
> - ⭐ It serves as a **universal benchmark** — any sophisticated model (ARIMA, Exponential Smoothing) must beat the Average Method to be considered useful.
> - ⭐ In KTU exams, the method is frequently tested alongside the **Naive Method** and **Drift Method** for comparison.
> - ⭐ The MAPE of a "good" baseline Average Method forecast is typically **< 10%**; values > 20% indicate the model is unsuitable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Naïve Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Naïve Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Naïve Forecast (Last Observation Carried Forward — LOCF)**
> The **Naïve Method** is the simplest univariate point forecasting technique in time series analysis in which the forecast for any future period is set equal to the actual observed value of the most recent past period. Formally, for a time series $\{y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_T\}$, the $h$-step-ahead naïve forecast is given by
> $$\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T, \quad h = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the naïve method is classified under **Baseline / Benchmark Forecasting Models** and serves as the *reference benchmark* against which the predictive performance of more sophisticated models (e.g., Exponential Smoothing, ARIMA, Holt-Winters) is compared. The forecast horizon $h$ is the number of future steps being predicted from the forecast origin $T$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Memory of a Goldfish"**
> Imagine you are standing at a bus stop and have absolutely no information about bus schedules. The last bus arrived **5 minutes ago**. The most logical (and naïve) guess for *when the next bus will come* is — **"right now, it should have just arrived"**, because the only piece of evidence you possess is the last event.
> 
> In time series language: *the most recent observation is your entire knowledge base.* You project it forward as if **time stands still**.

In essence, the Naïve Method assumes:
- The future will replicate the immediate past.
- The series has **no trend** and **no seasonality**.
- All stochastic information is already contained in the last data point.

### 1.3 Why Study a "Naïve" Method?

Even though the method is mathematically trivial, it is **academically indispensable** in KTU 2024 syllabus because:

1. It acts as a **performance floor** — any model that cannot outperform the naïve forecast is considered useless.
2. It is the **first benchmark** in any forecasting competition (e.g., M-competition, M4).
3. It provides a closed-form baseline for computing **error metrics** like MAE, MSE, RMSE, MAPE.
4. It is the **optimal forecast** under the random walk assumption: $y_t = y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$.

### 1.4 Key Terminology in Bold

- **Forecast Origin ($T$):** The time index from which future predictions begin.
- **Forecast Horizon ($h$):** Number of future periods predicted.
- **Forecast ($\hat{y}_{T+h}$):** The predicted value for period $T+h$.
- **Forecast Error ($e_t$):** $e_t = y_t - \hat{y}_t$ (also called residual).
- **Random Walk:** A stochastic process where each step is the previous step plus noise.
- **Baseline Benchmark:** Reference model for comparison.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Naïve Method operates on a single, deterministic principle. The operational logic is decomposed below:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Forecast Origin:** Locate the most recent observed data point $y_T$ in the series. This is the *only* input required.
- **Step 2 — Freeze the Last Value:** Treat $y_T$ as the "anchor" or "level" of the process.
- **Step 3 — Propagate Horizontally:** For every future period $T+1, T+2, \ldots, T+h$, output the same numerical value $y_T$.
- **Step 4 — Compute Errors (Post-hoc):** Once actual future values are realized, compute residuals $e_{T+k} = y_{T+k} - \hat{y}_{T+k}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Implicit Assumption — Stationarity of Level**
> The Naïve Method implicitly assumes that the *mean level* of the series remains constant over the forecast horizon. If the series exhibits a deterministic trend, the method will systematically **under-forecast** (in an uptrend) or **over-forecast** (in a downtrend).

### 2.2 Underlying Probabilistic Foundation

If we model the time series as a **Random Walk**:

$$y_t = y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma^2)$$

where $\text{WN}$ denotes **White Noise** with mean **0** and constant variance $\sigma^2$, then the *minimum mean squared error* forecast for any future period is exactly the last observation:

$$\hat{y}_{T+h} = \mathbb{E}[y_{T+h} \mid y_T, y_{T-1}, \ldots] = y_T$$

This is a key theoretical result: the naïve method is **statistically optimal** under the random walk model.

### 2.3 Error Metrics for the Naïve Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to compute and interpret the following accuracy metrics. All formulas are for *in-sample* or *hold-out* evaluation over $n$ forecasted periods.

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Interpretation** | **KTU Weightage** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Error (ME) | $ME = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t$ | Same as $y_t$ | Measures **bias**; should be near zero for unbiased forecasts | High |
| Mean Absolute Error | $MAE = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert e_t \vert$ | Same as $y_t$ | Average magnitude of errors; robust to outliers | High |
| Mean Squared Error | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^2$ | Squared unit of $y_t$ | Penalizes large errors heavily | High |
| Root Mean Squared Error | $RMSE = \sqrt{MSE}$ | Same as $y_t$ | Standard deviation of forecast errors; in same units as data | **Very High** |
| Mean Absolute Percentage Error | $MAPE = \dfrac{100}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \left\vert \dfrac{e_t}{y_t} \right\vert$ | Percentage | Scale-independent; useful for cross-series comparison | Medium |

> **Note on Notation:** In the formulas above, the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value (NOT a markdown table delimiter).

### 2.4 Forecast Interval (Approximate)

For a random walk process, the $h$-step-ahead forecast variance grows linearly with $h$:

$$\text{Var}(y_{T+h} - \hat{y}_{T+h}) = h \cdot \sigma^2$$

Hence, an approximate $100(1-\alpha)\%$ **prediction interval** is:

$$\hat{y}_{T+h} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma \sqrt{h}$$

where $z_{\alpha/2}$ is the standard normal critical value (e.g., **1.96** for 95% confidence) and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of the one-step-ahead forecast errors.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Demand Forecasting:** Short-horizon stock-level prediction in supply chains.
- **Signal Processing:** Used as a primitive predictor in adaptive filtering.
- **Telecommunications:** Network traffic baseline estimation.
- **IoT & Embedded Systems:** Zero-overhead predictor in low-memory devices.
- **Financial Engineering:** The random walk hypothesis underpins naïve stock price models.
- **Energy Systems:** Short-term load forecasting in microgrids (as a benchmark).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T$ is the Optimal MSE Forecast

Consider a generic time series $y_t$. We want to find a forecast function $f$ that minimizes the **expected squared forecast error**:

$$\mathbb{E}[(y_{T+h} - f)^2]$$

This is a standard least-squares minimization problem.

**Step 1 — Expand the squared term:**

$$\mathbb{E}[(y_{T+h} - f)^2] = \mathbb{E}[(y_{T+h} - \mu_{T+h} + \mu_{T+h} - f)^2]$$

where $\mu_{T+h} = \mathbb{E}[y_{T+h}]$.

**Step 2 — Split into variance + bias²:**

$$\mathbb{E}[(y_{T+h} - f)^2] = \underbrace{\text{Var}(y_{T+h})}_{\text{irreducible}} + \underbrace{(\mu_{T+h} - f)^2}_{\text{bias squared}}$$

**Step 3 — Minimize over $f$:**

Taking derivative with respect to $f$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{d}{df}\left[(\mu_{T+h} - f)^2\right] = -2(\mu_{T+h} - f) = 0$$

Solving yields $f^* = \mu_{T+h}$.

**Step 4 — Apply the random walk assumption:**

For a random walk $y_t = y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, we have:

$$\mathbb{E}[y_{T+h} \mid y_T] = y_T + \sum_{k=1}^{h}\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_{T+k}] = y_T + 0 = y_T$$

Therefore, the optimal MSE forecast is:

$$\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Fully Exhaustive)

**Given Data (Quarterly Sales in ₹ lakhs):**

| Period ($t$) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sales ($y_t$) | 50 | 55 | 52 | 58 | 60 | 57 | 62 | 65 |

**Task:** Generate naïve forecasts for periods 2 through 8 and compute MAE, MSE, RMSE, MAPE.

#### Step 1 — Generate Naïve Forecasts

The naïve forecast for any period $t$ is the previous period's actual value: $\hat{y}_t = y_{t-1}$.

$$\hat{y}_2 = y_1 = 50$$
$$\hat{y}_3 = y_2 = 55$$
$$\hat{y}_4 = y_3 = 52$$
$$\hat{y}_5 = y_4 = 58$$
$$\hat{y}_6 = y_5 = 60$$
$$\hat{y}_7 = y_6 = 57$$
$$\hat{y}_8 = y_7 = 62$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Forecast Errors

Using $e_t = y_t - \hat{y}_t$:

| $t$ | $y_t$ | $\hat{y}_t$ | $e_t$ | $e_t^2$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $\left\vert \dfrac{e_t}{y_t} \right\vert \times 100$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 55 | 50 | $5$ | $25$ | $5$ | $9.09$ |
| 3 | 52 | 55 | $-3$ | $9$ | $3$ | $5.77$ |
| 4 | 58 | 52 | $6$ | $36$ | $6$ | $10.34$ |
| 5 | 60 | 58 | $2$ | $4$ | $2$ | $3.33$ |
| 6 | 57 | 60 | $-3$ | $9$ | $3$ | $5.26$ |
| 7 | 62 | 57 | $5$ | $25$ | $5$ | $8.06$ |
| 8 | 65 | 62 | $3$ | $9$ | $3$ | $4.62$ |

#### Step 3 — Aggregate Sums

We have $n = 7$ forecast observations (periods 2 through 8):

$$\sum e_t = 5 + (-3) + 6 + 2 + (-3) + 5 + 3 = 15$$
$$\sum e_t^2 = 25 + 9 + 36 + 4 + 9 + 25 + 9 = 117$$
$$\sum \vert e_t \vert = 5 + 3 + 6 + 2 + 3 + 5 + 3 = 27$$
$$\sum \left\vert \dfrac{e_t}{y_t} \right\vert = 9.09 + 5.77 + 10.34 + 3.33 + 5.26 + 8.06 + 4.62 = 46.47$$

#### Step 4 — Compute Final Metrics

**Mean Error (ME):**

$$ME = \frac{\sum e_t}{n} = \frac{15}{7} \approx 2.143$$

**Mean Absolute Error (MAE):**

$$MAE = \frac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n} = \frac{27}{7} \approx 3.857 \text{ ₹ lakhs}$$

**Mean Squared Error (MSE):**

$$MSE = \frac{\sum e_t^2}{n} = \frac{117}{7} \approx 16.714 \text{ (₹ lakhs)}^2$$

**Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE):**

$$RMSE = \sqrt{MSE} = \sqrt{\frac{117}{7}} = \sqrt{16.714} \approx 4.088 \text{ ₹ lakhs}$$

**Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE):**

$$MAPE = \frac{1}{n}\sum \left\vert \frac{e_t}{y_t} \right\vert \times 100 = \frac{46.47}{7} \approx 6.638\,\%$$

### 3.3 Out-of-Sample Forecast Demonstration

Suppose we are required to forecast periods 9 and 10 (one-step and two-step ahead) from the same series.

**One-step-ahead forecast (from $T=8$):**

$$\hat{y}_9 = y_8 = 65$$

**Two-step-ahead forecast (from $T=8$):**

$$\hat{y}_{10} = y_8 = 65 \quad (\text{same value, flat projection})$$

**95% Prediction Interval for $\hat{y}_9$:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{MSE} = 4.088$$

$$\hat{y}_9 \pm 1.96 \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{1} = 65 \pm (1.96)(4.088) = 65 \pm 8.013$$

$$\therefore \text{95\% PI: } [56.99,\ 73.01] \text{ ₹ lakhs}$$

**95% Prediction Interval for $\hat{y}_{10}$:**

$$\hat{y}_{10} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{2} = 65 \pm 1.96 \cdot 4.088 \cdot 1.4142$$

$$= 65 \pm 11.33 \approx [53.67,\ 76.33] \text{ ₹ lakhs}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The prediction interval **widens with horizon** $h$ as $\sqrt{h}$ — this is a hallmark of the random walk structure.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Naïve Forecasting Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Time Series y1 to yT] --> B[Identify Last Observation yT]
    B --> C[Freeze yT as Anchor Value]
    C --> D[Forecast Horizon h defined]
    D --> E{Repeated h times}
    E --> F[Output yT for Period T plus 1]
    E --> G[Output yT for Period T plus 2]
    E --> H[Output yT for Period T plus h]
    F --> I[Generate Prediction Interval using sigma times sqrt h]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Forecast Vector yT to yT plus h]
    J --> K[Compute Error Metrics MAE MSE RMSE MAPE]
    K --> L[Compare with Advanced Models ARIMA ETS]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Flowchart — Naïve Method Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Past Data Available] --> Q{Length greater than 1}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Pick yT]
    Q -->|No| S[Method Not Applicable]
    R --> T[Set yHAT_Tplus1 = yT]
    R --> U[Set yHAT_Tplus2 = yT]
    R --> V[Set yHAT_TplusH = yT]
    T --> W[Flat Forecast Line]
    U --> W
    V --> W
    W --> X[Evaluate via Hold Out]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Forecast vs. Actual Visualization (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Past
        A1[y1] --> A2[y2] --> A3[y3] --> A4[y4] --> A5[yT]
    end
    subgraph Future Forecast Region
        A5 --> B1[yHAT T plus 1 equals yT]
        A5 --> B2[yHAT T plus 2 equals yT]
        A5 --> B3[yHAT T plus H equals yT]
    end
    B1 -.-> C1[Error eT plus 1]
    B2 -.-> C2[Error eT plus 2]
    B3 -.-> C3[Error eT plus H]
    C1 --> D[Aggregate into MAE MSE RMSE]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
```

### 4.4 Diagram — Error Metric Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[Compute Residuals e_t = y_t minus yHAT_t] --> E2[Square e_t]
    E1 --> E3[Absolute e_t]
    E1 --> E4[Percentage e_t over y_t]
    E2 --> F1[Sum and Divide by n gives MSE]
    E3 --> F2[Sum and Divide by n gives MAE]
    E4 --> F3[Sum and Divide by n times 100 gives MAPE]
    F1 --> G1[Square Root gives RMSE]
    F1 --> F4[Sum and Divide by n gives ME]
    G1 --> H[Final Accuracy Report]
    F2 --> H
    F3 --> H
    F4 --> H
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Define the Naïve forecasting method and state its key assumption.**
> 
> **Model Answer:**
> The Naïve forecasting method assumes that the most recent observation is the best estimate of all future values. Formally, $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T$ for $h \geq 1$. The key assumption is that the time series is a **random walk** with no trend and no seasonality — i.e., the level of the series remains constant over the forecast horizon. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q2. Why is the Naïve method used as a benchmark in time series forecasting?**
> 
> **Model Answer:**
> The Naïve method serves as a **benchmark** because it provides a baseline level of accuracy that any sophisticated model (e.g., ARIMA, Exponential Smoothing) must outperform to be considered useful. If a complex model cannot beat the Naïve forecast, it indicates overfitting, model misspecification, or that the series is genuinely random. The M-competitions historically used the Naïve method as a reference. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PECDT861]**
> 
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> 
> **(a)** Derive the Naïve forecast formula and explain its relationship with the random walk model. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Given the time series $y = \{23, 28, 25, 30, 32, 29, 34, 36\}$, apply the Naïve method and compute MAE, MSE, RMSE, and MAPE. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation and Random Walk Relationship (7 marks):**

The Naïve forecast is $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T$.

**Step 1:** The random walk model is defined as:

$$y_t = y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma^2)$$

**Step 2:** Taking conditional expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}[y_{T+h} \mid y_T] = y_T + \sum_{k=1}^{h} \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_{T+k}] = y_T$$

**Step 3:** Since the expected noise terms are zero, the optimal MSE forecast is the last observation.

**Step 4:** Conclusion — Under the random walk assumption, the Naïve forecast is **statistically optimal**, and the prediction interval widens as $\sigma\sqrt{h}$.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

| $t$ | $y_t$ | $\hat{y}_t = y_{t-1}$ | $e_t$ | $e_t^2$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $\vert e_t / y_t \vert \times 100$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 28 | 23 | 5 | 25 | 5 | 17.86 |
| 3 | 25 | 28 | -3 | 9 | 3 | 12.00 |
| 4 | 30 | 25 | 5 | 25 | 5 | 16.67 |
| 5 | 32 | 30 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 6.25 |
| 6 | 29 | 32 | -3 | 9 | 3 | 10.34 |
| 7 | 34 | 29 | 5 | 25 | 5 | 14.71 |
| 8 | 36 | 34 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 5.56 |

**Aggregates:** $\sum e_t = 13$, $\sum e_t^2 = 101$, $\sum \vert e_t \vert = 25$, $\sum \vert e_t/y_t \vert = 83.39$, $n = 7$.

**Final metrics:**

$$MAE = \frac{25}{7} \approx 3.571$$

$$MSE = \frac{101}{7} \approx 14.429$$

$$RMSE = \sqrt{14.429} \approx 3.798$$

$$MAPE = \frac{83.39}{7} \approx 11.913\%$$

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*
> 
> **(a)** Explain the Naïve forecasting method with its mathematical formulation. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A retail store records weekly sales (in units): $\{120, 135, 128, 142, 150, 145, 155, 160\}$. Using the Naïve method: (i) Generate forecasts for weeks 2–8, (ii) Compute MAE, MSE, RMSE, and MAPE, (iii) Generate a 95% prediction interval for the forecast of week 9. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Explanation, Advantages, Limitations (7 marks):**

**Mathematical Formulation:** $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T$.

**Advantages:**
- Simplicity: No parameter estimation required.
- Speed: Computationally O(1).
- Benchmark: Reference for advanced models.
- Optimal under random walk.
- Zero storage / zero model training overhead.

**Limitations:**
- Cannot capture trend.
- Cannot capture seasonality.
- Equal weight to all past data — ignores older information structure.
- Flat forecast profile regardless of horizon.
- Wide prediction intervals for long horizons.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

| $t$ | $y_t$ | $\hat{y}_t$ | $e_t$ | $e_t^2$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $\vert e_t / y_t \vert \times 100$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 135 | 120 | 15 | 225 | 15 | 11.11 |
| 3 | 128 | 135 | -7 | 49 | 7 | 5.47 |
| 4 | 142 | 128 | 14 | 196 | 14 | 9.86 |
| 5 | 150 | 142 | 8 | 64 | 8 | 5.33 |
| 6 | 145 | 150 | -5 | 25 | 5 | 3.45 |
| 7 | 155 | 145 | 10 | 100 | 10 | 6.45 |
| 8 | 160 | 155 | 5 | 25 | 5 | 3.13 |

**Aggregates:** $\sum e_t^2 = 684$, $\sum \vert e_t \vert = 64$, $\sum \vert e_t/y_t \vert = 44.80$, $n = 7$.

**Final metrics:**

$$MAE = \frac{64}{7} \approx 9.143$$

$$MSE = \frac{684}{7} \approx 97.714$$

$$RMSE = \sqrt{97.714} \approx 9.885$$

$$MAPE = \frac{44.80}{7} \approx 6.40\%$$

**(iii) 95% Prediction Interval for Week 9:**

$$\hat{y}_9 = y_8 = 160$$

$$\sigma = RMSE = 9.885$$

$$PI = 160 \pm 1.96 \times 9.885 \times \sqrt{1} = 160 \pm 19.375$$

$$\boxed{\text{95\% PI: } [140.625,\ 179.375] \text{ units}}$$

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Off-by-one in $n$:** Always use $n = T - 1$ (number of forecasts) and NOT $n = T$ (total observations) when computing error metrics. The first period has no forecast.
> 2. **Unit confusion in MSE vs RMSE:** MSE is in *squared units*; RMSE is in *original units*. Do not report both as having the same scale.
> 3. **MAPE division by zero:** If any $y_t = 0$, MAPE is undefined — KTU examiners penalize this.
> 4. **Prediction interval formula:** The square root term is $\sqrt{h}$ — many students forget the horizon multiplier.
> 5. **Confusing Naïve with Seasonal Naïve:** Naïve uses $y_T$; Seasonal Naïve uses $y_{T-s}$ where $s$ is the season length. Examiners often test this distinction.
> 6. **No unit labels:** Always carry units (₹, units, kg) in final numerical answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Naïve Method**

- **Core Formula:** $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T$ — *the last value is the only input.*
- **Underlying Model:** Random walk $y_t = y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$.
- **Optimality:** MSE-optimal under random walk assumption.
- **Forecast Profile:** Constant horizontal line for all $h$.
- **Error Metrics to Memorize:** ME, MAE, MSE, RMSE, MAPE.
- **Prediction Interval:** $\hat{y}_{T+h} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{h}$.
- **Variance Growth:** Forecast variance $\propto h$.
- **Critical Use:** **Benchmark model** — any advanced model must outperform this.
- **Limitations:** No trend, no seasonality, ignores historical structure.
- **Extension Awareness:** Seasonal Naïve uses $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_{T+h-s}$ where $s$ = season length.
- **Practical Tip:** When in doubt in an exam, always state the formula, then the assumption, then the limitation.
- **KTU 2024 Weightage:** Expect a 3-mark definition in Part A and a 7–14 mark numerical in Part B of ESE.
- **Z-value to remember:** $z_{0.025} = 1.96$ for 95% confidence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drift Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Drift Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Drift Method** is a fundamental benchmark forecasting technique in time series analysis, classified under the family of *simple extrapolation methods*. Formally, it is defined as a forecasting procedure in which the forecast for a future horizon $h$ is obtained by extrapolating the **average historical change** (termed the *drift*) observed between the first and last observations of the available training data.

Mathematically, the forecast $\hat{y}_{T+h}$ is given by:

$$\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T + h \cdot d, \quad d = \frac{y_T - y_1}{T - 1}$$

where:
- $y_1$ = first observation of the series
- $y_T$ = last observation of the series
- $T$ = total number of observations
- $d$ = average drift (change per time period)
- $h$ = forecast horizon (number of steps ahead)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Drift Method is taught in Module 1 as a **baseline benchmark** before students proceed to more sophisticated methods such as Exponential Smoothing (ETS) and ARIMA. It serves as a *reference forecast* against which the performance of advanced models is evaluated using error metrics such as MAPE, RMSE, and MASE.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **hiker tracking your elevation** over a 10-day trek. On day 1, you started at 1000 meters, and by day 10, you reached 2500 meters. You didn't climb in a straight line — some days were flat, some were steep, some you even descended. However, on **average**, you gained $(2500 - 1000) / 9 \approx 167$ meters per day.

If someone asks: *"Where will you be on day 15?"*, the **Drift Method** says: *"Just continue with the same average rate of climb."*

So your predicted elevation on day 15 = $2500 + 5 \times 167 = 3335$ meters.

In essence, the Drift Method **draws a single straight line** from your starting point to your ending point and **projects that line forward**, ignoring all the zigzags (seasonality, noise, cycles) in between.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Drift Method is geometrically equivalent to fitting a **linear regression line passing through the first and last observations** and extrapolating it. It captures the *first-order trend* of the data but completely ignores seasonality, cyclicality, and random fluctuations.

## 1.3 Properties and Characteristics

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Method Class** | Naive/Trend Benchmark |
| **Complexity** | Very Low (Trivial) |
| **Parameters Required** | 2 (first and last value) |
| **Trend Handling** | Linear (Constant Drift) |
| **Seasonality Handling** | None |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(T)$ |
| **Forecast Variance** | Increases with horizon $h$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric:** The drift $d$ has the same units as the original series divided by the unit of time (e.g., *units/month*, *₹/quarter*, *sales/year*).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

The Drift Method can be decomposed into the following sequential logic steps:

### Step 1: Identify the Boundary Observations
Extract the first observation $y_1$ and the last observation $y_T$ from the training time series of length $T$.

### Step 2: Compute the Total Change
Calculate the net change across the entire series:
$$\Delta_{\text{total}} = y_T - y_1$$

### Step 3: Compute the Average Drift
Divide the total change by the number of inter-observation intervals $(T-1)$:
$$d = \frac{y_T - y_1}{T - 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The denominator is $T - 1$ (not $T$) because there are $T - 1$ *gaps* between $T$ data points. This is the same convention used in sample variance calculation.

### Step 4: Generate the Forecast
For any forecast horizon $h = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, H$, extrapolate linearly:
$$\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T + h \cdot d$$

### Step 5 (Conceptual): Why It Works as a Benchmark
The Drift Method is *always available* without any model fitting. Even when the series is highly non-linear, it provides a **non-trivial baseline** — typically far better than simply predicting $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T$ (the pure naive method) for trending data.

## 2.2 Alternative Mathematical Formulations

The Drift Method can be equivalently expressed in three useful forms:

**Form 1 — Incremental Extrapolation:**
$$\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T + h \cdot \frac{y_T - y_1}{T - 1}$$

**Form 2 — Line Projection from Origin:**
$$\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_1 + (T - 1 + h) \cdot \frac{y_T - y_1}{T - 1}$$

This shows clearly that the forecast is a linear projection of the line from $y_1$ to $y_T$.

**Form 3 — Slope-Intercept Form:**
$$\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T + h \cdot \bar{d}$$
where $\bar{d}$ denotes the average first difference:
$$\bar{d} = \frac{1}{T-1} \sum_{t=2}^{T} (y_t - y_{t-1})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalence Proof Sketch:** Since the sum of consecutive first differences telescopes:
> $$\sum_{t=2}^{T} (y_t - y_{t-1}) = y_T - y_1$$
> It follows that $\bar{d} = (y_T - y_1)/(T-1) = d$. All three forms yield identical forecasts.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{y_T - y_1}{T - 1}$ | Average drift per period | (data units) / period |
| 2 | $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T + h \cdot d$ | $h$-step ahead forecast | data units |
| 3 | $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T + h \cdot \dfrac{y_T - y_1}{T - 1}$ | Expanded forecast form | data units |
| 4 | $e_t = y_t - \hat{y}_t$ | Forecast residual at time $t$ | data units |
| 5 | $\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum \left \vert \dfrac{y_t - \hat{y}_t}{y_t} \right \vert \times 100$ | Mean Absolute Percentage Error | percent (\%) |
| 6 | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n} \sum (y_t - \hat{y}_t)^2}$ | Root Mean Squared Error | data units |
| 7 | $h_{\max} \leq T$ | Practical horizon limit (data availability) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Var}(\hat{y}_{T+h}) = h^2 \cdot \sigma_d^2$ | Approximate forecast variance (large $h$) | (data units)$^2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Note for Tables:** The vertical bars for absolute value in MAPE have been escaped as `\left \vert` and `\right \vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. Do **not** use raw `\|` symbols inside table rows.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Drift Method, despite its simplicity, is extensively used in:

- **Demand Forecasting:** Initial baseline in retail and supply chain management before deploying ARIMA or ML models.
- **Energy Load Prediction:** Quick projection of baseline electricity consumption trends in power grids.
- **Financial Benchmarking:** Used as a "zero intelligence" baseline in algorithmic trading research.
- **Quality Control:** Drift detection in sensor networks (SPC charts) — the term "drift" here represents the same statistical concept.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Estimating long-term trends in pollution, temperature, or water level data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Direct Computation

**Problem Statement:**
A manufacturing unit records the following monthly production (in thousand units) for the first 6 months of 2024:

| Month ($t$) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Production ($y_t$) | 100 | 105 | 110 | 108 | 115 | 120 |

Using the **Drift Method**, compute the forecasts for months 7, 8, and 9 (i.e., $h = 1, 2, 3$).

### Solution:

**Step 1: Identify the boundary values**
- First observation: $y_1 = 100$
- Last observation: $y_T = y_6 = 120$
- Number of observations: $T = 6$

**Step 2: Compute the total change**
$$\Delta_{\text{total}} = y_6 - y_1 = 120 - 100 = 20 \text{ (thousand units)}$$

**Step 3: Compute the average drift**
$$d = \frac{y_T - y_1}{T - 1} = \frac{120 - 100}{6 - 1} = \frac{20}{5} = 4 \text{ (thousand units/month)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** On average, production has been increasing by **4 thousand units per month** over the 5 inter-period intervals.

**Step 4: Generate forecasts**

For $h = 1$ (Month 7):
$$\hat{y}_7 = y_6 + 1 \cdot d = 120 + 1 \cdot 4 = 124 \text{ thousand units}$$

For $h = 2$ (Month 8):
$$\hat{y}_8 = y_6 + 2 \cdot d = 120 + 2 \cdot 4 = 128 \text{ thousand units}$$

For $h = 3$ (Month 9):
$$\hat{y}_9 = y_6 + 3 \cdot d = 120 + 3 \cdot 4 = 132 \text{ thousand units}$$

**Final Answer:**

| Month | 7 | 8 | 9 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forecast (Drift Method) | 124 | 128 | 132 |

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Forecast Accuracy (RMSE Computation)

**Problem Statement:**
For the same data above, suppose the actual values for months 7, 8, 9 turned out to be **126, 127, 135** respectively. Compute the RMSE of the drift forecasts.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate residuals**

For $h = 1$: $e_7 = 126 - 124 = 2$
For $h = 2$: $e_8 = 127 - 128 = -1$
For $h = 3$: $e_9 = 135 - 132 = 3$

**Step 2: Square the residuals**
$$e_7^2 = 4, \quad e_8^2 = 1, \quad e_9^2 = 9$$

**Step 3: Compute mean squared error**
$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{3}(4 + 1 + 9) = \frac{14}{3} \approx 4.667$$

**Step 4: Take the square root**
$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}} = \sqrt{4.667} \approx 2.16 \text{ thousand units}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** An RMSE of ~2.16 thousand units over forecasts averaging ~128 thousand units represents an error of about **1.7%** — indicating the Drift Method performed well for this near-linear trend.

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Connection to Simple Linear Regression

**Claim:** The Drift Method forecast $\hat{y}_{T+h}$ is the predicted value of a simple linear regression line $y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \varepsilon_t$ when evaluated at the endpoints.

### Proof:

**Step 1: Set up OLS regression**
Assume the model:
$$y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 2: The OLS slope estimator**
$$\hat{\beta} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T} (t - \bar{t})(y_t - \bar{y})}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} (t - \bar{t})^2}$$

**Step 3: OLS intercept**
$$\hat{\alpha} = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta} \bar{t}$$

**Step 4: Forecast at $t = T + h$**
$$\hat{y}_{T+h} = \hat{\alpha} + \hat{\beta} (T + h)$$

**Step 5: The Drift Method Line**
The drift line passes through $(1, y_1)$ and $(T, y_T)$. Its slope is:
$$\beta_{\text{drift}} = \frac{y_T - y_1}{T - 1} = d$$

**Step 6: Drift Method Forecast (line equation)**
A point on the drift line at time $t$ is:
$$\hat{y}_t = y_1 + d \cdot (t - 1) = y_1 + \frac{y_T - y_1}{T - 1} (t - 1)$$

**Step 7: Compare at $t = T + h$**
$$\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_1 + \frac{y_T - y_1}{T - 1} (T + h - 1)$$

$$= y_1 + \frac{(y_T - y_1)(T - 1)}{T - 1} + \frac{(y_T - y_1) h}{T - 1}$$

$$= y_1 + (y_T - y_1) + h \cdot d = y_T + h \cdot d \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The Drift Method is *not* identical to OLS regression (which minimizes squared errors). Instead, it is a **two-point line projection** that is **deterministic** and ignores all intermediate observations. This is why it is robust to outliers at the endpoints but can be misleading if endpoint values are anomalous.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | Naive Method | Drift Method | OLS Linear Regression |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Forecast Formula** | $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T$ | $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T + h \cdot d$ | $\hat{y}_{T+h} = \hat{\alpha} + \hat{\beta}(T+h)$ |
| **Uses Trend** | No | Yes (constant) | Yes (constant) |
| **Uses All Data** | No (only $y_T$) | No (only $y_1, y_T$) | Yes (all $T$ points) |
| **Uses Seasonality** | No | No | No |
| **Optimization Criterion** | None | None | Minimize SSE |
| **Outlier Sensitivity** | Very High (endpoint) | Very High (endpoints) | Moderate |
| **Computational Cost** | Negligible | Negligible | $O(T)$ matrix solve |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Workflow of the Drift Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Time Series y1, y2, ..., yT] --> B[Extract y1: First Observation]
    A --> C[Extract yT: Last Observation]
    B --> D[Compute Total Change: yT minus y1]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Compute Average Drift: d = yT minus y1 divided by T minus 1]
    E --> F{User Specifies Forecast Horizon h}
    F --> G[For each h compute forecast yTh = yT + h times d]
    G --> H[Output Forecast Vector yTh1, yTh2, ..., yThH]
    H --> I[End: Forecasts Ready for Evaluation]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style I fill:#d4edda
    style E fill:#fff3cd
    style F fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Architecture of the Drift Forecasting Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        T1[Raw Time Series Data]
    end

    subgraph PREP[Preprocessing Layer]
        T2[Data Validation]
        T3[Missing Value Check]
        T4[Boundary Extraction: y1 and yT]
    end

    subgraph CORE[Drift Engine Core]
        T5[Drift Calculator: d = yT minus y1 over T minus 1]
        T6[Forecast Generator: yTh = yT + h times d]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Layer]
        T7[Forecast Vector]
        T8[Confidence Bounds h times sigma d]
    end

    subgraph EVAL[Evaluation Layer]
        T9[RMSE Computation]
        T10[MAPE Computation]
        T11[Benchmark Comparison]
    end

    T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4
    T4 --> T5 --> T6 --> T7
    T6 --> T8
    T7 --> T9
    T7 --> T10
    T7 --> T11

    style T5 fill:#ffeeba
    style T6 fill:#ffeeba
    style T11 fill:#c3e6cb
```

## 4.3 Visual Representation of the Drift Projection Concept

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Point 1: t=1, y=y1] -->|Slope d| P2[Point T: t=T, y=yT]
    P2 -->|Extrapolation Horizon h| P3[Forecast: t=T+h, y=yT + h times d]
    P1 -.->|Projected Trend Line| P3

    style P1 fill:#b8daff
    style P2 fill:#b8daff
    style P3 fill:#f5c6cb
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The **blue nodes** ($P_1$ and $P_T$) are the actual observed endpoints. The **red node** ($P_{T+h}$) is the forecasted future point. The straight line connecting all three is the **drift projection line** with constant slope $d$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]
**Define the Drift Method of forecasting. Write its mathematical formula and explain the significance of the drift parameter $d$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Drift Method** is a simple benchmark forecasting technique in time series analysis where the forecast for future periods is obtained by extrapolating the average rate of change (drift) observed between the first and last observations of the historical data.

The formula is:
$$\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T + h \cdot \frac{y_T - y_1}{T - 1}$$

The drift parameter $d = (y_T - y_1)/(T-1)$ represents the **average change per period** across the entire training data. It captures the **first-order linear trend** of the series and assumes this average rate of change will persist into the future. (3 Marks)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]
**How does the Drift Method differ from the Naive forecasting method? Under what conditions would the Drift Method be preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Naive method** simply sets $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T$, assuming the future will replicate the last observed value. In contrast, the **Drift Method** accounts for the underlying trend by extrapolating the average historical change, yielding $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T + h \cdot d$.

The Drift Method is preferred when:
1. The data exhibits a **persistent linear trend** (upward or downward).
2. The series has **no significant seasonality** that needs to be modeled separately.
3. A **quick benchmark** is needed before deploying more complex models.

When the data is purely stationary with no trend, both methods yield similar results, with the Drift Method providing only marginal benefit. (3 Marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question Paper]

**a)** Define the Drift Method. Derive the forecast formula $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T + h \cdot d$ and explain each term. Discuss **two advantages and two limitations** of the Drift Method as a forecasting tool. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Consider the annual sales (in ₹ lakhs) of a product for the years 2018–2024: **45, 50, 48, 55, 60, 58, 65**.
**(i)** Apply the Drift Method to forecast sales for 2025, 2026, and 2027.
**(ii)** If the actual values for 2025, 2026, 2027 turn out to be 68, 72, 75, compute the RMSE. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The Drift Method is a benchmark forecasting technique that projects future values by extrapolating the average change (drift) between the first and last observations of a time series. The formula is:
$$\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T + h \cdot d, \quad \text{where } d = \frac{y_T - y_1}{T - 1}$$

**Terms (2 Marks):**
- $y_T$: Last observed value in the training series.
- $y_1$: First observed value in the training series.
- $T$: Total number of observations.
- $d$: Average drift (rate of change per period).
- $h$: Forecast horizon (number of steps ahead).

**Advantages (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Simplicity:** Requires only two data points ($y_1$ and $y_T$); no model fitting needed.
2. **Interpretability:** The drift $d$ has an intuitive meaning — the average period-on-period change.

**Limitations (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Ignores Seasonality:** Cannot capture periodic fluctuations in the data.
2. **Endpoint Sensitivity:** Highly vulnerable to outliers or anomalies in $y_1$ or $y_T$.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**(i) Forecast Computation (4 Marks):**

Given: $y_1 = 45$, $y_7 = 65$, $T = 7$.

$$d = \frac{65 - 45}{7 - 1} = \frac{20}{6} = 3.333 \text{ ₹ lakhs/year}$$

Forecasts:
- 2025 ($h=1$): $\hat{y} = 65 + 1 \times 3.333 = 68.33$
- 2026 ($h=2$): $\hat{y} = 65 + 2 \times 3.333 = 71.67$
- 2027 ($h=3$): $\hat{y} = 65 + 3 \times 3.333 = 75.00$

**(ii) RMSE Computation (3 Marks):**

Residuals:
- $e_{2025} = 68 - 68.33 = -0.33$
- $e_{2026} = 72 - 71.67 = 0.33$
- $e_{2027} = 75 - 75.00 = 0.00$

Squared residuals: $0.109, 0.109, 0$

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{0.109 + 0.109 + 0}{3} = 0.0727$$

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{0.0727} \approx 0.27 \text{ ₹ lakhs}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]

**a)** Explain the concept of **forecast benchmark** in time series analysis. Why is the Drift Method commonly used as one of the standard benchmarks? Compare the Drift Method with the **Mean Method** and the **Naive Method** using a comparative table. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** The quarterly demand (in units) for a product over 8 quarters is: **200, 220, 210, 250, 240, 270, 260, 290**.
**(i)** Using the Drift Method, compute the forecast for the next 3 quarters (Q9, Q10, Q11).
**(ii)** Explain what would happen if the first observation $y_1$ was an outlier (say, $y_1 = 50$ instead of 200). How would this affect the drift and forecasts? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Forecast Benchmark Concept (2 Marks):**
A *forecast benchmark* is a simple, transparent forecasting method that serves as a **reference point** to evaluate the performance of more complex models. If a sophisticated model cannot beat a simple benchmark, it is considered inadequate for the given dataset.

**Why Drift Method as Benchmark (2 Marks):**
1. It is **easy to implement** (only two endpoints needed).
2. It captures **linear trend** better than the pure Naive method.
3. It is **deterministic** — same input always produces the same output.
4. It serves as a **fair comparison** for trend-aware models like ARIMA and Holt's linear method.

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Property | Mean Method | Naive Method | Drift Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $\hat{y}_{T+h} = \bar{y}$ | $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T$ | $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T + h \cdot d$ |
| **Uses Trend** | No | No | Yes |
| **Data Used** | All observations | Last value only | First and last values |
| **Best For** | Stationary, no trend | Stationary or weak trend | Strong linear trend |
| **Limitation** | Ignores trend & level | Ignores trend | Ignores seasonality |

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**(i) Drift Forecasts (3 Marks):**

Given: $y_1 = 200$, $y_8 = 290$, $T = 8$.

$$d = \frac{290 - 200}{8 - 1} = \frac{90}{7} \approx 12.857 \text{ units/quarter}$$

Forecasts:
- Q9 ($h=1$): $\hat{y} = 290 + 12.857 = 302.86$ units
- Q10 ($h=2$): $\hat{y} = 290 + 25.714 = 315.71$ units
- Q11 ($h=3$): $\hat{y} = 290 + 38.571 = 328.57$ units

**(ii) Outlier Effect Analysis (4 Marks):**

If $y_1 = 50$ (an outlier), the recomputed drift becomes:
$$d_{\text{outlier}} = \frac{290 - 50}{8 - 1} = \frac{240}{7} \approx 34.286 \text{ units/quarter}$$

This is **almost 2.7 times larger** than the original drift of 12.857. The revised forecasts become:
- Q9: $290 + 34.286 = 324.29$
- Q10: $290 + 68.571 = 358.57$
- Q11: $290 + 102.857 = 392.86$

**Critical Insight:** The Drift Method is **highly sensitive to outliers at the endpoints** because it relies on only two data points. An anomalous $y_1$ can dramatically inflate the drift and lead to over-projection. In contrast, the Mean Method would be less affected (since it uses all data), and the Naive Method would be unaffected (since it uses only $y_T$). This is a key **structural weakness** of the Drift Method.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Wrong Denominator:** Using $T$ instead of $T-1$ in the drift formula. Always remember: $T$ data points create $T-1$ intervals. (**−1 Mark**)
> 2. **Confusing Drift with First Difference:** The drift is the *average* of first differences, not a single consecutive difference like $y_t - y_{t-1}$. (**−1 Mark**)
> 3. **Forgetting to Multiply by $h$:** Some students write $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T + d$ instead of $y_T + h \cdot d$. The horizon multiplier $h$ is critical. (**−2 Marks**)
> 4. **Mixing Drift Method with Linear Regression:** The Drift Method is *not* OLS — it ignores all intermediate points. Do not write "the Drift Method minimizes squared errors." (**−2 Marks**)
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** Reporting drift without specifying units (e.g., just writing "5" instead of "5 units/month"). (**−0.5 Mark**)
> 6. **RMSE Formula Errors:** Forgetting the square root, or using $n-1$ instead of $n$ in the denominator for a population RMSE. (**−1 Mark**)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Drift Method**

- **Definition:** A benchmark forecasting method that extrapolates the **average historical change** between the first and last observations.
- **Core Formula:** $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T + h \cdot d$, where $d = (y_T - y_1)/(T-1)$.
- **Drift $d$:** Represents the **average per-period change**; units = (data units) / time.
- **Denominator is $T-1$:** Because $T$ points define $T-1$ intervals.
- **Geometric Interpretation:** A straight line drawn from $(1, y_1)$ to $(T, y_T)$, projected forward.
- **Not OLS Regression:** It is a **two-point line projection**, not a least-squares fit. It ignores all intermediate observations.
- **Use Case:** Strong **linear trend** with no seasonality. Quick baseline before ETS/ARIMA.
- **Limitation 1:** **Ignores seasonality** — for seasonal data, use Seasonal Naive or Holt-Winters.
- **Limitation 2:** **Endpoint outlier sensitivity** — a single bad $y_1$ or $y_T$ can corrupt forecasts.
- **Forecast Variance:** Grows quadratically with $h$: $\text{Var}(\hat{y}_{T+h}) \propto h^2 \sigma_d^2$.
- **Comparison:** Naive (level only) < Drift (linear trend) < OLS (optimized linear) < ARIMA (autoregressive) < ETS/Prophet (richer structure).
- **Evaluation Metrics:** RMSE, MAE, MAPE, MASE — always benchmark Drift against Naive and Seasonal Naive.
- **Practical Horizon:** Avoid $h > T$ (forecasts become extrapolations of extrapolations, highly unreliable).
- **Equivalent Form:** $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_1 + d \cdot (T - 1 + h)$ — useful when the problem gives $y_1$ directly.
- **Python Note:** In `R`, use `forecast::rwf(y, h, drift = TRUE)`; in `Python`, use `statsmodels.tsa.holtwinters` with trend='add' and damped=False (this gives exponential smoothing, not pure drift — be careful).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steps in Forecasting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steps in Forecasting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Time Series Modeling & Analysis** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, **forecasting** refers to the systematic process of making scientifically grounded predictions about future values of a variable based on historically observed time-ordered data points. A **time series** is a chronological sequence of observations recorded at successive, equally spaced time intervals, denoted mathematically as:

$$Y = \{y_1, y_2, y_3, \ldots, y_t\}$$

The **Steps in Forecasting** constitute a structured, sequential methodology that a data scientist, statistician, or operations research analyst must execute in order to transform raw historical observations into a reliable, quantitatively defensible future projection. These steps are not arbitrary; they are governed by the **Box-Jenkins methodology**, the **CRISP-DM framework**, and classical statistical decision theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The Board Examiner expects students to articulate the forecasting pipeline as a closed-loop system where Step 1 (Problem Definition) feeds into Step 2 (Data Collection), which flows into Steps 3-6 (Exploratory Analysis, Pre-processing, Decomposition, Model Selection), culminating in Steps 7-9 (Estimation, Validation, and Deployment) with continuous **monitoring and feedback**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Weather Forecaster

Imagine you are a **traditional farmer in Kerala** preparing for the next monsoon season. You have years of rainfall data scribbled in an old ledger. Before deciding what crop to plant, you would intuitively follow these steps:

1. **Look Back**: Review previous years' rainfall patterns (Data Collection).
2. **Spot Patterns**: Notice that June-July-August tend to be heavier than January-February (Trend & Seasonality Identification).
3. **Clean the Data**: Discard that one anomalous year where a cyclone skewed the readings (Outlier Treatment).
4. **Make a Guess**: Project next year's rainfall based on average past behavior (Model Application).
5. **Check Your Guess**: Compare last year's projection with what actually happened (Validation).
6. **Refine and Repeat**: Update your mental model for next year (Feedback Loop).

This human cognitive process is **mathematically formalized** into the 9 canonical steps of forecasting that we will master in this module.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Parallel:** In **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM)** of bridges, engineers apply identical forecasting steps to predict crack propagation, vibration amplitudes, and load-bearing capacity deterioration. The **Federal Highway Administration (FHWA)** mandates these exact sequential steps for any predictive maintenance model deployed on National Highway infrastructure.

### 1.3 Why Sequential Discipline Matters

A common KTU student error is to jump directly to applying ARIMA, Exponential Smoothing, or Neural Networks without performing the prerequisite exploratory steps. Such shortcuts lead to:
- **Garbage-In-Garbage-Out (GIGO)** syndromes.
- Misinterpreted seasonality.
- Spurious regression and autocorrelation artifacts.
- Failed KTU exam questions that demand process justification.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In Module 1, marks are awarded not for the final forecast value, but for the **logical sequence, justification, and mathematical correctness** of each preparatory step. Memorize the pipeline order.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 9 Canonical Steps in Forecasting (Decomposed)

Below is the exhaustive, exam-ready decomposition of the forecasting pipeline. Each step is presented with its **operational objective**, **mathematical underpinning**, and **engineering rationale**.

#### **Step 1: Problem Definition & Objective Setting**
- **Objective:** Translate the business/engineering question into a measurable statistical objective.
- **Key Questions:** What variable is being predicted? What is the forecast horizon (short-term, medium-term, long-term)? What is the desired accuracy tolerance?
- **Engineering Utility:** A power grid operator forecasting 15-minute load demand requires a different model than a structural engineer forecasting 50-year concrete creep.

#### **Step 2: Data Collection & Acquisition**
- **Objective:** Gather time-ordered observations at uniform intervals.
- **Sources:** Sensor logs, financial databases, government open-data portals (e.g., data.gov.in), enterprise ERP systems.
- **Frequency Considerations:** Hourly, daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, annual.

#### **Step 3: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)**
- **Objective:** Visually and statistically characterize the series before modeling.
- **Tools:** Line plots, histograms, Q-Q plots, autocorrelation function (ACF), partial autocorrelation function (PACF).
- **Key Visualizations:**
  - **Time Plot:** $y_t$ versus $t$.
  - **Lag Plot:** $y_t$ versus $y_{t-1}$.
  - **ACF Plot:** $\rho_k$ versus lag $k$.

#### **Step 4: Data Pre-processing & Cleaning**
- **Objective:** Rectify anomalies that would distort model parameters.
- **Sub-tasks:**
  - **Missing Value Imputation:** Mean, median, forward-fill (LOCF), or Kalman smoothing.
  - **Outlier Detection:** Z-score, IQR, or Tukey fences.
  - **Stationarity Transformation:** Differencing ($\nabla y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}$), logarithmic ($\log y_t$), or Box-Cox transformation.
  - **Variance Stabilization.**

#### **Step 5: Decomposition of Time Series Components**
- **Objective:** Isolate the four classical components: **Trend ($T_t$)**, **Cyclical ($C_t$)**, **Seasonal ($S_t$)**, and **Irregular/Residual ($I_t$)**.
- **Models:**
  - **Additive Model:** $y_t = T_t + C_t + S_t + I_t$
  - **Multiplicative Model:** $y_t = T_t \times C_t \times S_t \times I_t$
- **Methods:** Classical Decomposition, STL (Seasonal-Trend decomposition using LOESS), X-12-ARIMA.

#### **Step 6: Model Selection**
- **Objective:** Choose a parsimonious, theoretically justified model class.
- **Candidate Models for KTU Module 1:**
  - Naive Forecast: $\hat{y}_{t+1} = y_t$
  - Simple Average: $\hat{y}_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$
  - Simple Moving Average (SMA): $\hat{y}_{t+1} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=t-k+1}^{t} y_i$
  - Weighted Moving Average (WMA): $\hat{y}_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i y_{t-i+1}$, where $\sum w_i = 1$
  - Exponential Smoothing: $\hat{y}_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)\hat{y}_t$
  - Holt's Linear Trend (Double Exponential Smoothing)
  - Holt-Winters (Triple Exponential Smoothing)
  - ARIMA / SARIMA (covered in Module 2)

#### **Step 7: Parameter Estimation & Model Fitting**
- **Objective:** Numerically optimize the model coefficients against the training data.
- **Techniques:** Ordinary Least Squares (OLS), Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), Non-Linear Least Squares (NLS).

#### **Step 8: Model Validation & Diagnostic Checking**
- **Objective:** Quantify forecast accuracy and verify that residual assumptions are met.
- **Error Metrics** (see formula sheet below).
- **Residual Diagnostics:** Ljung-Box test for autocorrelation, Shapiro-Wilk test for normality, ADF test for stationarity.

#### **Step 9: Deployment, Monitoring & Iterative Refinement**
- **Objective:** Operationalize the model in production, then continuously retrain as new data arrives.
- **Concept:** **Walk-Forward Validation** or **Rolling Origin Cross-Validation** in time series.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Formula / Metric** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Purpose** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Absolute Error (MAE) | $MAE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert y_t - \hat{y}_t \vert$ | Measures average magnitude of errors | Same as $y$ |
| Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} (y_t - \hat{y}_t)^2$ | Penalizes large errors quadratically | (unit of $y$)$^2$ |
| Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) | $RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} (y_t - \hat{y}_t)^2$ | Penalizes large errors, same unit as $y$ | Same as $y$ |
| Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) | $MAPE = \frac{100}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \left\vert \frac{y_t - \hat{y}_t}{y_t} \right\vert$ | Scale-independent accuracy | Percentage (%) |
| Simple Moving Average | $SMA_k = \frac{y_t + y_{t-1} + \cdots + y_{t-k+1}}{k}$ | Smooths short-term fluctuations | Same as $y$ |
| Single Exponential Smoothing | $\hat{y}_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)\hat{y}_t$ | Weighted average of past observations | Same as $y$ |
| Autocovariance | $\gamma_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k} (y_t - \bar{y})(y_{t+k} - \bar{y})$ | Measures linear dependence at lag $k$ | (unit)$^2$ |
| Autocorrelation Function (ACF) | $\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0}$ | Normalized autocorrelation at lag $k$ | Dimensionless |
| Differencing Operator | $\nabla y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}$ | Removes trend to achieve stationarity | Same as $y$ |
| Seasonal Differencing | $\nabla_s y_t = y_t - y_{t-s}$ | Removes seasonal pattern with period $s$ | Same as $y$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to compute MAPE, the **denominator is $y_t$ (the actual)**, not $\hat{y}_t$. A common KTU evaluation pitfall is reversing this ratio.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of the Forecasting Pipeline

The 9-step pipeline is deployed universally across:

1. **Electrical Load Forecasting:** Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) uses Holt-Winters and SARIMA to predict 24-hour demand, optimizing generation from Idukki Hydro and Kayamkulam Thermal stations.
2. **Telecommunications:** Reliance Jio forecasts hourly call-drop rates to dynamically reallocate base-station bandwidth.
3. **Inventory Management:** BigBasket and Amazon India predict SKU-level demand to optimize warehouse replenishment cycles.
4. **Financial Engineering:** NIFTY-50 index forecasting for portfolio risk management.
5. **Aerospace:** ISRO forecasts satellite telemetry sensor drift to schedule corrective orbital maneuvers.
6. **Civil Engineering:** Predicting concrete strength gain curves to optimize formwork removal timing.

The pipeline acts as a **universal abstraction layer** that decouples the domain-specific problem from the statistical machinery.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Smoothing Constant $\alpha$ in Exponential Smoothing

The Single Exponential Smoothing (SES) recurrence is given by:

$$\hat{y}_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha) \hat{y}_t$$

where $\alpha \in [0, 1]$. We derive the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** of the one-step-ahead forecast and find the value of $\alpha$ that minimizes it.

**Step 1: Express the squared error function.**

$$E(\alpha) = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{t=1}^{n-1} (y_{t+1} - \hat{y}_{t+1})^2$$

**Step 2: Substitute the SES recurrence.**

$$E(\alpha) = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{t=1}^{n-1} \left[ y_{t+1} - \alpha y_t - (1-\alpha)\hat{y}_t \right]^2$$

**Step 3: Take the partial derivative with respect to $\alpha$ and set it to zero for the optimum.**

$$\frac{\partial E(\alpha)}{\partial \alpha} = \frac{2}{n-1} \sum_{t=1}^{n-1} \left[ y_{t+1} - \alpha y_t - (1-\alpha)\hat{y}_t \right] \cdot \left[ -y_t + \hat{y}_t \right] = 0$$

**Step 4: Simplify the bracketed terms. Let $e_t = y_t - \hat{y}_t$ denote the forecast error at time $t$. Then $-y_t + \hat{y}_t = -e_t$. Also, the prediction error term in the first bracket becomes:**

$$y_{t+1} - \alpha y_t - (1-\alpha)\hat{y}_t = y_{t+1} - \hat{y}_t - \alpha(y_t - \hat{y}_t) = e_{t+1} - \alpha e_t$$

Wait, careful: the forecast is for $t+1$ made at time $t$. Let us denote the actual forecast error after observing $y_{t+1}$ as $\epsilon_{t+1} = y_{t+1} - \hat{y}_{t+1}$. The recurrence yields:

$$\hat{y}_{t+1} = \hat{y}_t + \alpha (y_t - \hat{y}_t) = \hat{y}_t + \alpha \epsilon_t$$

**Step 5: Substitute back and rearrange.**

$$\frac{\partial E(\alpha)}{\partial \alpha} = \frac{2}{n-1} \sum_{t=1}^{n-1} \epsilon_{t+1} \cdot (-e_t) = 0$$

This gives the **first-order optimality condition**:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n-1} \epsilon_{t+1} e_t = 0$$

**Step 6: Solving analytically for $\alpha$ in the simple closed form requires expansion:**

$$\alpha^* = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n-1} (y_{t+1} - \hat{y}_t)(y_t - \hat{y}_t)}{\sum_{t=1}^{n-1} (y_t - \hat{y}_t)^2}$$

This is mathematically analogous to the OLS slope coefficient, confirming that exponential smoothing is essentially a **weighted OLS estimator with geometrically decaying weights**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** $\alpha \to 1$ means the forecast places nearly all weight on the most recent observation (volatile, responsive but noisy). $\alpha \to 0$ means the forecast is essentially the historical mean (stable but lagging). The optimal $\alpha$ balances **responsiveness** and **stability**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Bias-Variance Decomposition of Forecast Error

The expected squared forecast error at time $h$ can be decomposed as:

$$E\left[ (y_{t+h} - \hat{y}_{t+h})^2 \right] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Noise}$$

**Step 1: Add and subtract the conditional expectation $E[y_{t+h} \vert \mathcal{F}_t]$ (where $\mathcal{F}_t$ is the information set available at time $t$):**

$$y_{t+h} - \hat{y}_{t+h} = \left( y_{t+h} - E[y_{t+h} \vert \mathcal{F}_t] \right) + \left( E[y_{t+h} \vert \mathcal{F}_t] - \hat{y}_{t+h} \right)$$

**Step 2: The first term is the irreducible innovation (noise) $\eta_{t+h}$. The second term is the model error (Bias + Variance).**

$$y_{t+h} - \hat{y}_{t+h} = \eta_{t+h} + \left( E[y_{t+h} \vert \mathcal{F}_t] - \hat{y}_{t+h} \right)$$

**Step 3: Square both sides and take expectation. The cross-term vanishes under the orthogonality assumption $E[\eta_{t+h} \cdot \text{anything in } \mathcal{F}_t] = 0$:**

$$E[(y_{t+h} - \hat{y}_{t+h})^2] = E[\eta_{t+h}^2] + E\left[ (E[y_{t+h} \vert \mathcal{F}_t] - \hat{y}_{t+h})^2 \right]$$

**Step 4: Decompose the second term. Let $m(\mathcal{F}_t) = E[y_{t+h} \vert \mathcal{F}_t]$ be the true conditional mean. The expected squared deviation of $\hat{y}_{t+h}$ from $m(\mathcal{F}_t)$ is:**

$$E[(m - \hat{y})^2] = E[(m - E[\hat{y}])^2] + E[(E[\hat{y}] - \hat{y})^2]$$

**Step 5: Final decomposition:**

$$\underbrace{E[(y_{t+h} - \hat{y}_{t+h})^2]}_{\text{Total Expected Error}} = \underbrace{\sigma_\eta^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}} + \underbrace{\left( m - E[\hat{y}_{t+h}] \right)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{y}_{t+h})}_{\text{Variance}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This decomposition is the cornerstone of model selection. A simple model (e.g., Naive) has **high bias, low variance**. A complex model (e.g., deep LSTM) has **low bias, high variance**. The KTU board expects you to justify your model choice by referencing this trade-off.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: MAPE and RMSE Calculation

**Problem Statement:** Given the following 5 time-ordered observations and corresponding forecasts, compute MAE, RMSE, and MAPE.
