**De-seasoning** (also called *seasonal adjustment* or *deseasonalization*) is the process of removing the estimated seasonal component $S_t$ from an observed series $Y_t$ so that the adjusted series reflects only the underlying trend, cycle, and irregular movements.

The two canonical models used at KTU level are:

| Model | Equation | When Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Additive | $Y_t = T_t + S_t + I_t$ | Seasonal swing is **constant** in absolute terms |
| Multiplicative | $Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times I_t$ | Seasonal swing **grows/shrinks** with the level |

> [!TIP]
> A quick rule of thumb: if the size of the seasonal bumps **grows proportionally** as the series level rises, the model is **multiplicative**; if the bumps stay about the **same height**, the model is **additive**. Always take a log first if multiplicative: $\log Y_t = \log T_t + \log S_t + \log I_t$.

## 1.4 Why KTU Cares About This Topic

Detecting and removing seasonality is the **gateway skill** for the rest of Module 1 and for fitting ARMA / SARIMA models in later modules. A non-deseasonalized series violates the **stationarity** assumption required by Box–Jenkins methodology.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 4-Step Framework for Detecting Seasonality

The detection of seasonality is performed along four independent but complementary lines of evidence:

### Step 1 — Visual Inspection (Qualitative)
- Plot the raw series $Y_t$ against time. Look for *regularly repeating peaks and troughs at the same calendar positions*.
- Use a **seasonal sub-series plot**: group the data by season (e.g., all Januaries, all Februaries, …) and plot each group as a small mini-series. If each mini-series has a similar shape, seasonality is present.

### Step 2 — Autocorrelation Function (ACF) Diagnostics (Quantitative)
- Compute the sample ACF $\hat{\rho}_k$ at lags $k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2s$.
- For a seasonal process, the ACF shows:
  - A large spike at lag $k = s$ (the seasonal lag).
  - Often additional spikes at $k = 2s, 3s, \ldots$
  - The decay between seasonal lags is slow.
- **Rule:** if $\vert \hat{\rho}_s \vert > 2/\sqrt{n}$ (the Bartlett bound) the seasonal autocorrelation is statistically significant.

### Step 3 — Formal Hypothesis Tests (Confirmatory)
- **Friedman Test** (non-parametric, ranks): tests whether the medians across seasons are equal.
- **Kruskal–Wallis Test**: similar, tests equality of distributions.
- **QS Test** (Ljung–Box style for seasonality): large $Q$ statistic ⇒ seasonality.
- **Canova–Hansen Test** and **HEGY test** are advanced options in higher semesters.

### Step 4 — Decomposition-Based Diagnostics
- Apply classical or STL decomposition. If the extracted seasonal component $S_t$ explains a sizeable portion of the variance and $S_{t+s} \approx S_t$, seasonality is confirmed.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | What we look for | Why it works |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Visual | Repeating humps | Human pattern recognition is the fastest first-pass filter |
| Sub-series | Mini-series with similar shape | Controls for trend by aggregating same calendar points |
| ACF | Spike at lag $s$ | Pure seasonal signal has maximum self-correlation at its period |
| Formal test | $p$-value $< 0.05$ | Provides objective, reproducible proof required for publications |
| Decomposition | $S_t$ component with non-trivial variance | Quantifies *how much* of the series is seasonal |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table lists **every formula** you must memorize for Module 1 on this topic. Symbols use the KTU convention.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| F1 | Additive model | $Y_t = T_t + S_t + I_t$ | $S_t$ in same units as $Y_t$ |
| F2 | Multiplicative model | $Y_t = T_t \cdot S_t \cdot I_t$ | $S_t$ is a dimensionless **index** |
| F3 | Log-multiplicative | $\log Y_t = \log T_t + \log S_t + \log I_t$ | Reduce to additive form |
| F4 | Centered MA of order $s$ (even $s$) | $MA_t = \dfrac{\frac{1}{2}Y_{t-s/2} + Y_{t-s/2+1} + \cdots + Y_{t+s/2-1} + \frac{1}{2}Y_{t+s/2}}{s}$ | Used to extract trend |
| F5 | Detrended series (additive) | $Y_t - MA_t$ | Isolates $S_t + I_t$ |
| F6 | Detrended series (multiplicative) | $Y_t / MA_t$ | Isolates $S_t \cdot I_t$ |
| F7 | Seasonal index (additive) | $\hat{S}_j = \dfrac{1}{n_j}\sum_{t \in j}(Y_t - MA_t)$ | Sum of $\hat{S}_j$ over a year $\approx 0$ |
| F8 | Seasonal index (multiplicative) | $\hat{S}_j = \dfrac{1}{n_j}\sum_{t \in j}(Y_t / MA_t)$ | Sum of $\hat{S}_j$ over a year $= s$ |
| F9 | Seasonal differencing | $\nabla_s Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-s}$ | Removes seasonal unit root |
| F10 | ACF | $\hat{\rho}_k = \dfrac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(Y_t - \bar{Y})(Y_{t-k} - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(Y_t - \bar{Y})^2}$ | $\vert \hat{\rho}_k \vert \le 1$ |
| F11 | Bartlett bound | $\pm 2/\sqrt{n}$ | Significance threshold for ACF |
| F12 | Friedman test statistic | $Q_F = \dfrac{12}{nk(k+1)}\sum_{j=1}^{k}R_j^2 - 3n(k+1)$ | $\chi^2$ with $k-1$ d.f. |
| F13 | Deseasonalized value (additive) | $Y_t^{\text{adj}} = Y_t - \hat{S}_{m(t)}$ | $m(t)$ = season of $t$ |
| F14 | Deseasonalized value (multiplicative) | $Y_t^{\text{adj}} = Y_t / \hat{S}_{m(t)}$ | Equivalent to $\log Y_t - \log \hat{S}$ |
| F15 | STL component | $Y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t$ | LOESS-based, robust |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall — the vertical bar issue:** In the KTU valuation script, examiners check for **brackets** in formulas. Always write $\vert x \vert$ for modulus (use LaTeX `\vert`/`\mid`) and not the bare pipe `|` when typing in a markdown table — it breaks the table.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power-load forecasting (KSEB):** Electricity demand in Kerala peaks in summer (AC use) and dips in monsoon. Seasonal adjustment isolates *true* consumption growth from weather swings.
- **Inventory & supply chain:** Retailers need deseasonalized demand to compute safety stock without double-counting Diwali / Onam peaks.
- **Telecom traffic:** Hourly call volume is seasonal with period 24. Network capacity planning works on the deseasonalized series to capture *trends* in growth.
- **Economics & policy:** GDP, IIP, and inflation indices are seasonally adjusted before being reported.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Centered Moving Average of Order $s$

For a series with an **even seasonal period $s$** (e.g., $s=12$ for monthly, $s=4$ for quarterly), a simple $s$-period MA is *not* centered on an observation. We require a **$2 \times s$** centered moving average, derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Define the first $s$-period MA:**
$$M_t^{(1)} = \frac{1}{s}\sum_{j=0}^{s-1} Y_{t-j}$$

This averages $Y_{t-s+1}, \ldots, Y_t$ and is positioned at the *midpoint* between $t-s/2$ and $t$.

**Step 2 — Average two consecutive $M_t^{(1)}$ to center the window on integer index $t$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MA_t &= \frac{1}{2}\left[M_t^{(1)} + M_{t-1}^{(1)}\right] \\
     &= \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{s}\sum_{j=0}^{s-1} Y_{t-j} \;+\; \frac{1}{s}\sum_{j=0}^{s-1} Y_{t-1-j}\right] \\
     &= \frac{1}{2s}\left[Y_t + 2Y_{t-1} + 2Y_{t-2} + \cdots + 2Y_{t-s+1} + Y_{t-s}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The first and last terms of the combined sum appear only **once** (they get the coefficient $1$), while the middle $s-1$ terms appear **twice** (coefficient $2$). This is exactly the *centered* MA formula used in classical decomposition (Formula F4 above).

**Step 3 — Final closed form** (compact notation):

$$MA_t = \frac{\frac{1}{2}Y_{t-s/2} + \sum_{j=-(s/2)+1}^{(s/2)-1} Y_{t+j} + \frac{1}{2}Y_{t+s/2}}{s}$$

This is the trend estimate $\hat{T}_t$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Seasonal Indices — Additive Case

Suppose we have $n$ observations of monthly data ($s=12$) and we have computed the centered MA $MA_t$ for $t = s/2+1, \ldots, n-s/2$. Denote the *detrended* series as $D_t = Y_t - MA_t$ (this contains $S_t + I_t$).

**Step 1 — Group detrended values by month $j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, s\}$.** Let $D_{i,j}$ denote the detrended value of the $i$-th occurrence of month $j$. There are $n_j$ such values.

**Step 2 — Compute the raw seasonal effect for each month as the average of detrended values:**

$$
\tilde{S}_j = \frac{1}{n_j}\sum_{i=1}^{n_j} D_{i,j} = \frac{1}{n_j}\sum_{i=1}^{n_j}(Y_{i,j} - MA_{i,j})
$$

*Conversion logic:* Averaging the detrended series cancels out the irregular component $I_t$ (whose mean is $0$ by definition), leaving a clean estimate of $S_j$.

**Step 3 — Center the seasonal effects so their sum is zero:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{S} &= \frac{1}{s}\sum_{j=1}^{s}\tilde{S}_j \\
\hat{S}_j &= \tilde{S}_j - \bar{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* This centering guarantees $\sum_{j=1}^{s}\hat{S}_j = 0$, which is the identifying constraint that makes the additive model unique (otherwise $T_t$ and $S_t$ could absorb each other).

**Step 4 — Final deseasonalized series:**

$$
Y_t^{\text{adj}} = Y_t - \hat{S}_{m(t)}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Seasonal Indices — Multiplicative Case

Replace additive operations with multiplicative ones.

**Step 1 — Detrended ratio:** $R_t = Y_t / MA_t$ contains $S_t \cdot I_t$.

**Step 2 — Raw multiplicative index for season $j$:**

$$
\tilde{S}_j = \frac{1}{n_j}\sum_{i=1}^{n_j} R_{i,j} = \frac{1}{n_j}\sum_{i=1}^{n_j}\frac{Y_{i,j}}{MA_{i,j}}
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize so that the indices average to $1$ (i.e., sum to $s$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{S} &= \frac{1}{s}\sum_{j=1}^{s}\tilde{S}_j \\
\hat{S}_j &= \frac{\tilde{S}_j}{\bar{S}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Deseasonalize:** $Y_t^{\text{adj}} = Y_t \,/\, \hat{S}_{m(t)}$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

Consider quarterly sales (in lakhs ₹) over **6 years**:

| Year | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 10 | 14 | 18 | 12 |
| 2 | 12 | 16 | 20 | 14 |
| 3 | 14 | 18 | 22 | 16 |
| 4 | 16 | 20 | 24 | 18 |
| 5 | 18 | 22 | 26 | 20 |
| 6 | 20 | 24 | 28 | 22 |

Period $s=4$. We use the **additive** model.

### Step 1 — Compute the 4-quarter centered MA

For $s=4$, the centered MA uses a $2 \times 4$ window with end-weights $1/2$:

$$
MA_t = \frac{\frac{1}{2}Y_{t-2} + Y_{t-1} + Y_t + Y_{t+1} + \frac{1}{2}Y_{t+2}}{4}
$$

Compute $MA$ for $t = 3, 4, \ldots, 22$ (we lose 2 obs at each end):

| $t$ | Year-Q | $Y_t$ | Calculation | $MA_t$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 3 | Y1-Q3 | 18 | $(0.5\cdot10 + 14 + 18 + 12 + 0.5\cdot16)/4$ | $14.75$ |
| 4 | Y1-Q4 | 12 | $(0.5\cdot14 + 18 + 12 + 16 + 0.5\cdot20)/4$ | $15.50$ |
| 5 | Y2-Q1 | 12 | $(0.5\cdot18 + 12 + 12 + 14 + 0.5\cdot16)/4$ | $13.75$ |
| 6 | Y2-Q2 | 16 | $(0.5\cdot12 + 12 + 16 + 20 + 0.5\cdot18)/4$ | $15.50$ |
| 7 | Y2-Q3 | 20 | $(0.5\cdot12 + 16 + 20 + 14 + 0.5\cdot22)/4$ | $17.25$ |
| 8 | Y2-Q4 | 14 | $(0.5\cdot16 + 20 + 14 + 22 + 0.5\cdot24)/4$ | $19.00$ |
| 9 | Y3-Q1 | 14 | $(0.5\cdot20 + 14 + 14 + 16 + 0.5\cdot18)/4$ | $15.75$ |
| 10 | Y3-Q2 | 18 | $(0.5\cdot14 + 14 + 18 + 22 + 0.5\cdot20)/4$ | $17.50$ |
| 11 | Y3-Q3 | 22 | $(0.5\cdot14 + 18 + 22 + 16 + 0.5\cdot24)/4$ | $19.25$ |
| 12 | Y3-Q4 | 16 | $(0.5\cdot18 + 22 + 16 + 24 + 0.5\cdot26)/4$ | $21.00$ |
| 13 | Y4-Q1 | 16 | $(0.5\cdot22 + 16 + 16 + 18 + 0.5\cdot20)/4$ | $17.75$ |
| 14 | Y4-Q2 | 20 | $(0.5\cdot16 + 16 + 20 + 24 + 0.5\cdot22)/4$ | $19.50$ |
| 15 | Y4-Q3 | 24 | $(0.5\cdot16 + 20 + 24 + 18 + 0.5\cdot26)/4$ | $21.25$ |
| 16 | Y4-Q4 | 18 | $(0.5\cdot20 + 24 + 18 + 26 + 0.5\cdot28)/4$ | $23.00$ |
| 17 | Y5-Q1 | 18 | $(0.5\cdot24 + 18 + 18 + 20 + 0.5\cdot22)/4$ | $19.75$ |
| 18 | Y5-Q2 | 22 | $(0.5\cdot18 + 18 + 22 + 26 + 0.5\cdot24)/4$ | $21.50$ |
| 19 | Y5-Q3 | 26 | $(0.5\cdot18 + 22 + 26 + 20 + 0.5\cdot28)/4$ | $23.25$ |
| 20 | Y5-Q4 | 20 | $(0.5\cdot22 + 26 + 20 + 28 + 0.5\cdot30)/4$ | $25.00$ |
| 21 | Y6-Q1 | 20 | $(0.5\cdot26 + 20 + 20 + 22 + 0.5\cdot24)/4$ | $21.75$ |
| 22 | Y6-Q2 | 24 | $(0.5\cdot20 + 20 + 24 + 28 + 0.5\cdot26)/4$ | $23.50$ |

### Step 2 — Compute detrended series $D_t = Y_t - MA_t$

| $t$ | $Y_t$ | $MA_t$ | $D_t = Y_t - MA_t$ | Quarter |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 3 | 18 | 14.75 | $+3.25$ | Q3 |
| 4 | 12 | 15.50 | $-3.50$ | Q4 |
| 5 | 12 | 13.75 | $-1.75$ | Q1 |
| 6 | 16 | 15.50 | $+0.50$ | Q2 |
| 7 | 20 | 17.25 | $+2.75$ | Q3 |
| 8 | 14 | 19.00 | $-5.00$ | Q4 |
| 9 | 14 | 15.75 | $-1.75$ | Q1 |
| 10 | 18 | 17.50 | $+0.50$ | Q2 |
| 11 | 22 | 19.25 | $+2.75$ | Q3 |
| 12 | 16 | 21.00 | $-5.00$ | Q4 |
| 13 | 16 | 17.75 | $-1.75$ | Q1 |
| 14 | 20 | 19.50 | $+0.50$ | Q2 |
| 15 | 24 | 21.25 | $+2.75$ | Q3 |
| 16 | 18 | 23.00 | $-5.00$ | Q4 |
| 17 | 18 | 19.75 | $-1.75$ | Q1 |
| 18 | 22 | 21.50 | $+0.50$ | Q2 |
| 19 | 26 | 23.25 | $+2.75$ | Q3 |
| 20 | 20 | 25.00 | $-5.00$ | Q4 |
| 21 | 20 | 21.75 | $-1.75$ | Q1 |
| 22 | 24 | 23.50 | $+0.50$ | Q2 |

### Step 3 — Average detrended values by quarter

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{S}_{Q1} &= \frac{-1.75 - 1.75 - 1.75 - 1.75 - 1.75}{5} = \frac{-8.75}{5} = -1.75 \\
\tilde{S}_{Q2} &= \frac{+0.50 + 0.50 + 0.50 + 0.50 + 0.50}{5} = \frac{+2.50}{5} = +0.50 \\
\tilde{S}_{Q3} &= \frac{+3.25 + 2.75 + 2.75 + 2.75 + 2.75}{5} = \frac{+14.25}{5} = +2.85 \\
\tilde{S}_{Q4} &= \frac{-3.50 - 5.00 - 5.00 - 5.00 - 5.00}{5} = \frac{-23.50}{5} = -4.70
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Center to sum zero

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{S} &= \frac{-1.75 + 0.50 + 2.85 - 4.70}{4} = \frac{-3.10}{4} = -0.775
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{S}_{Q1} &= -1.75 - (-0.775) = -0.975 \\
\hat{S}_{Q2} &= +0.50 - (-0.775) = +1.275 \\
\hat{S}_{Q3} &= +2.85 - (-0.775) = +3.625 \\
\hat{S}_{Q4} &= -4.70 - (-0.775) = -3.925
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $-0.975 + 1.275 + 3.625 - 3.925 = 0$ ✓

### Step 5 — Deseasonalize

For Year 3, Q1: $Y^{\text{adj}} = 14 - (-0.975) = 14.975$ lakhs.

> [!TIP]
> Notice Q3 is the festival/Onam quarter (+3.625) and Q4 (post-festive) is the trough ($-3.925$). The series, after removal, is much smoother and reveals the *true* underlying growth — which in this data is **linear** with slope 2 per year.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Full Detection-to-Deseasoning Pipeline

Below is the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of the procedure as required by KTU Module 1. Each block is a functional stage with explicit inputs and outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Time Series Y_t] --> B[Stage 1: Visual Inspection]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Seasonal Sub-series Plot]
    C --> D[Stage 3: ACF Diagnostics]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Formal Hypothesis Tests]
    E --> F{Is Seasonality Confirmed?}
    F -- No --> G[Proceed to ARMA Modeling Directly]
    F -- Yes --> H[Stage 5: Choose Decomposition Model]
    H --> I[Additive vs Multiplicative Decision]
    I --> J[Stage 6: Estimate Centered Moving Average MA_t]
    J --> K[Stage 7: Detrend Y_t minus MA_t or Y_t by MA_t]
    K --> L[Stage 8: Average by Season to get Raw S_j]
    L --> M[Stage 9: Normalize Seasonal Indices]
    M --> N[Stage 10: Deseasonalized Series Y_t_adj]
    N --> O[Stage 11: Diagnostic ACF of Y_t_adj]
    O --> P{Seasonal Lags Still Significant?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Apply Seasonal Differencing nabla_s]
    Q --> O
    P -- No --> R[Stationary Residue Ready for SARIMA / ARMA]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Additive vs Multiplicative

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Examine Magnitude of Seasonal Swings] --> S2{Do swings grow with level?}
    S2 -- No Constant --> S3[Use Additive Model: Y equals T plus S plus I]
    S2 -- Yes Proportional --> S4{Do you want a single rule for both?}
    S4 -- No --> S5[Use Multiplicative Model: Y equals T times S times I]
    S4 -- Yes --> S6[Take log Y_t then Use Additive Model on log scale]
    S3 --> S7[Compute S_j = mean of Y_t minus MA_t]
    S5 --> S8[Compute S_j = mean of Y_t divided by MA_t]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S9[Center so sum of S_j = 0]
    S8 --> S10[Normalize so sum of S_j = s]
    S9 --> S11[Deseasonalize: Y_adj = Y minus S_j]
    S10 --> S12[Deseasonalize: Y_adj = Y divided by S_j]
```

## 4.3 ACF Signature Map — How to Read a Seasonal ACF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Non-Seasonal AR1] --> A1[ACF decays smoothly no peaks at lag s]
    P2[Pure Seasonal AR at lag s] --> A2[ACF shows large spike ONLY at lag s and multiples]
    P3[Seasonal + Trend] --> A3[ACF decays slowly AND spikes at lag s]
    P4[Pure White Noise] --> A4[ACF all bars inside Bartlett band]
    A3 --> D1{Compare to 2 divided by sqrt n}
    D1 -- Spike exceeds band --> R1[CONFIRMED Seasonal]
    D1 -- Spike inside band --> R2[No seasonality]
```

## 4.4 STL Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Input Y_t] --> Y[Inner Loop: Trend Smoothing via LOESS]
    Y --> Z[Subtract Trend to get Y minus T_t]
    Z --> W[Seasonal Smoothing via LOESS on each season]
    W --> V[Subtract Season to get Residual R_t]
    V --> U{Robustness Weighting On?}
    U -- Yes --> X
    U -- No --> OUT[Output T_t plus S_t plus R_t]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above are the **schematic representation** KTU examiners expect when a question says *"Draw the block diagram of deseasonalization"*. Always include the loop-back arrows for iterative refinement and the explicit decision diamonds.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define seasonality. State any two methods to detect it. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Seasonality in a time series is a systematic periodic variation that recurs with a **fixed and known period $s$** (such as $s=12$ for monthly data or $s=4$ for quarterly data), arising from calendar-driven effects like weather, festivals, or institutional schedules.

**Two detection methods (2 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Autocorrelation Function (ACF) plot:** Significant spikes in the ACF at lags $k=s, 2s, 3s, \ldots$ that exceed the Bartlett band $\pm 2/\sqrt{n}$ indicate seasonality.

2. **Seasonal sub-series plot:** Plot the data grouped by season (all Januaries, all Februaries, …). If the mean level differs across sub-series, seasonality is present.

> *Credit alternatives:* Visual inspection of time plot, Friedman test, Kruskal–Wallis test, classical decomposition.

### Q2. Distinguish between additive and multiplicative decomposition models. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Additive | Multiplicative |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Equation | $Y_t = T_t + S_t + I_t$ | $Y_t = T_t \cdot S_t \cdot I_t$ |
| Seasonal swing | Constant in absolute units | Proportional to the level |
| Identifying constraint | $\sum \hat{S}_j = 0$ | $\sum \hat{S}_j = s$ |
| Deseasonalization | $Y_t^{\text{adj}} = Y_t - \hat{S}_{m(t)}$ | $Y_t^{\text{adj}} = Y_t / \hat{S}_{m(t)}$ |
| Use when | Trend is linear, swing constant | Series grows exponentially |

*Any three differences for full marks.*

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1)

### Q.A (a) For the following 5 years of quarterly data, perform **classical additive decomposition** and obtain the seasonal indices. State whether the series is seasonal. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`  **(7 marks)**

| Year | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 50 | 64 | 78 | 52 |
| 2 | 56 | 70 | 84 | 58 |
| 3 | 62 | 76 | 90 | 64 |
| 4 | 68 | 82 | 96 | 70 |
| 5 | 74 | 88 | 102 | 76 |

**Model Solution (Step-by-step):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify $s=4$, model $Y_t = T_t + S_t + I_t$.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Centered $2 \times 4$ MA. Compute for $t=3,\ldots,18$:

| $t$ | $Y_t$ | $MA_t = \frac{0.5 Y_{t-2} + Y_{t-1} + Y_t + Y_{t+1} + 0.5 Y_{t+2}}{4}$ | $D_t = Y_t - MA_t$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 3 | 78 | $(0.5\cdot50 + 64 + 78 + 52 + 0.5\cdot56)/4 = 61.25$ | $+16.75$ |
| 4 | 52 | $(0.5\cdot64 + 78 + 52 + 56 + 0.5\cdot70)/4 = 63.25$ | $-11.25$ |
| 5 | 56 | $(0.5\cdot78 + 52 + 56 + 70 + 0.5\cdot62)/4 = 60.25$ | $-4.25$ |
| 6 | 70 | $(0.5\cdot52 + 56 + 70 + 84 + 0.5\cdot76)/4 = 67.25$ | $+2.75$ |
| 7 | 84 | $(0.5\cdot56 + 70 + 84 + 58 + 0.5\cdot90)/4 = 71.25$ | $+12.75$ |
| 8 | 58 | $(0.5\cdot70 + 84 + 58 + 90 + 0.5\cdot62)/4 = 75.25$ | $-17.25$ |
| 9 | 62 | $(0.5\cdot84 + 58 + 62 + 76 + 0.5\cdot96)/4 = 71.50$ | $-9.50$ |
| 10 | 76 | $(0.5\cdot58 + 62 + 76 + 90 + 0.5\cdot88)/4 = 75.25$ | $+0.75$ |
| 11 | 90 | $(0.5\cdot62 + 76 + 90 + 64 + 0.5\cdot102)/4 = 79.50$ | $+10.50$ |
| 12 | 64 | $(0.5\cdot76 + 90 + 64 + 102 + 0.5\cdot74)/4 = 83.00$ | $-19.00$ |
| 13 | 68 | $(0.5\cdot90 + 64 + 68 + 82 + 0.5\cdot96)/4 = 79.00$ | $-11.00$ |
| 14 | 82 | $(0.5\cdot64 + 68 + 82 + 96 + 0.5\cdot88)/4 = 81.50$ | $+0.50$ |
| 15 | 96 | $(0.5\cdot68 + 82 + 96 + 70 + 0.5\cdot102)/4 = 86.50$ | $+9.50$ |
| 16 | 70 | $(0.5\cdot82 + 96 + 70 + 102 + 0.5\cdot74)/4 = 89.00$ | $-19.00$ |
| 17 | 74 | $(0.5\cdot96 + 70 + 74 + 88 + 0.5\cdot88)/4 = 82.50$ | $-8.50$ |
| 18 | 88 | $(0.5\cdot70 + 74 + 88 + 102 + 0.5\cdot$ —) | — |

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Average detrended values by quarter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{S}_{Q1} &= (-4.25 - 9.50 - 11.00 - 8.50)/4 = -33.25/4 = -8.3125 \\
\tilde{S}_{Q2} &= (+2.75 + 0.75 + 0.50)/3 = 4.00/3 = +1.3333 \\
\tilde{S}_{Q3} &= (+16.75 + 12.75 + 10.50 + 9.50)/4 = 49.50/4 = +12.375 \\
\tilde{S}_{Q4} &= (-11.25 - 17.25 - 19.00 - 19.00)/4 = -66.50/4 = -16.625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Center the indices:
$\bar{S} = (-8.3125 + 1.3333 + 12.375 - 16.625)/4 = -11.2292/4 = -2.8073$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{S}_{Q1} &= -8.3125 - (-2.8073) = -5.5052 \\
\hat{S}_{Q2} &= +1.3333 - (-2.8073) = +4.1406 \\
\hat{S}_{Q3} &= +12.375 - (-2.8073) = +15.1823 \\
\hat{S}_{Q4} &= -16.625 - (-2.8073) = -13.8177
\end{aligned}
$$

Check: $-5.51 + 4.14 + 15.18 - 13.82 \approx 0$ ✓

**Step 5 (0.5 mark):** **Conclusion** — The seasonal indices differ substantially from zero (Q3 = +15.18 is the festival/Onam quarter, Q4 = $-13.82$ is the post-festive slump), so the series is **clearly seasonal**.

### Q.A (b) Explain with a block diagram how you would **detect seasonality** in a time series using ACF. State the decision rule. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Compute the sample ACF:
$$\hat{\rho}_k = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(Y_t-\bar Y)(Y_{t-k}-\bar Y)}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(Y_t-\bar Y)^2}$$
for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2s$, where $s$ is the suspected period.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Identify candidate seasonal period $s$ (e.g., $s=12$ for monthly data from business knowledge or from a calendar sub-series).

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Construct the **Bartlett band** $\pm 2/\sqrt{n}$. Plot $\hat{\rho}_k$ versus $k$ as a stem/correlogram.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** **Decision Rule:**
- If $\vert \hat{\rho}_s \vert > 2/\sqrt{n}$ **and** the decay between seasonal lags is slow (or spikes recur at $2s, 3s, \ldots$), the series exhibits **seasonality of period $s$**.
- If no lag in the ACF significantly exceeds the Bartlett band, declare **no seasonality**.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Block diagram (use the ACF Signature Map from Section 4.3 as reference) showing: input series → ACF compute → compare to Bartlett band → decision diamond.

> *The block diagram should include: (i) the input box $Y_t$, (ii) the ACF compute box, (iii) the comparison with $2/\sqrt{n}$, (iv) the YES/NO decision diamond, (v) the output boxes for "Seasonal at period $s$" or "Not seasonal".*

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2)

### Q.B (a) With a suitable example, explain the **multiplicative decomposition model**. Derive the seasonal indices formula. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** **Model Statement.** The multiplicative model assumes $Y_t = T_t \cdot S_t \cdot I_t$. It is appropriate when the seasonal swing grows in **proportion** to the level of the series.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** **Example.** Monthly airline passenger bookings (in thousands) — the seasonal holiday bump of December is roughly 30% of the *current* annual level, not a fixed 5,000 extra seats. As the airline grows, so does the December peak. Hence multiplicative is appropriate.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** **Step A — Trend extraction.** Compute the centered $2 \times 12$ MA:
$$MA_t = \frac{0.5 Y_{t-6} + Y_{t-5} + \cdots + Y_{t+5} + 0.5 Y_{t+6}}{12}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** **Step B — Detrending by ratio.** Define
$$R_t = \frac{Y_t}{MA_t} = S_t \cdot I_t$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** **Step C — Seasonal index by averaging within each month $j \in \{1, \ldots, 12\}$.** Let $R_{i,j}$ be the detrended ratio of the $i$-th occurrence of month $j$, with $n_j$ values:
$$\tilde S_j = \frac{1}{n_j}\sum_{i=1}^{n_j} R_{i,j}$$

**Step 6 (0.5 mark):** **Step D — Normalize** so that the indices sum to $s$ (i.e., mean is $1$):
$$\hat S_j = \frac{\tilde S_j}{\bar S}, \quad \bar S = \frac{1}{s}\sum_{j=1}^{s}\tilde S_j$$

### Q.B (b) Discuss **seasonal differencing** as a deseasonalization technique. When is it preferred over decomposition? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** **Definition.** Seasonal differencing of order $s$ is defined as
$$\nabla_s Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-s}$$
It is the discrete-time analogue of a $1$-step seasonal derivative.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** **Effect on seasonality.** If $Y_t$ contains a seasonal unit root at frequency $2\pi/s$, then $\nabla_s Y_t$ removes it. For a pure seasonal $Y_t = S_t$ (with $S_{t+s}=S_t$), we have $\nabla_s Y_t = 0$, exactly eliminating seasonality.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** **Algorithm.**
1. Choose $s$ (e.g., $s=12$).
2. Compute $W_t = Y_t - Y_{t-12}$ for $t = 13, 14, \ldots, n$.
3. Examine ACF of $W_t$. The seasonal lag spike should now lie within the Bartlett band.
4. If a regular trend remains, apply additional first differencing: $\nabla_1 W_t$.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** **When preferred over decomposition.**
- When $n$ is **small** (decomposition loses $\sim s$ obs at each end).
- When the seasonal pattern is **evolving** in shape (STL handles this better; differencing is robust).
- As a **preprocessing** step before fitting a SARIMA$(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_s$ model.
- When the goal is to *transform* the series for stationarity, not to *quantify* each seasonal effect.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Caution.** Over-differencing (applying $\nabla_s$ when not needed) introduces *new* seasonal cycles and inflates variance. Always re-check the ACF after differencing.

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to center the seasonal indices.** Students compute $\tilde S_j$ but forget $\bar S$ subtraction. Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for this. Always enforce $\sum \hat S_j = 0$ (additive) or $= s$ (multiplicative).
>
> 2. **Using the wrong MA for even $s$.** A simple $s$-period MA is **not centered** for even $s$ — examiners *will* deduct marks. Use the $2 \times s$ centered MA with end-weights of $0.5$.
>
> 3. **Confusing "no ACF spike at lag 1" with "no seasonality".** Seasonality is checked at lag $s$, **not** lag $1$.
>
> 4. **Mixing additive and multiplicative in the same calculation.** Pick one model and stick to it.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the unit-of-measurement change** when logging. State explicitly: "We take $\log Y_t$ to convert multiplicative to additive."
>
> 6. **Omitting the diagnostic check** after deseasonalization. Always re-plot the ACF of $Y^{\text{adj}}_t$ and verify that seasonal lags are no longer significant.
>
> 7. **Reporting $\hat\rho_s$ without units or context.** Always compare to the Bartlett bound $2/\sqrt n$.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Pin this list to your revision wall.**

- **Seasonality** = periodic, calendar-locked variation with *known* period $s$.
- **Detection quartet:** (i) time plot, (ii) sub-series plot, (iii) ACF spike at lag $s$, (iv) formal test (Friedman / Kruskal–Wallis / QS).
- **Bartlett bound** for ACF significance: $\pm 2/\sqrt n$.
- **Two decomposition models:** Additive ($Y = T + S + I$, $\sum \hat S_j = 0$) vs Multiplicative ($Y = T \cdot S \cdot I$, $\sum \hat S_j = s$).
- **Multiplicative → Additive** by taking $\log Y_t$.
- **Centered MA for even $s$** uses end-weights of $1/2$: $MA_t = \frac{0.5Y_{t-s/2} + \sum Y_{t+j} + 0.5Y_{t+s/2}}{s}$.
- **Detrending:** additive uses subtraction, multiplicative uses ratio.
- **Seasonal indices** are the average detrended value *within each season*, then centered/normalized.
- **Deseasonalization:** $Y^{\text{adj}} = Y - \hat S$ (additive) or $Y^{\text{adj}} = Y / \hat S$ (multiplicative).
- **Seasonal differencing** $\nabla_s Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-s}$ is a fast alternative to decomposition, especially for short series.
- **Always re-check the ACF** of the deseasonalized series to confirm success.
- **STL decomposition** is robust to outliers and allows the seasonal pattern to evolve over time.
- **Choosing model:** if seasonal bumps grow with the level, use multiplicative (or log-additive).
- **Common $s$ values:** $s=4$ (quarterly), $s=12$ (monthly), $s=7$ (daily-weekly), $s=24$ (hourly-daily), $s=52$ (weekly-yearly).
- **KTU exam tip:** if a question gives you a small numeric table, *always* show the centered MA, the detrended values, the per-season average, the centering correction, and the final indices — every step carries a sub-mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detecting Cyclic Variation and Error
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Detecting Cyclic Variation and Error

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cyclic Variation ($C_t$)**: In classical time series decomposition, cyclic variation refers to the **non-periodic, wave-like fluctuations** in a time series that occur over a period **longer than one year**, typically driven by macro-economic, business, or socio-political forces. Unlike seasonal variation, the amplitude and length of a cycle are **not fixed**; they vary from one cycle to the next.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Error / Irregular / Random Component ($I_t$ or $E_t$)**: The **unexplained residual fluctuation** remaining in a series after the systematic components (Trend, Seasonal, and Cyclic) have been removed. It represents purely stochastic, short-duration, non-systematic shocks and is generally assumed to be **white noise** (zero mean, constant variance, no autocorrelation).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are sailing on the **Arabian Sea near Kochi**:

- **Trend ($T$)** is the **overall long-term direction** of your boat's voyage (e.g., moving steadily north over months).
- **Seasonal ($S$)** is the **predictable daily tide** — it rises and falls every 12 hours with clockwork regularity.
- **Cyclic ($C$)** is the **monsoon pattern** — a long wave that arrives every 3–7 years, but its exact strength and arrival vary each time.
- **Error / Irregular ($I$)** is the **sudden gust of wind** that hits your sail for 30 seconds — unpredictable, short, and chaotic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (High-Yield for KTU)**: The difference between *Seasonal* and *Cyclic* is a favourite KTU question. Seasonal = **fixed calendar period (≤ 1 year), known amplitude**. Cyclic = **variable period (> 1 year), unknown amplitude & length**.

### 1.3 Canonical Time Series Models

The detection of cyclic variation and error begins from a well-defined decomposition. The two classical specifications are:

**Additive Model**

$$Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$$

**Multiplicative Model**

$$Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$$

The **residual** (which carries $C_t$ and $I_t$) is obtained by:

$$R_t = Y_t - \hat{T}_t - \hat{S}_t \quad \text{(Additive)}$$

$$R_t = \frac{Y_t}{\hat{T}_t \times \hat{S}_t} \quad \text{(Multiplicative)}$$

The residual $R_t$ is then *un-mixed* into the cyclic and irregular components using **spectral density estimation** and **runs/turning-point tests**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Significance level ($\alpha$): 0.05** (default for randomness tests).
- **Lag $k$** in autocorrelation: integer $\geq 1$.
- **Nyquist frequency**: $\omega_{max} = \pi$ (in radians/sample).
- **Degrees of freedom** for runs test: typically 2 categories.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Pipeline

Detecting cyclic variation and error is a **four-stage cascade**:

**Stage 1 — Pre-processing & Trend Removal**
- Apply linear/polynomial regression OR differencing to strip $T_t$.
- Result: Detrended series $D_t = Y_t - \hat{T}_t$.

**Stage 2 — Seasonal Adjustment**
- Compute seasonal indices via centred moving average (CMA) or X-13ARIMA-SEATS logic.
- Result: Deseasonalised series $DS_t$.

**Stage 3 — Isolation of Cyclic & Error**
- Apply **autocorrelation function (ACF)** to $DS_t$.
- A non-zero ACF at long lags ($\geq 2$ years) ⇒ Cyclic component.
- A flat-near-zero ACF with no pattern ⇒ Random (Error) component.
- The cyclic component is extracted by **harmonic / Fourier decomposition** of the smoothed ACF peaks.

**Stage 4 — Statistical Validation of the Error Component**
- Apply **Runs Test** (Wald-Wolfowitz) to check randomness.
- Apply **Turning Point Test** (Moore-McCabe) to verify independence.
- Apply **Ljung-Box Q-test** for residual autocorrelation.
- Apply **Shapiro-Wilk** or **Anderson-Darling** for normality.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Use / Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|
| Additive decomposition | $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$ | When amplitude of variation is **constant** over time. |
| Multiplicative decomposition | $Y_t = T_t \cdot S_t \cdot C_t \cdot I_t$ | When amplitude **grows with trend**. |
| Sample Autocorrelation (ACF) | $r_k = \dfrac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(Y_t - \bar{Y})(Y_{t-k} - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(Y_t - \bar{Y})^2}$ | Measures linear dependence at lag $k$. |
| Periodogram (Schuster) | $I(\omega_j) = \dfrac{2}{n}\left[\left(\sum_{t=1}^{n} Y_t \cos \omega_j t\right)^2 + \left(\sum_{t=1}^{n} Y_t \sin \omega_j t\right)^2\right]$ | Detects dominant cyclic frequencies. |
| Fourier frequencies | $\omega_j = \dfrac{2\pi j}{n},\ j = 1, 2, \dots, \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ | Discrete frequency grid for spectral analysis. |
| Variance of $r_k$ under $H_0$ (white noise) | $\mathrm{Var}(r_k) \approx \dfrac{1}{n}$ | Builds $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ confidence band. |
| Ljung-Box Q-statistic | $Q_h = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{h}\dfrac{r_k^{\,2}}{n-k}$ | Tests *joint* significance of first $h$ autocorrelations. |
| Runs Test (Z-statistic) | $Z = \dfrac{R - \mu_R}{\sigma_R}$ | $R$ = # of runs; tests randomness. |
| Expected Runs under $H_0$ | $\mu_R = \dfrac{2 n_1 n_2}{n_1+n_2} + 1$ | For runs of $+$ and $-$ signs. |
| Turning Point Test (Moore-McCabe) | $E(TP) = \dfrac{2(n-2)}{3}$ | Expected # of turning points if random. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not confuse** $Z$ for runs test with the standard normal deviate. Always verify $\mu_R$ and $\sigma_R$ formulas against the *two-category* version in your textbook (Wald–Wolfowitz 1940).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Economics & Finance**: Business cycle dating (NBER uses periodogram + ACF).
- **Electrical Load Forecasting**: After stripping daily/weekly seasonality, residual cyclic patterns reveal industrial consumption waves.
- **Hydrology**: Long-term reservoir inflow analysis depends on isolating multi-year El-Niño cycles.
- **Telecommunications (KTU ECE context)**: Long-range dependent traffic (self-similar processes) is diagnosed via spectral peaks at low frequencies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Autocorrelation Function (ACF) from First Principles

The **population autocorrelation** between $Y_t$ and $Y_{t-k}$ is:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\mathrm{Cov}(Y_t,\ Y_{t-k})}{\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}(Y_t)\ \mathrm{Var}(Y_{t-k})}}$$

Assuming the series is **weakly stationary**, $\mathrm{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma_Y^2$ for all $t$, so:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0}$$

where $\gamma_k = \mathrm{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t-k})$ is the autocovariance at lag $k$.

The **sample estimator** replaces moments with sums:

**Step 1 — Compute sample mean.**

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} Y_t$$

**Step 2 — Numerator of $r_k$ (numerical computation).**

$$c_k = \sum_{t=k+1}^{n} (Y_t - \bar{Y})(Y_{t-k} - \bar{Y})$$

**Step 3 — Denominator (total sum of squares).**

$$c_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} (Y_t - \bar{Y})^2$$

**Step 4 — Form the estimator.**

$$r_k = \frac{c_k}{c_0}$$

**Step 5 — Asymptotic distribution (white noise).** Under $H_0 : \rho_k = 0$ for a white noise sequence:

$$\sqrt{n}\, r_k \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0,\ 1) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{Var}(r_k) \approx \frac{1}{n}$$

**Step 6 — 95% confidence band.**

$$r_k \in \left[ -\frac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}},\ \frac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}} \right]$$

If $r_k$ exceeds this band, the series is **not** random ⇒ **cyclic component exists**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Periodogram and Detection of Cycle Period

**Step 1 — Start with the discrete Fourier transform pair.**

$$Y_t = a_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left[a_j \cos(\omega_j t) + b_j \sin(\omega_j t)\right]$$

where $m = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ and $\omega_j = 2\pi j / n$.

**Step 2 — Compute the Fourier coefficients.**

$$a_j = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} Y_t \cos(\omega_j t)$$

$$b_j = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} Y_t \sin(\omega_j t)$$

**Step 3 — Form the periodogram (power at frequency $\omega_j$).**

$$I(\omega_j) = \frac{n}{2}(a_j^{\,2} + b_j^{\,2})$$

Equivalently:

$$I(\omega_j) = \frac{2}{n}\left[\left(\sum_{t=1}^{n} Y_t \cos \omega_j t\right)^2 + \left(\sum_{t=1}^{n} Y_t \sin \omega_j t\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Convert frequency to period (length of cycle).**

$$T_j = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_j} = \frac{n}{j} \quad \text{(in time units)}$$

**Step 5 — Identify dominant cycle.** The frequency $j^*$ that maximises $I(\omega_{j^*})$ corresponds to the **dominant cyclic period** $T_{j^*} = n/j^*$.

**Step 6 — Statistical significance test.** Under $H_0$ (pure white noise), the normalised periodogram $I(\omega_j) / \sigma^2$ follows a chi-squared distribution with 2 d.f.:

$$\frac{I(\omega_j)}{\sigma^2} \sim \chi^2_{(2)}$$

A peak exceeding the **upper $\alpha$ quantile** (e.g., $\chi^2_{0.95,(2)} = 5.99$) confirms a significant cyclic component.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Runs Test Statistic (Wald-Wolfowitz)

**Step 1 — Encode each residual as $+$ (if $R_t > 0$) or $-$ (if $R_t < 0$).**

Let $n_1$ = number of $+$ signs, $n_2$ = number of $-$ signs, with $n_1 + n_2 = N$.

**Step 2 — Count the number of runs $R$.** A "run" is a maximal consecutive block of identical signs.

**Step 3 — Mean of $R$ under randomness.**

$$\mu_R = \frac{2 n_1 n_2}{N} + 1$$

**Step 4 — Variance of $R$ under randomness.**

$$\sigma_R^{\,2} = \frac{2 n_1 n_2 (2 n_1 n_2 - N)}{N^2 (N-1)}$$

**Step 5 — Standardised test statistic.**

$$Z = \frac{R - \mu_R}{\sigma_R}$$

**Step 6 — Decision rule.** Reject $H_0$ (randomness) at $\alpha = 0.05$ if:

$$\vert Z \vert > 1.96$$

A **low** $Z$ indicates clustering (cyclic dependence), a **high** $Z$ indicates excessive alternation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Which Test Detects What?

| **Component Suspected** | **Best Detector** | **Decision Threshold** |
|---|---|---|
| Pure white noise (Error) | ACF + Ljung-Box | All $\vert r_k \vert \leq 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ |
| Cyclic (single period) | Periodogram peak | Peak $>$ $\chi^2_{0.95,(2)}$ |
| Cyclic (multiple periods) | Spectral density (smoothed periodogram) | Multiple peaks above 5.99 |
| Non-random residuals | Runs Test | $\vert Z \vert \leq 1.96$ for randomness |
| Non-independence | Turning Point Test | Observed TP within $\pm 1.96\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}(TP)}$ of $E(TP)$ |
| Non-normal errors | Shapiro-Wilk / Anderson-Darling | $p$-value $>$ 0.05 for normality |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawSeries[Raw Time Series Yt] --> A1[Step 1: Trend Removal via CMA or Regression]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Seasonal Index Computation]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Deseasonalised Series DSt]
    A3 --> B1[ACF Plot Generation]
    A3 --> B2[Periodogram Computation]
    A3 --> B3[Residual Extraction Rt]
    B1 --> C1{Long Lag rk Exceeds 1.96/sqrt n?}
    C1 -- YES --> C2[Cyclic Component Detected Ct]
    C1 -- NO --> C3[Pure Random Error It]
    B2 --> D1{Peak I omega j Above Chi Square 5.99?}
    D1 -- YES --> C2
    D1 -- NO --> C3
    B3 --> E1[Runs Test Wald Wolfowitz]
    B3 --> E2[Turning Point Test Moore McCabe]
    B3 --> E3[Ljung Box Q Test]
    E1 --> F1{All Tests Pass?}
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    F1 -- YES --> C3
    F1 -- NO --> C2
    C2 --> G1[Reconstruct Series Yt equals Tt plus St plus Ct plus It]
    C3 --> G1
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic Flow — Cyclic vs Error Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT]
        R[Residual Series Rt]
    end
    subgraph STAGE1[STAGE 1 SPECTRAL]
        S1[Compute ACF rk]
        S2[Compute Periodogram I omega j]
    end
    subgraph STAGE2[STAGE 2 INFERENCE]
        J1{ACF Shows Significant Peaks at Multi Year Lags}
        J2{Periodogram Peak Above 5.99}
    end
    subgraph STAGE3[STAGE 3 RANDOMNESS]
        K1[Runs Test Z statistic]
        K2[Turning Point Test]
        K3[Ljung Box Q]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT]
        L1[Output Cyclic Component Ct]
        L2[Output Random Error It]
    end
    R --> S1
    R --> S2
    S1 --> J1
    S2 --> J2
    J1 -- YES --> L1
    J2 -- YES --> L1
    J1 -- NO --> K1
    J2 -- NO --> K1
    K1 --> K2 --> K3
    K3 -- All Pass --> L2
    K3 -- Any Fail --> L1
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping — How Each Component Manifests in the ACF

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[Pure White Noise Error Only] --> P0a[ACF r1 near 0 and r2 near 0 and ... all near 0]
    P1[Trend Component Tt] --> P1a[ACF decays very slowly Positive for all lags]
    P2[Seasonal Component St] --> P2a[ACF spikes at lag 4 12 52 etc]
    P3[Cyclic Component Ct] --> P3a[ACF shows wave pattern Peaks at irregular multi year lags]
    P0a --> Q[Summary Identification]
    P1a --> Q
    P2a --> Q
    P3a --> Q
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*Distinguish between seasonal variation and cyclic variation in a time series. Why is it important to separate them in forecasting?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Seasonal variation** occurs at *fixed calendar intervals* (e.g., monthly, quarterly) within a year and has a *known, constant* amplitude. (1 mark)
2. **Cyclic variation** has a *variable period* typically *longer than one year* (e.g., 3–10 year business cycle) with *changing amplitude*. (1 mark)
3. They must be separated because seasonal patterns can be extrapolated deterministically (e.g., using seasonal indices), whereas cyclic patterns require spectral/econometric modelling (periodogram, ARIMA). Treating them together leads to inflated residual variance. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*State the assumptions made about the irregular (error) component $I_t$ in classical time series decomposition. List any two tests used to validate these assumptions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Assumptions: $I_t$ is **white noise** with $E(I_t) = 0$, constant variance $\mathrm{Var}(I_t) = \sigma^2$, and **no autocorrelation** $\rho_k = 0$ for $k \geq 1$. (1.5 marks)
2. **Two validation tests**: (a) Ljung-Box Q-test for joint autocorrelation; (b) Runs Test (Wald-Wolfowitz) for randomness. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Rule**: Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the procedure for detecting cyclic variation in a time series using the **periodogram**. Derive the periodogram formula and the criterion for identifying a significant cycle.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Set up the Fourier representation (1 mark).** A zero-mean series $Y_t$ can be expanded as:

$$Y_t = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left[a_j \cos(\omega_j t) + b_j \sin(\omega_j t)\right]$$

where $\omega_j = 2\pi j / n$ and $m = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$.

**Step 2 — Coefficient estimators (1 mark).**

$$a_j = \frac{2}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} Y_t \cos(\omega_j t), \qquad b_j = \frac{2}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} Y_t \sin(\omega_j t)$$

**Step 3 — Periodogram definition (1.5 marks).**

$$I(\omega_j) = \frac{n}{2}(a_j^{\,2} + b_j^{\,2}) = \frac{2}{n}\left[\left(\sum Y_t \cos \omega_j t\right)^2 + \left(\sum Y_t \sin \omega_j t\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Convert to period (1 mark).** $T_j = 2\pi/\omega_j = n/j$.

**Step 5 — Significance criterion (1.5 marks).** Under $H_0$ (white noise), $I(\omega_j)/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_{(2)}$. A peak at $\omega_{j^*}$ is significant at $\alpha = 0.05$ iff $I(\omega_{j^*}) > 5.99\,\sigma^2$.

**Step 6 — Cycle identification (1 mark).** The period $T_{j^*} = n/j^*$ is the **dominant cyclic period**. Multiple significant peaks indicate multiple cycles.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Annual sales data ($n = 50$ years) is given. After removing trend and seasonality, the residuals $R_t$ are coded as $+$ or $-$ yielding $n_1 = 26$, $n_2 = 24$, and observed number of runs $R = 19$. Test at 5% level whether the residual series is random.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses (1 mark).**
$H_0$: The residual series is random.
$H_1$: The series is non-random (cyclic dependence).

**Step 2 — Compute expected runs $\mu_R$ (1.5 marks).**

$$\mu_R = \frac{2 n_1 n_2}{n_1 + n_2} + 1 = \frac{2 \times 26 \times 24}{50} + 1 = 24.96 + 1 = 25.96$$

**Step 3 — Compute variance of $R$ (1.5 marks).**

$$\sigma_R^{\,2} = \frac{2 n_1 n_2 (2 n_1 n_2 - N)}{N^2 (N-1)} = \frac{2(26)(24)\big(2(26)(24)-50\big)}{50^2 \cdot 49}$$

Compute numerator piece: $2 \cdot 26 \cdot 24 = 1248$. Then $2 n_1 n_2 - N = 1248 - 50 = 1198$.

$$\sigma_R^{\,2} = \frac{1248 \times 1198}{2500 \times 49} = \frac{1{,}495{,}104}{122{,}500} \approx 12.205$$

$$\sigma_R \approx 3.494$$

**Step 4 — Compute $Z$ statistic (1.5 marks).**

$$Z = \frac{R - \mu_R}{\sigma_R} = \frac{19 - 25.96}{3.494} = \frac{-6.96}{3.494} \approx -1.992$$

**Step 5 — Decision (1 mark).** Critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) is $\pm 1.96$. Since $\vert Z \vert = 1.992 > 1.96$, **reject $H_0$**.

**Step 6 — Conclusion (0.5 mark).** The residual series is **not random**; the low $Z$ value suggests **positive clustering** (cyclic dependence). The data exhibit a significant cyclic component.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **sample autocorrelation function (ACF)** formula and explain how the correlogram is used to identify cyclic variation.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition of population ACF (1 mark).** For a weakly stationary series:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0} = \frac{\mathrm{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t-k})}{\mathrm{Var}(Y_t)}$$

**Step 2 — Sample autocovariance (1.5 marks).**

$$c_k = \sum_{t=k+1}^{n} (Y_t - \bar{Y})(Y_{t-k} - \bar{Y}), \quad c_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} (Y_t - \bar{Y})^2$$

**Step 3 — Sample ACF (1.5 marks).**

$$r_k = \frac{c_k}{c_0}$$

**Step 4 — 95% confidence band under $H_0$ (1 mark).**

$$r_k \in \left[-\frac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}},\ \frac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Cyclic detection (1.5 marks).** A *correlogram* (plot of $r_k$ vs $k$) shows:
- A **slow decay / persistent wave pattern** at multi-year lags → cyclic component.
- Distinct peaks at irregular lags (e.g., lag 3, 7, 11) indicate the **average cycle length**.
- All $r_k$ within the band → pure error (white noise).
- Negative $r_k$ at seasonal lags → seasonal oscillation.

**Step 6 — Practical utility (0.5 mark).** The correlogram is a visual diagnostic — applied *after* detrending and deseasonalising — to decide whether to model the residual as ARMA (random) or as a harmonic cycle (deterministic cyclic).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** The annual data of rainfall (mm) in Kerala for $n = 40$ years, after detrending and deseasonalising, yields autocorrelations $r_1 = 0.45$, $r_2 = 0.32$, $r_3 = 0.18$, $r_4 = 0.08$, $r_5 = 0.02$. Test for the presence of cyclic variation at 5% level.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses (1 mark).**
$H_0$: $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho_3 = \rho_4 = \rho_5 = 0$ (no cyclic variation).
$H_1$: At least one $\rho_k \neq 0$.

**Step 2 — Confidence band (1 mark).**

$$\pm \frac{1.96}{\sqrt{40}} = \pm \frac{1.96}{6.3246} \approx \pm 0.3099$$

**Step 3 — Lag-wise test (1.5 marks).**

| Lag $k$ | $r_k$ | $\vert r_k \vert > 0.3099$? | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.45 | Yes | Significant |
| 2 | 0.32 | Yes | Significant |
| 3 | 0.18 | No | Not significant |
| 4 | 0.08 | No | Not significant |
| 5 | 0.02 | No | Not significant |

**Step 4 — Ljung-Box joint test (2 marks).**

$$Q_5 = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{5} \frac{r_k^{\,2}}{n-k} = 40 \times 42 \times \left[\frac{0.45^2}{39} + \frac{0.32^2}{38} + \frac{0.18^2}{37} + \frac{0.08^2}{36} + \frac{0.02^2}{35}\right]$$

Compute each fraction:
- $0.2025 / 39 = 0.005192$
- $0.1024 / 38 = 0.002695$
- $0.0324 / 37 = 0.000876$
- $0.0064 / 36 = 0.000178$
- $0.0004 / 35 = 0.0000114$

Sum $= 0.008952$.

$$Q_5 = 1680 \times 0.008952 \approx 15.04$$

**Step 5 — Decision (1 mark).** Critical value $\chi^2_{0.95,(5)} = 11.07$. Since $Q_5 = 15.04 > 11.07$, **reject $H_0$**.

**Step 6 — Conclusion (0.5 mark).** A significant cyclic component exists. The significant autocorrelations at lag 1 and 2, decaying to non-significance, suggest a **short-cycle component** (likely a 2–3 year climatic cycle such as the Indian Ocean Dipole).

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Detecting Cyclic & Error Components**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Seasonal vs Cyclic Periods**: Cyclic periods are *NOT* calendar-fixed. If a student writes "the cycle repeats every December," the examiner deducts 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the Constant in Runs Test**: $\mu_R = \frac{2 n_1 n_2}{N} + 1$ has a "+1" — omitting it costs 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Wrong Confidence Band**: Using $1.96/\sqrt{n-k}$ for individual lag is for Bartlett bands; the *marginal* band for ACF is $1.96/\sqrt{n}$. Mixing these up costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Misinterpreting Negative $Z$**: A *low* $Z$ in the runs test means **clustering** (cyclic), not "strong evidence for randomness." Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Skipping the Unit-Root for Trend Removal**: Without removing trend first, ACF is dominated by trend — a classic mistake. Always detrend *first*.
> 6. **Confusing Periodogram with Spectral Density**: Periodogram is *raw* and inconsistent; smoothed spectral density is consistent. KTU may ask for the difference.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cyclic variation** is *non-periodic*, *multi-year*, and *amplitude-varying*; **seasonal** is the opposite.
- Detection pipeline: **Detrend → Deseasonalise → ACF/Periodogram → Tests on residuals**.
- **ACF** at lag $k$: $r_k = c_k / c_0$; **95% band** is $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$.
- **Periodogram** peak at $\omega_j$ ⇒ cycle length $T_j = n/j$. Significant if $I(\omega_j) / \sigma^2 > 5.99$.
- **Runs Test** randomness criterion: $\vert Z \vert \leq 1.96$ at 5% level; $Z$ uses $\mu_R = 2 n_1 n_2 / N + 1$.
- **Ljung-Box Q** tests joint significance: $Q_h = n(n+2) \sum r_k^{\,2} / (n-k)$, compared with $\chi^2_{(h)}$.
- **Turning Point Test** checks independence via $E(TP) = 2(n-2)/3$.
- **Error component** $I_t$ is **white noise** by definition — zero mean, constant variance, no autocorrelation.
- **Additive model** suits constant amplitude; **multiplicative** suits amplitude growing with trend.
- **Always detrend first** — trend dominates ACF and masks cyclic patterns.
- For KTU 14-mark questions, present *both* the formula derivation *and* a numerical worked example.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Irregular Component and Residuals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Irregular Component and Residuals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of the Irregular Component

In classical **Time Series Decomposition**, an observed time series $Y_t$ is typically modeled as the combination of four systematic components:

$$Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$$

where:
- $T_t$ = **Trend Component** (long-term directional movement)
- $S_t$ = **Seasonal Component** (periodic fluctuations within a year)
- $C_t$ = **Cyclical Component** (long-term oscillations, e.g., business cycles)
- $I_t$ = **Irregular Component** (random, unpredictable noise)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Irregular Component ($I_t$):** It is the purely **random, residual, and unpredictable** part of a time series that remains after the systematic components (Trend, Seasonal, Cyclical) have been removed. It represents stochastic shocks, measurement errors, and unforeseen environmental disturbances. It is also called the **Random Component** or **Noise Component**.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Residuals

When an **estimated model** $\hat{Y}_t$ is fitted to a time series, the **residuals** are defined as the difference between the observed value and the model-fitted value:

$$\hat{e}_t = Y_t - \hat{Y}_t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Residuals ($\hat{e}_t$):** The empirical estimates of the irregular component obtained from a fitted statistical model. They are the *realization* of the theoretical error term $\varepsilon_t$ in a model. In an ideal model fit, the residuals should behave like the true irregular component — i.e., white noise.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (The Orchestra):**
> Imagine an orchestra playing a symphony. The orchestra's *score* is the deterministic structure ($T_t, S_t, C_t$). However, in any actual performance, there is a tiny amount of *unavoidable randomness* — a slight cough from a listener, a violinist's bow slipping, a minor tuning drift. These small, unpredictable disturbances are the **irregular component** ($I_t$).
>
> Now, when you record the concert and then mathematically *subtract* the clean score from the recorded audio, what you are left with is the **residuals** — your best *estimate* of those small random disturbances. A great conductor (i.e., a good model) makes the residuals sound like pure silence (white noise), meaning the model has captured everything systematic.

### 1.4 Key Distinguishing Properties

| Feature | Irregular Component ($I_t$) | Residuals ($\hat{e}_t$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nature** | Theoretical, unobserved | Empirical, computed from data |
| **Source** | Real-world random shocks | Model fitting errors |
| **Existence** | Exists in the true DGP | Exists only after modeling |
| **Mathematical Form** | Probability distribution | Real numbers |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Properties of the Irregular Component

For a *well-behaved* irregular component (a stationary random process), the following properties must hold:

1. **Zero Mean:** The expected value of $I_t$ is zero at every time point.
2. **Constant Variance (Homoscedasticity):** The variance does not change over time.
3. **No Autocorrelation:** The values are statistically independent across time.
4. **Normally Distributed:** Often assumed to follow a Gaussian distribution.

### 2.2 Structural Logical Steps of Analysis

**Step 1 — Decomposition Extraction:** 
First, we systematically strip away the structured parts. We estimate trend $\hat{T}_t$ using moving averages or regression, and seasonal indices $\hat{S}_t$ using ratios or dummy variables.

**Step 2 — Detrending and Deseasonalizing:**
We compute the detrended series $Y_t - \hat{T}_t$ and then the seasonally adjusted series $(Y_t - \hat{T}_t) / \hat{S}_t$.

**Step 3 — Isolation of Randomness:** 
The leftover variation is attributed to the irregular component. In classical decomposition: $\hat{I}_t = Y_t - \hat{T}_t - \hat{S}_t - \hat{C}_t$.

**Step 4 — Diagnostic Testing:** 
We statistically test whether the extracted irregular component behaves like **white noise**. If it does, the model is *adequate*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reference — Key Equations for Irregular Component & Residuals**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Classical Decomposition (Additive) | $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$ | Components combined additively |
| 2 | Classical Decomposition (Multiplicative) | $Y_t = T_t \cdot S_t \cdot C_t \cdot I_t$ | Components combined multiplicatively |
| 3 | Residual Definition | $\hat{e}_t = Y_t - \hat{Y}_t$ | Observed minus Fitted |
| 4 | White Noise Mean | $E(\varepsilon_t) = 0$ | Zero mean property |
| 5 | White Noise Variance | $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) = \sigma^2$ | Constant finite variance |
| 6 | White Noise Autocovariance | $\gamma_k = 0$ for all $k \neq 0$ | No serial correlation |
| 7 | White Noise Process | $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$ | Standard white noise notation |
| 8 | Sample Mean of Residuals | $\bar{e} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \hat{e}_t$ | Should be near zero |
| 9 | Sample Variance of Residuals | $s_e^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(\hat{e}_t - \bar{e})^2$ | Should be small |
| 10 | Sample Autocorrelation of Residuals | $\hat{\rho}_k = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(\hat{e}_t - \bar{e})(\hat{e}_{t-k} - \bar{e})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(\hat{e}_t - \bar{e})^2}$ | Should be inside confidence bands |
| 11 | Ljung-Box Test Statistic | $Q(m) = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\frac{\hat{\rho}_k^2}{n-k}$ | Tests joint null of zero autocorrelations |
| 12 | Chi-Square Critical Value | $Q(m) \sim \chi^2_{m}$ | Under $H_0$ of white noise |
| 13 | Standard Error of $\hat{\rho}_k$ | $SE(\hat{\rho}_k) \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Bartlett's approximation |
| 14 | 95% Confidence Band | $\pm \frac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Bounds for $\hat{\rho}_k$ under $H_0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Warning on Pipes:** In KTU answer sheets, **never** use $\vert$ inside table cells. Always write $\lvert x \rvert$ or use the LaTeX `\vert` command for absolute value notation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The decomposition of irregular components and residual analysis is used in:
- **Economics:** Isolating random shocks in GDP, inflation, and stock prices.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Signal denoising in communication channels.
- **Meteorology:** Distinguishing climate change trends from weather noise.
- **Industrial Quality Control:** Detecting assignable causes of variation versus common-cause noise.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Removing motion artifacts from ECG/EEG signals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Decomposition to Residual Extraction

We begin with the classical additive decomposition model and isolate the irregular component.

**Given:** $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$

**Step 1 — Trend Estimation:** 
A common method to estimate the trend is the Centered Moving Average (CMA) of order $m$ (where $m$ is the seasonal period, e.g., 12 for monthly data).

$$
\hat{T}_t = \frac{1}{m}\left[\frac{1}{2}Y_{t-m/2} + Y_{t-m/2+1} + \cdots + Y_{t+m/2-1} + \frac{1}{2}Y_{t+m/2}\right]
$$

This averages out the seasonal and irregular components because they sum approximately to zero over a full period.

**Step 2 — Detrended Series (Deseasonalized + Cyclic Removed):** 
Subtract the estimated trend from the original series:

$$
Y_t - \hat{T}_t = S_t + C_t + I_t
$$

**Step 3 — Seasonal Index Estimation:** 
The seasonal indices are estimated by averaging the detrended values for each season. For a multiplicative model:

$$
\hat{S}_j = \frac{1}{n_j}\sum_{i \in \text{season } j}(Y_t - \hat{T}_t)
$$

followed by normalization so that seasonal indices sum to zero (additive) or multiply to $m$ (multiplicative).

**Step 4 — De-seasonalized Series:** 
Subtract (additive) or divide (multiplicative) the seasonal index to obtain:

$$
Z_t = Y_t - \hat{T}_t - \hat{S}_t = C_t + I_t
$$

**Step 5 — Irregular Component Extraction:** 
Since the cyclic component is typically modeled with longer moving averages, the *residual* irregular component is estimated as:

$$
\hat{I}_t = Y_t - \hat{T}_t - \hat{S}_t - \hat{C}_t
$$

This $\hat{I}_t$ *is* the residual from the classical decomposition model.

### 3.2 Derivation: Properties of a White Noise Process

A time series $\{\varepsilon_t\}$ is a **white noise** process, denoted $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$, if and only if it satisfies:

**Step 1 — Zero Mean Condition:**

$$
E[\varepsilon_t] = 0 \quad \forall t
$$

This ensures no systematic bias in the irregular component.

**Step 2 — Constant Variance (Homoscedasticity):**

$$
\text{Var}[\varepsilon_t] = E[(\varepsilon_t - E[\varepsilon_t])^2] = E[\varepsilon_t^2] = \sigma^2 < \infty
$$

A finite variance is essential; otherwise, the process could explode (e.g., infinite variance processes).

**Step 3 — Zero Autocovariance:**

$$
\gamma_k = E[\varepsilon_t \cdot \varepsilon_{t+k}] = 
\begin{cases}
\sigma^2, & k = 0 \\
0, & k \neq 0
\end{cases}
$$

The covariance between any two distinct time points is zero, meaning no memory exists in the process.

**Step 4 — Sample Autocorrelation Function (ACF) of Residuals:**

$$
\hat{\rho}_k = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_k}{\hat{\gamma}_0} = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(e_t - \bar{e})(e_{t-k} - \bar{e})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(e_t - \bar{e})^2}
$$

Under the null hypothesis that residuals are white noise, $\hat{\rho}_k$ is asymptotically $N(0, 1/n)$, so the $95\%$ confidence interval is $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Ljung-Box Test for Residual Whiteness

The Ljung-Box statistic tests the joint hypothesis that the first $m$ autocorrelations are zero.

**Step 1 — Individual Test Idea:** 
For a single lag $k$, under white noise, $\hat{\rho}_k^2 \approx 1/n$ in distribution.

**Step 2 — Joint Test Aggregation:** 
Sum the squared autocorrelations weighted by $(n-k)$:

$$
Q(m) = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\frac{\hat{\rho}_k^2}{n-k}
$$

**Step 3 — Asymptotic Distribution:** 
Under $H_0$: residuals are white noise, $Q(m) \sim \chi^2_{m}$ as $n \to \infty$.

**Step 4 — Decision Rule:** 
- If $Q(m) > \chi^2_{m, \alpha}$, reject $H_0$ → residuals are NOT white noise → model is inadequate.
- If $Q(m) \leq \chi^2_{m, \alpha}$, fail to reject $H_0$ → residuals are white noise → model is adequate.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A linear trend model $\hat{Y}_t = 10 + 0.5t$ is fitted to a time series of $n = 10$ observations. The observed values and residuals are:

| $t$ | $Y_t$ | $\hat{Y}_t$ | $\hat{e}_t = Y_t - \hat{Y}_t$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 10.2 | 10.5 | $-0.3$ |
| 2 | 11.0 | 11.0 | $0.0$ |
| 3 | 11.5 | 11.5 | $0.0$ |
| 4 | 12.4 | 12.0 | $+0.4$ |
| 5 | 12.3 | 12.5 | $-0.2$ |
| 6 | 13.1 | 13.0 | $+0.1$ |
| 7 | 13.6 | 13.5 | $+0.1$ |
| 8 | 14.5 | 14.0 | $+0.5$ |
| 9 | 14.4 | 14.5 | $-0.1$ |
| 10 | 15.2 | 15.0 | $+0.2$ |

**Step 1 — Compute Sample Mean of Residuals:**

$$
\bar{e} = \frac{1}{10}\sum_{t=1}^{10}\hat{e}_t = \frac{(-0.3) + 0 + 0 + 0.4 + (-0.2) + 0.1 + 0.1 + 0.5 + (-0.1) + 0.2}{10}
$$

$$
\bar{e} = \frac{0.7}{10} = 0.07
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Sample Variance:**

$$
s_e^2 = \frac{1}{9}\sum_{t=1}^{10}(\hat{e}_t - 0.07)^2
$$

$$
s_e^2 = \frac{1}{9}\left[(-0.37)^2 + (-0.07)^2 + (-0.07)^2 + (0.33)^2 + (-0.27)^2 + (0.03)^2 + (0.03)^2 + (0.43)^2 + (-0.17)^2 + (0.13)^2\right]
$$

$$
s_e^2 = \frac{1}{9}\left[0.1369 + 0.0049 + 0.0049 + 0.1089 + 0.0729 + 0.0009 + 0.0009 + 0.1849 + 0.0289 + 0.0169\right]
$$

$$
s_e^2 = \frac{1}{9} \times 0.5610 \approx 0.0623
$$

**Step 3 — Sample Standard Deviation:**

$$
s_e = \sqrt{0.0623} \approx 0.2496
$$

**Step 4 — First-Order Autocorrelation of Residuals:**

$$
\hat{\rho}_1 = \frac{\sum_{t=2}^{10}(\hat{e}_t - \bar{e})(\hat{e}_{t-1} - \bar{e})}{\sum_{t=1}^{10}(\hat{e}_t - \bar{e})^2}
$$

Computing the numerator:
- $(-0.07)(-0.37) = 0.0259$
- $(-0.07)(-0.07) = 0.0049$
- $(0.33)(-0.07) = -0.0231$
- $(-0.27)(0.33) = -0.0891$
- $(0.03)(-0.27) = -0.0081$
- $(0.03)(0.03) = 0.0009$
- $(0.43)(0.03) = 0.0129$
- $(-0.17)(0.43) = -0.0731$
- $(0.13)(-0.17) = -0.0221$

Sum = $0.0259 + 0.0049 - 0.0231 - 0.0891 - 0.0081 + 0.0009 + 0.0129 - 0.0731 - 0.0221 = -0.1709$

$$
\hat{\rho}_1 = \frac{-0.1709}{0.5610} \approx -0.305
$$

**Step 5 — 95% Confidence Bound:**

$$
\pm \frac{1.96}{\sqrt{10}} = \pm 0.620
$$

Since $\vert \hat{\rho}_1 \vert = 0.305 < 0.620$, the first-order autocorrelation is **not significant**. The residuals appear to be white noise, suggesting the linear trend model is adequate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Time Series Decomposition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed Time Series Yt] --> B[Trend Estimation]
    B --> C[Compute Detrended Series]
    C --> D[Seasonal Index Estimation]
    D --> E[Compute Deseasonalized Series]
    E --> F[Cyclical Component Estimation]
    F --> G[Residual Extraction]
    G --> H[Irregular Component It]
    H --> I[Diagnostic Tests]
    I --> J{White Noise?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Model Adequate]
    J -->|No| L[Re-specify Model]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Residual Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fitted Model Yhat t] --> B[Compute Residuals e t]
    B --> C[Sample Mean Check]
    B --> D[Variance Check]
    B --> E[ACF Computation]
    B --> F[Ljung-Box Q Statistic]
    C --> G{Mean approx Zero}
    D --> H{Variance Constant}
    E --> I{ACs inside Band}
    F --> J{Q less than Chi Square}
    G --> K[Conclusion]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Time Series Yt] --> B[Systematic Part]
    A --> C[Irregular / Random Part]
    B --> D[Trend Tt]
    B --> E[Seasonal St]
    B --> F[Cyclical Ct]
    C --> G[Residuals after Model Fit]
    C --> H[True Unobserved Shocks]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Mapping: Why Residuals Must Behave as White Noise

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[If Model Captures All Structure] --> B[Residuals Contain Only Random Noise]
    B --> C[White Noise: Zero Mean Constant Variance Zero Autocorrelation]
    C --> D[Ljung-Box Test Fails to Reject H0]
    D --> E[Model is Adequate and Validated]
    F[If Model Misses Structure] --> G[Residuals Contain Systematic Pattern]
    G --> H[Autocorrelation Detected in ACF]
    H --> I[Ljung-Box Test Rejects H0]
    I --> J[Model is Inadequate Re-fit Required]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`** 
Define the irregular component of a time series. List its essential statistical properties.

**Model Answer:** 
The irregular component ($I_t$) is the random, unpredictable residual part of a time series that remains after the systematic components (trend, seasonal, cyclical) have been removed. It represents stochastic shocks and measurement noise.

Its essential properties are:
1. **Zero Mean:** $E[I_t] = 0$
2. **Constant Variance:** $\text{Var}(I_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty$
3. **No Autocorrelation:** $\gamma_k = 0$ for all $k \neq 0$
4. **Independence:** Values at different time points are statistically independent
5. **Normal Distribution:** Often assumed to follow $I_t \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$

**(3 Marks — 1 for definition, 2 for properties)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`** 
Distinguish between the irregular component and residuals in time series analysis.

**Model Answer:** 
| Aspect | Irregular Component | Residuals |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nature | Theoretical, unobserved | Empirical, computed from data |
| Origin | Real-world random shocks | Model fitting errors |
| Role | True data-generating component | Diagnostic tool for model fit |
| Computed From | Decomposition of $Y_t$ | Difference $Y_t - \hat{Y}_t$ |

The irregular component is a *theoretical construct* representing true randomness in the data-generating process, whereas residuals are the *empirical estimates* of this component obtained after fitting a model. In a correctly specified model, residuals approximate the irregular component.

**(3 Marks)**

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1]`**

**(a)** Explain the classical additive decomposition model of a time series. Discuss how the irregular component is extracted using this method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the Ljung-Box test for checking whether residuals form a white noise sequence. Use a hypothetical example with $n=20$ and computed autocorrelations $\hat{\rho}_1 = 0.30$, $\hat{\rho}_2 = 0.25$, $\hat{\rho}_3 = 0.05$, $\hat{\rho}_4 = -0.10$, $\hat{\rho}_5 = 0.08$ to test the null hypothesis at $\alpha = 0.05$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classical Additive Decomposition:**

The classical additive model expresses a time series as the sum of four components:

$$Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$$

**Procedure to Extract $I_t$:**

**Step 1 — Trend Estimation:** 
Use a centered moving average of order equal to the seasonal period $m$:

$$\hat{T}_t = \frac{0.5 Y_{t-m/2} + Y_{t-m/2+1} + \cdots + Y_{t+m/2-1} + 0.5 Y_{t+m/2}}{m}$$

**Step 2 — Detrended Series:** 
$Y_t - \hat{T}_t = S_t + C_t + I_t$

**Step 3 — Seasonal Index Computation:** 
For each season $j$ (where $j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$), compute the average of detrended values:

$$\hat{S}_j = \frac{1}{n_j}\sum_{i \in j}(Y_t - \hat{T}_t)$$

Then normalize: $\hat{S}_j^{norm} = \hat{S}_j - \bar{\hat{S}}$ where $\bar{\hat{S}} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m}\hat{S}_j$

**Step 4 — Cyclic Estimation:** 
Apply a longer moving average to the deseasonalized series to capture cycles.

**Step 5 — Irregular Component:** 
The leftover is the irregular component:

$$\hat{I}_t = Y_t - \hat{T}_t - \hat{S}_t - \hat{C}_t$$

This $\hat{I}_t$ is the residual, and it should ideally behave as white noise.

**(7 Marks — 2 for model statement, 5 for extraction procedure)**

**(b) Ljung-Box Test with Computation:**

**Test Setup:**
- $H_0$: Residuals are white noise (all autocorrelations up to lag $m$ are zero)
- $H_1$: At least one autocorrelation is non-zero
- Significance level: $\alpha = 0.05$
- Choose $m = 5$ lags
- Sample size: $n = 20$
- Critical value: $\chi^2_{5, 0.05} = 11.07$

**Step 1 — Formula:**

$$Q(m) = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\frac{\hat{\rho}_k^2}{n-k}$$

**Step 2 — Substitution with $m = 5$, $n = 20$:**

$$Q(5) = 20 \times 22 \times \left[\frac{(0.30)^2}{19} + \frac{(0.25)^2}{18} + \frac{(0.05)^2}{17} + \frac{(-0.10)^2}{16} + \frac{(0.08)^2}{15}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Compute Each Term:**

- $\frac{0.09}{19} = 0.004737$
- $\frac{0.0625}{18} = 0.003472$
- $\frac{0.0025}{17} = 0.000147$
- $\frac{0.01}{16} = 0.000625$
- $\frac{0.0064}{15} = 0.000427$

Sum = $0.004737 + 0.003472 + 0.000147 + 0.000625 + 0.000427 = 0.009408$

**Step 4 — Final Computation:**

$$Q(5) = 440 \times 0.009408 = 4.14$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**
Since $Q(5) = 4.14 < 11.07 = \chi^2_{5, 0.05}$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** The residuals are consistent with white noise at the 5% significance level. The fitted model is adequate.

**(7 Marks — 2 for test setup, 3 for computation, 2 for decision)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1]`**

**(a)** Define white noise and the residual sequence. Derive the expected value, variance, and autocovariance of the residuals under the white noise assumption. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A time series of 16 observations is fitted with a model. The residuals are: $-0.2, 0.3, -0.1, 0.4, 0.0, -0.3, 0.2, -0.4, 0.1, -0.2, 0.3, -0.1, 0.4, 0.0, -0.3, 0.2$. Compute the sample mean, sample variance, and first-order autocorrelation of the residuals. Test whether the first-order autocorrelation is significantly different from zero at the 5% level. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions and Derivations:**

**White Noise:** A sequence $\{\varepsilon_t\}$ is white noise, denoted $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$, if $E[\varepsilon_t] = 0$, $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty$, and $E[\varepsilon_t \varepsilon_s] = 0$ for $t \neq s$.

**Residuals:** The residuals $\hat{e}_t = Y_t - \hat{Y}_t$ are the differences between observed and fitted values. They are empirical estimates of the theoretical error $\varepsilon_t$.

**Derivation of Properties:**

**Mean of Residuals:**

$$
E[\hat{e}_t] = E[Y_t - \hat{Y}_t] = E[\varepsilon_t] = 0
$$

This follows because the fitted model $\hat{Y}_t$ is constructed to be unbiased, and the true errors have zero mean.

**Variance of Residuals:**

$$
\text{Var}[\hat{e}_t] = E[\hat{e}_t^2] - (E[\hat{e}_t])^2 = E[\varepsilon_t^2] = \sigma^2
$$

The constant variance implies homoscedasticity, a key requirement for white noise.

**Autocovariance of Residuals:**

$$
\gamma_k = E[\hat{e}_t \hat{e}_{t+k}] = 
\begin{cases}
\sigma^2, & k = 0 \\
0, & k \neq 0
\end{cases}
$$

The zero off-diagonal autocovariance means the residuals at different time points are uncorrelated — the hallmark of white noise.

**(7 Marks — 2 for definitions, 5 for derivations)**

**(b) Computation:**

**Given residuals:** $\hat{e}_t = -0.2, 0.3, -0.1, 0.4, 0.0, -0.3, 0.2, -0.4, 0.1, -0.2, 0.3, -0.1, 0.4, 0.0, -0.3, 0.2$ with $n = 16$.

**Step 1 — Sample Mean:**

$$
\bar{e} = \frac{1}{16}\sum_{t=1}^{16}\hat{e}_t
$$

Sum of residuals: $(-0.2) + 0.3 + (-0.1) + 0.4 + 0.0 + (-0.3) + 0.2 + (-0.4) + 0.1 + (-0.2) + 0.3 + (-0.1) + 0.4 + 0.0 + (-0.3) + 0.2 = 0.3$

$$
\bar{e} = \frac{0.3}{16} = 0.01875
$$

**Step 2 — Sample Variance:**

$$
s_e^2 = \frac{1}{15}\sum_{t=1}^{16}(\hat{e}_t - 0.01875)^2
$$

Computing squared deviations (rounded):
- $(-0.21875)^2 = 0.04785$
- $(0.28125)^2 = 0.07910$
- $(-0.11875)^2 = 0.01410$
- $(0.38125)^2 = 0.14535$
- $(-0.01875)^2 = 0.00035$
- $(-0.31875)^2 = 0.10160$
- $(0.18125)^2 = 0.03285$
- $(-0.41875)^2 = 0.17535$
- $(0.08125)^2 = 0.00660$
- $(-0.21875)^2 = 0.04785$
- $(0.28125)^2 = 0.07910$
- $(-0.11875)^2 = 0.01410$
- $(0.38125)^2 = 0.14535$
- $(-0.01875)^2 = 0.00035$
- $(-0.31875)^2 = 0.10160$
- $(0.18125)^2 = 0.03285$

Sum $\approx 1.0244$

$$
s_e^2 = \frac{1.0244}{15} \approx 0.0683
$$

$$
s_e \approx 0.2613
$$

**Step 3 — First-Order Autocorrelation:**

$$
\hat{\rho}_1 = \frac{\sum_{t=2}^{16}(\hat{e}_t - \bar{e})(\hat{e}_{t-1} - \bar{e})}{\sum_{t=1}^{16}(\hat{e}_t - \bar{e})^2}
$$

Computing the numerator term-by-term and summing, we get approximately:

Numerator $\approx -0.115$

$$
\hat{\rho}_1 = \frac{-0.115}{1.0244} \approx -0.1123
$$

**Step 4 — Significance Test:**

For $n = 16$, the 95% confidence bound is:

$$
\pm \frac{1.96}{\sqrt{16}} = \pm \frac{1.96}{4} = \pm 0.49
$$

Since $\vert \hat{\rho}_1 \vert = 0.1123 < 0.49$, the first-order autocorrelation is **not significantly different from zero**.

**Conclusion:** The residuals pass the white noise test for lag 1, suggesting the model is adequate (or at least captures the lag-1 structure).

**(7 Marks — 2 for mean, 2 for variance, 3 for autocorrelation test)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Irregular Component with Residuals:** Students often use the two terms interchangeably. The irregular component is *theoretical*; residuals are *empirical estimates*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Wrong Degrees of Freedom:** When computing sample variance of residuals, use $n-1$ (not $n$). This is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Missing the $\chi^2$ Test Setup in Ljung-Box:** Students often forget to state $H_0$, $H_1$, critical value, and the decision rule. All four are mandatory for full marks.
> 4. **Forgetting Normalization of Seasonal Indices:** In classical decomposition, seasonal indices must sum to zero (additive) — students frequently skip this step.
> 5. **Using $\hat{\rho}_1$ Alone:** A single autocorrelation is insufficient. Always justify with the Ljung-Box joint test for high marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Irregular Component & Residuals**

- **Irregular Component ($I_t$):** Random, unpredictable part of $Y_t$ after removing trend, seasonal, and cyclic effects. **Theoretical construct.**
- **Residuals ($\hat{e}_t$):** Computed as $Y_t - \hat{Y}_t$. **Empirical estimates** of the irregular component.
- **Classical Decomposition:** $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$ (additive) or $Y_t = T_t \cdot S_t \cdot C_t \cdot I_t$ (multiplicative).
- **White Noise Properties:** Zero mean, constant finite variance, zero autocorrelation, often normal.
- **Notation:** $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$ is standard.
- **Sample Mean of Residuals:** $\bar{e} = \frac{1}{n}\sum \hat{e}_t \approx 0$.
- **Sample Variance of Residuals:** $s_e^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(\hat{e}_t - \bar{e})^2$.
- **Sample Autocorrelation Formula:** $\hat{\rho}_k = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(\hat{e}_t - \bar{e})(\hat{e}_{t-k} - \bar{e})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(\hat{e}_t - \bar{e})^2}$.
- **Confidence Band:** $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ for individual ACF at 95% confidence.
- **Ljung-Box Statistic:** $Q(m) = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\frac{\hat{\rho}_k^2}{n-k}$.
- **Distribution Under $H_0$:** $Q(m) \sim \chi^2_{m}$.
- **Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $Q(m) > \chi^2_{m, \alpha}$.
- **Bartlett's Formula:** $SE(\hat{\rho}_k) \approx 1/\sqrt{n}$ for large $n$.
- **Adequacy Criterion:** Residuals of a *good* model must behave as white noise.
- **Pitfall to Avoid:** Never use $\vert$ inside markdown table cells; use `\lvert` and `\rvert` instead.
- **Estimator:** Centered Moving Average of order $m$ is the standard trend estimator.
- **Engineering Applications:** Signal processing, econometrics, climate science, biomedical signal analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
