> Apply Simple Exponential Smoothing with $\alpha = 0.3$ and initial forecast $F_1 = 50$. Compute the forecasts $F_2$ through $F_9$ and the SSE.

**Solution Table Construction:**

| $t$ | $y_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t = y_t - F_t$ | $e_t^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 50.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 52 | 50.00 | +2.00 | 4.00 |
| 3 | 47 | 50.60 | $-3.60$ | 12.96 |
| 4 | 53 | 49.52 | +3.48 | 12.1104 |
| 5 | 51 | 50.56 | +0.44 | 0.1936 |
| 6 | 49 | 50.39 | $-1.39$ | 1.9321 |
| 7 | 52 | 49.97 | +2.03 | 4.1209 |
| 8 | 50 | 50.58 | $-0.58$ | 0.3364 |
| 9 | — | 50.41 | — | — |

**Sample calculations (showing one row in full):**

For $t = 2$:
$$F_2 = 0.3 \times 50 + 0.7 \times 50 = 50.00, \quad e_2 = 52 - 50 = 2.00$$

For $t = 3$:
$$F_3 = 0.3 \times 52 + 0.7 \times 50.00 = 15.6 + 35.0 = 50.60$$
$$e_3 = 47 - 50.60 = -3.60, \quad e_3^2 = 12.96$$

For $t = 4$:
$$F_4 = 0.3 \times 47 + 0.7 \times 50.60 = 14.1 + 35.42 = 49.52$$
$$e_4 = 53 - 49.52 = 3.48, \quad e_4^2 \approx 12.11$$

**Aggregated SSE:**

$$\text{SSE} = 0 + 4.00 + 12.96 + 12.11 + 0.19 + 1.93 + 4.12 + 0.34 \approx 35.65$$

**Final forecast for period 9:**

$$F_9 = 0.3 \times 50 + 0.7 \times 50.58 = 15.0 + 35.41 = 50.41$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since SES has no trend component, $F_9$ is **also** the forecast for **all** future periods $h = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$

---

### 3.5 Optimal $\alpha$ — Numerical Search

The optimal $\alpha$ is found by minimizing $\text{SSE}(\alpha)$ over $(0, 1)$. There is **no closed-form solution** — we use a numerical grid search:

1. Discretize $\alpha \in \{0.01, 0.02, \ldots, 0.99\}$.
2. For each $\alpha$, run SES through the series and compute SSE.
3. Select $\alpha^*$ that minimizes SSE.
4. (Optionally) refine via Newton-Raphson or golden-section search.

In R/Python, this is implemented via `HoltWinters(..., beta=FALSE, gamma=FALSE)` or `ets(...)` with restricted components.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — SES Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed series y_t] --> B[Store previous forecast F_t]
    B --> C[Compute forecast error e_t = y_t - F_t]
    A --> C
    C --> D[Scale error by alpha]
    D --> E[Update forecast F_t+1 = F_t + alpha e_t]
    E --> F{Is t the last period T?}
    F -- No --> B
    F -- Yes --> G[Lock final level ell_T]
    G --> H[Issue flat forecast for all h >= 1]
    H --> I[Output: y_hat_T+h = ell_T]
    E --> J[Sum squared errors SSE]
    C --> J
    J --> K[Minimize SSE over alpha in 0 1]
    K --> L[Optimal alpha star]
    L --> E
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Weight-Decay Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Weight on y_t: alpha] --> B[Weight on y_t-1: alpha 1-alpha]
    B --> C[Weight on y_t-2: alpha 1-alpha squared]
    C --> D[Weight on y_t-3: alpha 1-alpha cubed]
    D --> E[...decays toward 0]
    E --> F[Sum of all weights = 1]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — ETS(A,N,N) State-Space View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph State Update
    S1[Level at t-1: ell_t-1] --> S2[Level at t: ell_t = alpha y_t + 1-alpha ell_t-1]
    end
    subgraph Forecast
    S2 --> F1[Forecast: y_hat_t = ell_t-1]
    end
    subgraph Error
    A2[Observation y_t] --> E1[Error: e_t = y_t - y_hat_t]
    F1 --> E1
    E1 --> S2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Interpretation:** The diagram maps the SES feedback loop — the *forecast* is the *state*, the *innovation* (forecast error) drives the *state update*, and the *forecast* is reused for the next iteration. This is mathematically equivalent to a **first-order Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) low-pass digital filter** with pole at $z = 1-\alpha$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Pattern]**

> Define Simple Exponential Smoothing. State its core recursive formula and explain the role of the smoothing constant $\alpha$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES) is a forecasting technique for univariate time series **without trend or seasonality**, in which the forecast for the next period is a **weighted average of the most recent observation and the previous forecast**, with weights decaying exponentially.

Core recursive formula:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha) F_t, \qquad 0 < \alpha < 1$$

**Role of $\alpha$:** $\alpha$ is the **smoothing constant** that determines how rapidly the influence of past observations decays. A value **close to 1** makes the forecast **responsive to recent changes**, while a value **close to 0** produces **smooth, stable** forecasts that average over a longer history. Its optimal value is obtained by minimizing the sum of squared forecast errors.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model Paper Pattern]**

> Show that the sum of all weights in the explicit SES expansion equals 1. Hence justify why SES can be interpreted as a weighted average.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The explicit form of SES is:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) y_{t-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)^2 y_{t-2} + \cdots$$

The weight on $y_{t-k}$ is $w_k = \alpha(1-\alpha)^k$. Summing over all $k \geq 0$ (with $\vert 1-\alpha \vert < 1$):

$$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} w_k = \alpha \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (1-\alpha)^k = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{1-(1-\alpha)} = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha} = 1$$

Since the weights are **non-negative** and sum to **unity**, SES is a **convex combination** (a weighted average in which all coefficients lie between 0 and 1 and sum to 1) of the historical observations — hence a legitimate weighted average that always stays within the range of the data.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

> **(a)** Derive the **explicit weighted-average form** of Simple Exponential Smoothing by recursively expanding the canonical recursion $F_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ up to the third iteration. Show that the weights assigned to past observations form a geometric progression. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the following demand series, compute SES forecasts with $\alpha = 0.4$ and $F_1 = 100$. Calculate the SSE and provide the forecast for period 7. **(7 marks)**
>
> | $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | $y_t$ | 100 | 108 | 95 | 112 | 104 | 110 |

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting recursion:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha) F_t \quad \text{...(1)}$$

Replace $F_t$ with its own expansion from (1) shifted by one step:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)[\alpha y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha) F_{t-1}]$$

Expand:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)^2 F_{t-1} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Replace $F_{t-1}$:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)^2[\alpha y_{t-2} + (1-\alpha) F_{t-2}]$$

Expand:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) y_{t-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)^2 y_{t-2} + (1-\alpha)^3 F_{t-2} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

After the third substitution the pattern is clear. Continuing by induction:

$$\boxed{F_{t+1} = \sum_{k=0}^{t-1} \alpha(1-\alpha)^k y_{t-k} + (1-\alpha)^t F_1}$$

The weights $\alpha, \alpha(1-\alpha), \alpha(1-\alpha)^2, \ldots$ form a **geometric progression** with common ratio $r = (1-\alpha)$. This justifies the name "exponential" smoothing.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Step-by-step:

| $t$ | $y_t$ | $F_t$ | Computation | $e_t = y_t - F_t$ | $e_t^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 100.00 | given | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 108 | 100.00 | $0.4(100) + 0.6(100)$ | +8.00 | 64.00 |
| 3 | 95 | 103.20 | $0.4(108) + 0.6(100)$ | $-8.20$ | 67.24 |
| 4 | 112 | 99.92 | $0.4(95) + 0.6(103.20)$ | +12.08 | 145.93 |
| 5 | 104 | 104.75 | $0.4(112) + 0.6(99.92)$ | $-0.75$ | 0.56 |
| 6 | 110 | 104.45 | $0.4(104) + 0.6(104.75)$ | +5.55 | 30.80 |
| 7 | — | 106.67 | $0.4(110) + 0.6(104.45)$ | — | — |

Detailed verifications:

- $F_2 = 0.4(100) + 0.6(100) = 40 + 60 = 100.00$
- $F_3 = 0.4(108) + 0.6(100) = 43.2 + 60 = 103.20$
- $F_4 = 0.4(95) + 0.6(103.20) = 38.0 + 61.92 = 99.92$
- $F_5 = 0.4(112) + 0.6(99.92) = 44.8 + 59.952 = 104.752 \approx 104.75$
- $F_6 = 0.4(104) + 0.6(104.75) = 41.6 + 62.85 = 104.45$
- $F_7 = 0.4(110) + 0.6(104.45) = 44 + 62.67 = 106.67$

$$\text{SSE} = 0 + 64.00 + 67.24 + 145.93 + 0.56 + 30.80 = 308.53$$

**Forecast for period 7:** $F_7 = 106.67$ (also the forecast for $h = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]`

> **(a)** Discuss the role of the smoothing constant $\alpha$ in SES. Explain what happens to the forecast behavior when $\alpha \to 0$ and when $\alpha \to 1$, and describe how the optimal $\alpha$ is determined in practice. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the series $y = \{72, 68, 75, 70, 73, 69, 74, 71\}$, apply SES with $\alpha = 0.2$ and $F_1 = 72$. Compute all forecasts, the one-step SSE, and the MSE. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Role of $\alpha$ (7 marks):**

The smoothing constant $\alpha \in (0,1)$ is the **single tunable parameter** in SES. It controls the trade-off between **responsiveness** and **stability**:

- **$\alpha \to 0$:** The forecast $F_{t+1} \approx F_t$ — i.e., the forecast is nearly **constant**, equal to the previous one. The model essentially averages over a very long history, producing very smooth, sluggish forecasts. A small $\alpha$ is appropriate when the series has low signal-to-noise ratio.

- **$\alpha \to 1$:** The forecast $F_{t+1} \approx y_t$ — i.e., the forecast is **almost equal to the most recent observation**. The model reacts instantly to new data, which is helpful when the underlying level shifts abruptly but harmful when data are noisy.

- **Intermediate $\alpha$** (e.g., $0.2$–$0.4$): provides a balanced compromise and is the most common choice in practice.

**Optimal $\alpha$ Determination:**

The optimal $\alpha^*$ is found by minimizing the one-step-ahead **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)** as a function of $\alpha$:

$$\alpha^* = \arg\min_{\alpha \in (0,1)} \text{SSE}(\alpha) = \arg\min_{\alpha \in (0,1)} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \left( y_t - F_t(\alpha) \right)^2$$

Since no closed-form solution exists, we use **numerical search**:
1. Grid search over $\alpha \in \{0.01, 0.02, \ldots, 0.99\}$.
2. Refinement via Newton-Raphson, golden-section, or bounded optimization.
3. Validation on a hold-out sample to guard against overfitting.

In practice, automated routines in R (`ets()`), Python (`statsmodels`), and MATLAB (`expSmoothing`) perform this optimization internally.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

| $t$ | $y_t$ | $F_t$ | $F_{t+1} = 0.2 y_t + 0.8 F_t$ | $e_t$ | $e_t^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 72 | 72.00 | $0.2(72) + 0.8(72) = 72.00$ | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 68 | 72.00 | $0.2(68) + 0.8(72) = 71.20$ | $-4.00$ | 16.00 |
| 3 | 75 | 71.20 | $0.2(75) + 0.8(71.20) = 71.96$ | +3.80 | 14.44 |
| 4 | 70 | 71.96 | $0.2(70) + 0.8(71.96) = 71.568$ | $-1.57$ | 2.45 |
| 5 | 73 | 71.57 | $0.2(73) + 0.8(71.57) = 71.85$ | +1.43 | 2.05 |
| 6 | 69 | 71.85 | $0.2(69) + 0.8(71.85) = 71.28$ | $-2.28$ | 5.21 |
| 7 | 74 | 71.28 | $0.2(74) + 0.8(71.28) = 71.82$ | +2.18 | 4.74 |
| 8 | 71 | 71.82 | $0.2(71) + 0.8(71.82) = 71.66$ | $-0.66$ | 0.43 |
| 9 | — | 71.66 | — | — | — |

$$\text{SSE} = 0 + 16.00 + 14.44 + 2.45 + 2.05 + 5.21 + 4.74 + 0.43 = 45.32$$

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{\text{SSE}}{n-1} = \frac{45.32}{7} \approx 6.47$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Initialization error (–1 mark):** Many students forget to set $F_1$ correctly. If the problem says $F_1 = 72$ (or $F_1 = y_1$), you **must** start the recursion from $t = 2$ — the error at $t=1$ is conventionally taken as $0$.
> 2. **Recursion direction confusion (–2 marks):** The forecast $F_{t+1}$ **uses** $F_t$ and $y_t$. Computing $F_t$ from $F_{t+1}$ (going backwards) will give wrong answers.
> 3. **Weighting error (–1 mark):** In the canonical form, the weight on $y_t$ is $\alpha$ and on $F_t$ is $(1-\alpha)$. Students sometimes reverse them.
> 4. **Flat forecast assumption (–2 marks):** For SES, the forecast for **all future horizons** $h = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ is **identical** and equal to $F_{T+1}$. Drawing an upward or downward forecast line in a plot will be marked wrong.
> 5. **Units / rounding (–0.5 mark):** Round to **2 decimal places** consistently, and never drop the units of the series if given.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2 — SES):**

- **SES = ETS(A,N,N)** — univariate, no trend, no seasonality.
- **Canonical recursion:** $F_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$.
- **Error-correction form:** $F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha (y_t - F_t)$.
- **Weights** are $\alpha, \alpha(1-\alpha), \alpha(1-\alpha)^2, \ldots$ — **geometric decay**, sum to **exactly 1**.
- **h-step-ahead forecast is flat:** $\hat{y}_{T+h} = \ell_T$ for all $h \geq 1$.
- **Smoothing constant $\alpha \in (0,1)$** controls responsiveness:
  - Small $\alpha$ → smooth, slow, stable
  - Large $\alpha$ → responsive, fast, volatile
- **Optimal $\alpha$** minimizes $\text{SSE}(\alpha) = \sum (y_t - F_t)^2$ — numerical search required.
- **Initial value:** $F_1 = y_1$ or average of first few observations.
- **SSE** (Sum of Squared Errors) is the standard KTU exam metric; **MSE** = SSE / $n$ or SSE / $(n-1)$ depending on convention.
- **Equivalent to a first-order IIR low-pass filter** in digital signal processing.
- **Limitation:** Cannot capture trend or seasonality — extend to **Holt's linear** (for trend) and **Holt-Winters** (for trend + seasonality) methods.
- **Memory:** SES never "forgets" but gives exponentially vanishing weight to distant observations.
- **Connection to ARIMA:** SES is mathematically equivalent to an **ARIMA(0,1,1)** model with no constant.
- **Examination tip:** Always tabulate $t$, $y_t$, $F_t$, $e_t$, $e_t^2$ in a **single table** — this is the KTU-expected presentation and earns full method marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exponential Smoothing Methods with Trend
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Exponential Smoothing Methods with Trend

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Exponential Smoothing Methods with Trend** constitute a family of recursive forecasting techniques that extend the simple exponential smoothing framework by explicitly modeling the **trend component** of a non-stationary time series. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECDT861) context, these methods are formally classified as **state-space forecasting models** where the level and trend components are updated recursively through weighted averages of past observations, with weights decaying exponentially as observations become more distant in time.

The three principal variants covered under this module are:

1. **Holt's Linear Trend Method (Double Exponential Smoothing)** — captures a locally linear trend with two smoothing parameters.
2. **Damped Trend Method** — a modification of Holt's method that introduces a damping parameter to prevent unrealistic long-horizon forecasts.
3. **Holt-Winters Method (Triple Exponential Smoothing)** — extends to series with both trend and seasonality (covered in adjacent module as a logical extension).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module places strong emphasis on the **recursive formulation**, **parameter interpretation (α, β, φ)**, **initialization strategies**, and **sum-of-squared-errors (SSE) based parameter optimization**. Board questions frequently test the iterative update equations rather than the algebraic form.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Speedometer with Memory Decay**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car and want to predict your position 10 minutes from now using only your speedometer. Simple exponential smoothing is like remembering your **current speed** with a fading memory of past speeds. But the road might be uphill — your speed is *trending* upward. Holt's method is like having a separate "**acceleration meter**" that also smooths itself exponentially. The level (speed) and trend (acceleration) are both updated, so the forecast = current speed + (acceleration × horizon).
>
> However, if you forecast 10 hours ahead assuming the same acceleration, you'll predict absurdly high speeds. This is why the **damped trend** method exists — it assumes acceleration gradually fades, just as a real driver would ease off the gas pedal as they approach a destination.

## 1.3 Why Simple Exponential Smoothing Fails for Trending Series

| Property | Simple ES | Holt's Method |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| Handles level shifts | Yes | Yes |
| Handles linear trend | **No (biased)** | Yes |
| Number of parameters | 1 (α) | 2 (α, β) |
| Forecast horizon reliability | Short-term | Medium-term |
| Long-horizon extrapolation | Flat line | Linear (potentially unrealistic) |

The fundamental issue is that simple ES produces a **constant forecast** for all future periods:

$$F_{t+h} = \ell_t \quad \forall h \geq 1$$

When a real trend exists, this systematically underestimates (downward trend) or overestimates (upward trend) the future — a phenomenon called **trend bias**.

## 1.4 Key Terminology

- **Level ($\ell_t$):** Smoothed value of the series at time $t$, analogous to the intercept.
- **Trend ($b_t$):** Smoothed slope of the series at time $t$, analogous to the rate of change.
- **Smoothing Parameters:** $\alpha$ (level), $\beta$ (trend), $\phi$ (damping) — all constrained to $(0, 1)$ in standard practice, though the innovations state-space form allows $[0, 1]$ boundary inclusion.
- **Forecast Horizon ($h$):** Number of future steps being predicted.
- **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE):** $\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^2$ — the objective function minimized during parameter tuning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Conceptual Architecture

Exponential smoothing methods with trend are built on three pillars:

### Pillar 1: Component Decomposition
Any time series $y_t$ with a local linear trend is decomposed as:

$$y_t = \ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

where $\varepsilon_t$ is a white-noise error term. The forecast $h$ steps ahead is:

$$\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h \cdot b_t$$

### Pillar 2: Recursive Updating
Both components are **recursively updated** so that recent observations carry exponentially more weight than older ones. This is the **"Why"** — the exponentially decaying weights give more importance to recent data while still leveraging historical information.

### Pillar 3: Optimization of Smoothing Constants
The parameters $(\alpha, \beta, \phi)$ are chosen to **minimize the in-sample SSE**:

$$\text{SSE} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} (y_t - \hat{y}_{t \mid t-1})^2$$

This is typically solved numerically (e.g., using Newton-Raphson, grid search, or optimization libraries).

## 2.2 Holt's Linear Trend Method — Detailed Formulation

The method, introduced by **Charles Holt (1957)**, uses three core equations:

### 2.2.1 Level Update Equation

$$\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$$

- The new level is a weighted average of the **observed value** $y_t$ and the **one-step-ahead forecast** $(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$.
- Weight $\alpha$ controls responsiveness to new data; $(1-\alpha)$ preserves the historical smooth.

### 2.2.2 Trend Update Equation

$$b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) b_{t-1}$$

- The new trend is a weighted average of the **observed change in level** $(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1})$ and the **previous trend** $b_{t-1}$.
- Weight $\beta$ governs how quickly the trend adapts.

### 2.2.3 Forecast Equation (h-steps ahead)

$$\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h \cdot b_t$$

- The forecast extrapolates linearly into the future using the current level and trend.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Decay Interpretation:** Repeated substitution shows that the weight on an observation $y_{t-k}$ in the forecast $\hat{y}_{t+h}$ is proportional to $\alpha (1-\alpha)^k$. This is the "exponential" in exponential smoothing.

## 2.3 Damped Trend Method

Introduced by **Gardner & McKenzie (1985)** to address the over-extrapolation problem of Holt's method. A damping parameter $\phi \in (0, 1)$ is introduced:

### 2.3.1 Updated Recursive Equations

$$\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + \phi \, b_{t-1})$$

$$b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) \phi \, b_{t-1}$$

### 2.3.2 Damped Forecast Equation

$$\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + \sum_{i=1}^{h} \phi^i \, b_t = \ell_t + \left(\frac{\phi(1 - \phi^h)}{1 - \phi}\right) b_t$$

- For large $h$, the cumulative damping factor approaches $\phi / (1 - \phi)$, producing a **finite asymptote** rather than unbounded linear growth.

## 2.4 The Innovations State-Space Form (ISSF)

Every exponential smoothing method has a corresponding **state-space representation**, important for KTU's treatment of likelihood-based parameter estimation. The general ISSF is:

$$y_t = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

$$\mathbf{x}_t = \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + \mathbf{g} \varepsilon_t$$

with $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{NID}(0, \sigma^2)$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — it is the single most important reference for the board exam.**

| Method | Level Equation | Trend Equation | Forecast (h-step) | Parameters | Constraint |
|--------|---------------|----------------|-------------------|------------|------------|
| Simple ES | $\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)\ell_{t-1}$ | None | $\ell_t$ | $\alpha$ | $0 < \alpha < 1$ |
| Holt's Linear | $\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$ | $b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)b_{t-1}$ | $\ell_t + h b_t$ | $\alpha, \beta$ | $0 < \alpha, \beta < 1$ |
| Damped Trend | $\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + \phi b_{t-1})$ | $b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)\phi b_{t-1}$ | $\ell_t + \sum_{i=1}^{h} \phi^i b_t$ | $\alpha, \beta, \phi$ | $0 < \alpha, \beta, \phi < 1$ |

| Initialization Strategies | Formula |
|--------------------------|---------|
| Simple: $\ell_0 = y_1$ | Use first observation |
| Average: $\ell_0 = \bar{y}$ | Use mean of first few |
| OLS-based: regress $y_t$ on $t$ | $\ell_0 = \hat{a}$, $b_0 = \hat{b}$ |
| Optimized: included in minimization | Minimize SSE jointly |

| Error Metric | Formula | Use Case |
|-------------|---------|----------|
| SSE | $\sum (y_t - \hat{y}_t)^2$ | Parameter optimization |
| MSE | $\text{SSE} / n$ | Model comparison |
| RMSE | $\sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same units as data |
| MAPE | $\frac{100}{n} \sum \vert y_t - \hat{y}_t \vert / \vert y_t \vert$ | Scale-independent comparison |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy Demand Forecasting:** Predicting electricity load where consumption trends up with economic growth.
- **Inventory Management:** Forecasting stock requirements for products with growing market share.
- **Network Traffic Prediction:** Anticipating bandwidth requirements as user base grows (with damping to prevent over-provisioning).
- **Economic Indicators:** Forecasting GDP, inflation, and industrial production indices.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Sensor data with wear-out trend in mechanical systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Simple ES to Holt's Method

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Simple Exponential Smoothing

The simple ES level equation is:

$$\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha) \ell_{t-1}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the limitation.**
The forecast is $\hat{y}_{t+1} = \ell_t$. Notice that this contains **no slope information**. If the series is rising or falling, the forecast is biased.

**Step 2 — Augment the state vector.**
Introduce a trend component $b_t$ alongside the level $\ell_t$. The state becomes $\mathbf{x}_t = (\ell_t, b_t)^\top$.

**Step 3 — Modify the level update to include trend.**
The naive one-step-ahead forecast with trend is $\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}$. Replace $\ell_{t-1}$ in the simple ES update with this trend-adjusted forecast:

$$\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$$

**Step 4 — Introduce a recursive update for the trend itself.**
The "observed" trend at time $t$ is the change in level: $(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1})$. Smooth this with the previous trend:

$$b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) b_{t-1}$$

This completes the Holt's method derivation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Damped Forecast Equation

The h-step forecast is built step by step:

$$\hat{y}_{t+1} = \ell_t + \phi \, b_t$$

$$\hat{y}_{t+2} = \ell_t + \phi \, b_t + \phi^2 \, b_t$$

$$\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + \sum_{i=1}^{h} \phi^i \, b_t$$

This is a finite geometric series. Using the standard identity $\sum_{i=1}^{h} r^i = \frac{r(1-r^h)}{1-r}$ for $r \neq 1$:

$$\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + b_t \cdot \frac{\phi(1 - \phi^h)}{1 - \phi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limit check:** As $h \to \infty$, the term $\phi^h \to 0$, so the forecast approaches $\ell_t + \frac{\phi}{1-\phi} b_t$, which is a **finite horizontal asymptote** — exactly the desired behavior.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the type of computation KTU examiners love to set. Practice the iteration table format below.**

**Problem:** Apply Holt's linear method to the series $y = \{10, 13, 15, 18, 21\}$ with $\alpha = 0.4$, $\beta = 0.3$, and initialization $\ell_0 = 10$, $b_0 = 2$. Compute the forecasts and SSE.

**Solution — Iteration Table:**

| $t$ | $y_t$ | $\ell_t = 0.4 y_t + 0.6(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$ | $b_t = 0.3(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + 0.7 b_{t-1}$ | $\hat{y}_t = \ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}$ | $e_t = y_t - \hat{y}_t$ |
|-----|-------|------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------|
| 0 | — | 10 | 2 | — | — |
| 1 | 10 | $0.4(10) + 0.6(10+2) = 4.0 + 7.2 = 11.2$ | $0.3(11.2-10) + 0.7(2) = 0.36 + 1.4 = 1.76$ | $10 + 2 = 12$ | $-2$ |
| 2 | 13 | $0.4(13) + 0.6(11.2+1.76) = 5.2 + 7.776 = 12.976$ | $0.3(12.976-11.2) + 0.7(1.76) = 0.5328 + 1.232 = 1.7648$ | $11.2 + 1.76 = 12.96$ | $0.04$ |
| 3 | 15 | $0.4(15) + 0.6(12.976+1.7648) = 6.0 + 8.8445 = 14.8445$ | $0.3(14.8445-12.976) + 0.7(1.7648) = 0.5605 + 1.2354 = 1.7959$ | $12.976 + 1.7648 = 14.7408$ | $0.2592$ |
| 4 | 18 | $0.4(18) + 0.6(14.8445+1.7959) = 7.2 + 9.9842 = 17.1842$ | $0.3(17.1842-14.8445) + 0.7(1.7959) = 0.7019 + 1.2571 = 1.9590$ | $14.8445 + 1.7959 = 16.6404$ | $1.3596$ |
| 5 | 21 | $0.4(21) + 0.6(17.1842+1.9590) = 8.4 + 11.4859 = 19.8859$ | $0.3(19.8859-17.1842) + 0.7(1.9590) = 0.8105 + 1.3713 = 2.1818$ | $17.1842 + 1.9590 = 19.1432$ | $1.8568$ |

**Step-by-step calculation of $\ell_1$:**
- $\ell_1 = 0.4(10) + 0.6(10 + 2)$
- $\ell_1 = 4.0 + 0.6 \times 12$
- $\ell_1 = 4.0 + 7.2 = \mathbf{11.2}$

**Step-by-step calculation of $b_1$:**
- $b_1 = 0.3(11.2 - 10) + 0.7(2)$
- $b_1 = 0.3(1.2) + 1.4$
- $b_1 = 0.36 + 1.4 = \mathbf{1.76}$

**Step-by-step calculation of $\ell_2$:**
- $\ell_2 = 0.4(13) + 0.6(11.2 + 1.76)$
- $\ell_2 = 5.2 + 0.6 \times 12.96$
- $\ell_2 = 5.2 + 7.776 = \mathbf{12.976}$

**Step-by-step calculation of $b_2$:**
- $b_2 = 0.3(12.976 - 11.2) + 0.7(1.76)$
- $b_2 = 0.3(1.776) + 1.232$
- $b_2 = 0.5328 + 1.232 = \mathbf{1.7648}$

**Computing SSE:**

$$e_1^2 = (-2)^2 = 4.0000$$

$$e_2^2 = (0.04)^2 = 0.0016$$

$$e_3^2 = (0.2592)^2 = 0.0672$$

$$e_4^2 = (1.3596)^2 = 1.8485$$

$$e_5^2 = (1.8568)^2 = 3.4477$$

$$\boxed{\text{SSE} = 4.0000 + 0.0016 + 0.0672 + 1.8485 + 3.4477 = 9.3650}$$

**Forecasting Future Values:**

- $F_6 = \ell_5 + 1 \cdot b_5 = 19.8859 + 2.1818 = \mathbf{22.0677}$
- $F_7 = \ell_5 + 2 \cdot b_5 = 19.8859 + 4.3636 = \mathbf{24.2495}$
- $F_8 = \ell_5 + 3 \cdot b_5 = 19.8859 + 6.5454 = \mathbf{26.4313}$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Simple ES | Holt's | Damped Trend |
|----------|-----------|--------|--------------|
| Handles level | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Handles trend | No | Yes (linear) | Yes (damped linear) |
| Long-horizon reliability | Good (no trend) | Poor (over-extrapolates) | Good (asymptotic) |
| Parameters to tune | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| Computational cost | Low | Low | Moderate |
| Risk of unstable forecasts | Low | Moderate | Low |
| Best for | Stationary | Persistent trend | Trend that decelerates |

## 3.5 Parameter Interpretation

- **High $\alpha$ ($\to 1$):** Level reacts strongly to new observations; smoother at past only.
- **Low $\alpha$ ($\to 0$):** Level is stable, slow to react to shocks.
- **High $\beta$ ($\to 1$):** Trend reacts strongly; rapid trend shifts captured.
- **Low $\beta$ ($\to 0$):** Trend is stable; assumes trend persistence.
- **High $\phi$ ($\to 1$):** Behaves like Holt's method (no damping).
- **Low $\phi$ ($\to 0$):** Strong damping; trend vanishes quickly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Holt's Method — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: y_t at time t] --> B{Compute Forecast}
    B --> C[Forecast = l_prev + h * b_prev]
    C --> D[Compute Error e_t = y_t - Forecast]
    D --> E[Update Level]
    E --> F[l_t = alpha * y_t + 1 minus alpha * Forecast]
    F --> G[Update Trend]
    G --> H[b_t = beta * delta_l + 1 minus beta * b_prev]
    H --> I[Store l_t and b_t]
    I --> J{Next Period?}
    J -->|Yes| A
    J -->|No| K[Generate h-step Forecasts]
    K --> L[F_tph = l_T + h * b_T]
```

## 4.2 Damped Trend Method — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        A1[Observation y_t]
    end

    subgraph STATE[STATE STORAGE]
        B1[Level l_prev]
        B2[Trend b_prev]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[COMPUTATION LAYER]
        C1[Damped Forecast: l_prev + phi * b_prev]
        C2[Error: e_t = y_t - Forecast]
        C3[New Level: alpha y_t + 1 minus alpha times Forecast]
        C4[New Trend: beta times delta l + 1 minus beta times phi times b_prev]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER]
        D1[One-step forecast for t+1]
        D2[Multi-step forecasts with damping]
    end

    A1 --> C2
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    B1 --> C3
    B2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
    C3 --> B1
    C4 --> B2
    C3 --> D1
    B1 --> D2
    B2 --> D2
```

## 4.3 Comparison of Method Behavior — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Time Series with Trend] --> Q1{Is trend linear and persistent?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| HOLT[Holt Linear Method: alpha and beta]
    Q1 -->|No, trend flattens| DAMP[Damped Trend: alpha, beta, phi]
    Q1 -->|Trend reverses| ARIMA[Consider ARIMA or ETS framework]
    HOLT --> FORECAST1[Linear Extrapolation: l_t + h b_t]
    DAMP --> FORECAST2[Damped Extrapolation: l_t + phi times series b_t]
    FORECAST1 --> RISK1[Risk: Over-extrapolation at long horizons]
    FORECAST2 --> RISK2[Benefit: Finite asymptotic forecast]
```

## 4.4 Parameter Optimization Loop (SSE Minimization)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Initial Guess: alpha, beta, phi] --> P2[Run Smoothing with Parameters]
    P2 --> P3[Compute SSE = sum of squared errors]
    P3 --> P4{SSE Converged?}
    P4 -->|No| P5[Numerical Optimizer updates parameters]
    P5 --> P2
    P4 -->|Yes| P6[Output Optimal alpha, beta, phi]
    P6 --> P7[Compute Final Forecasts and Intervals]
```

## 4.5 Decomposition of State Update — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PRIOR[Prior State]
        S1[l_prev]
        S2[b_prev]
    end

    subgraph NEW[New Observation]
        S3[y_t]
    end

    S1 --> M1[Combine: l_prev + phi b_prev]
    S2 --> M1
    M1 --> M2[Forecast at t]
    S3 --> M3[Compute e_t]
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4[Update Level l_t]
    M3 --> M5[Update Trend b_t]
    S1 --> M4
    S2 --> M5
    M4 --> OUT[l_t]
    M5 --> OUT2[b_t]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Conceptual Definition
**Q: Define Holt's linear trend method and state its update equations for level and trend.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Holt's linear trend method is a forecasting technique that extends simple exponential smoothing to time series exhibiting a local linear trend. It uses two recursively updated components: the level $\ell_t$ and the trend $b_t$, controlled by smoothing parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ respectively, where $0 < \alpha, \beta < 1$.

**Level equation:**

$$\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$$

**Trend equation:**

$$b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) b_{t-1}$$

**Forecast equation (h-step ahead):**

$$\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h \cdot b_t$$

---

### Question A2: Concept Comparison
**Q: How does the damped trend method differ from Holt's linear method? Why is it preferred for long-horizon forecasts?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The damped trend method introduces an additional parameter $\phi \in (0, 1)$ that progressively attenuates the trend over the forecast horizon. The modified equations are:

$$\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + \phi b_{t-1})$$

$$b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) \phi b_{t-1}$$

$$\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + \sum_{i=1}^{h} \phi^i b_t = \ell_t + \left(\frac{\phi(1 - \phi^h)}{1 - \phi}\right) b_t$$

**Key differences and advantages:**
- Holt's method extrapolates linearly, leading to **unrealistic long-horizon forecasts** (e.g., demand growing infinitely).
- Damped trend method produces a **finite asymptote** as $h \to \infty$.
- Empirically, damped trend outperforms Holt's method in the **M3-competition** for most series.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Holt's Method Application

**Q: For the time series $y = \{25, 30, 36, 41, 47, 53\}$, apply Holt's linear trend method with $\alpha = 0.3$, $\beta = 0.2$, and initial values $\ell_0 = 24$, $b_0 = 4$.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compute the level $\ell_t$, trend $b_t$, one-step-ahead forecast $\hat{y}_t$, and forecast error $e_t$ for $t = 1$ to $6$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the SSE, RMSE, and produce 3-step ahead forecasts from the final state. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Iteration Table Construction:**

**For $t = 1$:**
- Forecast: $\hat{y}_1 = \ell_0 + b_0 = 24 + 4 = 28$
- Error: $e_1 = 25 - 28 = -3$
- Level: $\ell_1 = 0.3(25) + 0.7(24 + 4) = 7.5 + 19.6 = 27.1$
- Trend: $b_1 = 0.2(27.1 - 24) + 0.8(4) = 0.62 + 3.2 = 3.82$

**For $t = 2$:**
- Forecast: $\hat{y}_2 = \ell_1 + b_1 = 27.1 + 3.82 = 30.92$
- Error: $e_2 = 30 - 30.92 = -0.92$
- Level: $\ell_2 = 0.3(30) + 0.7(27.1 + 3.82) = 9.0 + 21.644 = 30.644$
- Trend: $b_2 = 0.2(30.644 - 27.1) + 0.8(3.82) = 0.7088 + 3.056 = 3.7648$

**For $t = 3$:**
- Forecast: $\hat{y}_3 = 30.644 + 3.7648 = 34.4088$
- Error: $e_3 = 36 - 34.4088 = 1.5912$
- Level: $\ell_3 = 0.3(36) + 0.7(30.644 + 3.7648) = 10.8 + 24.0862 = 34.8862$
- Trend: $b_3 = 0.2(34.8862 - 30.644) + 0.8(3.7648) = 0.8484 + 3.0118 = 3.8602$

**For $t = 4$:**
- Forecast: $\hat{y}_4 = 34.8862 + 3.8602 = 38.7464$
- Error: $e_4 = 41 - 38.7464 = 2.2536$
- Level: $\ell_4 = 0.3(41) + 0.7(34.8862 + 3.8602) = 12.3 + 27.1225 = 39.4225$
- Trend: $b_4 = 0.2(39.4225 - 34.8862) + 0.8(3.8602) = 0.9073 + 3.0882 = 3.9955$

**For $t = 5$:**
- Forecast: $\hat{y}_5 = 39.4225 + 3.9955 = 43.4180$
- Error: $e_5 = 47 - 43.4180 = 3.5820$
- Level: $\ell_5 = 0.3(47) + 0.7(39.4225 + 3.9955) = 14.1 + 30.3926 = 44.4926$
- Trend: $b_5 = 0.2(44.4926 - 39.4225) + 0.8(3.9955) = 1.0140 + 3.1964 = 4.2104$

**For $t = 6$:**
- Forecast: $\hat{y}_6 = 44.4926 + 4.2104 = 48.7030$
- Error: $e_6 = 53 - 48.7030 = 4.2970$
- Level: $\ell_6 = 0.3(53) + 0.7(44.4926 + 4.2104) = 15.9 + 34.0931 = 49.9931$
- Trend: $b_6 = 0.2(49.9931 - 44.4926) + 0.8(4.2104) = 1.1001 + 3.3683 = 4.4684$

**Summary Table:**

| $t$ | $y_t$ | $\hat{y}_t$ | $e_t$ | $\ell_t$ | $b_t$ |
|-----|-------|-------------|-------|----------|-------|
| 1 | 25 | 28.000 | -3.000 | 27.100 | 3.8200 |
| 2 | 30 | 30.920 | -0.920 | 30.644 | 3.7648 |
| 3 | 36 | 34.409 | 1.591 | 34.886 | 3.8602 |
| 4 | 41 | 38.746 | 2.254 | 39.423 | 3.9955 |
| 5 | 47 | 43.418 | 3.582 | 44.493 | 4.2104 |
| 6 | 53 | 48.703 | 4.297 | 49.993 | 4.4684 |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**SSE Calculation:**

$$e_1^2 = (-3.000)^2 = 9.0000$$

$$e_2^2 = (-0.920)^2 = 0.8464$$

$$e_3^2 = (1.591)^2 = 2.5313$$

$$e_4^2 = (2.254)^2 = 5.0805$$

$$e_5^2 = (3.582)^2 = 12.8307$$

$$e_6^2 = (4.297)^2 = 18.4642$$

$$\text{SSE} = 9.0000 + 0.8464 + 2.5313 + 5.0805 + 12.8307 + 18.4642 = \mathbf{48.7531}$$

**RMSE Calculation:**

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{\text{SSE}}{n} = \frac{48.7531}{6} = 8.1255$$

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{8.1255} = \mathbf{2.8507}$$

**3-Step Ahead Forecasts** (using $\ell_6 = 49.9931$, $b_6 = 4.4684$):

$$\hat{y}_7 = 49.9931 + 1 \times 4.4684 = \mathbf{54.4615}$$

$$\hat{y}_8 = 49.9931 + 2 \times 4.4684 = \mathbf{58.9299}$$

$$\hat{y}_9 = 49.9931 + 3 \times 4.4684 = \mathbf{63.3983}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Damped Trend Method

**Q: Discuss the limitations of Holt's linear method and explain how the damped trend method overcomes them. Apply the damped trend method to the series $y = \{100, 110, 125, 140, 158\}$ with $\alpha = 0.5$, $\beta = 0.3$, $\phi = 0.8$, and initial values $\ell_0 = 95$, $b_0 = 10$.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the damped trend update equations and explain the role of $\phi$ in controlling long-horizon forecasts. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the iteration table and produce 2-step ahead forecasts. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Limitations of Holt's Linear Method:**
- The forecast $\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h b_t$ grows **linearly without bound** as $h$ increases.
- For series where the trend is **not persistent** (e.g., market saturation, technology lifecycle), Holt's method over-extrapolates.
- In the M3-competition, damped trend methods consistently outperformed pure Holt's method.

**Damped Trend Update Equations (derived from the Innovations State-Space form):**

$$\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + \phi b_{t-1})$$

$$b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) \phi b_{t-1}$$

**Forecast Equation (h-step ahead):**

$$\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + \sum_{i=1}^{h} \phi^i b_t = \ell_t + \left(\frac{\phi(1 - \phi^h)}{1 - \phi}\right) b_t$$

**Role of $\phi$:**
- The parameter $\phi \in (0, 1)$ multiplies the trend at each future step, causing it to **decay geometrically**.
- As $h \to \infty$, the cumulative trend contribution converges to the finite value $\frac{\phi}{1 - \phi} b_t$, producing a **horizontal asymptote**.
- $\phi = 1$ reduces the method to standard Holt's; $\phi \to 0$ reduces it to simple ES.
- Typical empirical values: $0.80 \leq \phi \leq 0.98$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Iteration Table:**

**For $t = 1$:**
- Forecast: $\hat{y}_1 = \ell_0 + \phi b_0 = 95 + 0.8(10) = 95 + 8 = 103$
- Error: $e_1 = 100 - 103 = -3$
- Level: $\ell_1 = 0.5(100) + 0.5(95 + 0.8 \times 10) = 50 + 0.5(103) = 50 + 51.5 = 101.5$
- Trend: $b_1 = 0.3(101.5 - 95) + 0.7(0.8 \times 10) = 0.3(6.5) + 0.7(8) = 1.95 + 5.6 = 7.55$

**For $t = 2$:**
- Forecast: $\hat{y}_2 = \ell_1 + \phi b_1 = 101.5 + 0.8(7.55) = 101.5 + 6.04 = 107.54$
- Error: $e_2 = 110 - 107.54 = 2.46$
- Level: $\ell_2 = 0.5(110) + 0.5(101.5 + 0.8 \times 7.55) = 55 + 0.5(101.5 + 6.04) = 55 + 53.77 = 108.77$
- Trend: $b_2 = 0.3(108.77 - 101.5) + 0.7(0.8 \times 7.55) = 0.3(7.27) + 0.7(6.04) = 2.181 + 4.228 = 6.409$

**For $t = 3$:**
- Forecast: $\hat{y}_3 = 108.77 + 0.8(6.409) = 108.77 + 5.1272 = 113.8972$
- Error: $e_3 = 125 - 113.8972 = 11.1028$
- Level: $\ell_3 = 0.5(125) + 0.5(108.77 + 0.8 \times 6.409) = 62.5 + 0.5(108.77 + 5.1272) = 62.5 + 56.9486 = 119.4486$
- Trend: $b_3 = 0.3(119.4486 - 108.77) + 0.7(0.8 \times 6.409) = 0.3(10.6786) + 0.7(5.1272) = 3.2036 + 3.5890 = 6.7926$

**For $t = 4$:**
- Forecast: $\hat{y}_4 = 119.4486 + 0.8(6.7926) = 119.4486 + 5.4341 = 124.8827$
- Error: $e_4 = 140 - 124.8827 = 15.1173$
- Level: $\ell_4 = 0.5(140) + 0.5(119.4486 + 0.8 \times 6.7926) = 70 + 0.5(119.4486 + 5.4341) = 70 + 62.4414 = 132.4414$
- Trend: $b_4 = 0.3(132.4414 - 119.4486) + 0.7(0.8 \times 6.7926) = 0.3(12.9928) + 0.7(5.4341) = 3.8978 + 3.8039 = 7.7017$

**For $t = 5$:**
- Forecast: $\hat{y}_5 = 132.4414 + 0.8(7.7017) = 132.4414 + 6.1614 = 138.6028$
- Error: $e_5 = 158 - 138.6028 = 19.3972$
- Level: $\ell_5 = 0.5(158) + 0.5(132.4414 + 0.8 \times 7.7017) = 79 + 0.5(132.4414 + 6.1614) = 79 + 69.3014 = 148.3014$
- Trend: $b_5 = 0.3(148.3014 - 132.4414) + 0.7(0.8 \times 7.7017) = 0.3(15.86) + 0.7(6.1614) = 4.758 + 4.3130 = 9.0710$

**Summary Table:**

| $t$ | $y_t$ | $\hat{y}_t$ | $e_t$ | $\ell_t$ | $b_t$ |
|-----|-------|-------------|-------|----------|-------|
| 1 | 100 | 103.000 | -3.000 | 101.500 | 7.5500 |
| 2 | 110 | 107.540 | 2.460 | 108.770 | 6.4090 |
| 3 | 125 | 113.897 | 11.103 | 119.449 | 6.7926 |
| 4 | 140 | 124.883 | 15.117 | 132.441 | 7.7017 |
| 5 | 158 | 138.603 | 19.397 | 148.301 | 9.0710 |

**2-Step Ahead Forecasts** (using $\ell_5 = 148.3014$, $b_5 = 9.0710$):

$$\hat{y}_6 = 148.3014 + \phi b_5 = 148.3014 + 0.8(9.0710) = 148.3014 + 7.2568 = \mathbf{155.5582}$$

$$\hat{y}_7 = 148.3014 + (\phi + \phi^2) b_5 = 148.3014 + (0.8 + 0.64)(9.0710) = 148.3014 + 12.6127 = \mathbf{160.9141}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forecast Equation Mix-up (Most Common):** In the damped trend method, the h-step forecast is **NOT** $\ell_t + h b_t$ (that's Holt's). Students frequently write $\ell_t + h \phi b_t$ which is wrong. The correct form is $\ell_t + \sum_{i=1}^h \phi^i b_t$ or its closed form $\ell_t + \frac{\phi(1-\phi^h)}{1-\phi} b_t$.
>
> 2. **Initial Forecast Error:** Many students compute the first forecast as $\hat{y}_1 = \ell_0$ (just the level). The correct first forecast is $\hat{y}_1 = \ell_0 + \phi b_0$ in damped, or $\ell_0 + b_0$ in Holt's. This propagates errors through the entire iteration.
>
> 3. **Confusing $\hat{y}_t$ with $\ell_t$:** The forecast is the one-step-ahead prediction **before** updating, not the smoothed level after updating.
>
> 4. **Parameter Boundary Errors:** Writing $\alpha = 0$ or $\beta = 1$ without justification; the constraints $0 < \alpha, \beta < 1$ are not always strict.
>
> 5. **Decimal Precision Loss:** KTU valuer awards marks for showing **all** decimal steps. Rounding to integers mid-calculation loses significant marks.
>
> 6. **Sign Error in $b_t$ Update:** The trend equation is $\beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)b_{t-1}$, NOT $\beta(b_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1})$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

### Core Equations (Holt's)
- $\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$
- $b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)b_{t-1}$
- $\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h b_t$

### Core Equations (Damped)
- $\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + \phi b_{t-1})$
- $b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)\phi b_{t-1}$
- $\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + \frac{\phi(1-\phi^h)}{1-\phi} b_t$

### Key Constraints
- All parameters: $0 < \alpha, \beta, \phi < 1$
- Initialization: $\ell_0$ and $b_0$ must be supplied; common methods: $y_1$, mean of first few, OLS regression.

### Critical Distinctions
- **Simple ES:** 1 parameter, flat forecasts.
- **Holt's:** 2 parameters, linear extrapolation.
- **Damped:** 3 parameters, asymptotic forecast.
- **Holt-Winters:** 3 parameters + seasonals, for trend + seasonality.

### Performance Metrics
- **SSE** for optimization.
- **RMSE** for general accuracy.
- **MAPE** for scale-independent reporting.

### Empirical Insights
- Damped trend typically outperforms Holt's in M-competitions.
- Damping parameter $\phi$ typically in $[0.8, 0.98]$.
- Parameter optimization should be done via numerical minimization (not grid search on coarse grid).

### Initialization Tips for Exams
- If a series starts low and grows: $\ell_0 = y_1$, $b_0 \approx y_2 - y_1$.
- If a series oscillates: use average of first 2–3 observations for $\ell_0$ and 0 or small value for $b_0$.
- OLS initialization: regress $y_t = a + bt + \varepsilon_t$ on first few points; $\ell_0 = \hat{a}$, $b_0 = \hat{b}$.

### Common Exam Traps
- Question asking for SSE after only 5 observations — don't divide by $n$ for SSE.
- Distinguishing between one-step-ahead in-sample fit and h-step-ahead forecast.
- Remember: forecasts generated at the **last training observation** are denoted $F_{T+h}$ or $\hat{y}_{T+h \mid T}$.
- State-space formulation is the theoretically rigorous form — know the ISSF representation for full marks on theory questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exponential Smoothing Methods with Seasonality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Exponential Smoothing Methods with Seasonality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — Exponential Smoothing Methods with Seasonality

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exponential Smoothing (ES):** A family of recursive forecasting techniques that produce forecasts as weighted averages of past observations, where the weights **decay exponentially** with the age of the observation. Older observations receive exponentially smaller weights, while recent observations dominate the forecast.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECDT861)**, Exponential Smoothing is treated as the benchmark linear, model-based approach to short-term forecasting. It is parameterised by a small set of **smoothing constants** and is structurally equivalent to specific state-space models (ETS — Error, Trend, Seasonality).

### 1.1.1 Taxonomy of Exponential Smoothing Methods

The family of ES methods is classified by the three structural components the model can capture:

| # | Method | Trend | Seasonality | # of Parameters |
|---|--------|-------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)** | ✗ | ✗ | 1 (α) |
| 2 | **Holt's Linear Trend (Double ES)** | ✓ | ✗ | 2 (α, β) |
| 3 | **Damped Trend Method** | ✓ (damped) | ✗ | 3 (α, β, φ) |
| 4 | **Holt–Winters Additive (Triple ES)** | ✓ | ✓ (Additive) | 3 (α, β, γ) |
| 5 | **Holt–Winters Multiplicative (Triple ES)** | ✓ | ✓ (Multiplicative) | 3 (α, β, γ) |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU Module 2 terminology, the term **"Exponential Smoothing Methods with Seasonality"** is used as an umbrella phrase that explicitly refers to **Holt–Winters Additive** and **Holt–Winters Multiplicative** methods, with Holt's linear method covered as the prerequisite.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Forgetting Thermometer"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a weather forecaster trying to predict tomorrow's maximum temperature. The forecaster's memory behaves like a stretched rubber band: yesterday's reading contributes fully, last week's reading contributes weakly, and last year's reading has almost no effect.
>
> Mathematically, the "memory" is encoded in the **smoothing constant α ∈ (0, 1)**. A value of **α ≈ 1** means "trust only the most recent observation"; **α ≈ 0** means "trust history more than the latest data point." This exponential decay of memory is the defining feature of the entire ES family.

The same idea extends to **trend** (β) and **seasonality** (γ) — each component has its own forgetting constant, so the model can independently decide *how fast to forget* the level, the slope, and the seasonal pattern.

---

## 1.2 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) maps this topic primarily to:

- **CO2:** *Apply classical decomposition and exponential smoothing techniques to time series data.*
- **CO3:** *Evaluate forecast accuracy using appropriate error metrics.*

The **12-Mark to 14-Mark** End Semester questions on this topic routinely require:
1. Writing down the three recursive equations,
2. Performing **iterative hand-computation** on a small dataset (4–8 observations),
3. Generating **multi-step forecasts** h = 1, 2, …, m periods ahead.

Mastery of Holt–Winters is therefore **non-negotiable** for the ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of Holt–Winters

Holt–Winters decomposes a time series $y_t$ into **three orthogonal components** that are updated *recursively* at every step:

1. **Level** $\ell_t$ — the locally smooth baseline value of the series.
2. **Trend** $b_t$ — the locally estimated slope (rate of change of the level).
3. **Seasonal** $s_t$ — the periodic deviation (additive) or ratio (multiplicative) of length $m$.

At every time $t$, the model reads the *actual* observation $y_t$ and *corrects* its three internal components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recursive = Memoryless update:** Each new component value is computed using *only* the previous components and the current observation. There is **no need to retain the entire past history**, which is what makes ES computationally attractive for real-time forecasting (e.g., KTU's classic retail-sales and electricity-load examples).

---

## 2.2 Additive vs. Multiplicative Seasonality — The Decisive Distinction

| Property | Additive Model | Multiplicative Model |
|----------|---------------|----------------------|
| **When to use** | Seasonal swing is **constant in absolute units** | Seasonal swing is **proportional to the level** |
| **Decomposition** | $y_t = \ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + s_{t-m} + \varepsilon_t$ | $y_t = (\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) \cdot s_{t-m} \cdot \varepsilon_t$ |
| **Dimensionality** of $s_t$ | Same units as $y_t$ (e.g., units) | Dimensionless (ratio, ≈ 1.0) |
| **Typical example** | Monthly temperature (°C) | Monthly retail revenue (₹) |
| **Constraint on $s_t$** | None | Product over a season $\approx m$ |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** you need for KTU ESE 14-mark questions on this topic. **Memorise the structure**, not just the symbols — the same template reappears in damped-trend and innovation-state-space variants.

| # | Component / Equation | Holt's Linear (No Seasonality) | Holt–Winters Additive | Holt–Winters Multiplicative |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Level update** | $\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$ | $\ell_t = \alpha (y_t - s_{t-m}) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$ | $\ell_t = \alpha \dfrac{y_t}{s_{t-m}} + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$ |
| 2 | **Trend update** | $b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) b_{t-1}$ | $b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) b_{t-1}$ | $b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) b_{t-1}$ |
| 3 | **Seasonal update** | — | $s_t = \gamma (y_t - \ell_t) + (1-\gamma) s_{t-m}$ | $s_t = \gamma \dfrac{y_t}{\ell_t} + (1-\gamma) s_{t-m}$ |
| 4 | **h-step forecast** | $\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h b_t$ | $\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h b_t + s_{t+h-m\lfloor (h-1)/m \rfloor - m}$ | $\hat{y}_{t+h} = (\ell_t + h b_t) \cdot s_{t+h-m\lfloor (h-1)/m \rfloor - m}$ |
| 5 | **Smoothing parameters** | $\alpha, \beta \in (0,1)$ | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma \in (0,1)$ | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma \in (0,1)$ |
| 6 | **Seasonal period** | — | $m$ (e.g., 4 for quarterly, 12 for monthly) | $m$ (e.g., 4, 12, 7) |
| 7 | **Initial level** | $\ell_0 = y_1$ | $\ell_m = \bar{y}_{\text{season-1}}$ | $\ell_m = \bar{y}_{\text{season-1}}$ |
| 8 | **Initial trend** | $b_0 = y_2 - y_1$ | $b_m = \dfrac{\bar{y}_{\text{last}} - \bar{y}_{\text{first}}}{m-1}$ | $b_m = \dfrac{\bar{y}_{\text{last}} - \bar{y}_{\text{first}}}{m-1}$ |
| 9 | **Initial seasonal** | — | $s_i = y_i - \ell_m$ for $i = 1,\dots,m$ | $s_i = y_i / \ell_m$ for $i = 1,\dots,m$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick exam mnemonic — "A T S F"**: every Holt–Winters model has **A**verage-of-update, **T**rend-recursion, **S**easonal-recursion, and **F**orecast-combination. Memorising the column order in the table guarantees marks.

---

## 2.4 Why the Same Trend Equation Appears in All Variants

The trend update $b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) b_{t-1}$ is **identical** in all three models because:

- The trend is fundamentally a property of the **level's evolution**, not the seasonal effect.
- The smoothing constant **β** controls how rapidly the model updates its slope estimate — high β means the model reacts quickly to slope changes; low β means the slope is treated as a slowly varying long-run feature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **load forecasting** (electricity boards, Kerala KSEB datasets), the trend captures the secular growth in consumption, while the seasonal component captures the **summer–winter air-conditioning/heating cycle**. In **retail revenue forecasting** (Kerala co-operative sector datasets used as KTU lab exercises), the multiplicative form is more appropriate because the absolute size of festival-season spikes grows with the overall business volume.

---

## 2.5 Choosing Between Additive and Multiplicative

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** A common 1-mark loss occurs when students fail to justify the choice of model. Always state:
> 1. The *qualitative* reason (constant swing vs. proportional swing), **and**
> 2. The *quantitative* reason (compute the ratio of seasonal range to mean across seasons — if it is roughly constant, use additive; if it grows/shrinks with the level, use multiplicative).

---

## 2.6 Smoothing Parameter Optimisation

Although KTU 14-mark questions typically *prescribe* the values of α, β, γ, the underlying principle is the **minimisation of the Sum of Squared Forecast Errors (SSFE):**

$$SSFE = \sum_{t=m+1}^{n} (y_t - \hat{y}_{t \mid t-1})^2$$

or equivalently the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** of the one-step-ahead in-sample residuals. The minimisation is done numerically (e.g., L-BFGS in `ets()` from the R `forecast` package, or `HoltWinters()` in R).

---

## 2.7 Damped Trend Variant (Frequently Asked as 7-Mark Sub-Part)

To prevent unrealistic long-horizon forecasts, Holt's method is often augmented with a **damping parameter φ ∈ (0, 1):**

$$\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + \phi b_{t-1})$$

$$b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)\phi b_{t-1}$$

$$\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + \sum_{i=1}^{h} \phi^{i} b_t = \ell_t + \frac{\phi(1-\phi^{h})}{1-\phi} b_t$$

> [!NOTE]
> The damped trend is *not* the headline of this module but appears regularly as a 7-mark sub-part in ESE. Treat it as the natural next step after mastering Holt–Winters.

---

# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides an **exhaustive, line-by-line hand-computation** of the Holt–Winters **Additive** method on a quarterly dataset. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly so that a KTU student can replicate the method on any similar question.

## 3.1 Problem Statement (Worked Numerical — Additive)

> **Data:** Quarterly sales (in units) of a KTU hypothetical firm for 2 years:
>
> | Quarter | $t$ | $y_t$ |
> |---------|-----|-------|
> | Y1 Q1 | 1 | 100 |
> | Y1 Q2 | 2 | 120 |
> | Y1 Q3 | 3 | 110 |
> | Y1 Q4 | 4 | 140 |
> | Y2 Q1 | 5 | 110 |
> | Y2 Q2 | 6 | 130 |
> | Y2 Q3 | 7 | 120 |
> | Y2 Q4 | 8 | 150 |
>
> **Given:** Seasonal period $m = 4$, smoothing constants $\alpha = 0.3$, $\beta = 0.2$, $\gamma = 0.4$.
>
> **Required:** (a) Initialise the level, trend, and seasonal indices.
> (b) Iteratively update all components for $t = 5, 6, 7, 8$.
> (c) Forecast for $t = 9, 10, 11, 12$ (i.e., Year 3 quarters 1–4).

---

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Initialisation at $t = m = 4$

**Initial level $\ell_4$:** Take the simple average of the first complete season (first $m = 4$ observations).

$$\ell_4 = \frac{y_1 + y_2 + y_3 + y_4}{m} = \frac{100 + 120 + 110 + 140}{4} = \frac{470}{4} = 117.50$$

**Initial trend $b_4$:** The most defensible KTU-style choice is the average difference between corresponding quarters of season 1 and the prior "phantom" period. With only one complete season available, use the simple average difference between consecutive observations within season 1, divided by $(m - 1)$:

$$b_4 = \frac{(y_2 - y_1) + (y_3 - y_2) + (y_4 - y_3)}{m - 1} = \frac{20 + (-10) + 30}{3} = \frac{40}{3} \approx 13.33$$

However, the KTU convention (and the safer choice for hand calculation when the trend is modest) is to set the initial trend to **zero** to avoid amplifying noise. We use $b_4 = 0$ in this worked example to keep arithmetic clean. *(Both approaches are accepted in ESE; state which one you adopt.)*

**Initial seasonal indices $s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4$:** For the additive model, the seasonal index is the deviation of each observation from the initial level.

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_1 &= y_1 - \ell_4 = 100 - 117.50 = -17.50 \\
s_2 &= y_2 - \ell_4 = 120 - 117.50 = \phantom{-}2.50 \\
s_3 &= y_3 - \ell_4 = 110 - 117.50 = \phantom{-} -7.50 \\
s_4 &= y_4 - \ell_4 = 140 - 117.50 = \phantom{-}22.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sanity check:** The seasonal indices must sum to approximately **zero** for an additive model. Indeed, $-17.50 + 2.50 - 7.50 + 22.50 = 0$. ✓

---

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Recursive Update at $t = 5$ (Y2 Q1)

The seasonal index of the corresponding quarter one year ago is $s_{5-4} = s_1 = -17.50$.

**Level update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell_5 &= \alpha (y_5 - s_{5-4}) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_4 + b_4) \\
      &= 0.30 \cdot (110 - (-17.50)) + 0.70 \cdot (117.50 + 0) \\
      &= 0.30 \cdot 127.50 + 0.70 \cdot 117.50 \\
      &= 38.25 + 82.25 \\
      &= 120.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trend update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_5 &= \beta(\ell_5 - \ell_4) + (1-\beta) b_4 \\
    &= 0.20 \cdot (120.50 - 117.50) + 0.80 \cdot 0 \\
    &= 0.20 \cdot 3.00 + 0 \\
    &= 0.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Seasonal update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_5 &= \gamma (y_5 - \ell_5) + (1-\gamma) s_{5-4} \\
    &= 0.40 \cdot (110 - 120.50) + 0.60 \cdot (-17.50) \\
    &= 0.40 \cdot (-10.50) + (-10.50) \\
    &= -4.20 - 10.50 \\
    &= -14.70
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Recursive Update at $t = 6$ (Y2 Q2)

Now $s_{6-4} = s_2 = 2.50$.

**Level update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell_6 &= \alpha (y_6 - s_2) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_5 + b_5) \\
      &= 0.30 \cdot (130 - 2.50) + 0.70 \cdot (120.50 + 0.60) \\
      &= 0.30 \cdot 127.50 + 0.70 \cdot 121.10 \\
      &= 38.25 + 84.77 \\
      &= 123.02
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trend update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_6 &= \beta(\ell_6 - \ell_5) + (1-\beta) b_5 \\
    &= 0.20 \cdot (123.02 - 120.50) + 0.80 \cdot 0.60 \\
    &= 0.20 \cdot 2.52 + 0.48 \\
    &= 0.504 + 0.48 \\
    &= 0.984
\end{aligned}
$$

**Seasonal update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_6 &= \gamma (y_6 - \ell_6) + (1-\gamma) s_2 \\
    &= 0.40 \cdot (130 - 123.02) + 0.60 \cdot 2.50 \\
    &= 0.40 \cdot 6.98 + 1.50 \\
    &= 2.792 + 1.50 \\
    &= 4.292
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Recursive Update at $t = 7$ (Y2 Q3)

Now $s_{7-4} = s_3 = -7.50$.

**Level update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell_7 &= \alpha (y_7 - s_3) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_6 + b_6) \\
      &= 0.30 \cdot (120 - (-7.50)) + 0.70 \cdot (123.02 + 0.984) \\
      &= 0.30 \cdot 127.50 + 0.70 \cdot 124.004 \\
      &= 38.25 + 86.8028 \\
      &= 125.0528
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trend update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_7 &= \beta(\ell_7 - \ell_6) + (1-\beta) b_6 \\
    &= 0.20 \cdot (125.0528 - 123.02) + 0.80 \cdot 0.984 \\
    &= 0.20 \cdot 2.0328 + 0.7872 \\
    &= 0.40656 + 0.7872 \\
    &= 1.19376
\end{aligned}
$$

**Seasonal update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_7 &= \gamma (y_7 - \ell_7) + (1-\gamma) s_3 \\
    &= 0.40 \cdot (120 - 125.0528) + 0.60 \cdot (-7.50) \\
    &= 0.40 \cdot (-5.0528) - 4.50 \\
    &= -2.02112 - 4.50 \\
    &= -6.52112
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Recursive Update at $t = 8$ (Y2 Q4)

Now $s_{8-4} = s_4 = 22.50$.

**Level update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell_8 &= \alpha (y_8 - s_4) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_7 + b_7) \\
      &= 0.30 \cdot (150 - 22.50) + 0.70 \cdot (125.0528 + 1.19376) \\
      &= 0.30 \cdot 127.50 + 0.70 \cdot 126.24656 \\
      &= 38.25 + 88.372592 \\
      &= 126.622592
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trend update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_8 &= \beta(\ell_8 - \ell_7) + (1-\beta) b_7 \\
    &= 0.20 \cdot (126.622592 - 125.0528) + 0.80 \cdot 1.19376 \\
    &= 0.20 \cdot 1.569792 + 0.955008 \\
    &= 0.3139584 + 0.955008 \\
    &= 1.2689664
\end{aligned}
$$

**Seasonal update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_8 &= \gamma (y_8 - \ell_8) + (1-\gamma) s_4 \\
    &= 0.40 \cdot (150 - 126.622592) + 0.60 \cdot 22.50 \\
    &= 0.40 \cdot 23.377408 + 13.50 \\
    &= 9.3509632 + 13.50 \\
    &= 22.8509632
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Forecast Generation for $h = 1, 2, 3, 4$

The general additive forecast formula is:

$$\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h \cdot b_t + s_{t+h-m(k+1)}, \quad k = \left\lfloor \frac{h-1}{m} \right\rfloor$$

Since we forecast from $t = 8$ and $m = 4$, the index for $s$ is simply $t + h - m$ (because $h \leq 4$ gives $k = 0$, so $t + h - m(0+1) = t + h - m$).

| $h$ | Target Quarter | Formula | Substitution | Forecast |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Y3 Q1 | $\ell_8 + 1\cdot b_8 + s_{5}$ | $126.622592 + 1.2689664 + (-14.70)$ | **113.19** |
| 2 | Y3 Q2 | $\ell_8 + 2\cdot b_8 + s_{6}$ | $126.622592 + 2.5379328 + 4.292$ | **133.45** |
| 3 | Y3 Q3 | $\ell_8 + 3\cdot b_8 + s_{7}$ | $126.622592 + 3.8068992 + (-6.52112)$ | **123.91** |
| 4 | Y3 Q4 | $\ell_8 + 4\cdot b_8 + s_{8}$ | $126.622592 + 5.0758656 + 22.8509632$ | **154.55** |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix (KTU Quick-Reference Table for the Worked Example)

| $t$ | $y_t$ | $\ell_t$ | $b_t$ | $s_t$ | $\hat{y}_t$ (1-step) | $e_t = y_t - \hat{y}_t$ |
|-----|-------|----------|--------|--------|------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | 100.00 | — | — | -17.50 | — | — |
| 2 | 120.00 | — | — | 2.50 | — | — |
| 3 | 110.00 | — | — | -7.50 | — | — |
| 4 | 140.00 | **117.50** | **0.00** | 22.50 | — | — |
| 5 | 110.00 | 120.50 | 0.60 | -14.70 | 100.00 *(=117.5+0+(-17.5))* | 10.00 |
| 6 | 130.00 | 123.02 | 0.984 | 4.292 | 123.10 *(=120.5+0.6+2.5)* | 6.90 |
| 7 | 120.00 | 125.0528 | 1.19376 | -6.52112 | 115.514 *(=123.02+0.984+(-7.5))* | 4.486 |
| 8 | 150.00 | 126.622592 | 1.2689664 | 22.8509632 | 140.706 *(=125.0528+1.19376+22.5)* | 9.294 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation:** The one-step residuals $e_t$ in the table above are used to compute MSE, MAE, and MAPE for the model — this directly addresses **CO3 (forecast accuracy evaluation)** in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

**Mean Squared Error (MSE) from in-sample residuals:**

$$
MSE = \frac{1}{4}\sum_{t=5}^{8} e_t^{2} = \frac{10.00^2 + 6.90^2 + 4.486^2 + 9.294^2}{4} = \frac{100 + 47.61 + 20.12 + 86.38}{4} = \frac{254.11}{4} = 63.53
$$

**Root MSE (RMSE):** $\sqrt{63.53} \approx 7.97$ units.

**Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE):**

$$
MAPE = \frac{100}{4} \sum_{t=5}^{8} \left\vert \frac{e_t}{y_t} \right\vert = 25 \cdot (0.0909 + 0.0531 + 0.0374 + 0.0620) = 25 \cdot 0.2434 = 6.08\%
$$

(Here, the absolute value bars $\vert \cdot \vert$ have been **spaced out** in text to avoid interfering with the markdown table column separator. In your answer sheet, write them as $\lvert e_t \rvert / \lvert y_t \rvert$ with no spacing.)

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Multiplicative Variant (Compact)

If the same data were modelled with a **multiplicative** seasonal structure (with $\alpha = 0.3, \beta = 0.2, \gamma = 0.4$ and the same initialisation $\ell_4 = 117.50, b_4 = 0$):

**Initial seasonal ratios** (additive shift replaced by multiplicative ratio):

$$
s_i = \frac{y_i}{\ell_4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_1 = \tfrac{100}{117.5}=0.851, \; s_2 = 1.021, \; s_3 = 0.936, \; s_4 = 1.191
$$

**Sanity check:** Product over one season should equal 4 (i.e., $m$) for a multiplicative model. Indeed, $0.851 \times 1.021 \times 0.936 \times 1.191 \approx 0.992 \approx 1$, so we then **normalise** by dividing each $s_i$ by the season mean $(0.992/4) = 0.248$ and multiplying by $m = 4$ to keep the product equal to 4 — a step called **seasonal index normalisation**.

**Update at $t = 5$:**

$$
\ell_5 = \alpha \cdot \frac{y_5}{s_1} + (1-\alpha)(\ell_4 + b_4) = 0.3 \cdot \frac{110}{0.851} + 0.7 \cdot 117.5 = 0.3 \cdot 129.26 + 82.25 = 38.78 + 82.25 = 121.03
$$

$$
b_5 = \beta(\ell_5 - \ell_4) + (1-\beta)b_4 = 0.2 \cdot (121.03 - 117.5) + 0 = 0.706
$$

$$
s_5 = \gamma \cdot \frac{y_5}{\ell_5} + (1-\gamma) s_1 = 0.4 \cdot \frac{110}{121.03} + 0.6 \cdot 0.851 = 0.4 \cdot 0.9089 + 0.5106 = 0.3636 + 0.5106 = 0.8742
$$

The procedure is identical in structure — only the **arithmetic operator** changes ($y - s \to y / s$, $y - \ell \to y / \ell$).

---

## 3.4 Forecast Equation for Multiplicative at $h$ Steps

For multiplicative Holt–Winters, the forecast is:

$$\hat{y}_{t+h} = (\ell_t + h b_t) \cdot s_{t+h-m\lfloor (h-1)/m \rfloor - m}$$

For $h = 1, 2, 3, 4$ from $t = 8$ (using hypothetical $\ell_8, b_8, s_5, s_6, s_7, s_8$):

| $h$ | Forecast |
|---|---|
| 1 | $(\ell_8 + 1\cdot b_8) \cdot s_5$ |
| 2 | $(\ell_8 + 2\cdot b_8) \cdot s_6$ |
| 3 | $(\ell_8 + 3\cdot b_8) \cdot s_7$ |
| 4 | $(\ell_8 + 4\cdot b_8) \cdot s_8$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the multiplicative case, the seasonal index **modulates** the trend-projected level. This is exactly the behaviour observed in real retail/electricity data, where the festival-month peak grows larger as the business grows.

---

# 🗺️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are rendered as **Mermaid** flowcharts. They map the logical flow of Exponential Smoothing components and provide a KTU-exam-ready visual aid for describing the architecture of Holt–Winters.

## 4.1 Diagram A — Decision Tree for Selecting the Correct ES Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Examine Time Series y_t"] --> nodeB{"Is a long-term trend present?"}
    nodeB -- "NO" --> nodeC{"Is seasonality present?"}
    nodeB -- "YES" --> nodeD{"Is seasonality present?"}

    nodeC -- "NO" --> nodeE["Simple Exponential Smoothing<br/>Parameters: alpha only<br/>Forecast: L_t"]
    nodeC -- "YES" --> nodeF["Use deseasonalised SES<br/>or Winters no-trend model"]

    nodeD -- "NO" --> nodeG["Holt Linear Trend Method<br/>Parameters: alpha beta<br/>Forecast: L_t + h * B_t"]
    nodeD -- "YES" --> nodeH{"Is seasonal amplitude<br/>constant in level?"}

    nodeH -- "YES / approximately constant" --> nodeI["Holt Winters Additive<br/>Parameters: alpha beta gamma<br/>Forecast: L_t + h * B_t + S_index"]
    nodeH -- "NO / grows with level" --> nodeJ["Holt Winters Multiplicative<br/>Parameters: alpha beta gamma<br/>Forecast: L_t + h * B_t times S_index"]

    nodeI --> nodeK["Optional: Apply Damping<br/>Replace h * B_t with sum phi^i B_t"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeG --> nodeK

    nodeK --> nodeL["Validate via MSE MAE MAPE<br/>Compare with naive baseline"]
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Block Architecture of Holt–Winters (Additive)

This block diagram shows how the **level, trend, and seasonal blocks** interact at each time step $t$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    obsY["Observation y_t"] --> summer1["Subtract<br/>s_t_minus_m"]
    summer1 --> comp1["Combine with<br/>1 minus alpha times<br/>L_t_minus_1 plus B_t_minus_1"]
    comp1 --> levelOut["Level L_t<br/>(updated)"]

    obsY --> comp2["Compute difference<br/>L_t minus L_t_minus_1"]
    comp2 --> comp3["Combine with<br/>1 minus beta times B_t_minus_1"]
    comp3 --> trendOut["Trend B_t<br/>(updated)"]

    obsY --> summer2["Compute difference<br/>y_t minus L_t"]
    summer2 --> comp4["Combine with<br/>1 minus gamma times<br/>s_t_minus_m"]
    comp4 --> seasonalOut["Seasonal S_t<br/>(updated)"]

    levelOut --> adder1["Combine L_t and h times B_t"]
    trendOut --> adder1
    adder1 --> adder2["Add S_index for target horizon"]
    seasonalOut --> adder2
    adder2 --> forecastOut["Forecast hat y at t plus h"]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — Information Flow in the Recursive Update

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeT1["State at t minus 1<br/>L_t_minus_1, B_t_minus_1, S_t_minus_m"] --> nodeT2{"Receive new<br/>observation y_t"}
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3["Compute L_t using y_t<br/>and old L, B, S"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT4["Compute B_t using<br/>new L_t and old L_t_minus_1, B_t_minus_1"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT5["Compute S_t using y_t<br/>and new L_t and old S_t_minus_m"]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT6["State at t<br/>L_t, B_t, S_t"]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT6
    nodeT5 --> nodeT6
    nodeT6 --> nodeT7["Generate forecast<br/>for any horizon h greater than 0"]
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram D — Multiplicative Block Architecture (Companion View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    obsM["Observation y_t"] --> div1["Divide by<br/>s_t_minus_m"]
    div1 --> combine1["Weighted mix with<br/>L_t_minus_1 plus B_t_minus_1"]
    combine1 --> levelM["Multiplicative Level L_t"]

    obsM --> div2["Divide y_t by L_t"]
    div2 --> combine2["Weighted mix with<br/>s_t_minus_m"]
    combine2 --> seasonalM["Multiplicative Seasonal S_t"]

    levelM --> mult1["Multiply L_t plus h times B_t"]
    obsM --> mult1
    mult1 --> mult2["Multiply by S_index for horizon h"]
    seasonalM --> mult2
    mult2 --> forecastM["Forecast hat y at t plus h"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When the question is "Draw the block diagram of Holt–Winters", present both the **information flow** and the **arithmetic flow** (Diagram A + Diagram B above). Most toppers in KTU 2023 ESE secured full 14-marks by combining a flow diagram with the corresponding equations.
