## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Simple Exponential Smoothing, Holt's Linear Method, and Holt–Winters Method. State one real-world scenario where each is most appropriate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Method | Components Modelled | Real-World Scenario |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Exponential Smoothing** | Level only (no trend, no seasonality) | Forecasting the daily closing price of a stable commodity (e.g., gold) with no growth trend and no weekly cycle |
| **Holt's Linear Method** | Level and trend (no seasonality) | Forecasting annual sales of an established product growing steadily year-on-year |
| **Holt–Winters Method** | Level, trend, and seasonality (additive or multiplicative) | Forecasting monthly electricity demand in Kerala, which shows an annual cycle (peak in summer) and a year-on-year growth trend |

> **Marking Note:** Full 3 marks require: (a) one-line definition of each, (b) explicit mention of which components are modelled, (c) a real-world example for each.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of additive and multiplicative seasonality in Holt–Winters method. Under what conditions would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Additive seasonality** assumes that the seasonal swing is **constant in absolute units** across all levels of the series. Mathematically, the observed value is decomposed as $y_t = \ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + s_{t-m} + \varepsilon_t$.

- **Multiplicative seasonality** assumes that the seasonal swing is **proportional to the level** of the series. Mathematically, $y_t = (\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) \cdot s_{t-m} \cdot \varepsilon_t$, where $s_t$ is dimensionless and ideally averages to 1 within each season.

- **Decision rule:** If the *ratio* of peak-to-trough seasonal deviation to the local level remains approximately **constant** over time, use **additive**. If this *ratio grows or shrinks* as the series level changes, use **multiplicative**. A quick diagnostic: plot the seasonal sub-series; if the spread of the seasonal indices widens with the level, choose multiplicative.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has TWO internal choices (A or B), each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ❓ CHOICE 1 — Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Derive the recursive equations of the **Holt–Winters Additive** method and explain the role of each smoothing constant. State the conditions for stability of the model. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of components:**
- $\ell_t$ = level at time $t$ (locally smoothed value of the series)
- $b_t$ = trend (slope) at time $t$ (locally estimated rate of change)
- $s_t$ = seasonal index at time $t$ (periodic deviation of period $m$)

**Recursive equations:**

$$
\ell_t = \alpha (y_t - s_{t-m}) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})
$$

$$
b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) b_{t-1}
$$

$$
s_t = \gamma (y_t - \ell_t) + (1-\gamma) s_{t-m}
$$

**Role of each smoothing constant:**
- $\alpha$ (level smoothing): $\alpha \to 1$ means the level reacts aggressively to new observations; $\alpha \to 0$ means the level evolves slowly. Typical KTU-accepted range: $0.1 \leq \alpha \leq 0.5$.
- $\beta$ (trend smoothing): controls how rapidly the slope is updated. A high $\beta$ allows the trend to adapt quickly; a low $\beta$ enforces a stable long-run slope.
- $\gamma$ (seasonal smoothing): governs how quickly the seasonal pattern is revised. High $\gamma$ means the model picks up changing seasonality quickly; low $\gamma$ enforces a stable seasonal calendar.

**Forecast equation:**

$$\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h \cdot b_t + s_{t+h-m(k+1)}, \quad k = \left\lfloor \frac{h-1}{m} \right\rfloor$$

**Stability conditions:** All smoothing parameters must lie in the open interval $(0, 1)$:
$$0 < \alpha < 1, \quad 0 < \beta < 1, \quad 0 < \gamma < 1$$
The seasonal indices in the additive model should sum to approximately **zero** over a complete season; in the multiplicative model, the seasonal indices should average to **1** over a complete season.

---

#### (b) A retail outlet records the following quarterly sales (in ₹ lakhs):

| Year | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2022 | 20 | 30 | 25 | 40 |
| 2023 | 24 | 36 | 30 | 48 |

Apply the **Holt–Winters Additive** method with $\alpha = 0.4, \beta = 0.3, \gamma = 0.5$, $m = 4$. Take initial level $\ell_4 = \bar{y}_{\text{season-1}}$, initial trend $b_4 = 0$, and initial seasonal indices as deviations from $\ell_4$. Compute the components up to $t = 8$ and **forecast for 2024**. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initialisation at $t = 4$:**

$$\ell_4 = \frac{20 + 30 + 25 + 40}{4} = \frac{115}{4} = 28.75$$

$$b_4 = 0$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_1 &= 20 - 28.75 = -8.75 \\
s_2 &= 30 - 28.75 = \phantom{-}1.25 \\
s_3 &= 25 - 28.75 = -3.75 \\
s_4 &= 40 - 28.75 = 11.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Check:** Sum $= -8.75 + 1.25 - 3.75 + 11.25 = 0$. ✓

**Step 2 — Update at $t = 5$ (2023 Q1):** $s_1 = -8.75$.

$$
\ell_5 = 0.4 \cdot (24 - (-8.75)) + 0.6 \cdot (28.75 + 0) = 0.4 \cdot 32.75 + 17.25 = 13.10 + 17.25 = 30.35
$$

$$
b_5 = 0.3 \cdot (30.35 - 28.75) + 0.7 \cdot 0 = 0.3 \cdot 1.60 = 0.48
$$

$$
s_5 = 0.5 \cdot (24 - 30.35) + 0.5 \cdot (-8.75) = 0.5 \cdot (-6.35) - 4.375 = -3.175 - 4.375 = -7.55
$$

**Step 3 — Update at $t = 6$ (2023 Q2):** $s_2 = 1.25$.

$$
\ell_6 = 0.4 \cdot (36 - 1.25) + 0.6 \cdot (30.35 + 0.48) = 0.4 \cdot 34.75 + 0.6 \cdot 30.83 = 13.90 + 18.498 = 32.398
$$

$$
b_6 = 0.3 \cdot (32.398 - 30.35) + 0.7 \cdot 0.48 = 0.3 \cdot 2.048 + 0.336 = 0.6144 + 0.336 = 0.9504
$$

$$
s_6 = 0.5 \cdot (36 - 32.398) + 0.5 \cdot 1.25 = 0.5 \cdot 3.602 + 0.625 = 1.801 + 0.625 = 2.426
$$

**Step 4 — Update at $t = 7$ (2023 Q3):** $s_3 = -3.75$.

$$
\ell_7 = 0.4 \cdot (30 - (-3.75)) + 0.6 \cdot (32.398 + 0.9504) = 0.4 \cdot 33.75 + 0.6 \cdot 33.3484 = 13.50 + 20.009 = 33.509
$$

$$
b_7 = 0.3 \cdot (33.509 - 32.398) + 0.7 \cdot 0.9504 = 0.3 \cdot 1.111 + 0.6653 = 0.3333 + 0.6653 = 0.9986
$$

$$
s_7 = 0.5 \cdot (30 - 33.509) + 0.5 \cdot (-3.75) = 0.5 \cdot (-3.509) - 1.875 = -1.7545 - 1.875 = -3.6295
$$

**Step 5 — Update at $t = 8$ (2023 Q4):** $s_4 = 11.25$.

$$
\ell_8 = 0.4 \cdot (48 - 11.25) + 0.6 \cdot (33.509 + 0.9986) = 0.4 \cdot 36.75 + 0.6 \cdot 34.5076 = 14.70 + 20.7046 = 35.4046
$$

$$
b_8 = 0.3 \cdot (35.4046 - 33.509) + 0.7 \cdot 0.9986 = 0.3 \cdot 1.8956 + 0.6990 = 0.5687 + 0.6990 = 1.2677
$$

$$
s_8 = 0.5 \cdot (48 - 35.4046) + 0.5 \cdot 11.25 = 0.5 \cdot 12.5954 + 5.625 = 6.2977 + 5.625 = 11.9227
$$

**Step 6 — Forecast for 2024 (h = 1 to 4):**

| $h$ | Quarter | $\hat{y}_{8+h}$ | Calculation | Forecast (₹ lakhs) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2024 Q1 | $\ell_8 + 1\cdot b_8 + s_5$ | $35.4046 + 1.2677 - 7.55$ | **29.12** |
| 2 | 2024 Q2 | $\ell_8 + 2\cdot b_8 + s_6$ | $35.4046 + 2.5354 + 2.426$ | **40.37** |
| 3 | 2024 Q3 | $\ell_8 + 3\cdot b_8 + s_7$ | $35.4046 + 3.8031 - 3.6295$ | **35.58** |
| 4 | 2024 Q4 | $\ell_8 + 4\cdot b_8 + s_8$ | $35.4046 + 5.0708 + 11.9227$ | **52.40** |

> **Final Forecasts for 2024:** **Q1 = ₹29.12 lakhs, Q2 = ₹40.37 lakhs, Q3 = ₹35.58 lakhs, Q4 = ₹52.40 lakhs.**

---

### ❓ CHOICE 2 — Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the **Holt–Winters Multiplicative** method with all recursive equations. Compare it with the additive method in terms of (i) assumption on seasonal structure, (ii) update equations, and (iii) forecast formula. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

The multiplicative variant models a series whose seasonal amplitude **scales with the level** — a common situation in revenue, cost, and population data.

**Recursive equations:**

$$
\ell_t = \alpha \cdot \frac{y_t}{s_{t-m}} + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})
$$

$$
b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) b_{t-1}
$$

$$
s_t = \gamma \cdot \frac{y_t}{\ell_t} + (1-\gamma) s_{t-m}
$$

**Forecast equation:**

$$\hat{y}_{t+h} = (\ell_t + h \cdot b_t) \cdot s_{t+h-m\lfloor(h-1)/m\rfloor - m}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Additive | Multiplicative |
|---|---|---|
| (i) **Seasonal assumption** | Constant-amplitude swing in absolute units | Proportional-amplitude swing relative to level |
| (ii) **Update — level** | Subtracts $s_{t-m}$ from $y_t$ | Divides $y_t$ by $s_{t-m}$ |
| (ii) **Update — seasonal** | Computes $y_t - \ell_t$ | Computes $y_t / \ell_t$ |
| (iii) **Forecast combination** | Adds seasonal index | Multiplies by seasonal index |
| **Constraint on $s_t$** | Sum over season $\approx 0$ | Product over season $\approx m$ (often 4 or 12) |
| **Diagnostic** | Seasonal sub-series plot shows constant band | Seasonal sub-series plot shows fan-shape |

> **Intuition:** The multiplicative form is more "elastic" — when the business is booming, festival-month peaks are bigger; when the business shrinks, the same festival peaks shrink proportionally.

---

#### (b) Consider the same data as in Question 1(a). Apply the **Holt–Winters Multiplicative** method with $\alpha = 0.4, \beta = 0.3, \gamma = 0.5$, $m = 4$. Initialise $\ell_4 = 28.75$, $b_4 = 0$, and compute multiplicative seasonal ratios for 2022. Generate forecasts for 2024. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initialisation at $t = 4$:**

$\ell_4 = 28.75, \quad b_4 = 0$.

**Initial multiplicative seasonal ratios** $s_i = y_i / \ell_4$:

$$
s_1 = 20 / 28.75 = 0.6957, \quad s_2 = 30 / 28.75 = 1.0435, \quad s_3 = 25 / 28.75 = 0.8696, \quad s_4 = 40 / 28.75 = 1.3913
$$

**Check (sum of ratios should equal $m = 4$):** $0.6957 + 1.0435 + 0.8696 + 1.3913 = 4.0001 \approx 4$. ✓

**Step 2 — Update at $t = 5$ (2023 Q1):**

$$
\ell_5 = 0.4 \cdot \frac{24}{0.6957} + 0.6 \cdot 28.75 = 0.4 \cdot 34.495 + 17.25 = 13.798 + 17.25 = 31.048
$$

$$
b_5 = 0.3 \cdot (31.048 - 28.75) + 0.7 \cdot 0 = 0.3 \cdot 2.298 = 0.6894
$$

$$
s_5 = 0.5 \cdot \frac{24}{31.048} + 0.5 \cdot 0.6957 = 0.5 \cdot 0.7729 + 0.3479 = 0.3865 + 0.3479 = 0.7343
$$

**Step 3 — Update at $t = 6$ (2023 Q2):**

$$
\ell_6 = 0.4 \cdot \frac{36}{1.0435} + 0.6 \cdot (31.048 + 0.6894) = 0.4 \cdot 34.498 + 0.6 \cdot 31.737 = 13.799 + 19.042 = 32.841
$$

$$
b_6 = 0.3 \cdot (32.841 - 31.048) + 0.7 \cdot 0.6894 = 0.3 \cdot 1.793 + 0.4826 = 0.5379 + 0.4826 = 1.0205
$$

$$
s_6 = 0.5 \cdot \frac{36}{32.841} + 0.5 \cdot 1.0435 = 0.5 \cdot 1.0962 + 0.5218 = 0.5481 + 0.5218 = 1.0698
$$

**Step 4 — Update at $t = 7$ (2023 Q3):**

$$
\ell_7 = 0.4 \cdot \frac{30}{0.8696} + 0.6 \cdot (32.841 + 1.0205) = 0.4 \cdot 34.495 + 0.6 \cdot 33.8615 = 13.798 + 20.317 = 34.115
$$

$$
b_7 = 0.3 \cdot (34.115 - 32.841) + 0.7 \cdot 1.0205 = 0.3 \cdot 1.274 + 0.7144 = 0.3822 + 0.7144 = 1.0966
$$

$$
s_7 = 0.5 \cdot \frac{30}{34.115} + 0.5 \cdot 0.8696 = 0.5 \cdot 0.8794 + 0.4348 = 0.4397 + 0.4348 = 0.8745
$$

**Step 5 — Update at $t = 8$ (2023 Q4):**

$$
\ell_8 = 0.4 \cdot \frac{48}{1.3913} + 0.6 \cdot (34.115 + 1.0966) = 0.4 \cdot 34.498 + 0.6 \cdot 35.2116 = 13.799 + 21.127 = 34.926
$$

$$
b_8 = 0.3 \cdot (34.926 - 34.115) + 0.7 \cdot 1.0966 = 0.3 \cdot 0.811 + 0.7676 = 0.2433 + 0.7676 = 1.0109
$$

$$
s_8 = 0.5 \cdot \frac{48}{34.926} + 0.5 \cdot 1.3913 = 0.5 \cdot 1.3744 + 0.6957 = 0.6872 + 0.6957 = 1.3829
$$

**Step 6 — Forecast for 2024:**

| $h$ | Quarter | $\hat{y}_{8+h}$ | Calculation | Forecast (₹ lakhs) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2024 Q1 | $(\ell_8 + 1\cdot b_8)\cdot s_5$ | $(34.926 + 1.0109) \cdot 0.7343$ | $35.937 \cdot 0.7343 = \mathbf{26.39}$ |
| 2 | 2024 Q2 | $(\ell_8 + 2\cdot b_8)\cdot s_6$ | $(34.926 + 2.0218) \cdot 1.0698$ | $36.948 \cdot 1.0698 = \mathbf{39.52}$ |
| 3 | 2024 Q3 | $(\ell_8 + 3\cdot b_8)\cdot s_7$ | $(34.926 + 3.0327) \cdot 0.8745$ | $37.959 \cdot 0.8745 = \mathbf{33.19}$ |
| 4 | 2024 Q4 | $(\ell_8 + 4\cdot b_8)\cdot s_8$ | $(34.926 + 4.0436) \cdot 1.3829$ | $38.970 \cdot 1.3829 = \mathbf{53.89}$ |

> **Final Multiplicative Forecasts for 2024:** **Q1 = ₹26.39 lakhs, Q2 = ₹39.52 lakhs, Q3 = ₹33.19 lakhs, Q4 = ₹53.89 lakhs.**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the seasonal index inside the level update** — the term $(y_t - s_{t-m})$ is frequently written as $y_t$ alone, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong seasonal index in the forecast** — for $h = 1$ from $t = 8$, students often use $s_4$ instead of $s_5$. The correct index is the one belonging to the *same season-position* of the forecast horizon.
> 3. **Mixing up additive and multiplicative formulas** — using $y_t - s_{t-m}$ inside a multiplicative update is a structural error; ensure the operator matches the model variant.
> 4. **Omitting the floor-function index in the forecast equation** — for $h > m$ (e.g., forecasting 5 quarters ahead with $m = 4$), the seasonal index must wrap around. Use the correct $k = \lfloor (h-1)/m \rfloor$.
> 5. **Failing to report residual error metrics** — for a 14-mark question, computing MSE/MAPE on the in-sample residuals is a **2-mark bonus** under CO3.
>
> **Additional Tip:** Always carry **4 decimal places** in the level and trend during intermediate steps. KTU valuators award 0.5–1 bonus mark for clean, well-rounded intermediate arithmetic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES):** $\hat{y}_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)\hat{y}_t$. Used when there is no trend and no seasonality.
- **Holt's Linear Method:** Two equations — one for level, one for trend. Used when there is a trend but no seasonality.
- **Holt–Winters Additive:** Three equations. Use when seasonal swing is **constant in absolute units**. Seasonal indices sum to zero over one season.
- **Holt–Winters Multiplicative:** Three equations. Use when seasonal swing is **proportional to the level**. Seasonal indices multiply to $m$ (the period) over one season.
- **Smoothing constants:** $\alpha$ (level), $\beta$ (trend), $\gamma$ (seasonal). All must lie in $(0, 1)$. They are typically estimated by minimising in-sample MSE.
- **Initialisation rules:**
  - $\ell_m$ = mean of first complete season.
  - $b_m$ = average inter-season difference (or set to 0 if data is short).
  - $s_i = y_i - \ell_m$ (additive) or $s_i = y_i / \ell_m$ (multiplicative), for $i = 1, \ldots, m$.
- **Forecast index for the seasonal component:** $s_{t+h-m(k+1)}$ where $k = \lfloor (h-1)/m \rfloor$.
- **Damped trend:** Replace $h b_t$ in the forecast by $(\phi + \phi^2 + \cdots + \phi^h) b_t = \phi(1-\phi^h) b_t / (1-\phi)$. Improves long-horizon stability.
- **Performance metrics:** MSE, RMSE, MAE, MAPE. MAPE is the most easily interpreted by a non-technical audience.
- **Cross-validation:** For seasonal data, use a forecast horizon equal to the season length $m$ when validating the model on a hold-out sample.
- **Limitation of ES methods:** Cannot model complex nonlinear patterns, exogenous regressors, or volatility clustering — use ARIMA, SARIMAX, or GARCH instead for such data.
- **ETS correspondence:** Holt–Winters Additive = ETS(A,A,A); Holt–Winters Multiplicative = ETS(A,A,M) in the Hyndman–Athanasopoulos taxonomy. (KTU may include this as a 2-mark "state the ETS equivalent" sub-part.)
- **Kerala-specific KTU context:** Examples in KTU question papers often use (i) KSEB monthly electricity demand (multiplicative seasonality), (ii) Kerala monsoon rainfall data (additive seasonality with damped trend), (iii) Kochi port cargo volume (multiplicative with structural break).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation and Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation and Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Time Series Analysis**, *Estimation* refers to the statistical procedure of inferring the unknown parameters of a postulated time series model (such as the coefficients $\phi_i$, $\theta_j$, and the white noise variance $\sigma_Z^2$ in an ARMA$(p,q)$ process) from a finite observed realization $\{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$. *Modelling* (or Model Building) refers to the broader iterative process of selecting an appropriate model class, fitting it to data, and validating its adequacy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Estimation is the act of determining the numerical values of the model parameters that maximize the agreement between the model output and the observed data, while modelling encompasses the complete Box-Jenkins paradigm of Identification $\rightarrow$ Estimation $\rightarrow$ Diagnostic Checking.

The two principal estimation techniques emphasized in the **PECDT861** syllabus are:

1. **Method of Least Squares (LS / OLS):** Minimizes the sum of squared residuals.
2. **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE):** Maximizes the probability (likelihood) that the observed data was generated by the chosen model with specific parameter values.

A closely related special case used heavily for **Autoregressive (AR)** processes is the **Yule-Walker Estimation**, which solves the system of Yule-Walker equations derived from the autocovariance structure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Weather Detective"**
>
> Imagine you are a detective trying to figure out the "rule" behind a strange sequence of daily temperatures: 21°C, 23°C, 22°C, 24°C, 23°C, 25°C, ...
>
> 1. **Modelling** is the act of *guessing the rule* (e.g., "Tomorrow's temperature is roughly today's temperature plus a small random wiggle"). This is *model selection / identification*.
> 2. **Estimation** is the act of *measuring the exact strength* of that rule (e.g., "Tomorrow is, on average, **0.8 times** today's temperature, plus a random wiggle of standard deviation 2°C"). Here, $0.8$ and $2$ are the *estimated parameters*.
> 3. **Diagnostic Checking** is testing whether the rule actually works — by looking at the *leftover wiggles* (residuals) to see if they are truly random.

In engineering terms, you assume the data $\{x_t\}$ was generated by a stochastic process and you are trying to recover the "DNA" of that process using a single observed sequence.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Parameter Vector | $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ | The collection of unknown coefficients to be estimated. |
| Residual | $\hat{e}_t$ | The leftover after fitting: $x_t - \hat{x}_t$. |
| Likelihood Function | $L(\boldsymbol{\beta})$ | Probability of observing the data given parameters $\boldsymbol{\beta}$. |
| Log-Likelihood | $\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \log L(\boldsymbol{\beta})$ | Used in practice because logs convert products to sums. |
| Akaike Information Criterion | $AIC$ | Penalty-based criterion for model parsimony. |
| Goodness-of-Fit | — | How well the fitted model matches the historical data. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Estimation (KTU Priority)

The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests three estimation paradigms. Each is suited to a different model structure.

### Pillar 1 — Method of Least Squares (LS)
- **Idea:** Choose $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ that minimizes the **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**: $S(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^2$.
- **Why it works:** Squaring penalizes large deviations heavily; leads to a closed-form solution for *linear* models (AR, linear regression).
- **Best for:** AR models (becomes the *conditional* least squares).

### Pillar 2 — Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)
- **Idea:** Treat the data as a realization of a joint probability distribution. Choose $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ that makes the observed data *most probable*.
- **Why it works:** Asymptotically **efficient** (achieves the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound) and **consistent**.
- **Best for:** ARMA, MA, and non-Gaussian models.

### Pillar 3 — Method of Moments / Yule-Walker
- **Idea:** Equate *theoretical* autocovariances to *sample* autocovariances; solve the resulting linear system.
- **Why it works:** Closed-form solution; excellent for AR processes.
- **Drawback:** Less efficient than MLE for finite samples.

## 2.2 Detailed Estimation Algorithms

### 2.2.1 Least Squares Estimation for AR$(p)$ Model

For an AR$(p)$ process $X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \dots + \phi_p X_{t-p} + Z_t$, we define:

$$S(\boldsymbol{\phi}) = \sum_{t=p+1}^{n} \left( x_t - \phi_1 x_{t-1} - \phi_2 x_{t-2} - \dots - \phi_p x_{t-p} \right)^2$$

Setting the partial derivatives $\frac{\partial S}{\partial \phi_i} = 0$ for all $i = 1, \dots, p$, the **normal equations** can be written in matrix form as:

$$\mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X} \, \boldsymbol{\hat{\phi}} = \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{x}$$

where the design matrix $\mathbf{X}$ is $(n-p) \times p$, the response vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_{p+1}, \dots, x_n)^{\top}$, and the parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\phi} = (\phi_1, \phi_2, \dots, \phi_p)^{\top}$.

The closed-form solution is:

$$\boldsymbol{\hat{\phi}}_{LS} = \left( \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{x}$$

### 2.2.2 Yule-Walker Estimation for AR$(p)$

The Yule-Walker equations relate the autocovariances to the AR parameters:

$$\gamma(k) = \phi_1 \gamma(k-1) + \phi_2 \gamma(k-2) + \dots + \phi_p \gamma(k-p) \quad \text{for } k \geq 1$$

Dividing through by $\gamma(0)$, we get the autocorrelation form:

$$\rho(k) = \phi_1 \rho(k-1) + \phi_2 \rho(k-2) + \dots + \phi_p \rho(k-p) \quad \text{for } k \geq 1$$

The **Yule-Walker estimator** replaces the theoretical $\rho(k)$ with sample autocorrelations $r(k)$:

$$\mathbf{R} \, \boldsymbol{\hat{\phi}}_{YW} = \mathbf{r}$$

$$\boldsymbol{\hat{\phi}}_{YW} = \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{r}$$

where $\mathbf{R}$ is the $p \times p$ *Toeplitz* autocorrelation matrix and $\mathbf{r} = (r(1), r(2), \dots, r(p))^{\top}$.

The corresponding **white noise variance** estimator is:

$$\hat{\sigma}_Z^2 = \hat{\gamma}(0) \left( 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \hat{\phi}_i \, r(i) \right)$$

### 2.2.3 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (Gaussian Assumption)

For a Gaussian ARMA$(p,q)$ process, the exact likelihood is intractable. KTU 2024 typically focuses on the **conditional likelihood** for AR models, which is exact and Gaussian:

For AR$(p)$: $X_t \mid X_{t-1}, \dots, X_{t-p} \sim \mathcal{N}(\phi_1 x_{t-1} + \dots + \phi_p x_{t-p}, \, \sigma_Z^2)$.

The conditional log-likelihood is:

$$\ell(\boldsymbol{\phi}, \sigma_Z^2) = -\frac{n-p}{2} \log(2\pi) - \frac{n-p}{2} \log(\sigma_Z^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma_Z^2} \sum_{t=p+1}^{n} \left( x_t - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i x_{t-i} \right)^2$$

Maximizing yields:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}}_{MLE} = \boldsymbol{\hat{\phi}}_{LS} \quad \text{(same as conditional LS for AR)}$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_Z^2 = \frac{1}{n-p} \sum_{t=p+1}^{n} \hat{e}_t^2 \quad \text{(with MLE divisor)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **ARMA** models, the MLE is computed via the **Innovations Algorithm** (Brockwell & Davis), which recursively evaluates the one-step-ahead prediction errors and their variances to construct the exact (Gaussian) likelihood.

## 2.3 Model Selection Criteria — Choosing $p$ and $q$

After fitting multiple candidate models, we use penalized-likelihood criteria to balance fit and parsimony.

| Criterion | Formula | Philosophy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Akaike Information Criterion | $AIC = -2 \log L + 2k$ | Asymptotically equivalent to cross-validation; tends to slightly over-parameterize. |
| Corrected AIC | $AIC_c = AIC + \dfrac{2k(k+1)}{n-k-1}$ | Better for small samples. |
| Bayesian Information Criterion | $BIC = -2 \log L + k \log n$ | Consistent; heavier penalty; prefers simpler models. |
| Adjusted $R^2$ (regression analog) | $1 - \dfrac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-k-1}$ | Increases only if improvement exceeds cost. |

Here, $k = p + q + 1$ is the number of estimated parameters, $n$ is the sample size, and $L$ is the maximized likelihood.

> **Decision Rule:** Select the model with the **smallest** AIC, $AIC_c$, or BIC value.

## 2.4 Diagnostic Checking

After estimation, residuals $\hat{e}_t = x_t - \hat{x}_t$ must behave like **white noise**. Tests include:

**Ljung-Box (Portmanteau) Test:**

$$Q(m) = n(n+2) \sum_{k=1}^{m} \frac{\hat{\rho}_e^2(k)}{n-k} \;\;\sim \;\; \chi^2_{(m-p-q)}$$

Reject the model if $Q(m)$ is large (or $p$-value $< 0.05$).

## 2.5 The Box-Jenkins Modelling Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The complete *Modelling* workflow follows the **Box-Jenkins three-stage iterative cycle**:
>
> 1. **Identification** — Use ACF, PACF, and unit-root tests (ADF) to guess $(p, d, q)$ for an ARIMA$(p,d,q)$ model.
> 2. **Estimation** — Use LS / MLE / YW to compute parameter values.
> 3. **Diagnostic Checking** — Test residuals for white-noise behavior; if inadequate, return to Step 1.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| LS Estimator (AR) | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}} = (\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{x}$ | Closed-form AR$(p)$ estimation. |
| Yule-Walker | $\boldsymbol{\hat{\phi}}_{YW} = \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{r}$ | AR$(p)$ via sample autocorrelations. |
| Noise Variance (YW) | $\hat{\sigma}_Z^2 = \hat{\gamma}(0) \left( 1 - \sum \hat{\phi}_i r(i) \right)$ | Residual variance after AR fit. |
| Gaussian Log-Likelihood | $\ell = -\frac{n}{2} \log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum e_t^2$ | Basis of MLE for Gaussian models. |
| AIC | $AIC = -2 \log L + 2k$ | Model selection (smallest wins). |
| BIC | $BIC = -2 \log L + k \log n$ | Conservative model selection. |
| Ljung-Box Statistic | $Q(m) = n(n+2) \sum_{k=1}^{m} \dfrac{\hat{\rho}_e^2(k)}{n-k}$ | Test residuals $\to$ white noise. |
| Sample Autocovariance | $\hat{\gamma}(k) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n-k} (x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x})$ | Empirical input to YW. |
| Innovations Variance | $\hat{v}_t$ from recursion | ARMA likelihood via Innovations Algo. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Least Squares Estimator for AR$(1)$

We derive the closed-form LS estimator for the simplest AR model: $X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + Z_t$, observed as $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$.

**Step 1 — Write the objective function.**
The sum of squared residuals (conditioning on $x_1$ as "given") is:

$$S(\phi) = \sum_{t=2}^{n} \left( x_t - \phi \, x_{t-1} \right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\phi$.**
Using the chain rule on $S(\phi) = \sum (x_t^2 - 2\phi x_t x_{t-1} + \phi^2 x_{t-1}^2)$:

$$\frac{dS}{d\phi} = \sum_{t=2}^{n} \left( -2 x_t x_{t-1} + 2 \phi x_{t-1}^2 \right) = -2 \sum_{t=2}^{n} x_t x_{t-1} + 2 \phi \sum_{t=2}^{n} x_{t-1}^2$$

**Step 3 — Set the derivative to zero and solve.**

$$0 = -2 \sum_{t=2}^{n} x_t x_{t-1} + 2 \hat{\phi} \sum_{t=2}^{n} x_{t-1}^2$$

$$\hat{\phi}_{LS} = \frac{\sum_{t=2}^{n} x_t x_{t-1}}{\sum_{t=2}^{n} x_{t-1}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize this as a sample version of the theoretical result.**
The theoretical autocorrelation of AR$(1)$ is $\rho(1) = \phi$. The sample autocorrelation is:

$$r(1) = \frac{\sum_{t=2}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t-1} - \bar{x})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})^2} \approx \frac{\sum_{t=2}^{n} x_t x_{t-1}}{\sum_{t=2}^{n} x_{t-1}^2}$$

So $\hat{\phi}_{LS} \approx r(1)$, confirming the Yule-Walker estimator for $p=1$ matches the LS estimator (asymptotically).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MLE for Gaussian AR$(1)$

Assume $X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + Z_t$ with $Z_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ i.i.d.

**Step 1 — Write the joint density as a product of conditionals.**
For $t = 2, \dots, n$, the conditional density of $X_t$ given $X_{t-1} = x_{t-1}$ is:

$$f(x_t \mid x_{t-1}; \phi, \sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x_t - \phi x_{t-1})^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Form the (conditional) likelihood.**
Treat $x_1$ as fixed and multiply over $t = 2, \dots, n$:

$$L(\phi, \sigma^2) = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-\frac{n-1}{2}} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{t=2}^{n} (x_t - \phi x_{t-1})^2 \right)$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural log.**

$$\ell(\phi, \sigma^2) = -\frac{n-1}{2} \log(2\pi) - \frac{n-1}{2} \log(\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{t=2}^{n} (x_t - \phi x_{t-1})^2$$

**Step 4 — Maximize w.r.t. $\phi$.**
Setting $\partial \ell / \partial \phi = 0$:

$$0 = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \cdot 2 \sum_{t=2}^{n} (-x_{t-1})(x_t - \phi x_{t-1}) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{t=2}^{n} x_{t-1}(x_t - \phi x_{t-1})$$

$$\hat{\phi}_{MLE} = \frac{\sum_{t=2}^{n} x_t x_{t-1}}{\sum_{t=2}^{n} x_{t-1}^2}$$

This is **identical** to the LS estimator $\hat{\phi}_{LS}$.

**Step 5 — Maximize w.r.t. $\sigma^2$.**
Setting $\partial \ell / \partial \sigma^2 = 0$:

$$0 = -\frac{n-1}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2\sigma^4} \sum_{t=2}^{n} (x_t - \hat{\phi} x_{t-1})^2$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_{MLE}^2 = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{t=2}^{n} (x_t - \hat{\phi}_{MLE} x_{t-1})^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the divisor: for **LS** we divide by $n - 1$; for **MLE** we also divide by $n - 1$ (since the likelihood is conditional on $x_1$). For an *unconditional* MLE, the divisor would be $n$ (a *biased* estimator) and the recursion would include lagged residuals.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Yule-Walker Equations from Autocovariance Definitions

For a stationary AR$(p)$ process $X_t - \phi_1 X_{t-1} - \dots - \phi_p X_{t-p} = Z_t$, multiply both sides by $X_{t-k}$ and take expectation:

$$E[X_{t-k} X_t] = \phi_1 E[X_{t-k} X_{t-1}] + \phi_2 E[X_{t-k} X_{t-2}] + \dots + \phi_p E[X_{t-k} X_{t-p}] + E[X_{t-k} Z_t]$$

Since $Z_t$ is white noise and independent of past $X$'s, $E[X_{t-k} Z_t] = 0$ for $k \geq 1$. Using $\gamma(j) = E[X_t X_{t-j}]$:

$$\gamma(k) = \phi_1 \gamma(k-1) + \phi_2 \gamma(k-2) + \dots + \phi_p \gamma(k-p), \quad k \geq 1$$

Dividing by $\gamma(0)$ gives the autocorrelation form $\rho(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \rho(k-i)$, which is the Yule-Walker recursion.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — LS vs YW vs MLE

| Property | Least Squares | Yule-Walker | MLE (Gaussian) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Closed-form?** | Yes (AR only) | Yes (AR only) | No (in general; needs Innovations Algo) |
| **Asymptotic Efficiency** | Yes (for AR) | No (less efficient) | Yes (optimal) |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | Very Low | High (iterative / Innovations) |
| **Handles ARMA** | Approximately | No (AR only) | Yes (exact) |
| **Bias** | Small (large $n$) | Larger (autocovariance estimates biased) | Small |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High | High | High |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — The Innovations Algorithm (Recap)

For an ARMA process, the exact Gaussian likelihood requires the one-step-ahead prediction error variances $\hat{v}_t$ for $t = 1, \dots, n$. The **Innovations Algorithm** computes these recursively:

Initialize: $\hat{x}_1 = 0$, $\hat{v}_1 = \gamma(0)$.

For $t = 2, 3, \dots, n$:

$$\hat{x}_t = \sum_{j=1}^{t-1} \theta_{t,j} (x_{t-j} - \hat{x}_{t-j})$$

$$\hat{v}_t = \gamma(0) - \sum_{j=1}^{t-1} \theta_{t,j}^2 \hat{v}_{t-j}$$

The Gaussian log-likelihood is then:

$$\ell = -\frac{n}{2} \log(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \left[ \log \hat{v}_t + \frac{(x_t - \hat{x}_t)^2}{\hat{v}_t} \right]$$

This likelihood is maximized numerically (e.g., via Newton-Raphson) to obtain $\hat{\phi}_i$, $\hat{\theta}_j$, and $\hat{\sigma}_Z^2$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Box-Jenkins Iterative Modelling Cycle

This is the master workflow for *Modelling* a time series — a high-frequency KTU 2024 exam topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Observed Series x1 x2 ... xn] --> B[Step 1: Identification]
    B --> B1[Plot Series: Check Stationarity]
    B1 --> B2[Compute ACF and PACF]
    B2 --> B3{Stationary?}
    B3 -- No --> B4[Apply Differencing to get d]
    B4 --> B2
    B3 -- Yes --> C[Guess Orders p and q from ACF PACF tails]
    C --> D[Step 2: Estimation]
    D --> D1{Model Type}
    D1 -- AR --> D2[Use LS or Yule-Walker]
    D1 -- ARMA --> D3[Use MLE Innovations Algorithm]
    D2 --> E[Step 3: Diagnostic Checking]
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Compute Residuals e hat sub t]
    E1 --> E2{Ljung Box Test p value greater than 0.05}
    E2 -- No --> B[Return to Identification]
    E2 -- Yes --> F[Step 4: Model Selection]
    F --> F1[Compute AIC BIC for candidates]
    F1 --> F2[Select Model with Minimum AIC BIC]
    F2 --> G[Final ARIMA Model Ready for Forecasting]
```

## 4.2 Estimation Method Decision Tree

Helps students pick the correct estimator based on model class (commonly tested).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: You have data and a guessed model] --> Q1{Model Class?}
    Q1 -- AR p --> Q2{Need closed form?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> M1[Use Yule Walker]
    Q2 -- No preference --> M2[Use Least Squares]
    Q1 -- MA q or ARMA p q --> M3[Use Maximum Likelihood]
    M3 --> M4[Innovations Algorithm]
    Q1 -- ARIMA p d q --> M5[Differencing first]
    M5 --> M3
    M1 --> OUT[Output: Parameter Estimates]
    M2 --> OUT
    M4 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — The Estimation Pipeline

A block-level functional architecture of how a Time Series Estimator is structured in software.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER
        I1[Raw Time Series Data]
        I2[Initial Guessed Orders p d q]
    end

        P1[Stationarity Test ADF KPSS]

    end
    subgraph ESTIMATION_ENGINE

    end
    subgraph DIAGNOSTIC_MODULE

    end
    I1 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3
    I2 --> E1

    E1 --> E2
    E1 --> E3

    E4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
```

## 4.4 Residual Analysis Flow — Why Diagnostic Checking Matters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Residuals e hat sub t] --> R1[Plot Residuals vs Time]
    R --> R2[Plot ACF of Residuals]
    R --> R3[Histogram and QQ Plot]
    R1 --> C[Visual Check: Constant Mean Zero Variance]
    R2 --> C2[Check: 95 percent of autocorrelations within band]
    R3 --> C3[Check: Normality of residuals]

    C3 --> J
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the method of least squares for estimating the parameters of an AR$(p)$ model. Write the expression for the LS estimator in matrix form.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The method of least squares estimates the unknown AR parameters $\phi_1, \phi_2, \dots, \phi_p$ by minimizing the sum of squared residuals:

$$S(\boldsymbol{\phi}) = \sum_{t=p+1}^{n} \left( x_t - \phi_1 x_{t-1} - \phi_2 x_{t-2} - \dots - \phi_p x_{t-p} \right)^2$$

The matrix-form solution is obtained by writing the model as $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\phi} + \mathbf{e}$ and solving the normal equations $\mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\hat{\phi}} = \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{x}$, giving:

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\hat{\phi}}_{LS} = \left( \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{x}}$$

where $\mathbf{x} = (x_{p+1}, \dots, x_n)^{\top}$ and the $i$-th column of $\mathbf{X}$ is $(x_{p+1-i}, \dots, x_{n-i})^{\top}$. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)? How is it used in time series model selection?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)** is a penalized-likelihood measure used to compare candidate models. It is defined as:

$$AIC = -2 \log L_{\max} + 2k$$

where $L_{\max}$ is the maximized likelihood of the fitted model and $k$ is the number of estimated parameters.

**Usage in Model Selection (2 Marks):**
- Compute AIC for several candidate models (e.g., AR$(1)$, AR$(2)$, ARMA$(1,1)$).
- **Select the model with the smallest AIC value.**
- The penalty term $2k$ discourages over-parameterization, encouraging parsimony.
- A related, more conservative criterion is **BIC** $= -2 \log L + k \log n$, which penalizes complexity more heavily for large $n$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the **Yule-Walker equations** for an AR$(2)$ process. Express the system in matrix form and write the formula for the white noise variance estimator. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the AR$(1)$ model $X_t = 0.7 X_{t-1} + Z_t$ with $Z_t \sim WN(0, 1)$, compute the **log-likelihood** for an observed sample of size $n = 100$ and explain how the MLE is obtained. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a) — Yule-Walker for AR$(2)$ (7 Marks)

For the AR$(2)$ model: $X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + Z_t$.

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Multiply both sides by $X_{t-k}$ and take expectations for $k = 1$ and $k = 2$:

For $k = 1$: $E[X_{t-1} X_t] = \phi_1 E[X_{t-1}^2] + \phi_2 E[X_{t-2} X_{t-1}] + E[X_{t-1} Z_t]$

$$ \gamma(1) = \phi_1 \gamma(0) + \phi_2 \gamma(1) + 0 $$

For $k = 2$: $E[X_{t-2} X_t] = \phi_1 E[X_{t-2} X_{t-1}] + \phi_2 E[X_{t-2}^2] + 0$

$$ \gamma(2) = \phi_1 \gamma(1) + \phi_2 \gamma(0) $$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Divide by $\gamma(0)$ to get the autocorrelation form:

$$ \rho(1) = \phi_1 + \phi_2 \rho(1) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho(1)(1 - \phi_2) = \phi_1 $$

$$ \rho(2) = \phi_1 \rho(1) + \phi_2 $$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Express in matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \rho(1) \\ \rho(1) & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \phi_1 \\ \phi_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \rho(1) \\ \rho(2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Solve (Yule-Walker estimator replaces $\rho(k)$ with sample $r(k)$):

$$\hat{\phi}_1 = \frac{r(1)(1 - r(2))}{1 - r(1)^2}, \qquad \hat{\phi}_2 = \frac{r(2) - r(1)^2}{1 - r(1)^2}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** White noise variance estimator:

$$ \hat{\sigma}_Z^2 = \hat{\gamma}(0) \left( 1 - \hat{\phi}_1 r(1) - \hat{\phi}_2 r(2) \right) $$

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b) — Log-Likelihood for AR$(1)$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** The AR$(1)$ model is $X_t = 0.7 X_{t-1} + Z_t$ with $\sigma_Z^2 = 1$ and $n = 100$.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** The conditional Gaussian density of $X_t \mid X_{t-1} = x_{t-1}$ is:

$$ f(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 1}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x_t - 0.7 x_{t-1})^2}{2} \right) $$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** The conditional log-likelihood (over $t = 2, \dots, 100$):

$$ \ell(\phi = 0.7, \sigma^2 = 1) = -\frac{99}{2} \log(2\pi) - \frac{99}{2} \log(1) - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{t=2}^{100} (x_t - 0.7 x_{t-1})^2 $$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** To obtain the MLE in general, we maximize the log-likelihood over both $\phi$ and $\sigma^2$. Taking derivatives and setting to zero:

$$ \hat{\phi}_{MLE} = \frac{\sum_{t=2}^{n} x_t x_{t-1}}{\sum_{t=2}^{n} x_{t-1}^2} \quad \text{(coincides with LS)} $$

$$ \hat{\sigma}_{MLE}^2 = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{t=2}^{n} (x_t - \hat{\phi} x_{t-1})^2 $$

The numerical value of $\ell$ for a given observed sample is computed by plugging in the data and summing the squared residuals.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **Box-Jenkins methodology** for building an ARIMA model. List and briefly describe the three iterative stages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider three candidate models fitted to the same dataset of size $n = 200$:

| Model | $k$ (parameters) | $-2 \log L$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| AR$(1)$ | 2 | 580 |
| AR$(2)$ | 3 | 540 |
| ARMA$(1,1)$ | 3 | 545 |

Compute the **AIC** and **BIC** for each model and identify the best model. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a) — Box-Jenkins Methodology (7 Marks)

The **Box-Jenkins methodology** (1976) is the standard three-stage iterative procedure for building ARIMA$(p,d,q)$ models:

**Stage 1 — Identification (2 Marks):**
- Plot the series to detect trend, seasonality, and variance changes.
- Apply stationarity tests (ADF, KPSS) and unit-root analysis.
- If non-stationary, apply differencing (once or more) to obtain the stationary series and determine $d$.
- Examine the **ACF** and **PACF** of the stationary series to guess $p$ (PACF cutoff) and $q$ (ACF cutoff).

**Stage 2 — Estimation (2 Marks):**
- Use estimation methods (LS, Yule-Walker, MLE, Innovations Algorithm) to compute the parameters $\phi_i$, $\theta_j$, and $\sigma_Z^2$.
- The MLE via Innovations Algorithm is preferred for ARMA models.
- For AR processes, LS and Yule-Walker give closed-form solutions.

**Stage 3 — Diagnostic Checking (2 Marks):**
- Extract residuals $\hat{e}_t = x_t - \hat{x}_t$.
- Test residuals for white-noise behavior using the **Ljung-Box** $Q(m)$ statistic.
- Check residual normality via histograms and Q-Q plots.
- If residuals are not white noise, return to Stage 1 with adjusted $(p, d, q)$.
- Use **AIC** and **BIC** to compare multiple adequate models and select the most parsimonious.

**Iteration and Final Selection (1 Mark):** The cycle repeats until an adequate, parsimonious model is found, after which it is used for forecasting.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b) — AIC and BIC Comparison (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Recall the formulas:
- $AIC = -2 \log L + 2k$
- $BIC = -2 \log L + k \log n$, with $n = 200$, so $\log 200 \approx 5.298$.

**Step 2 (3 Marks):** Compute the values for each model.

For **AR$(1)$** ($k = 2$, $-2 \log L = 580$):
- $AIC = 580 + 2 \cdot 2 = 584$
- $BIC = 580 + 2 \cdot 5.298 = 580 + 10.596 = 590.596$

For **AR$(2)$** ($k = 3$, $-2 \log L = 540$):
- $AIC = 540 + 2 \cdot 3 = 546$
- $BIC = 540 + 3 \cdot 5.298 = 540 + 15.894 = 555.894$

For **ARMA$(1,1)$** ($k = 3$, $-2 \log L = 545$):
- $AIC = 545 + 2 \cdot 3 = 551$
- $BIC = 545 + 3 \cdot 5.298 = 545 + 15.894 = 560.894$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Tabulate and select the best.

| Model | $k$ | $-2 \log L$ | AIC | BIC |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| AR$(1)$ | 2 | 580 | 584 | 590.596 |
| AR$(2)$ | 3 | 540 | **546** | **555.894** |
| ARMA$(1,1)$ | 3 | 545 | 551 | 560.894 |

**Conclusion:** Both **AIC and BIC select AR$(2)$** as the best model. ARMA$(1,1)$ has slightly worse fit ($-2 \log L = 545$ vs $540$) with the same number of parameters, so it loses. AR$(1)$ is under-parameterized despite its simplicity. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here):**
> 1. **Forgetting the penalty term:** Many students compute only $-2 \log L$ and ignore the $2k$ term in AIC / $k \log n$ in BIC. *Always include the penalty.* **(−1 Mark)**
> 2. **Wrong MLE divisor:** LS estimator divides SSE by $n$ (or $n-p$); MLE divides by $n$ for unconditional likelihood. Mixing them up is a frequent error. **(−1 Mark)**
> 3. **Skipping the differencing step:** When the KTU question says "ARIMA", remember to first determine $d$ from stationarity testing; do not jump directly to ARMA. **(−2 Marks)**
> 4. **Confusing LS and Yule-Walker:** Both have closed forms for AR, but Yule-Walker uses sample autocorrelations while LS uses raw data. Cite both accurately.
> 5. **Omitting the Innovations Algorithm:** For ARMA MLE, do NOT just say "numerical optimization" — explicitly mention the Innovations Algorithm for partial credit. **(−1 Mark)**
> 6. **Forgetting to state the model selection rule:** Always close your answer with *"select the model with the smallest AIC/BIC"* — this one-liner is a guaranteed half-mark.

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Know for KTU 2024):**

- [ ] **Estimation** = computing parameter values; **Modelling** = the entire Box-Jenkins cycle.
- [ ] For AR$(p)$: **LS, YW, and MLE all give asymptotically equivalent estimates** but differ in finite-sample efficiency.
- [ ] **Yule-Walker** = sample autocorrelation-based; **LS** = data-based; **MLE** = likelihood-based.
- [ ] **Yule-Walker matrix** is **Toeplitz** (constant along diagonals) and **symmetric** — exploit this for computational efficiency.
- [ ] **White noise variance estimator** (YW): $\hat{\sigma}_Z^2 = \hat{\gamma}(0)\left(1 - \sum \hat{\phi}_i r(i)\right)$.
- [ ] **MLE Gaussian log-likelihood** for AR: $\ell = -\frac{n-p}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum (x_t - \sum \phi_i x_{t-i})^2$.
- [ ] **ARMA MLE** requires the **Innovations Algorithm** for tractability — no closed form.
- [ ] **AIC** $= -2 \log L + 2k$ and **BIC** $= -2 \log L + k \log n$; both **minimize** to select.
- [ ] **BIC penalty is heavier** than AIC for $n > 8$, so BIC favors simpler (more parsimonious) models.
- [ ] **Box-Jenkins stages:** Identification $\rightarrow$ Estimation $\rightarrow$ Diagnostic Checking. **Iterative**.
- [ ] **Ljung-Box statistic** $Q(m) \sim \chi^2_{(m - p - q)}$; reject model if $p$-value $< 0.05$.
- [ ] **Method of Least Squares** is the *conditional* MLE for Gaussian AR; for MA/ARMA it is only approximate.
- [ ] Always **check stationarity** (ADF test) **before** fitting ARMA — differencing may be required.
- [ ] **Sample autocovariance** $\hat{\gamma}(k) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x})$ — required input to Yule-Walker.
- [ ] **Parsimony principle:** Always prefer the simplest model that adequately describes the data.
- [ ] **Overfitting** is detectable by: (i) insignificance of added parameters, (ii) high AIC/BIC, (iii) residual autocorrelations that suddenly "disappear" (over-fitting to noise).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forecasting with ETS Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Forecasting with ETS Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the framework codified by **Hyndman & Athanasopoulos** (which is the prescribed reference for the KTU 2024 PECDT861 syllabus), the **ETS (Error–Trend–Seasonality) model** is a state-space formulation of exponential smoothing. It parameterizes a univariate time series using three structural components:

1. **Error (E):** The stochastic innovation component of the series (Additive **A** or Multiplicative **M**).
2. **Trend (T):** The long-term direction of the series (None **N**, Additive **A**, Additive Damped **A$_d$**, Multiplicative **M**, or Multiplicative Damped **M$_d$**).
3. **Seasonality (S):** The periodic fluctuation (None **N**, Additive **A**, or Multiplicative **M**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The ETS acronym is read as a triplet **E.T.S.**, and the chosen letters for each component uniquely identify one of the (theoretically) 30 possible exponential smoothing models. The most frequently tested variants in KTU examinations are **ETS(A,N,N)**, **ETS(A,A,N)**, **ETS(A,A,A)**, and **ETS(A,A,M)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Dashboard" Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a long highway:
- The **Error (E)** is the *road vibration* — small random bumps that the car's suspension filters out.
- The **Trend (T)** is the *throttle / accelerator* — whether the car is speeding up, slowing down, or holding steady.
- The **Seasonality (S)** is the *daily traffic pattern* — heavier congestion at 9 AM, lighter at 11 PM, repeating every 24 hours.

An **ETS model** is simply a mathematical "dashboard" that asks: *For this particular road (your dataset), is the vibration additive or multiplicative? Is the throttle constant, ramping, or damped? And is the traffic pattern additive or multiplicative?* Once the three switches are set, the model produces a forecast.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in **Bold**

- **Smoothing parameters:** $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \phi \in [0, 1]$
- **Seasonal period:** $m$ (e.g., $m = 12$ for monthly data with annual seasonality, $m = 4$ for quarterly data)
- **Level, Trend, and Seasonal state variables:** $\ell_t, b_t, s_t$
- **Innovation variance:** $\sigma^2$ (used in Maximum Likelihood Estimation)
- **Forecast horizon:** $h = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$

> [!NOTE]
> The smoothing parameters are **constrained to the closed interval [0, 1]** because they represent convex weights — a value of $\alpha = 1$ means "trust only the most recent observation," while $\alpha = 0$ means "ignore new data entirely and use the previous level."

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of ETS

The exponential smoothing family evolves progressively along three axes. Understanding this progression is the **single most important step** to mastering KTU Module 2.

### Step 1 — Simple Exponential Smoothing (ETS(A,N,N))

Used when the series has **neither trend nor seasonality**. The forecast is a weighted average of all past observations, with weights decaying exponentially.

$$
\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)\ell_{t-1}
$$

$$
\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t} = \ell_t
$$

### Step 2 — Holt's Linear Trend Method (ETS(A,A,N))

Introduces a **trend component** $b_t$ evolving in parallel with the level. Two smoothing parameters control the system.

$$
\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})
$$

$$
b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta)b_{t-1}
$$

$$
\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t} = \ell_t + h b_t
$$

### Step 3 — Damped Trend (ETS(A,A$_d$,N))

A damping factor $\phi \in [0,1]$ prevents the linear trend from extrapolating to infinity, which is mathematically necessary for long-horizon forecasts.

$$
\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + \phi b_{t-1})
$$

$$
b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta)\phi b_{t-1}
$$

$$
\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t} = \ell_t + \sum_{i=1}^{h} \phi^{i} b_t
$$

### Step 4 — Holt-Winters Additive Seasonality (ETS(A,A,A))

Adds a seasonal index $s_t$ of period $m$ that fluctuates additively around the deseasonalized level.

$$
\ell_t = \alpha(y_t - s_{t-m}) + (1 - \alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})
$$

$$
b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta)b_{t-1}
$$

$$
s_t = \gamma(y_t - \ell_t) + (1 - \gamma)s_{t-m}
$$

$$
\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t} = \ell_t + h b_t + s_{t + h - m \lfloor (h-1)/m \rfloor - m}
$$

(Equivalently, $s_{t+h-m(k+1)}$ where $k = \lfloor (h-1)/m \rfloor$.)

### Step 5 — Holt-Winters Multiplicative Seasonality (ETS(A,A,M))

Used when the **amplitude of seasonal swings grows with the level** of the series. The seasonal index multiplies the deseasonalized value.

$$
\ell_t = \alpha \left(\frac{y_t}{s_{t-m}}\right) + (1 - \alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})
$$

$$
b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta)b_{t-1}
$$

$$
s_t = \gamma \left(\frac{y_t}{\ell_t}\right) + (1 - \gamma)s_{t-m}
$$

$$
\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t} = (\ell_t + h b_t)\, s_{t+h-m(k+1)}
$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Model | Level Equation $\ell_t$ | Trend Equation $b_t$ | Seasonal Equation $s_t$ | Point Forecast $\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ETS(A,N,N)** | $\alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)\ell_{t-1}$ | — | — | $\ell_t$ |
| **ETS(A,A,N)** | $\alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1}+b_{t-1})$ | $\beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)b_{t-1}$ | — | $\ell_t + h b_t$ |
| **ETS(A,A$_d$,N)** | $\alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1}+\phi b_{t-1})$ | $\beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)\phi b_{t-1}$ | — | $\ell_t + \sum_{i=1}^{h}\phi^{i} b_t$ |
| **ETS(A,A,A)** | $\alpha(y_t - s_{t-m}) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1}+b_{t-1})$ | $\beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)b_{t-1}$ | $\gamma(y_t - \ell_t) + (1-\gamma)s_{t-m}$ | $\ell_t + h b_t + s_{t+h-m(k+1)}$ |
| **ETS(A,A,M)** | $\alpha(y_t / s_{t-m}) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1}+b_{t-1})$ | $\beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)b_{t-1}$ | $\gamma(y_t / \ell_t) + (1-\gamma)s_{t-m}$ | $(\ell_t + h b_t)s_{t+h-m(k+1)}$ |
| **ETS(M,A,N)** | $\alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1}+b_{t-1})$ | $\beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)b_{t-1}$ | — | $\ell_t + h b_t$ (errors multiplicative) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule for KTU Scripts:** The symbol $\lfloor x \rfloor$ denotes the **greatest integer less than or equal to $x$** (the floor function). When writing the seasonal index subscript $s_{t+h-m(k+1)}$, the term $k = \lfloor (h-1)/m \rfloor$ ensures the correct seasonal lag is selected for forecast horizon $h$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Retail Demand Forecasting:** ETS(A,A,M) for monthly sales of seasonal apparel where summer peaks grow proportionally with overall brand growth.
- **Energy Load Forecasting:** ETS(A,A,A) for daily electricity demand with weekly seasonality and a slow growth trend.
- **Network Traffic Engineering:** ETS(M,A,N) for bandwidth utilization when the *percentage* growth in noise is more meaningful than absolute growth.
- **Inventory Management:** Damped trend models (ETS(A,A$_d$,N)) for slow-moving SKUs where demand is reaching saturation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Simple Exponential Smoothing as an ARIMA(0,1,1) Equivalent

This is a high-yield derivation for KTU Module 2, as it bridges the exponential smoothing family to ARIMA — another Module 2 topic.

**Step 1 — Write the recursion.**

$$
\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)\ell_{t-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the previous recursion by repeatedly unrolling.**

$$
\ell_{t-1} = \alpha y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)\ell_{t-2}
$$

**Step 3 — Insert into Step 1 and continue unrolling to infinity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell_t &= \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)\bigl[\alpha y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)\ell_{t-2}\bigr] \\
\ell_t &= \alpha y_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)^2 \ell_{t-2} \\
\ell_t &= \alpha \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} (1-\alpha)^{j} y_{t-j} + (1-\alpha)^{k} \ell_{t-k}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $k \to \infty$.** Since $0 \leq 1-\alpha < 1$, the term $(1-\alpha)^{k} \to 0$, and the weights $\alpha(1-\alpha)^{j}$ form a **geometric series** that sums to $1$:

$$
\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \alpha(1-\alpha)^{j} = \frac{\alpha}{1-(1-\alpha)} = 1
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude that $\ell_t$ is a weighted average of *all* past $y$ values with exponentially decaying weights.**

**Step 6 — Convert to an ARIMA form.** Define the one-step-ahead forecast error $e_t = y_t - \ell_{t-1}$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_t &= \ell_{t-1} + e_t \\
y_t - y_{t-1} &= \ell_{t-1} + e_t - [\ell_{t-2} + e_{t-1}] \\
y_t &= y_{t-1} + (\ell_{t-1} - \ell_{t-2}) + (e_t - e_{t-1}) \\
y_t &= y_{t-1} + \alpha e_{t-1} + (e_t - e_{t-1}) \\
y_t &= y_{t-1} + e_t - (1-\alpha) e_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is precisely the **ARIMA(0,1,1)** model with $\theta_1 = 1-\alpha$, confirming the classical equivalence.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **favourite 14-mark question** in KTU 2024 scheme exams. The examiner expects you to show all five substitution steps explicitly.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection Rules for Choosing an ETS Variant

The following decision matrix is the **canonical KTU reference** for selecting the appropriate ETS model.

| Series Property | Recommended Trend | Recommended Seasonality | Full ETS Tag |
|---|---|---|---|
| No trend, no seasonality | N | N | **ETS(A,N,N)** |
| Linear trend, no seasonality | A | N | **ETS(A,A,N)** |
| Saturating growth, no seasonality | A$_d$ | N | **ETS(A,A$_d$,N)** |
| Linear trend, constant seasonal amplitude | A | A | **ETS(A,A,A)** |
| Linear trend, growing seasonal amplitude | A | M | **ETS(A,A,M)** |
| Multiplicative error structure (proportional noise) | A | N | **ETS(M,A,N)** |
| Exponential growth, no seasonality | M | N | **ETS(M,M,N)** |
| Exponential growth, growing seasonality | M | M | **ETS(M,M,M)** |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — ETS(A,N,N) Initialization

**Given:** A 5-observation series $y_1, y_2, y_3, y_4, y_5 = 10, 12, 13, 11, 14$ and $\alpha = 0.3$.

**Initialization:** $\ell_1 = y_1 = 10$.

**Step-by-step recursion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell_2 &= 0.3(12) + 0.7(10) = 3.6 + 7.0 = 10.6 \\
\ell_3 &= 0.3(13) + 0.7(10.6) = 3.9 + 7.42 = 11.32 \\
\ell_4 &= 0.3(11) + 0.7(11.32) = 3.3 + 7.924 = 11.224 \\
\ell_5 &= 0.3(14) + 0.7(11.224) = 4.2 + 7.8568 = 12.0568
\end{aligned}
$$

**One-step-ahead forecast for $h=1$:** $\hat{y}_{6 \vert 5} = \ell_5 = 12.0568$.

**Two-step-ahead forecast for $h=2$:** For ETS(A,N,N), the forecast is *constant* across all horizons:

$$
\hat{y}_{6+h \vert 5} = \ell_5 = 12.0568, \quad \forall h \geq 1
$$

This **flat-line behaviour** is the defining weakness of SES — it cannot capture trend, which is exactly why Holt extended it.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics


## 4.2 Information Flow Within a Single ETS Iteration

```mermaid
graph LR
    obsA["Observation y sub t"]
    stateL["Level l sub t"]
    stateB["Trend b sub t"]
    stateS["Seasonal s sub t"]

    err["Innovation e sub t"]
    obsA -- additive correction --> stateL
    obsA -- additive correction --> stateS
    stateL -- previous level plus trend --> stateL
    stateB -- difference of levels --> stateB
    stateS -- lagged seasonal --> stateS
    stateL --> fcst
    stateB --> fcst
    stateS --> fcst
    err -- shocks the level --> stateL
    err -- shocks the trend --> stateB
    err -- shocks the seasonal --> stateS
```

## 4.3 Decision Block Diagram — Choosing the Correct ETS Variant

```mermaid

    out3["ETS A A sub d N"]
```
