    q1 -- Yes --> q3
    q2 -- No --> q4
    q2 -- Yes --> out4
    q3 -- Yes --> q4
    q3 -- No --> out5
    q4 -- No --> out2
    q4 -- Yes --> out3
    q2 -- No and additive error --> out1
    q1 -- Yes and multiplicative error --> out6
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above use **alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters** (e.g., `nodeRoot`, `stateL`) and **plain uppercase text labels** — this complies with the Mermaid compilation safeguard rules.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the ETS framework and name the two possible error structures. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **ETS (Error–Trend–Seasonality) framework** is a systematic classification of exponential smoothing methods proposed by Hyndman et al. (2002). It characterizes a univariate time series using three structural components: **Error (E)**, **Trend (T)**, and **Seasonality (S)**. Each component can take one of several possible forms.

The **two possible error structures** are:

1. **Additive error (A):** The random shocks enter the model additively, so the one-step-ahead forecast errors $\varepsilon_t$ are independent and identically distributed with mean zero and constant variance $\sigma^2$. The observation equation is $y_t = \ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + s_{t-m} + \varepsilon_t$.

2. **Multiplicative error (M):** The random shocks are proportional to the level of the series, so the observation equation becomes $y_t = (\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + s_{t-m}) \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_t)$. This is appropriate when the variability of the data grows with the mean.

### Question 2: State any three differences between additive and multiplicative seasonality in the ETS framework. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Additive Seasonality | Multiplicative Seasonality |
|---|---|---|
| Seasonal equation | $s_t = \gamma(y_t - \ell_t) + (1-\gamma)s_{t-m}$ | $s_t = \gamma(y_t / \ell_t) + (1-\gamma)s_{t-m}$ |
| Amplitude behaviour | Constant across all levels of the series | Amplitude grows with the level of the series |
| Forecast equation | $\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h b_t + s_{t+h-m(k+1)}$ | $\hat{y}_{t+h} = (\ell_t + h b_t) s_{t+h-m(k+1)}$ |
| Data requirement | Series with zero or near-zero values acceptable | Series must be strictly positive |
| Forecast variance | Constant across horizon | Grows with horizon and level |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Module 2** regulations, students are expected to attempt **one full 14-mark question**, which is internally divided into two 7-mark sub-parts. The two alternatives below represent the actual "either/or" choice pattern.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) Derive the equivalence between Simple Exponential Smoothing and ARIMA(0,1,1). State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the SES recursion.**

$$
\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)\ell_{t-1}, \quad \hat{y}_{t+1 \vert t} = \ell_t
$$

**Step 2 — Define the one-step forecast error.** Let $\varepsilon_t = y_t - \ell_{t-1}$. Then $\ell_{t-1} = y_t - \varepsilon_t$.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the SES recursion.**

$$
\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(y_t - \varepsilon_t) = y_t - (1-\alpha)\varepsilon_t
$$

**Step 4 — Express $y_{t+1} - y_t$ in terms of the errors.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{t+1} - y_t &= \ell_t + \varepsilon_{t+1} - y_t \\
&= y_t - (1-\alpha)\varepsilon_t + \varepsilon_{t+1} - y_t \\
&= \varepsilon_{t+1} - (1-\alpha)\varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the ARIMA(0,1,1) form.** The general ARIMA(0,1,1) model is:

$$
y_t = y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t - \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}
$$

Matching, we identify $\theta_1 = 1 - \alpha$.

**Step 6 — State the assumptions:**

- $\{\varepsilon_t\}$ is a white noise sequence with mean $0$ and variance $\sigma^2$.
- The smoothing parameter $\alpha$ is fixed in $[0, 1]$.
- The level $\ell_t$ is *initialized* as $\ell_0 = y_1$ (or some other reasonable choice).
- The errors are **independent** of past observations.

Hence, **SES is mathematically equivalent to ARIMA(0,1,1) with $\theta = 1 - \alpha$**. **(7 marks)**

**(b) A retail company records the following monthly sales (in lakhs ₹) for 5 months: 50, 52, 54, 53, 55. Using Simple Exponential Smoothing with $\alpha = 0.4$, compute the level estimates and forecast the value for month 6. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initialize** $\ell_1 = y_1 = 50$.

**Step 2 — Iterate the recursion** $\ell_t = 0.4 y_t + 0.6 \ell_{t-1}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell_2 &= 0.4(52) + 0.6(50) = 20.8 + 30.0 = 50.8 \\
\ell_3 &= 0.4(54) + 0.6(50.8) = 21.6 + 30.48 = 52.08 \\
\ell_4 &= 0.4(53) + 0.6(52.08) = 21.2 + 31.248 = 52.448 \\
\ell_5 &= 0.4(55) + 0.6(52.448) = 22.0 + 31.4688 = 53.4688
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Forecast for month 6:**

$$
\hat{y}_{6 \vert 5} = \ell_5 = 53.4688 \text{ lakhs}
$$

**Step 4 — All future forecasts (any $h$):**

$$
\hat{y}_{6+h \vert 5} = 53.4688, \quad \forall h \geq 1
$$

**(7 marks — full marks if the recursion and forecast constant are explicitly shown)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the Holt-Winters multiplicative method. Write down the model equations and the forecast equation. When is this method preferred over the additive version? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Holt-Winters multiplicative method (ETS(A,A,M))** is an extension of Holt's linear trend method that incorporates a **multiplicative seasonal component** of period $m$.

**Model Equations:**

$$
\ell_t = \alpha \left(\frac{y_t}{s_{t-m}}\right) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})
$$

$$
b_t = \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)b_{t-1}
$$

$$
s_t = \gamma \left(\frac{y_t}{\ell_t}\right) + (1-\gamma)s_{t-m}
$$

**Forecast Equation:**

$$
\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t} = (\ell_t + h b_t) \cdot s_{t+h-m(k+1)}
$$

where $k = \lfloor (h-1)/m \rfloor$.

**Component Roles:**

- $\ell_t$ is the deseasonalized **level** at time $t$.
- $b_t$ is the **trend slope** at time $t$.
- $s_t$ is the **seasonal index** at time $t$, normalized around $1$ in the multiplicative case.
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma \in [0, 1]$ are the smoothing parameters for level, trend, and seasonality respectively.

**When to prefer the multiplicative version:**

The multiplicative seasonality model is preferred when:

1. The **amplitude of seasonal variations is proportional to the level of the series** — i.e., the seasonal swings grow as the series grows. Example: monthly ice-cream sales that peak higher every summer as the brand expands.
2. The data are **strictly positive** (a necessary condition for multiplicative decomposition).
3. Box-Cox transformation cannot be applied easily, or interpretability of raw seasonal multipliers is desired.

In contrast, the additive version (ETS(A,A,A)) is used when seasonal swings are of approximately **constant magnitude** regardless of the series level. **(7 marks)**

**(b) For a quarterly time series with period $m = 4$, the level $\ell_4 = 120$, trend $b_4 = 5$, and the four most recent seasonal indices are $s_1 = 0.8, s_2 = 1.0, s_3 = 1.2, s_4 = 1.0$. Using ETS(A,A,M), compute forecasts for the next 8 quarters (i.e., $h = 1$ to $8$). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the forecast equation:**

$$
\hat{y}_{4+h \vert 4} = (\ell_4 + h b_4) \cdot s_{4+h-m(k+1)}
$$

where $k = \lfloor (h-1)/4 \rfloor$.

**Step 2 — Tabulate $k$ and the seasonal index for each $h$.**

| $h$ | $h-1$ | $\lfloor (h-1)/4 \rfloor = k$ | Seasonal index $s_{4+h-4(k+1)}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | $s_{4+1-4(1)} = s_1 = 0.8$ |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | $s_{4+2-4(1)} = s_2 = 1.0$ |
| 3 | 2 | 0 | $s_{4+3-4(1)} = s_3 = 1.2$ |
| 4 | 3 | 0 | $s_{4+4-4(1)} = s_4 = 1.0$ |
| 5 | 4 | 1 | $s_{4+5-8} = s_1 = 0.8$ |
| 6 | 5 | 1 | $s_2 = 1.0$ |
| 7 | 6 | 1 | $s_3 = 1.2$ |
| 8 | 7 | 1 | $s_4 = 1.0$ |

**Step 3 — Apply the forecast equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{5 \vert 4} &= (120 + 1 \cdot 5)(0.8) = 125 \times 0.8 = 100.0 \\
\hat{y}_{6 \vert 4} &= (120 + 2 \cdot 5)(1.0) = 130 \times 1.0 = 130.0 \\
\hat{y}_{7 \vert 4} &= (120 + 3 \cdot 5)(1.2) = 135 \times 1.2 = 162.0 \\
\hat{y}_{8 \vert 4} &= (120 + 4 \cdot 5)(1.0) = 140 \times 1.0 = 140.0 \\
\hat{y}_{9 \vert 4} &= (120 + 5 \cdot 5)(0.8) = 145 \times 0.8 = 116.0 \\
\hat{y}_{10 \vert 4} &= (120 + 6 \cdot 5)(1.0) = 150 \times 1.0 = 150.0 \\
\hat{y}_{11 \vert 4} &= (120 + 7 \cdot 5)(1.2) = 155 \times 1.2 = 186.0 \\
\hat{y}_{12 \vert 4} &= (120 + 8 \cdot 5)(1.0) = 160 \times 1.0 = 160.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** The forecast values illustrate the hallmark behaviour of **ETS(A,A,M)** — the deseasonalized trend grows linearly (125, 130, 135, 140, …) and is then modulated by the seasonal indices, producing a *repeating* seasonal envelope of constant width 0.8, 1.0, 1.2, 1.0. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in ETS Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the floor function** $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ when computing the seasonal index for $h > m$. Many students incorrectly assume the seasonal cycle restarts at $h = m+1$ without using $k = \lfloor (h-1)/m \rfloor$. This *alone* costs up to **2 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Initializing $\ell_0$ and $b_0$ incorrectly.** For SES, $\ell_1 = y_1$ is acceptable. For Holt's method, a common correct initialization is $\ell_1 = y_1$ and $b_1 = y_2 - y_1$. State the initialization explicitly in your answer.
> 3. **Confusing the model name.** ETS(A,N,N) means **Additive error, No trend, No seasonality**, NOT "additive, none, none" interpreted as additive trend. The triplet order is **Error–Trend–Seasonality**, not Trend–Seasonality–Error.
> 4. **Multiplicative seasonality on negative data.** You will lose full marks if you apply ETS(A,A,M) to a series with zeros or negative values. State the positivity assumption explicitly.
> 5. **Skipping the step-by-step recursion.** Examiners allocate marks for *each iteration step* in numerical problems. A single-line "answer = 53.47" without the four intermediate $\ell_t$ values will earn at most **1 mark** out of 7.
> 6. **Mixing additive and multiplicative forms.** A model cannot have additive trend with multiplicative seasonal index *and* additive error — be consistent across all three components.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ETS = Error–Trend–Seasonality** is the canonical classification of exponential smoothing methods.
- The triplet **E.T.S.** takes values from {A, M} × {N, A, A$_d$, M, M$_d$} × {N, A, M}, giving up to 30 models (some are statistically inadmissible and excluded).
- The **smoothing parameters** $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \phi$ are bounded in $[0, 1]$ and are estimated by **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** in the innovation state-space form.
- **SES (ETS(A,N,N))** is mathematically equivalent to **ARIMA(0,1,1)** with $\theta_1 = 1 - \alpha$.
- **Holt's linear method (ETS(A,A,N))** introduces a trend state $b_t$ and forecasts $\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h b_t$ — the forecast grows **linearly** with horizon $h$.
- **Damped trend (ETS(A,A$_d$,N))** uses a damping factor $\phi$ to make long-horizon forecasts flatten out, giving the **finite-sum forecast** $\ell_t + (\phi + \phi^2 + \cdots + \phi^h) b_t$.
- **Holt-Winters Additive (ETS(A,A,A))** uses $y_t - s_{t-m}$ to deseasonalize; the seasonal index is **additive**.
- **Holt-Winters Multiplicative (ETS(A,A,M))** uses $y_t / s_{t-m}$; the seasonal index is **multiplicative** and requires strictly positive data.
- The seasonal index for forecast horizon $h$ is given by $s_{t+h-m(k+1)}$ where $k = \lfloor (h-1)/m \rfloor$.
- **Multiplicative error (M)** models proportional noise: the variance grows with the level of the series.
- For **long-horizon forecasts**, damped trend models are strongly preferred because pure linear extrapolation is rarely realistic.
- Model selection is typically performed using **AIC, AIC$_c$, or BIC** on the in-sample fit; out-of-sample validation uses **cross-validation** on a rolling-origin forecast origin.
- Always **state the initialization, smoothing parameters, and assumptions** when presenting an ETS model in the KTU exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression Extension Techniques for Time Series Data - Types of Stationary Behaviour in Time Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regression Extension Techniques for Time Series Data - Types of Stationary Behaviour in Time Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Regression Extension Techniques for Time Series Data

A standard ordinary least squares (OLS) regression assumes that the regressors are *fixed* and that the error term satisfies classical assumptions. In time series settings, however, regressors are themselves random, autocorrelated, and frequently non‑stationary. **Regression Extension Techniques** are a family of statistical procedures developed to handle these realities — they relax the strict exogeneity assumption, accommodate lagged dependent variables, allow for non‑stationary regressors, and explicitly model serial dependence in the residuals.

The principal members of this family that KTU 2024 emphasises are:

| Technique | Full Name | Core Idea |
|---|---|---|
| AR(p) | Autoregressive Model | Regress $Y_t$ on its own past |
| MA(q) | Moving Average Model | Regress $Y_t$ on past white-noise shocks |
| ARMA(p,q) | Mixed Autoregressive Moving Average | Combine AR and MA structures |
| ARIMA(p,d,q) | Integrated form | Differences the series to achieve stationarity |
| ARDL(p,q) | Autoregressive Distributed Lag | Regress $Y_t$ on its own lags + current/lagged $X_t$ |
| ECM | Error Correction Model | Captures short-run dynamics + long-run equilibrium |
| VAR / VECM | Vector AutoRegression / VECM | Multivariate extension for several stationary / co‑integrated series |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why extend plain regression?**  
> Because ignoring serial correlation or non‑stationarity in the regressors leads to:
> (i) **Biased** and **inconsistent** OLS estimates,  
> (ii) **Invalid** standard errors, t‑statistics and confidence intervals, and  
> (iii) **Spurious regression** in the worst case (very high $R^2$ with nonsense coefficients — Yule 1926; Granger & Newbold 1974).

## 1.2 Types of Stationary Behaviour in Time Series

A stochastic process $\{Y_t\}$ is said to be **stationary** in some sense if its probabilistic / moment structure does not change with time. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires mastery of the following *hierarchy* of stationarity concepts.

### 1.2.1 Strict Stationarity (Strong Stationarity)
A process $\{Y_t\}$ is **strictly stationary** if, for *every* $k \in \mathbb{N}$, every set of time points $(t_1, t_2, \dots, t_k)$ and every integer shift $h$,

$$F_{Y_{t_1}, \dots, Y_{t_k}}(y_1, \dots, y_k) \;=\; F_{Y_{t_1+h}, \dots, Y_{t_k+h}}(y_1, \dots, y_k)$$

i.e. the **entire joint distribution** is time‑invariant. In particular, the *median*, *quantiles*, *moments of all orders* and *dependence structure* are all preserved under a shift of the time origin.

> [!NOTE]
> Strict stationarity is a very strong requirement — the series is not allowed to change *any* distributional aspect over time. Pure white noise $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{iid}(0,\sigma^2)$ is a textbook example.

### 1.2.2 Weak / Wide‑Sense / Second‑Order Stationarity (WSS)
A process is **weakly stationary** (or **wide‑sense stationary**, WSS) if the following *three* finite‑moment conditions hold:

1. The mean is constant:  
   $$\mu(t) = E(Y_t) = \mu \quad \forall t$$
2. The variance is finite and constant:  
   $$\text{Var}(Y_t) = E[(Y_t - \mu)^2] = \gamma(0) \quad \forall t$$
3. The autocovariance depends only on the lag, not on $t$:  
   $$\gamma(t, t+h) = E[(Y_t - \mu)(Y_{t+h} - \mu)] = \gamma(h) \quad \forall t,\; h$$

The corresponding autocorrelation function is

$$\rho(h) = \dfrac{\gamma(h)}{\gamma(0)} \quad \text{with} \quad \rho(0)=1,\; \vert\rho(h)\vert \le 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict $\Rightarrow$ Weak** (with finite second moments), but **Weak $\not\Rightarrow$ Strict**. The MA(1) process with *uniform* shocks is WSS but the marginal distribution is non‑normal and time‑invariant — both can hold simultaneously; in general, however, WSS is the working assumption in econometrics and engineering signal processing.

### 1.2.3 Trend Stationarity
A process $\{Y_t\}$ is **trend stationary (TS)** if it can be written as a *deterministic* trend plus a stationary disturbance:

$$Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + u_t \quad \text{with} \quad u_t \sim \text{stationary}$$

**Key properties:**
- The mean is time‑varying: $E(Y_t) = \alpha + \beta t$, but it follows a *fixed* deterministic function.
- A single realisation is *mean‑reverting* to the trend line; shocks have only a *transient* effect.
- The autocorrelation function $\rho(h)$ is **identical** to that of $u_t$ — it does *not* depend on $t$.

### 1.2.4 Difference Stationarity (Unit‑Root / DS Processes)
A process is **difference stationary (DS)** if it becomes stationary only after applying the **first‑difference operator** $\Delta Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$ a *finite* number of times. The canonical example is the **Random Walk**:

$$Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma^2)$$

For a Random Walk with $Y_0 = 0$:
$$Y_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_i \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Var}(Y_t) = t\sigma^2 \to \infty$$

**Key properties:**
- The unconditional mean is constant ($E(Y_t)=0$ or $E(Y_t)=Y_0$), but the *variance* is **time‑dependent** — a hallmark of non‑stationarity.
- Shocks have a *permanent* effect on the level of the series (innovations never die out).
- The first‑difference $\Delta Y_t = \varepsilon_t$ is white noise, hence WSS.

### 1.2.5 Stationary in a Non‑Linear Sense
Some non‑linear time series are stationary *as a process* even though their marginal moments do not exist. Examples are ARCH/GARCH conditional‑variance processes, bilinear models and certain chaotic deterministic systems. The KTU syllabus refers to these as **conditionally stationary** processes.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

> **Stationarity is the property that the statistical "rules of the game" do not change with time.**

Think of a **calm lake** versus a **flooding river**:
- A **strictly stationary** series is a perfectly calm lake — at *any* time of day the water surface looks the same statistically: same mean level, same wave heights, same ripple pattern.
- A **weakly stationary** series only promises that the *average* level and the *typical* wave height stay the same — the underlying chemistry (e.g. shape of waves) could shift, but the first two moments are frozen.
- A **trend‑stationary** series is a lake whose water level rises by **exactly 1 cm every year** (deterministic trend) and around which small ripples are stationary.
- A **difference‑stationary** series is a **leaking bucket**: every disturbance to the water level is *remembered forever*. Even a tiny splash raises the level permanently; to make it stationary, you must compare the *changes* in level — i.e. **difference** the data.

## 1.4 Why Does Stationarity Matter for the Regression Extensions?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The famous **Granger–Newbold (1974) result** showed that running OLS on two independent non‑stationary (DS) series can produce $R^2 > 0.9$ with completely meaningless coefficients — the *"spurious regression"* problem. Hence the *Box–Jenkins* (1976) workflow:
> 1. Identify → 2. Estimate → 3. Diagnose → 4. Forecast  
> and the cornerstone of the *Identify* stage is testing and **enforcing stationarity** before fitting any ARMA/ARIMA/ARDL model.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High‑Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Organisation of Stationarity Concepts

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects the student to be able to **order** the stationarity types from *weakest* to *strongest* and to **recognise** the diagnostic signature of each. The hierarchy is:

$$\boxed{\;\text{Strict Stationarity} \;\Longleftarrow\; \text{Weak Stationarity} \;\Longleftarrow\; \text{Trend / Difference Stationarity} \;\Longleftarrow\; \text{Generic Non‑Stationarity}\;}$$

*Strict stationarity (with finite second moments) implies weak stationarity, but not vice‑versa.*

## 2.2 The Backbone Operators

| Symbol | Operator | Action on $\{Y_t\}$ |
|---|---|---|
| $L$ | **Lag** | $L Y_t = Y_{t-1}$ |
| $L^h$ | Lag by $h$ | $L^h Y_t = Y_{t-h}$ |
| $\Delta = 1 - L$ | **Difference** | $\Delta Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$ |
| $\Delta^d$ | $d$‑fold difference | $\Delta^d Y_t$ |
| $B$ | Backshift (alt. notation) | $B Y_t = Y_{t-1}$ |

## 2.3 Regression Extension Models — Decomposition & Rationale

### 2.3.1 Autoregressive (AR) Model of order $p$
$$Y_t = c + \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \phi_2 Y_{t-2} + \dots + \phi_p Y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t,\quad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma^2)$$

Written with the lag operator:

$$\Phi(L)\,Y_t = c + \varepsilon_t,\quad \Phi(L)=1-\phi_1 L - \phi_2 L^2 - \dots - \phi_p L^p$$

*Stationarity condition:* all roots of $\Phi(z)=0$ lie **outside** the unit circle ($\vert z_i \vert > 1$).

### 2.3.2 Moving Average (MA) Model of order $q$
$$Y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \varepsilon_{t-2} + \dots + \theta_q \varepsilon_{t-q}$$

*Always weakly stationary* provided the variance of the white noise is finite — the moving‑average structure has a finite memory and produces a finite variance.

### 2.3.3 ARMA(p, q) Model
$$\Phi(L)\,Y_t = c + \Theta(L)\,\varepsilon_t$$

*Stationarity* is determined entirely by $\Phi(L)$; *invertibility* by $\Theta(L)$.

### 2.3.4 ARIMA(p, d, q) Model
$$\Phi(L)\,\Delta^d Y_t = c + \Theta(L)\,\varepsilon_t$$

Here $d$ is the **order of differencing** required to make $Y_t$ stationary. The Box–Jenkins practical heuristic:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Seasonal or trending series $\Rightarrow$ difference.
> - Variance changes with mean $\Rightarrow$ take a logarithmic / Box‑Cox transform **before** differencing.

### 2.3.5 Autoregressive Distributed Lag — ARDL(p, q)
$$Y_t = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \alpha_i Y_{t-i} + \sum_{j=0}^{q} \beta_j X_{t-j} + u_t$$

**Why this is a *regression extension*:** the regression of $Y$ on $X$ is extended to include (i) lagged values of the dependent variable and (ii) lagged values of the independent variable(s). It is the bridge between single‑equation time‑series regression and the full information VAR approach of Sims (1980).

### 2.3.6 Error Correction Model (ECM)
If $Y_t$ and $X_t$ are cointegrated of order (1,1), the ARDL collapses to:

$$\Delta Y_t = \phi_0 + \phi_1 \Delta X_t - \lambda\bigl(Y_{t-1} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_{t-1}\bigr) + v_t$$

* $\lambda$ is the **error‑correction speed**; $-1 < \lambda < 0$ for stability.
* Long‑run equilibrium: $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$.
* Short‑run dynamics: $\phi_1 \Delta X_t$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory)

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities below are testable in the **University End‑Semester Examination (ESE)** and the **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)**.

| # | Concept | Formula | Domain / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Strict stationarity | $F_{Y_{t_1},\dots,Y_{t_k}} = F_{Y_{t_1+h},\dots,Y_{t_k+h}}$ | $\forall k, h$ |
| 2 | Weak stationarity mean | $E(Y_t) = \mu$ | constant |
| 3 | Weak stationarity autocovariance | $\gamma(t,t+h) = \gamma(h)$ | lag‑dependent only |
| 4 | Autocorrelation | $\rho(h) = \gamma(h) / \gamma(0)$ | $\vert \rho(h) \vert \le 1$ |
| 5 | Lag operator | $L Y_t = Y_{t-1}$ | — |
| 6 | First difference | $\Delta Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1} = (1-L)Y_t$ | — |
| 7 | Trend‑stationary | $Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + u_t$ | $u_t$ WSS |
| 8 | Random walk (DS) | $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | $\varepsilon_t$ WN |
| 9 | Variance of RW | $\text{Var}(Y_t) = t\sigma^2$ | $\to \infty$ |
| 10 | AR(p) | $\Phi(L) Y_t = c + \varepsilon_t$ | $\vert z_i \vert > 1$ for $\Phi(z_i)=0$ |
| 11 | MA(q) | $Y_t = \mu + \Theta(L)\varepsilon_t$ | always WSS if $\sigma^2 < \infty$ |
| 12 | ARMA(p,q) | $\Phi(L)Y_t = c + \Theta(L)\varepsilon_t$ | — |
| 13 | ARIMA(p,d,q) | $\Phi(L)\Delta^d Y_t = c + \Theta(L)\varepsilon_t$ | $d \ge 0$ integer |
| 14 | ARDL(p,q) | $Y_t = \alpha_0 + \sum \alpha_i Y_{t-i} + \sum \beta_j X_{t-j} + u_t$ | OVB‑free |
| 15 | ECM speed | $\Delta Y_t = \phi_0 + \phi_1 \Delta X_t - \lambda(Y_{t-1} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_{t-1}) + v_t$ | $-1 < \lambda < 0$ |
| 16 | Yule–Walker (AR1) | $\rho(1) = \phi_1,\;\rho(h) = \phi_1^h$ | $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$ |

**Engineering Real‑World Utility**

* **Signal processing** — verifying that the spectral density of a process exists (WSS is the *sine‑qua‑non* for power‑spectral analysis).
* **Structural health monitoring** — vibration sensors are *trend* + *stationary* residual in a fault‑free regime; failure onset injects a *unit‑root* signature.
* **Forecasting & econometrics** — differencing converts DS series to WSS, enabling ARMA on the differenced series (ARIMA).
* **Control systems** — discrete‑time controllers are usually analysed assuming WSS disturbances.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step‑by‑Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section delivers the rigorous mathematical derivations that examiners love to ask. **Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.**

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean and Variance of a Random Walk

**Statement.** If $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ with $Y_0 = 0$ and $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma^2)$, prove that $E(Y_t)=0$ and $\text{Var}(Y_t)=t\sigma^2$.

**Step 1 — Unroll the recursion.**

$$Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t = Y_{t-2} + \varepsilon_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t = \dots = Y_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{t}\varepsilon_i$$

**Step 2 — Apply the linear operator of expectation.**

$$E(Y_t) = E\!\left(Y_0\right) + \sum_{i=1}^{t} E(\varepsilon_i)$$

Using $E(\varepsilon_i)=0$ for all $i$ and $E(Y_0)=0$:

$$E(Y_t) = 0 + 0 = 0 \quad\blacksquare$$

**Step 3 — Compute the variance.** Since $Y_0$ is constant and the $\varepsilon_i$ are *mutually independent* with variance $\sigma^2$:

$$\text{Var}(Y_t) = \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{t}\varepsilon_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = t\sigma^2 \quad\blacksquare$$

**Conclusion.** The variance is a *linear, unbounded* function of $t$, which directly violates the WSS requirement $\text{Var}(Y_t) = \text{constant}$. Hence the Random Walk is **not** weakly stationary, although its *first difference* $\Delta Y_t = \varepsilon_t$ is trivially WSS.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Autocovariance Function of an AR(1) Process

**Statement.** For $Y_t = c + \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ with $\vert\phi_1\vert < 1$ and $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma^2)$, derive $\gamma(h)$.

**Step 1 — Long‑run (unconditional) mean.**

Iterating the recursion and using $\vert\phi_1\vert<1$:

$$E(Y_t) = \frac{c}{1-\phi_1}\equiv \mu$$

**Step 2 — Centred representation.** Define $Z_t = Y_t - \mu$. Then

$$Z_t = \phi_1 Z_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 3 — Unconditional variance ($h=0$).** Multiply by $Z_t$ and take expectations:

$$E(Z_t^2) = \phi_1^2 E(Z_{t-1}^2) + \sigma^2$$

Stationarity of $Z_t$ requires $E(Z_t^2)=E(Z_{t-1}^2)\equiv \gamma(0)$:

$$\gamma(0) = \phi_1^2 \gamma(0) + \sigma^2 \;\Rightarrow\; \gamma(0) = \frac{\sigma^2}{1-\phi_1^2}$$

**Step 4 — Lag‑1 autocovariance.** Multiply the centred recursion by $Z_{t-1}$ and take expectations:

$$E(Z_t Z_{t-1}) = \phi_1 E(Z_{t-1}^2) + E(\varepsilon_t Z_{t-1})$$

The cross‑term vanishes because $\varepsilon_t$ is white noise independent of past $Z$s:

$$\gamma(1) = \phi_1 \gamma(0) = \frac{\phi_1 \sigma^2}{1-\phi_1^2}$$

**Step 5 — General lag $h$.** Multiply by $Z_{t-h}$ and iterate the recursion repeatedly:

$$\gamma(h) = \phi_1 \gamma(h-1) = \phi_1^2 \gamma(h-2) = \dots = \phi_1^h \gamma(0)$$

Hence the **Yule–Walker** formula for AR(1):

$$\boxed{\;\gamma(h) = \dfrac{\phi_1^h \sigma^2}{1-\phi_1^2}\;,\qquad \rho(h)=\phi_1^h\;}$$

**Step 6 — Verification of WSS.** The mean $\mu$ is constant; the variance $\gamma(0)=\sigma^2/(1-\phi_1^2)$ is finite when $\vert\phi_1\vert<1$; and the autocovariance depends only on $h$. Thus the AR(1) process is **weakly stationary** iff $\vert\phi_1\vert<1$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Autocovariance of a Moving‑Average MA(1) Process

**Statement.** For $Y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}$, compute $\gamma(0),\gamma(1),\gamma(h)$.

**Step 1 — Centred process.** $Z_t = Y_t - \mu = \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}$.

**Step 2 — Lag‑0 variance.** Using independence $E(\varepsilon_t \varepsilon_{t-1})=0$:

$$\gamma(0) = E(Z_t^2) = E(\varepsilon_t^2) + \theta_1^2 E(\varepsilon_{t-1}^2) = (1+\theta_1^2)\sigma^2$$

**Step 3 — Lag‑1 covariance.**

$$\gamma(1) = E(Z_t Z_{t-1}) = E\bigl[(\varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1})(\varepsilon_{t-1} + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-2})\bigr]$$

Expanding and using the white‑noise property (only the cross‑term $\theta_1 E(\varepsilon_{t-1}^2)$ survives):

$$\gamma(1) = \theta_1 \sigma^2$$

**Step 4 — Higher‑order lags.** All terms involve at least one $\varepsilon_t$ paired with a future white noise or with a *two‑step* removed white noise — both have zero expectation:

$$\gamma(h) = 0,\quad h \ge 2$$

**Final piecewise autocovariance:**

$$\gamma(h)=\begin{cases} (1+\theta_1^2)\sigma^2 & h=0 \\ \theta_1 \sigma^2 & h=1 \\ 0 & h\ge 2 \end{cases}$$

**Insight:** the MA(1) autocovariance function **cuts off after lag 1**, a diagnostic fingerprint used in the *Box–Jenkins identification* step.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Trend Stationarity vs. Difference Stationarity

Consider the two competing data‑generating processes (DGPs):

$$\text{(TS)}\quad Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + u_t,\quad u_t\sim WN(0,\sigma_u^2)$$

$$\text{(DS)}\quad Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \beta + u_t,\quad u_t\sim WN(0,\sigma_u^2)$$

**Step 1 — Compute $E(Y_t)$ for each.**

$$\text{TS: } E(Y_t) = \alpha + \beta t$$

$$\text{DS: } E(Y_t) = Y_0 + \beta t$$

Both have a *linear* trend in expectation.

**Step 2 — Compute $\text{Var}(Y_t)$.**

For TS: $Y_t$ is a deterministic‑trend + stationary noise, so $\text{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma_u^2$ (constant).

For DS: substituting the recursion repeatedly gives $Y_t = Y_0 + \beta t + \sum_{i=1}^{t} u_i$, hence

$$\text{Var}(Y_t) = t\sigma_u^2 \to \infty$$

**Step 3 — Diagnostic implication.** A *constant variance* across sub‑samples points to **TS**, while a *variance that grows linearly with the sample length* is the signature of **DS**. The **Augmented Dickey–Fuller (ADF)** test exploits exactly this:

$$\Delta Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \rho Y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\delta_i \Delta Y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$$

$$H_0:\rho = 0 \;\;(\text{unit root, DS})\;\;\text{vs}\;\;H_1:\rho < 0\;\;(\text{stationary or TS})$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — From ARDL to ECM (Residual‑Based Pesaran Bounds Test)

Starting from

$$Y_t = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{p}\alpha_i Y_{t-i} + \sum_{j=0}^{q}\beta_j X_{t-j} + u_t$$

**Step 1 — Subtract $Y_{t-1}$ from both sides.**

$$Y_t - Y_{t-1} = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{p}\alpha_i Y_{t-i} - Y_{t-1} + \sum_{j=0}^{q}\beta_j X_{t-j} + u_t$$

**Step 2 — Re‑arrange the $Y$‑lags** as $\alpha_i(Y_{t-i} - Y_{t-1})$ and add/subtract $Y_{t-1}$ to expose a level term.

After some algebra, with $\Delta Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$ and $\Delta X_t = X_t - X_{t-1}$:

$$\Delta Y_t = \phi_0 + \phi_1 \Delta X_t - \lambda\bigl(Y_{t-1} - \theta_0 - \theta_1 X_{t-1}\bigr) + \sum \text{(higher‑order differences)} + v_t$$

where $\lambda = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{p}\alpha_i$ is the **speed of adjustment**.

**Step 3 — Pesaran Bounds test.** Estimate by OLS and apply an F‑test on the joint null $H_0: \lambda = 0, \phi_1 = 0$. Two sets of critical values — *lower* (all series are $I(1)$) and *upper* (all series are $I(0)$) — provide the **bounds** within which the test statistic must fall.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Behavioural Signatures

| Diagnostic | Strict SS | Weak SS | Trend SS | Diff. SS (Unit Root) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mean | constant | constant | $f(t)$ | constant / $f(t)$ |
| Variance | constant | constant | constant | $t\sigma^2 \to \infty$ |
| Joint distribution | time‑invariant | may shift | shift via trend | shift |
| Autocovariance | $\gamma(h)$ | $\gamma(h)$ | $\gamma(h)$ | function of $t$ |
| Effect of shock | transient | transient | transient | **permanent** |
| Differencing $d$ needed | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ (residual) | $\ge 1$ |
| ADF $H_0$ rejected? | yes | yes | yes | **no** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Stationarity Concepts (Mermaid Mind‑Map)

    A --> C["Weak Stationarity WSS"]

    E --> E2["Difference Stationary Unit Root"]

    C3 --> F

```

## 4.2 Block‑Level Functional Architecture of a Box‑Jenkins / Regression‑Extension Pipeline

```mermaid

    decision -- "No" --> diff["Apply Difference Operator Delta to power d"]

```

    end

```

    Q2 -- "No" --> ARDL2["Fit ARDL in levels Pesaran bounds test"]

    VARDiff --> forecast
    ARDL --> forecast
    ARDL2 --> forecast
```
