# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short‑Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between strict stationarity and weak stationarity. State the conditions under which a time series is said to be weakly stationary.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
- **Strict stationarity** requires the *entire joint cumulative distribution function* to be invariant under any time shift, i.e.  
  $F_{Y_{t_1}, \dots, Y_{t_k}}(y_1,\dots,y_k) = F_{Y_{t_1+h},\dots,Y_{t_k+h}}(y_1,\dots,y_k)$ for all $k$, all $h$ and all $t_i$.
- **Weak (wide‑sense) stationarity** only imposes three moment conditions:  
  (i) $E(Y_t) = \mu$ — constant mean,  
  (ii) $\text{Var}(Y_t) = \gamma(0)$ — finite constant variance,  
  (iii) $\text{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t+h}) = \gamma(h)$ — autocovariance depends only on lag.
- Strict stationarity *with finite second moments* implies weak stationarity; the converse is not necessarily true.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is a random walk? Why is it classified as a difference‑stationary process?**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
- A **random walk** is defined by $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ with $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma^2)$ and $Y_0$ fixed.
- It is **difference stationary** because $\Delta Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1} = \varepsilon_t$, which is white noise and therefore *weakly stationary*.
- The series itself is **not** stationary: $E(Y_t) = Y_0$ (mean constant) but $\text{Var}(Y_t) = t\sigma^2$ grows without bound as $t$ increases.
- Hence, the series requires **differencing** of order $d=1$ to become stationary — making it a member of the ARIMA($p,1,q$) family.

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall** — for stationarity questions, students frequently:
> 1. Forget to state the *finite second‑moment* condition for WSS.
> 2. Confuse **trend‑stationary** (deterministic trend) with **difference‑stationary** (stochastic trend).
> 3. Skip the *autocovariance depends only on lag* condition in their written answer.
> 4. Apply OLS directly to non‑stationary data without testing — full marks lost on the "diagnostic" sub‑question.
> Always write the *three* WSS conditions *explicitly* and label the order of integration $I(0)$ vs $I(1)$ wherever relevant.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Set A]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, distinguish between **trend‑stationary** and **difference‑stationary** time series. Show mathematically that a random walk is non‑stationary but its first difference is weakly stationary. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the AR(1) process $Y_t = 5 + 0.7 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,4)$. Compute the long‑run mean, unconditional variance and the autocorrelation at lag 3. Comment on the stationarity of the process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (7 marks)**
- *Definition (1 mark).* A **trend‑stationary (TS)** process is $Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + u_t$ with $u_t$ weakly stationary. A **difference‑stationary (DS)** process requires differencing to achieve stationarity.
- *Sketch (2 marks).* A line drawing showing: TS — fluctuating series around a deterministic straight line; DS — random walk wandering with growing amplitude.
- *Random walk proof (4 marks).* With $Y_0=0$, $Y_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_i$. $E(Y_t) = 0$, but $\text{Var}(Y_t) = t\sigma^2$, which depends on $t$, violating WSS. Differencing, $\Delta Y_t = \varepsilon_t$ has constant mean $0$, constant variance $\sigma^2$ and $\text{Cov}(\Delta Y_t, \Delta Y_{t+h}) = 0$ for $h\neq 0$, hence WSS.

**(b) (7 marks)**
- *Long‑run mean (2 marks):* $c = 5$, $\phi_1 = 0.7$.  
  $\mu = c/(1-\phi_1) = 5/(1-0.7) = 5/0.3 = 16.667$.
- *Unconditional variance (2 marks):*  
  $\gamma(0) = \sigma^2/(1-\phi_1^2) = 4/(1-0.49) = 4/0.51 = 7.843$.
- *Autocorrelation at lag 3 (2 marks):*  
  $\rho(3) = \phi_1^3 = 0.7^3 = 0.343$.
- *Stationarity comment (1 mark):* $\vert\phi_1\vert = 0.7 < 1$, so the process is **weakly stationary**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Set B]`

> **(a)** Explain the concept of a **regression extension** for time series data. Derive the **Autoregressive Distributed Lag (ARDL)** representation and show how it reduces to an **Error Correction Model (ECM)** when the variables are cointegrated. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A financial analyst models the log‑return $Y_t$ of a stock index using an AR(2) process: $Y_t = 0.2 Y_{t-1} - 0.15 Y_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$, $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,1)$.  
> (i) Write the model in **lag‑operator** form and check the **stationarity condition** using the characteristic polynomial.  
> (ii) Compute the **Yule–Walker** equations for $\rho(1)$ and $\rho(2)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (7 marks)**
- *Concept (2 marks).* A regression extension relaxes the standard OLS assumption of strictly exogenous, non‑autocorrelated regressors. It accommodates lagged dependent variables, distributed lags of regressors and serial dependence in errors, leading to **ARDL(p, q)**.
- *ARDL derivation (2 marks).*  
  $Y_t = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{p}\alpha_i Y_{t-i} + \sum_{j=0}^{q}\beta_j X_{t-j} + u_t$.
- *Reduction to ECM (3 marks).* Subtract $Y_{t-1}$ from both sides, regroup the $Y$‑lags as first differences and expose the long‑run cointegrating relation $Y_{t-1} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_{t-1}$:  
  $\Delta Y_t = \phi_0 + \phi_1 \Delta X_t - \lambda(Y_{t-1} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_{t-1}) + v_t$  
  with $\lambda = 1 - \sum\alpha_i$ being the **speed of adjustment**, $-1<\lambda<0$ for stability.

**(b) (7 marks)**
- *(i) Lag‑operator form + stationarity (3 marks).*  
  $\Phi(L) = 1 - 0.2 L + 0.15 L^2$.  
  Characteristic equation $1 - 0.2 z + 0.15 z^2 = 0$. Using the quadratic formula:  
  $z = \dfrac{0.2 \pm \sqrt{0.04 - 0.60}}{0.30} = \dfrac{0.2 \pm \sqrt{-0.56}}{0.30} = \dfrac{0.2 \pm 0.748\, i}{0.30}$.  
  Modulus: $\vert z \vert = \sqrt{0.667^2 + 2.493^2} \approx 2.58 > 1$. Both roots lie **outside** the unit circle → **stationary**.
- *(ii) Yule–Walker equations (4 marks).* For AR(2):  
  $\rho(1) = \phi_1 \rho(1) + \phi_2 \rho(-1) + \sigma^2 / \gamma(0)$? — No, the Yule–Walker system is:  
  $\rho(1) = \phi_1 + \phi_2 \rho(1)$ → $\rho(1) = \phi_1 / (1 - \phi_2) = 0.2 / (1 + 0.15) = 0.174$.  
  $\rho(2) = \phi_1 \rho(1) + \phi_2$ → $\rho(2) = 0.2(0.174) - 0.15 = 0.0348 - 0.15 = -0.115$.  
  Hence $\rho(1) \approx 0.174$, $\rho(2) \approx -0.115$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stationarity hierarchy**: Strict $\Rightarrow$ Weak $\Rightarrow$ Trend/Difference $\Rightarrow$ Non‑stationary.
- A **weakly stationary** series requires **(i)** constant mean, **(ii)** finite constant variance, **(iii)** autocovariance function $\gamma(h)$ depending only on lag.
- **White noise** $W\!N(0,\sigma^2)$ is strictly stationary; an iid sequence is the *cleanest* example.
- **Random walk** is the canonical *difference‑stationary* series: $Y_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\varepsilon_i$ has variance $t\sigma^2$.
- **AR(1)** is WSS iff $\vert\phi_1\vert < 1$; otherwise unit root (non‑stationary).
- **MA(q)** is *always* weakly stationary if the underlying white noise has finite variance.
- The **Box–Jenkins (1976) workflow**: *Identify $\to$ Estimate $\to$ Diagnose $\to$ Forecast*. Stationarity lies at the heart of the *Identify* stage.
- **Spurious regression** (Granger–Newbold 1974): OLS on two independent DS series yields nonsense high $R^2$ — always test for stationarity (ADF, KPSS, PP) first.
- **ARDL(p, q)** is the *bridge* between single‑equation time‑series regression and unrestricted VAR.
- **ECM** = ARDL + cointegration; the coefficient $\lambda$ on the lagged cointegrating residual is the **speed of adjustment** and must satisfy $-1 < \lambda < 0$.
- **Pesaran bounds test** handles the *mixed $I(0)/I(1)$* case in ARDL — lower bound (all $I(1)$) and upper bound (all $I(0)$).
- Diagnostic fingerprints:
  - MA(1): ACF cuts off after lag 1.
  - AR(1): ACF decays geometrically; PACF cuts off after lag 1.
  - Random Walk: ACF at lag $h$ is $\rho(h) \to 1$ as $h \to 0$ (non‑summable, slowly decaying).
- Examiner's mantra: **"State the three WSS conditions explicitly, then proceed."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Making Data Stationary
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Making Data Stationary

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Stationarity

A time series $\{Y_t\}$ is said to be **strictly stationary** if the joint distribution of $(Y_{t_1}, Y_{t_2}, \dots, Y_{t_k})$ is identical to that of $(Y_{t_1+\tau}, Y_{t_2+\tau}, \dots, Y_{t_k+\tau})$ for all $k$, all time points $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_k$, and all time lags $\tau$.

In practice, a **weak stationarity** (or second-order stationarity) is imposed, requiring only:

$$E[Y_t] = \mu \quad \text{(constant mean)}$$

$$Var(Y_t) = \sigma^2 \quad \text{(constant variance)}$$

$$Cov(Y_t, Y_{t+k}) = \gamma_k \quad \text{(autocovariance depends only on lag } k\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Making Data Stationary" refers to the application of systematic mathematical transformations that convert a non-stationary time series into a stationary one, which is the **mandatory prerequisite** for fitting ARMA, ARIMA, SARIMA, and other linear stochastic models.

### 1.2 What is "Making Data Stationary"?

*Making data stationary* is the process of removing trend, seasonality, heteroscedasticity, and other time-dependent structures from a series so that its statistical properties become invariant with respect to time. The output is a series whose past behavior is statistically representative of its future behavior.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Calibrated Ruler

Imagine you are measuring the height of a growing child every month. The raw readings are useless for predicting next month's height using fixed statistics, because the mean is drifting upward (growth trend), and the spread of readings keeps changing (variance shifting). 

Now suppose you **subtract each reading from the previous one** — you get monthly *increments*. These increments look much more like a stationary signal: a child's growth spurts and slowdowns average out around a constant, and the variability feels roughly the same whether the child is 5 or 15. 

This is exactly what "making data stationary" achieves: it converts a wandering, drifting signal into a calm, oscillating one whose statistical fingerprint stays constant over time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Stationarity is **not** a property of the data alone — it is a property of the *data-generating process* assumed by your model. Most classical time series models (Box-Jenkins family) require the residuals/process to be stationary.

### 1.4 Why Stationarity Matters in Engineering Applications

- **Forecasting reliability:** Stationary processes yield forecasts whose confidence intervals remain valid.
- **Spectral analysis (signal processing):** Power Spectral Density (PSD) estimation assumes WSS (Wide-Sense Stationary) signals.
- **Control systems:** Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system theory requires stationary inputs.
- **Vibration analysis, biomedical signals, financial engineering:** All rely on this assumption.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Non-Stationarity

A time series may be non-stationary due to one or more of the following:

1. **Deterministic Trend** — mean drifts linearly or polynomially with time.
2. **Stochastic Trend** — non-stationarity arising from a *unit root* (random walk behavior).
3. **Seasonality** — periodic mean/variance shifts with period $s$.
4. **Heteroscedasticity (Variance Instability)** — variance grows/shrinks with $t$ or with $Y_t$.
5. **Structural Breaks** — sudden regime shifts (e.g., policy change, equipment overhaul).

### 2.2 The Three Pillars of Stationarization

#### Pillar 1 — Differencing (Removes Trend & Unit Roots)

The **backshift operator** $B$ is defined as $B Y_t = Y_{t-1}$, and the **difference operator** $\nabla = 1 - B$.

**First Difference:**

$$\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1} = (1 - B)Y_t$$

**Second Difference:**

$$\nabla^2 Y_t = \nabla Y_t - \nabla Y_{t-1} = Y_t - 2Y_{t-1} + Y_{t-2} = (1 - B)^2 Y_t$$

**Seasonal Difference (period $s$):**

$$\nabla_s Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-s} = (1 - B^s) Y_t$$

> [!TIP]
> **Empirical Rule (Box-Jenkins):** If the ACF of $Y_t$ dies out very slowly and the PACF cuts off after lag 1, a first difference suffices. If it still dies slowly, apply a second difference. **Over-differencing** introduces artificial negative autocorrelation in the ACF — stop at the minimum order that yields stationarity.

#### Pillar 2 — Variance-Stabilizing Transformations

When variance grows with the level of the series, **Box-Cox transformation** is the canonical remedy:

$$
Y_t^{(\lambda)} =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{Y_t^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda}, & \lambda \neq 0 \\
\ln(Y_t), & \lambda = 0
\end{cases}
$$

Common special cases:

- $\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow$ **log transformation** — useful when variance scales with the square of the level.
- $\lambda = 0.5 \Rightarrow$ **square-root transformation** — useful for count data (Poisson-like).
- $\lambda = 1 \Rightarrow$ **no transformation** (linear shift only).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Transformation must be applied **before** differencing, especially when both variance instability and trend are present. The typical pipeline is: *Transform $\rightarrow$ Test $\rightarrow$ Difference $\rightarrow$ Test again*.

#### Pillar 3 — Detrending (Deterministic Trend Removal)

For a deterministic linear trend $Y_t = a + bt + \varepsilon_t$, the OLS residuals are:

$$\hat{\varepsilon}_t = Y_t - (\hat{a} + \hat{b}t)$$

For polynomial trends of degree $p$:

$$Y_t = \beta_0 + \beta_1 t + \beta_2 t^2 + \dots + \beta_p t^p + \varepsilon_t$$

fit by polynomial regression and subtract the fitted deterministic component.

> [!WARNING]
> Detrending assumes the trend is **deterministic** (functional form known). If the trend is **stochastic** (unit root), detrending leaves residual non-stationarity. Use unit-root tests to distinguish.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Operator** | **Effect** | **When to Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| First Difference | $\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$ | Removes linear trend / random walk | ACF decays slowly, PACF cuts at lag 1 |
| Second Difference | $\nabla^2 Y_t = Y_t - 2Y_{t-1} + Y_{t-2}$ | Removes quadratic trend | First diff still non-stationary |
| Seasonal Difference | $\nabla_s Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-s}$ | Removes seasonal period-$s$ pattern | ACF peaks at lag $s$ |
| Box-Cox | $(Y_t^{\lambda}-1)/\lambda$ | Stabilizes variance | Variance grows with level |
| Log Transform | $\ln Y_t$ | Stabilizes variance, linearizes growth | Multiplicative / exponential growth |
| Linear Detrend | $Y_t - (\hat{a}+\hat{b}t)$ | Removes deterministic linear trend | Series clearly linear, no unit root |
| ADF Test Statistic | $\tau = \hat{\rho}/SE(\hat{\rho})$ from $\nabla Y_t = \alpha + \rho Y_{t-1} + \sum \beta_i \nabla Y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$ | Tests for unit root | Confirm stationarity numerically |
| KPSS Test | LM statistic on $Y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t$ with cumulative sum | Tests stationarity directly | Complementary to ADF |
| Ljung-Box Q | $Q = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{h}\frac{\hat{\rho}_k^{\,2}}{n-k}$ | Checks residual whiteness | Post-stationarity validation |

> [!NOTE]
> In all formulas above, $\nabla$ denotes the difference operator, $B$ the backshift operator, $\lambda$ the Box-Cox parameter, and $\hat{\rho}_k$ the sample autocorrelation at lag $k$.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

| **Domain** | **Application** | **Stabilization Technique Used** |
|---|---|---|
| Econometrics (GDP, CPI) | Removing inflation drift | Log + first differencing (gives growth rate) |
| Electrical Load Forecasting | Daily/weekly seasonality | Seasonal differencing ($s=24$ or $s=7$) |
| Vibration Signal Analysis | Bearing fault detection | High-pass filtering + detrending |
| Stock Price Analysis | Price $\to$ Return conversion | First difference of log prices |
| Climate Science (Temperature) | Removing anthropogenic trend | Linear/quadratic detrending |
| Biomedical (ECG/EEG) | Baseline wander removal | Differencing + smoothing |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why First Differencing Eliminates a Random Walk

**Setup.** Consider a random walk with drift:

$$Y_t = \mu + Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$$

**Step 1.** Express $Y_t$ in terms of initial value $Y_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_1 &= \mu + Y_0 + \varepsilon_1 \\
Y_2 &= \mu + Y_1 + \varepsilon_2 = 2\mu + Y_0 + \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 \\
Y_t &= Y_0 + t\mu + \sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Compute the mean of $Y_t$:

$$E[Y_t] = Y_0 + t\mu$$

The mean is a *linear function of $t$* — non-constant ⇒ **non-stationary**.

**Step 3.** Compute the variance of $Y_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Var(Y_t) &= Var\left( \sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_i \right) = \sum_{i=1}^{t} Var(\varepsilon_i) = t\sigma^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Variance grows with $t$ ⇒ **non-stationary**.

**Step 4.** Apply first difference $\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla Y_t &= (\mu + Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t) - Y_{t-1} \\
\nabla Y_t &= \mu + \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.** Examine the new series $W_t = \nabla Y_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[W_t] &= E[\mu + \varepsilon_t] = \mu \quad \text{(constant)} \\
Var(W_t) &= Var(\varepsilon_t) = \sigma^2 \quad \text{(constant)} \\
Cov(W_t, W_{t+k}) &= 0 \quad \text{for } k \geq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

All three conditions of weak stationarity are satisfied. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Differencing the random walk recovers the *innovation process* $\{\varepsilon_t\}$, which is white noise and therefore stationary. This is the fundamental reason first-differencing is so effective on financial and economic series.

### 3.2 Derivation: Box-Cox Transformation Optimal $\lambda$

**Step 1.** Profile log-likelihood for a transformation parameter $\lambda$, with a Gaussian assumption on the transformed data $Y_t^{(\lambda)}$:

$$Z_t^{(\lambda)} = \frac{Y_t^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda \cdot \dot{Y}^{\lambda-1}}$$

where $\dot{Y}$ is the geometric mean of $Y_t$.

**Step 2.** For a given $\lambda$, the MLE of the variance is:

$$\hat{\sigma}^2(\lambda) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}\left(Z_t^{(\lambda)} - \overline{Z^{(\lambda)}}\right)^2$$

**Step 3.** The log-likelihood (up to constants) is:

$$\ell(\lambda) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln \hat{\sigma}^2(\lambda) + (\lambda - 1)\sum_{t=1}^{n}\ln Y_t$$

**Step 4.** Maximize $\ell(\lambda)$ numerically over $\lambda \in [-2, 2]$ (typical search range). The optimal $\lambda^*$ satisfies:

$$\left.\frac{d\ell}{d\lambda}\right|_{\lambda=\lambda^*} = 0$$

which, in closed form for the normal-equation component, reduces to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\ell}{d\lambda} &= -\frac{n}{2}\cdot \frac{1}{\hat{\sigma}^2}\cdot \frac{d\hat{\sigma}^2}{d\lambda} + \sum_{t=1}^{n}\ln Y_t = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solved iteratively (e.g., Newton-Raphson) for $\lambda^*$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Choosing a Transformation

| **Observed Symptom in Plot / ACF** | **Likely Cause** | **Recommended Fix** | **Apply Order** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Variance increases with level | Multiplicative / exponential growth | Box-Cox or Log transform | **First** |
| Linear upward/downward trend, no seasonal pattern | Deterministic linear trend | Linear detrending OR first differencing | After variance fix |
| Slow-decaying ACF, PACF = 1 at lag 1 | Random walk (stochastic trend) | First differencing $\nabla Y_t$ | After variance fix |
| ACF peaks at lag $s$ | Seasonality (period $s$) | Seasonal differencing $\nabla_s Y_t$ | Often combined with regular differencing |
| Slow decay after first differencing | Higher-order integration | Second differencing $\nabla^2 Y_t$ | Last resort |
| Strong cycle without unit root | Deterministic cyclicality | Detrend + seasonal adjust | Before model fitting |
| Sudden level shift | Structural break | Intervention / break-point modeling | Specialized |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Making a Series Stationary

**Given series** (n = 8): $Y_t = 2, 5, 9, 14, 20, 27, 35, 44$

**Step 1 — Check trend visually:** Differences are 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9. Constant second difference $\Rightarrow$ quadratic trend.

**Step 2 — Apply first difference $\nabla Y_t$:**

$$3,\; 4,\; 5,\; 6,\; 7,\; 8,\; 9$$

**Step 3 — Apply second difference $\nabla^2 Y_t$:**

$$1,\; 1,\; 1,\; 1,\; 1,\; 1$$

**Step 4 — Constant $\Rightarrow$ perfectly stationary.** $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> The decision to apply first vs. second difference can also be guided by the **variogram** or the **augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test**: if the ADF $p$-value remains $> 0.05$ after one round of differencing, apply a second difference.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Stationarization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Time Series Yt] --> B[Visual Inspection: Plot Yt vs t]
    B --> C{Detect Non-Stationarity}
    C -- Trend Only --> D[Apply Differencing: nabla Yt]
    C -- Seasonality --> E[Apply Seasonal Differencing: nabla_s Yt]
    C -- Variance Drift --> F[Apply Box Cox or Log Transform]
    C -- Combined --> G[Transform THEN Difference]
    D --> H[Conduct ADF Unit Root Test]
    E --> H
    F --> I[Re-inspect Transformed Series]
    G --> I
    I --> H
    H --> J{p value less than 0.05}
    J -- Yes --> K[Series is Stationary]
    J -- No --> L[Increase Differencing Order or Try Detrending]
    L --> H
    K --> M[Fit ARMA or ARIMA Model]
    M --> N[Residual Diagnostics: Ljung Box]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Workflow — Transformation Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Plot Series and ACF] --> S2[Step 2: Variance Stable?]
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Step 3: Apply Differencing]
    S2 -- No --> S4[Step 4: Box Cox Transform with optimal lambda]
    S4 --> S3
    S3 --> S5[Step 5: Run ADF Test]
    S5 --> S6{Stationary Confirmed?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Proceed to ARIMA Fitting]
    S6 -- No --> S8[Apply Seasonal Differencing or Second Difference]
    S8 --> S5
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram — Sources and Remedies

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Deterministic Trend] --> R1[Detrending: OLS Regression]
    P2[Stochastic Trend / Unit Root] --> R2[Differencing Operator nabla]
    P3[Seasonality Period s] --> R3[Seasonal Differencing nabla_s]
    P4[Heteroscedastic Variance] --> R4[Box Cox or Log Transform]
    P5[Structural Break] --> R5[Intervention Analysis]
    R1 --> O[Stationary Series Wt]
    R2 --> O
    R3 --> O
    R4 --> O
    R5 --> O
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams follow the canonical Box-Jenkins methodology: **Identify $\rightarrow$ Transform $\rightarrow$ Difference $\rightarrow$ Diagnose $\rightarrow$ Model**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*Define stationarity. Why is it essential to make a time series stationary before fitting an ARMA model?*

**Model Answer:**

A time series $\{Y_t\}$ is **stationary** (in the weak sense) if:

(i) $E[Y_t] = \mu$ (constant mean across time)  
(ii) $Var(Y_t) = \sigma^2$ (constant variance across time)  
(iii) $Cov(Y_t, Y_{t+k}) = \gamma_k$ (autocovariance depends only on lag $k$, not on $t$)

ARMA models rely on the assumption that the autocovariance structure is time-invariant. If the series has a trend or seasonal pattern, the autocovariances drift with $t$, violating the model's foundational assumption. This leads to biased coefficient estimates, unreliable forecasts, and invalid confidence intervals. Therefore, transforming the data to a stationary form is a mandatory prerequisite.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*What is the role of the Box-Cox transformation in making a time series stationary?*

**Model Answer:**

The Box-Cox transformation is a **variance-stabilizing transformation** defined as:

$$
Y_t^{(\lambda)} =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{Y_t^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda}, & \lambda \neq 0 \\[4pt]
\ln Y_t, & \lambda = 0
\end{cases}
$$

It addresses **heteroscedasticity** — a situation where the variance of $Y_t$ changes with the level of $Y_t$ or with time. By selecting an appropriate $\lambda$ (estimated via maximum likelihood), the transformed series exhibits approximately constant variance, satisfying the second stationarity condition. The transformation is typically applied **before** differencing, as differencing alone cannot stabilize variance.

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain the Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test with its hypothesis setup. How do you interpret the test statistic and $p$-value to confirm stationarity? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the series $Y_t = \{2, 5, 9, 14, 20, 27, 35, 44\}$, apply the appropriate differencing operations to make the data stationary. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**The ADF Test Setup.** The Augmented Dickey-Fuller test checks for the presence of a *unit root* in the series, which is the hallmark of stochastic non-stationarity. The test is based on the regression:

$$
\nabla Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \rho Y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \gamma_i \nabla Y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t
$$

where:
- $\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$ is the first difference,
- $p$ is the lag order chosen (typically via AIC/BIC),
- $\varepsilon_t$ is white noise.

**Hypotheses:**

$$H_0: \rho = 0 \quad \text{(unit root present, series is NON-stationary)}$$

$$H_1: \rho < 0 \quad \text{(no unit root, series is stationary)}$$

**Test Statistic:**

$$\tau_{ADF} = \frac{\hat{\rho}}{SE(\hat{\rho})}$$

The critical values are **non-standard** (Dickey-Fuller distribution, more negative than the standard $t$-distribution).

**Decision Rule:**

- If $p$-value $< \alpha$ (e.g., 0.05), **reject $H_0$** $\Rightarrow$ series is stationary.
- If $p$-value $\geq \alpha$, **fail to reject $H_0$** $\Rightarrow$ series is non-stationary; differencing is needed.

**Practical Note:** A *complement* test, the **KPSS test**, reverses the hypotheses ($H_0$: stationary). Used together, they provide robust confirmation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Original series:** $Y_t = \{2, 5, 9, 14, 20, 27, 35, 44\}$

**Step 2 — First differences $\nabla Y_t$:**

$$
\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}
$$

| $t$ | $Y_t$ | $\nabla Y_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | 5 | 3 |
| 3 | 9 | 4 |
| 4 | 14 | 5 |
| 5 | 20 | 6 |
| 6 | 27 | 7 |
| 7 | 35 | 8 |
| 8 | 44 | 9 |

So $\nabla Y_t = \{3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9\}$.

**Step 3 — Inspect $\nabla Y_t$:** Still trending upward (linear). Not yet stationary.

**Step 4 — Second differences $\nabla^2 Y_t$:**

| $t$ | $\nabla Y_t$ | $\nabla^2 Y_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| 3 | 4 | 1 |
| 4 | 5 | 1 |
| 5 | 6 | 1 |
| 6 | 7 | 1 |
| 7 | 8 | 1 |
| 8 | 9 | 1 |

So $\nabla^2 Y_t = \{1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1\}$ — **constant series**.

**Step 5 — Verification of stationarity of $\nabla^2 Y_t$:**

- Mean $= 1$ (constant)  
- Variance $= 0$ (constant)  
- Autocovariances $= 0$

All three stationarity conditions are satisfied. $\blacksquare$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Discuss the Box-Cox transformation in detail. Derive the relationship between first-differencing a series and its logarithmic transformation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A retail company's monthly sales (in lakhs) over 24 months are observed to grow multiplicatively. Suggest and justify an appropriate transformation pipeline to make the data stationary, mentioning how you would test the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**The Box-Cox Transformation.** The Box-Cox family of power transformations is defined as:

$$
Y_t^{(\lambda)} =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{Y_t^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda}, & \lambda \neq 0 \\[6pt]
\ln Y_t, & \lambda = 0
\end{cases}
$$

The parameter $\lambda$ is chosen by maximizing the log-likelihood:

$$\ell(\lambda) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln\hat{\sigma}^2(\lambda) + (\lambda - 1)\sum_{t=1}^{n}\ln Y_t$$

**Key special cases:**

| $\lambda$ | Transformation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| $-1$ | $1/Y_t$ | Inverse relationship |
| $0$ | $\ln Y_t$ | Multiplicative / exponential growth |
| $0.5$ | $\sqrt{Y_t}$ | Count data (Poisson) |
| $1$ | $Y_t - 1$ (linear) | No transformation needed |

**Connection: First-Differencing a Log-Series ≈ Growth Rate.**

Let $Z_t = \ln Y_t$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla Z_t &= \ln Y_t - \ln Y_{t-1} \\
&= \ln\left(\frac{Y_t}{Y_{t-1}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For small relative changes, using the approximation $\ln(1+x) \approx x$ for small $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla Z_t &= \ln\left(1 + \frac{Y_t - Y_{t-1}}{Y_{t-1}}\right) \approx \frac{Y_t - Y_{t-1}}{Y_{t-1}} = \frac{\nabla Y_t}{Y_{t-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The first difference of the log-transformed series is approximately equal to the **percentage change (growth rate)** of the original series. Hence, the pipeline $\ln Y_t \rightarrow \nabla \ln Y_t$ converts a multiplicatively-growing non-stationary series into a stationary one representing its growth dynamics. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Diagnosis of Multiplicative Growth.**

When sales grow multiplicatively (e.g., compounding growth or seasonal multiplicative effects), the variance is proportional to the square of the level: $Var(Y_t) \propto Y_t^2$. This violates the constant-variance stationarity condition.

**Step 2 — Recommended Pipeline:**

**(a) Log Transformation (First):**

$$Z_t = \ln Y_t$$

This converts multiplicative growth into additive growth: if $Y_t = Y_{t-1} \cdot (1+r_t)$, then $Z_t - Z_{t-1} = \ln(1+r_t) \approx r_t$. Variance is now approximately constant.

**(b) First Differencing (Second):**

$$W_t = \nabla Z_t = Z_t - Z_{t-1} = \ln Y_t - \ln Y_{t-1}$$

This removes the linear trend in $Z_t$, yielding a series whose mean is approximately constant.

**(c) Seasonal Differencing (If Applicable):**

If monthly sales exhibit yearly seasonality (period $s=12$):

$$W'_t = W_t - W_{t-12}$$

**Step 3 — Final Stationarity Testing:**

Apply the **ADF test** on the final series $W'_t$:

- Regress: $\nabla W'_t = \alpha + \rho W'_{t-1} + \sum \gamma_i \nabla W'_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$
- Compute $p$-value; if $< 0.05$, stationarity is confirmed.

**Step 4 — Supplementary Validation:**

- Inspect the **ACF** of $W'_t$: should decay quickly to zero.
- Apply **Ljung-Box Q test** on residuals of any fitted ARMA model: $Q \sim \chi^2_h$, $p$-value $> 0.05$ confirms no remaining autocorrelation.

**Justification Summary:**  
The log-difference pipeline is the gold standard for multiplicative economic/retail data because it simultaneously stabilizes variance, removes trend, and yields a directly interpretable quantity (the growth rate). **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Order of operations matters:** Students often difference *before* transforming, but variance stabilization (Box-Cox / log) must come **first** for multiplicative series.
> 2. **Over-differencing is penalized:** If the examiner's key shows negative ACF at lag 1 after differencing, you have over-differenced. Stop one order earlier.
> 3. **Misinterpreting ADF:** Rejecting $H_0$ means **stationary**, *not* non-stationary. Several students invert this conclusion.
> 4. **Forgetting to state hypotheses explicitly** in Part A answers costs 1 mark. Always write $H_0$ and $H_1$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stationarity** = constant mean, constant variance, lag-dependent-only autocovariance.
- **Three remediation pillars:** Differencing (trend/unit root), Box-Cox / Log (variance), Detrending (deterministic trend).
- **First difference formula:** $\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$ — removes a random walk's drift.
- **Second difference formula:** $\nabla^2 Y_t = Y_t - 2Y_{t-1} + Y_{t-2}$ — removes quadratic trend.
- **Seasonal difference formula:** $\nabla_s Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-s}$ — removes periodicity of period $s$.
- **Box-Cox formula:** $(Y_t^{\lambda} - 1)/\lambda$ for $\lambda \neq 0$; $\ln Y_t$ for $\lambda = 0$.
- **Log-difference identity:** $\nabla \ln Y_t = \ln(Y_t / Y_{t-1}) \approx$ percentage change.
- **ADF Test:** $H_0$: unit root (non-stationary); reject $H_0$ if $p < 0.05$.
- **Pipeline order:** **Variance transform first $\rightarrow$ Differencing second $\rightarrow$ ADF test third.**
- **Never over-difference:** ACF with negative lag-1 value is a sign to stop.
- **Deterministic vs. stochastic trend:** Use unit root tests (ADF, KPSS, PP) to differentiate before choosing detrending vs. differencing.
- **Always validate post-transformation** with both numerical tests (ADF) and visual tools (ACF/PACF plots).
- **Stationarity enables:** ARMA/ARIMA fitting, valid spectral density estimation, time-invariant forecasting intervals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) Test** is a formal statistical hypothesis test used in time series econometrics and engineering signal analysis to determine the presence of a **unit root** in an autoregressive (AR) time series model of arbitrary order $p$. It is a direct extension of the original Dickey-Fuller (DF) test, formulated by statisticians David Dickey and Wayne Fuller, and later augmented by Said and Dickey to overcome the DF test's restriction to AR(1) processes.

Mathematically, the ADF test estimates the following general regression equation and tests the null hypothesis that the coefficient on the lagged level term equals **unity** (i.e., a unit root is present):

$$\Delta y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$$

where $\gamma = (\rho - 1)$ is the parameter of interest, and the test evaluates $H_0 : \gamma = 0$ (unit root) against $H_1 : \gamma < 0$ (stationarity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The ADF test is a cornerstone of Module 2 in *Time Series Modeling & Analysis*. Mastery of the three model variants (none, constant, constant + trend) and the critical value lookup tables is mandatory for ESE questions worth 14 marks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Drifting Boat"**
> 
> Imagine you are watching a small boat floating in the ocean. You drop a marker at its current position every hour.
> - If the boat **drifts endlessly** away from its starting point (without returning), the ocean current is strong and persistent — the boat's position is like a **non-stationary series with a unit root**. Each step depends heavily on the previous one.
> - If the boat **wobbles around a fixed location** (the harbor), always returning close to the start, the series is **stationary**. Shocks to its position fade away over time.
> 
> The **Dickey-Fuller test** asks a single question: "Does the boat keep drifting, or does it come back home?" The **Augmented Dickey-Fuller test** is the same question, but it accounts for the fact that the boat's motion is not just based on where it was an hour ago — it might remember where it was two, three, or more hours back. So we add **lagged position changes** to the equation to make the test fair.

## 1.3 Why Augment the Dickey-Fuller Test?

The original DF test assumes the data generating process is an **AR(1)** model. If the true process is actually AR(2), AR(3), or higher, applying the plain DF test leads to **spurious results** and the test statistic does not follow the Dickey-Fuller distribution.

The augmentation solves this by **incorporating lagged first-differences** of the series, $\Delta y_{t-i}$, on the right-hand side, which soak up the higher-order dynamic structure and leave the lagged level term $y_{t-1}$ to be tested cleanly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The augmentation does *not* change the null hypothesis or the critical values. The "Augmented" in ADF refers to the **right-hand side structure**, not to the test distribution.

## 1.4 Standardized Notation Table

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Notation |
|:-------|:--------|:------------------|
| $y_t$ | Time series observation at time $t$ | Dependent variable |
| $\Delta y_t$ | First difference: $y_t - y_{t-1}$ | Stationarity-inducing operator |
| $\alpha$ | Drift / constant (intercept) | Deterministic level |
| $\beta$ | Coefficient on deterministic time trend | Deterministic trend |
| $\gamma$ | Test parameter: $(\rho - 1)$ | Coefficient on lagged level |
| $\delta_i$ | Coefficients on lagged differences | Short-run dynamics |
| $\varepsilon_t$ | White noise innovation | Error term, $i.i.d.(0, \sigma^2)$ |
| $p$ | Lag order of augmentation | Selected via AIC / SIC |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three ADF Model Variants

Depending on the assumed deterministic structure of the data, the ADF test is implemented in one of three forms. The choice critically affects the **critical values** used for inference.

### 2.1.1 Model 1 — No Constant, No Trend (Pure Random Walk)

$$\Delta y_t = \gamma \, y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \, \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Used when:** The series is believed to fluctuate around zero with no level or trend. This is the rarest practical case.

### 2.1.2 Model 2 — With Constant (Drift), No Trend

$$\Delta y_t = \alpha + \gamma \, y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \, \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Used when:** The series has a non-zero mean but no deterministic trend. This is the **most commonly used** form in practice.

### 2.1.3 Model 3 — With Constant and Time Trend

$$\Delta y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma \, y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \, \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Used when:** The series exhibits a deterministic upward or downward trend over time (e.g., GDP, population, climate indices).

## 2.2 Hypothesis Testing Framework

| Element | Statement | Interpretation |
|:--------|:----------|:---------------|
| **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$)** | $\gamma = 0$ (equivalently $\rho = 1$) | **Unit root present** → series is **non-stationary** |
| **Alternative ($H_1$)** | $\gamma < 0$ (equivalently $\vert \rho \vert < 1$) | **No unit root** → series is **stationary** |
| **Test Statistic** | $\tau$ (tau) = estimated $\gamma$ / standard error | Compared to non-standard critical values |
| **Decision Rule** | Reject $H_0$ if $\tau <$ critical value | Fails to reject → differencing needed |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall — One-Sided Test:** Unlike a standard t-test, the ADF test is **strictly a one-sided left-tail test**. We reject $H_0$ only if the test statistic is *more negative* than the critical value. Do **not** apply a two-sided t-distribution comparison — this is a common KTU valuation error.

## 2.3 Why Standard t-Distribution Cannot Be Used

Under the null hypothesis $H_0: \gamma = 0$, the estimator $\hat{\gamma}$ does **not** follow a Student-$t$ distribution. The non-stationarity introduces a bias that shifts the entire distribution to the left. Therefore, the Dickey and Fuller (1979, 1981) tables — derived via Monte Carlo simulation — provide special **non-standard critical values**.

| Model Form | 1% Critical Value | 5% Critical Value | 10% Critical Value |
|:-----------|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|
| **No constant / No trend** | $-2.5658$ | $-1.9393$ | $-1.6156$ |
| **With constant (no trend)** | $-3.4301$ | $-2.8612$ | $-2.5672$ |
| **With constant and trend** | $-3.9591$ | $-3.4100$ | $-3.1270$ |

*(Values shown are illustrative asymptotic values from the Dickey-Fuller distribution — actual finite-sample values may vary slightly.)*

## 2.4 Lag Order Selection ($p$)

The number of lagged differences $p$ must be chosen carefully:

- **Too small $p$:** Residual autocorrelation persists → invalid inference.
- **Too large $p$:** Loss of degrees of freedom → reduced test power.

**Standard Selection Criteria:**

1. **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC):** $AIC = -2 \ln(L) + 2k$
2. **Schwarz / Bayesian Information Criterion (SIC / BIC):** $BIC = -2 \ln(L) + k \ln(T)$
3. **General-to-Specific (Hendry's approach):** Start with a large $p$ and drop insignificant lags sequentially.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Economic Utility

| Domain | Application |
|:-------|:------------|
| **Financial Engineering** | Testing whether stock prices, exchange rates, or commodity prices follow a random walk — fundamental to pairs trading and mean-reversion strategies |
| **Macroeconomic Forecasting** | Determining if GDP, inflation, or unemployment series need differencing before ARIMA modeling |
| **Structural Engineering** | Detecting stationarity in vibration response signals of structures under seismic loading |
| **Hydrology & Climate Science** | Assessing stationarity of rainfall, river flow, and temperature time series for flood/drought prediction |
| **Biomedical Signal Processing** | Testing stationarity of EEG/ECG signals for diagnostic feature extraction |

## 2.6 Decision Workflow Summary

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard ADF Decision Logic:**
> 1. Specify the appropriate model form (constant? trend?).
> 2. Choose lag order $p$ via AIC or SIC.
> 3. Estimate the model via OLS and obtain the $\tau$ statistic.
> 4. Compare $\tau$ to the Dickey-Fuller critical values for the chosen model form.
> 5. If $\tau <$ critical value → **reject** $H_0$ → series is **stationary**.
> 6. If $\tau >$ critical value → **fail to reject** $H_0$ → differencing required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From AR(1) to the Dickey-Fuller Form

We begin with a standard **autoregressive process of order 1**, AR(1):

$$y_t = \rho \, y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$$

**Step 1 — Subtract $y_{t-1}$ from both sides to form the first difference:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_t - y_{t-1} &= \rho \, y_{t-1} - y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t \\
\Delta y_t &= (\rho - 1) \, y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* We rewrite the equation so that the dependent variable is now the first-differenced series $\Delta y_t$, and the lagged level $y_{t-1}$ becomes the regressor of interest.

**Step 2 — Define the test parameter $\gamma$:**

$$\gamma \equiv \rho - 1$$

*Explanation:* This is the canonical parameterization. Note that $\gamma = 0$ is exactly the unit root condition ($\rho = 1$), and $\gamma < 0$ is the stationarity condition ($\vert \rho \vert < 1$).

**Step 3 — Write the test equation in DF form:**

$$\Delta y_t = \gamma \, y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

*Explanation:* This is the **basic Dickey-Fuller regression**. The constant $\alpha$ and trend $\beta t$ are added in the augmented versions.

**Step 4 — Incorporate higher-order AR dynamics via augmentation:**

When the true DGP is AR($p$), we include $p-1$ lagged first-differences on the right-hand side:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_t &= \rho \, y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p-1} \psi_i \, y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t \\
\text{Manipulating as before:} \quad \Delta y_t &= \gamma \, y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p-1} \delta_i \, \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

where the $\delta_i$ are linear combinations of the original AR coefficients.

*Explanation:* The lagged differences capture the short-run dynamics, while $y_{t-1}$ cleanly reflects the long-run (persistent) behavior. The augmentation ensures the residual $\varepsilon_t$ is approximately white noise.

## 3.2 Derivation: Connection Between $\gamma$ and Stationarity

The characteristic equation of the AR process $y_t = \rho y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ has a single root at $z = \rho$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stationarity condition:} \quad & \vert \rho \vert < 1 \\
\text{Unit root condition:} \quad & \rho = 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \gamma = 0 \\
\text{Explosive (non-stationary):} \quad & \vert \rho \vert > 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \gamma > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* When $\gamma < 0$, shocks to $y_t$ decay geometrically over time — the defining property of stationarity. When $\gamma = 0$, shocks persist forever — the defining property of a unit root.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing DF and ADF Tests

| Feature | Dickey-Fuller (DF) Test | Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) Test |
|:--------|:------------------------|:-----------------------------------|
| **Underlying Process** | AR(1) only | AR($p$) of arbitrary order |
| **Equation Form** | $\Delta y_t = \gamma y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | $\Delta y_t = \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$ |
| **Number of Right-Side Variables** | 1 (the lagged level) | $1 + p$ (lagged level + $p$ lagged differences) |
| **Hypotheses** | $H_0: \gamma = 0$ vs $H_1: \gamma < 0$ | Identical to DF |
| **Critical Values** | Dickey-Fuller tables (non-standard) | Identical to DF tables |
| **Risk of Invalid Inference** | High if true process is AR($p$), $p \geq 2$ | Minimal when lag order is correctly chosen |
| **Test Statistic** | $\tau = \hat{\gamma} / SE(\hat{\gamma})$ | Identical formula |
| **Degrees of Freedom** | $T - 2$ (Model 1) | $T - (p + k)$, where $k$ is deterministic terms |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Illustrative)

**Problem:** Given a time series of length $T = 100$, after running the ADF regression (Model 2 — constant, no trend) with $p = 2$ lags, we obtain:

- $\hat{\gamma} = -0.3421$
- Standard error of $\hat{\gamma}$: $SE(\hat{\gamma}) = 0.1234$

**Step 1 — Compute the ADF test statistic:**

$$
\tau = \frac{\hat{\gamma}}{SE(\hat{\gamma})} = \frac{-0.3421}{0.1234} = -2.7723
$$

**Step 2 — Compare to the 5% critical value for Model 2 (with constant):**

From the Dickey-Fuller table: $\tau_{0.05} = -2.8612$

**Step 3 — Apply the decision rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{computed} &= -2.7723 \\
\tau_{critical} &= -2.8612 \\
\text{Since } -2.7723 &> -2.8612, \text{ we FAIL TO REJECT } H_0.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

The series contains a **unit root** and is **non-stationary**. The first-differenced series $\Delta y_t$ should be examined (or further differencing/seasonal differencing applied) before fitting a stationary model.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Error:** Students often confuse $-2.7723$ being "more negative" than $-2.8612$. In fact, $-2.7723$ is **closer to zero** (less negative), so we fail to reject. Always draw a number line to verify!

## 3.5 Generalized Power and Size Properties

- **As $T \to \infty$:** The ADF test has **unit power** against the stationary alternative, meaning it correctly detects stationarity with probability approaching 1.
- **Finite samples:** The test has low power when the autoregressive root is **near unity** (e.g., $\rho = 0.95$). This is a well-known drawback.
- **Size distortions:** The ADF test can over-reject $H_0$ in finite samples, especially with incorrectly specified deterministic terms. The Elliott–Rothenberg–Stock (1996) **DF-GLS** test is a more powerful alternative.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ADF Test Decision Flow

The following Mermaid block provides a comprehensive block-level functional architecture for the Augmented Dickey-Fuller testing procedure, capturing all decision branches encountered in KTU exam-style problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Time Series Data y sub t] --> B[Step 1: Plot and Visually Inspect]
    B --> C{Deterministic Structure}
    C -->|No Level, No Trend| D1[Model 1: Pure Random Walk]
    C -->|With Constant| D2[Model 2: With Drift]
    C -->|With Constant and Trend| D3[Model 3: With Drift and Trend]
    D1 --> E[Step 2: Choose Lag Order p]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F{Selection Criterion}
    F -->|AIC| G1[Minimize AIC over p]
    F -->|SIC or BIC| G2[Minimize SIC over p]
    F -->|General to Specific| G3[Sequential Lag Dropping]
    G1 --> H[Step 3: Estimate ADF Regression by OLS]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Step 4: Extract tau Statistic]
    I --> J[Step 5: Lookup Dickey Fuller Critical Value]
    J --> K{Comparison Decision}
    K -->|tau less than critical value| L1[Reject H zero: Series is Stationary]
    K -->|tau greater than critical value| L2[Fail to Reject H zero: Unit Root Exists]
    L1 --> M1[Proceed to ARIMA Modeling with Original Series]
    L2 --> M2[Difference the Series and Retest]
    M2 --> N{Stationary After Differencing}
    N -->|Yes| M3[Fit ARIMA Model]
    N -->|No| M4[Apply Second or Seasonal Differencing]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Architecture: ADF vs DF Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[AR 1 Model] --> B1[Standard Dickey Fuller Test]
    A2[AR p Model of Order p greater than 1] --> B2[Augmented Dickey Fuller Test]
    B1 --> C1[Single Lagged Level Term]
    B2 --> C2[Lagged Level Plus Lagged Differences]
    C1 --> D1[Uses DF Critical Values]
    C2 --> D2[Uses DF Critical Values Identical]
    D1 --> E1[Risk: Invalid Inference if AR p true]
    D2 --> E2[Robust to Higher Order Dynamics]
```

## 4.3 Hypothesis Testing Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H0[H zero: gamma equals zero, unit root present] -->|Test Statistic more negative than critical| R[Reject H zero]
    H0 -->|Test Statistic not more negative than critical| F[Fail to Reject H zero]
    R --> S1[Stationary Series: No Differencing Required]
    F --> S2[Non Stationary Series: Apply First Difference]
    S2 --> R2[Re Run ADF on Differenced Series]
    R2 --> H0
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain the limitation of the standard Dickey-Fuller test that motivates the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test.**

**Model Answer:**

The standard Dickey-Fuller (DF) test assumes that the underlying data generating process follows a **first-order autoregressive model**, AR(1), of the form $y_t = \rho y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$. However, in many real-world time series — particularly economic, financial, and engineering data — the true process is of higher order, such as AR(2), AR(3), or higher.

When the DF test is applied to a series that is actually generated by an AR($p$) process with $p \geq 2$, the error term in the test equation is no longer white noise — it contains **autocorrelation** from the omitted higher-order dynamics. This violates a key OLS assumption, leading to:

1. **Biased** estimates of $\hat{\gamma}$
2. **Invalid** standard errors
3. **Unreliable** t-statistics that do not follow the Dickey-Fuller distribution

The **Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test** overcomes this limitation by augmenting the right-hand side of the regression with $p-1$ lagged first-differences of the series, which absorb the higher-order AR dynamics and ensure the residual $\varepsilon_t$ is approximately white noise. The null hypothesis, test statistic, and critical values remain identical to the DF test, but inference becomes valid for AR($p$) processes of any order. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and explain the null and alternative hypotheses of the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test. Why is a one-sided test used?**

**Model Answer:**

The hypotheses of the ADF test are formulated in terms of the test parameter $\gamma = \rho - 1$:

- **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$): $\gamma = 0$** — This is the unit root condition, equivalent to $\rho = 1$. Under $H_0$, shocks to the series have **permanent effects**, and the series is **non-stationary**.

- **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$): $\gamma < 0$** — This is the stationarity condition, equivalent to $\vert \rho \vert < 1$. Under $H_1$, shocks **decay over time** and the series reverts to its mean, satisfying the **weak stationarity** property.

A **one-sided (left-tailed) test** is used because:
1. Stationarity is a one-directional condition ($\gamma < 0$ only).
2. The case $\gamma > 0$ corresponds to an **explosive** (non-stationary) process, which is a different kind of non-stationarity and is rarely tested in standard applications.
3. The Dickey-Fuller distribution is **asymmetric**, with critical values defined only for the left tail.

**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $\tau < \tau_{critical}$ (i.e., the computed statistic is more negative than the critical value). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q. (a) Derive the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test equation from a general AR($p$) process. Clearly state the null and alternative hypotheses. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Begin with a general AR($p$) process:**

$$y_t = \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \phi_2 y_{t-2} + \cdots + \phi_p y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 2 — Subtract $y_{t-1}$ from both sides and rearrange to isolate the unit root component:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_t - y_{t-1} &= (\phi_1 - 1) y_{t-1} + \phi_2 y_{t-2} + \cdots + \phi_p y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t \\
\Delta y_t &= (\phi_1 - 1) y_{t-1} + \phi_2 y_{t-2} + \cdots + \phi_p y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite lagged levels in terms of lagged first-differences:**

Using the identity $y_{t-k} = y_{t-1} - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \Delta y_{t-j}$, we can substitute and collect terms. After algebraic manipulation, the equation becomes:

$$\Delta y_t = \gamma \, y_{t-1} + \delta_1 \Delta y_{t-1} + \delta_2 \Delta y_{t-2} + \cdots + \delta_{p-1} \Delta y_{t-(p-1)} + \varepsilon_t$$

where $\gamma = \phi_1 + \phi_2 + \cdots + \phi_p - 1$ and $\delta_i$ are functions of the original $\phi_j$ coefficients.

**Step 4 — State the hypotheses:**

- $H_0 : \gamma = 0$ (unit root, non-stationary)
- $H_1 : \gamma < 0$ (no unit root, stationary)

**Step 5 — The OLS estimate of $\gamma$ divided by its standard error gives the ADF test statistic $\tau$, compared to Dickey-Fuller critical values.** **(7 marks)**

---

**Q. (b) Discuss the procedure for selecting the lag order $p$ in the ADF test. What are the consequences of choosing $p$ too small or too large? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The lag order $p$ in the ADF regression controls the trade-off between **bias** and **efficiency**. There are three standard procedures:

**1. Information Criteria (Most Common):**

- **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC):** $AIC(p) = \ln(\hat{\sigma}^2_p) + \frac{2p}{T}$

  Choose $p$ that **minimizes** $AIC(p)$. Tends to select larger $p$.

- **Schwarz / Bayesian Information Criterion (SIC / BIC):** $SIC(p) = \ln(\hat{\sigma}^2_p) + \frac{p \ln T}{T}$

  Choose $p$ that **minimizes** $SIC(p)$. Tends to select smaller, **parsimonious** $p$ because the $\ln T$ penalty grows with sample size.

**2. General-to-Specific (Hendry's Approach):**

Start with a sufficiently large $p_{max}$ (e.g., $p_{max} = \lfloor 12 (T/100)^{1/4} \rfloor$ per Schwert's rule). Sequentially drop the **least significant** lagged term (highest $p$-value) until the highest-order retained lag is significant at the 5% level.

**3. Sequential Testing:**

Test the significance of the highest-order lag $\delta_p$ at the 5% level. If insignificant, reduce $p$ by 1 and retest.

**Consequences of Poor Lag Choice:**

| Scenario | Consequence |
|:---------|:------------|
| **$p$ too small** | Residual autocorrelation remains → **invalid inference**, biased $\hat{\gamma}$, oversized test |
| **$p$ too large** | Loss of degrees of freedom → **reduced test power**, especially near the unit root |
| **$p$ correctly chosen** | Residual is approximately white noise → valid Dickey-Fuller distribution applies |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**
> - Students often forget to write the *general AR(p)* equation before manipulation — **always start from the full model**.
> - Many confuse $\gamma = \phi_1 - 1$ (for AR(1)) with $\gamma = \sum \phi_i - 1$ (for AR($p$)). The full sum is required for AR($p$).
> - Lag order justification must include **both** selection criterion *and* the consequence of mis-selection.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q. (a) Explain the three model forms of the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test. For each, state when it is appropriate and provide the relevant 5% critical value from the Dickey-Fuller distribution. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The ADF test admits three model forms based on the deterministic specification:

**Form 1 — No Constant, No Trend:**

$$\Delta y_t = \gamma \, y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \, \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$$

- **When appropriate:** Series has zero mean and no deterministic trend — e.g., centered residuals or theoretical constructs.
- **5% Critical Value:** $\tau_{0.05} = -1.9393$
- **Rarely used in practice.**

**Form 2 — With Constant (Drift), No Trend:**

$$\Delta y_t = \alpha + \gamma \, y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \, \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$$

- **When appropriate:** Series has a non-zero mean (most economic and engineering data). The constant allows for a non-zero intercept.
- **5% Critical Value:** $\tau_{0.05} = -2.8612$
- **Most commonly used form.**

**Form 3 — With Constant and Linear Time Trend:**

$$\Delta y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma \, y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \, \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$$

- **When appropriate:** Series exhibits a deterministic trend (e.g., GDP, temperature, population). The trend term captures the systematic growth.
- **5% Critical Value:** $\tau_{0.05} = -3.4100$
- **Used cautiously** — over-specifying trends can reduce power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Heuristic:** Plot the series first. If a clear upward/downward slope is visible, use Form 3. If the series oscillates around a constant mean, use Form 2. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q. (b) A time series of length $T = 200$ is tested using the ADF regression (with constant, no trend) with $p = 3$ lags. The estimated coefficient on $y_{t-1}$ is $\hat{\gamma} = -0.185$ with standard error $0.078$. Test the null hypothesis of a unit root at the 5% significance level. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the hypotheses:**

- $H_0 : \gamma = 0$ (unit root present → non-stationary)
- $H_1 : \gamma < 0$ (no unit root → stationary)

**Step 2 — Compute the ADF test statistic:**

$$
\tau = \frac{\hat{\gamma}}{SE(\hat{\gamma})} = \frac{-0.185}{0.078} = -2.3718
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the critical value:**

For ADF Model 2 (with constant, no trend) at the 5% level: $\tau_{0.05} = -2.8612$

**Step 4 — Apply the decision rule (one-sided left-tail test):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{computed} &= -2.3718 \\
\tau_{critical} &= -2.8612 \\
\text{Comparison: } -2.3718 &> -2.8612
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the computed test statistic is **less negative** (i.e., greater) than the critical value, we **fail to reject** the null hypothesis.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

At the 5% significance level, there is **insufficient evidence** to conclude that the series is stationary. The series contains a **unit root** and is **non-stationary**. The next step is to difference the series and re-apply the ADF test on $\Delta y_t$. If the differenced series is stationary, an ARIMA($p, 1, q$) model can be fitted. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> - **Sign comparison trap:** Many students incorrectly write "since $-2.3718 < -2.8612$" — a sign error. The correct statement is $-2.3718$ is **closer to zero** than $-2.8612$.
> - Students often use a standard t-distribution critical value ($-1.96$ for 5%). **This is wrong** — the ADF test requires Dickey-Fuller non-standard critical values.
> - Always specify which model form (1, 2, or 3) is being used before quoting a critical value.
> - Forgetting to state the decision rule before computing loses marks for procedural completeness.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test**

- [ ] **Purpose:** Tests for the presence of a unit root (non-stationarity) in a time series.
- [ ] **General Equation:** $\Delta y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$
- [ ] **Test Parameter:** $\gamma = \rho - 1$, where $\rho$ is the autoregressive coefficient.
- [ ] **Null Hypothesis:** $H_0 : \gamma = 0$ (unit root, **non-stationary**)
- [ ] **Alternative:** $H_1 : \gamma < 0$ (**stationary**)
- [ ] **One-Sided Left-Tail Test:** Reject $H_0$ only if $\tau$ is **more negative** than critical value.
- [ ] **Three Model Forms:** (1) None, (2) Constant only, (3) Constant + Trend.
- [ ] **Critical Values:** **Non-standard** Dickey-Fuller tables — NOT the Student-$t$ distribution.
- [ ] **Common 5% Critical Values:** $-1.9393$ (none), $-2.8612$ (constant), $-3.4100$ (trend).
- [ ] **Lag Order $p$:** Selected via AIC, SIC, or General-to-Specific. Wrong $p$ = invalid inference.
- [ ] **Augmentation Logic:** Lagged differences $\Delta y_{t-i}$ absorb higher-order AR dynamics.
- [ ] **DF Limitation:** Valid only for AR(1). ADF extends to AR($p$) of any order.
- [ ] **Degrees of Freedom:** $T - p - k$, where $k$ is the number of deterministic terms.
- [ ] **Decision After Failing to Reject:** Difference the series and re-test on $\Delta y_t$.
- [ ] **Practical Note:** The ADF test has **low power** when the true root is near unity (e.g., $\rho = 0.95$).
- [ ] **Alternative Test:** DF-GLS (Elliott-Rothenberg-Stock, 1996) is more powerful in finite samples.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Stationary Data Techniques - Differencing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Using Stationary Data Techniques - Differencing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stationarity — The Bedrock of Time Series Modeling

Before we discuss differencing, we must formally lock in the concept of **stationarity**, because differencing exists for one and only one reason — to *manufacture* stationarity from non-stationary data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Strict Stationarity):** A stochastic process $\{X_t\}$ is *strictly stationary* if the joint distribution of $(X_{t_1}, X_{t_2}, \ldots, X_{t_k})$ is identical to the joint distribution of $(X_{t_1+h}, X_{t_2+h}, \ldots, X_{t_k+h})$ for every $k \in \mathbb{N}$ and every time shift $h$. In plain words — the statistical fingerprint of the series does not change with time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Weak / Wide-Sense Stationarity):** A process is *weakly stationary* (the version used in **99% of KTU and Box–Jenkins practice**) if it satisfies three conditions:
> 1. $E[X_t] = \mu$ is constant (does not depend on $t$).
> 2. $\text{Var}(X_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty$ is finite and constant.
> 3. $\text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t+h}) = \gamma(h)$ depends **only on the lag $h$**, not on the absolute time $t$.

## 1.2 What is Differencing?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Differencing):** *Differencing* is a non-parametric, deterministic transformation applied to a non-stationary time series $\{X_t\}$ that replaces each observation with the change between consecutive observations. The resulting *differenced series* often exhibits constant mean and constant variance, i.e., becomes stationary.

The **first difference** of a series $\{X_t\}$ is defined mathematically as:

$$\nabla X_t = X_t - X_{t-1}$$

where $\nabla$ (read "nabla" or "delta") is the **difference operator**. The transformed series $\{\nabla X_t\}$ may itself be differenced again, giving a **second difference**:

$$\nabla^2 X_t = \nabla(\nabla X_t) = \nabla X_t - \nabla X_{t-1}$$

## 1.3 The Backshift (Lag) Operator

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Backshift Operator $B$):** The *backshift operator* $B$ is a notational shorthand that shifts a series one step back in time. Formally, $B X_t = X_{t-1}$. Iterating $k$ times: $B^k X_t = X_{t-1}$.

Using $B$, the first difference can be written compactly as:

$$\nabla X_t = (1 - B) X_t$$

and the seasonal difference with period $s$ as:

$$\nabla_s X_t = (1 - B^s) X_t$$

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English

> [!TIP]
> **Think of differencing as measuring *velocity* rather than *position*.** Imagine a car whose odometer (position) is constantly rising — the odometer reading is *non-stationary* because the value keeps changing. However, the *speed* of the car (change per unit time) might hover around a steady 60 km/h — that is *stationary*.
>
> - The **odometer reading** $= X_t$ (non-stationary, trending upward).
> - The **change in odometer per second** $= X_t - X_{t-1}$ (stationary, the "differenced" series).
>
> Differencing is the statistical equivalent of throwing away the absolute values and keeping only the *changes*. This is exactly why financial analysts work with **stock returns** (percentage changes) rather than **stock prices** (absolute levels) — returns are stationary; prices generally are not.

A second analogy: **temperature fluctuation vs. absolute temperature.** The Earth's average temperature drifts up due to global warming (non-stationary). But the *difference* between today's temperature and yesterday's remains roughly stable (stationary). Differencing strips out the drift and keeps the local behavior.

## 1.5 Why Differencing is Needed — The Engineering Motivation

| Phenomenon in Data | Symptom in Plots | Differencing Fixes? |
|---|---|---|
| Linear trend (e.g., population growth) | Mean drifts upward | ✅ First difference |
| Quadratic trend | Mean curvature | ✅ Second difference |
| Random walk (e.g., stock prices) | Variance grows with $t$ | ✅ First difference |
| Seasonal pattern (e.g., quarterly sales) | Repeating peaks every $s$ steps | ✅ Seasonal difference |
| Heteroscedastic variance | Variance grows over time | ⚠️ Log transform first, then difference |
| Structural breaks | Sudden level shifts | ❌ Differencing will not fix this |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECDT861, Module 2):** *"Using Stationary Data Techniques — Differencing"* is the *transformative bridge* that converts a real-world, messy, non-stationary series into one to which the ARMA toolkit can be confidently applied. Differencing is the **'$d$'** in the famous **ARIMA$(p,d,q)$** model.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Hierarchies of Differencing

Time series data in engineering and economics exhibits non-stationarity at three hierarchical levels. Each level requires its own dedicated differencing operator.

### 2.1.1 First-Order (Regular) Differencing

Removes a **linear trend** and stabilizes the mean.

**Definition:**

$$\nabla X_t = X_t - X_{t-1} = (1 - B) X_t$$

**Why it works:** Suppose $X_t = a + bt + \varepsilon_t$ where $\varepsilon_t$ is white noise. Then:

$$\nabla X_t = (a + bt + \varepsilon_t) - (a + b(t-1) + \varepsilon_{t-1}) = b + (\varepsilon_t - \varepsilon_{t-1})$$

The drift $a + bt$ is annihilated, leaving a constant mean $b$ and a stationary MA(1)-type residual.

### 2.1.2 Second-Order Differencing

Required only when the original series exhibits a **quadratic trend** ($X_t = a + bt + ct^2 + \varepsilon_t$).

**Definition:**

$$\nabla^2 X_t = \nabla X_t - \nabla X_{t-1}$$

**Expanded form:**

$$\nabla^2 X_t = X_t - 2X_{t-1} + X_{t-2} = (1 - B)^2 X_t$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Box & Jenkins Caution:** *"Over-differencing is worse than under-differencing."* If the original series only needs one round of differencing, applying $\nabla^2$ injects *additional* moving-average structure (it can turn a stationary ARMA into an ARIMA with unnecessary MA roots near the unit circle), increasing standard errors of forecasts. **Never difference twice by reflex.**

### 2.1.3 Seasonal Differencing

Applied to series with **periodic fluctuations** of known period $s$ (e.g., $s = 12$ for monthly data, $s = 4$ for quarterly, $s = 7$ for daily with weekly cycle).

**Definition:**

$$\nabla_s X_t = X_t - X_{t-s} = (1 - B^s) X_t$$

For monthly data with annual seasonality, $s = 12$:

$$\nabla_{12} X_t = X_t - X_{t-12}$$

**Combined Operator:** When both trend and seasonality are present, the *full transformation* is:

$$W_t = \nabla^d \nabla_s^D X_t$$

where $d$ is the number of regular differences and $D$ is the number of seasonal differences. The combined series $W_t$ is then modeled as **ARIMA$(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_s$**.

## 2.2 Algebraic Properties of the Backshift Operator $B$

The operator $B$ behaves like a *polynomial* in the algebraic ring of operators, with $B$ as the "indeterminate."

| Property | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Identity | $B^0 X_t = X_t$ | Zero shifts leave the series unchanged |
| Linearity | $B(aX_t + cY_t) = a BX_t + c BY_t$ | $B$ is a linear operator |
| Distributivity | $(1 - B)X_t = X_t - BX_t$ | Operator polynomial expands normally |
| Inverse | $B^{-1} X_t = F X_t = X_{t+1}$ | Forward shift |
| Multiplicative inverse of $\nabla$ | $\nabla^{-1} = (1-B)^{-1} = 1 + B + B^2 + B^3 + \cdots$ | Cumulative sum |
| Commutativity | $\nabla \cdot \nabla_s = \nabla_s \cdot \nabla$ | Order of differencing doesn't matter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric-Series Insight:** Since $(1-B)^{-1} = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} B^j$, the *inverse* of differencing is *summation*. This is intuitive: a series built by cumulating a stationary process is non-stationary, and differencing undoes this cumulation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Operator | LaTeX | Use-Case in KTU Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First difference | $\nabla X_t = X_t - X_{t-1}$ | Removing linear trend |
| 2 | Second difference | $\nabla^2 X_t = X_t - 2X_{t-1} + X_{t-2}$ | Removing quadratic trend |
| 3 | Seasonal difference | $\nabla_s X_t = X_t - X_{t-s}$ | Removing period-$s$ cycle |
| 4 | Backshift definition | $B X_t = X_{t-1}$ | Compact notation |
| 5 | First diff via $B$ | $\nabla = 1 - B$ | Operator algebra |
| 6 | Seasonal diff via $B$ | $\nabla_s = 1 - B^s$ | Seasonal operator |
| 7 | Combined operator | $W_t = (1-B)^d (1-B^s)^D X_t$ | General ARIMA transformation |
| 8 | Random walk | $X_t = X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | Canonical non-stationary process |
| 9 | Mean after differencing | $E[\nabla X_t] = E[X_t] - E[X_{t-1}]$ | For $X_t = a + bt + \varepsilon_t$, gives $b$ |
| 10 | Mean after 2nd differencing | $E[\nabla^2 X_t] = 0$ | When trend is at most linear |

## 2.4 Unit Roots — The Mathematical Reason Differencing is Needed

A process $\{X_t\}$ has a **unit root** if the characteristic polynomial $\phi(B) = 0$ has a root on the unit circle $\vert B \vert = 1$.

For example, the random walk $X_t = X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ can be rewritten as $(1 - B)X_t = \varepsilon_t$. The characteristic polynomial $\phi(B) = 1 - B$ has root $B = 1$ — *exactly on the unit circle*. This single fact is the mathematical signature of non-stationarity.

Differencing the random walk:

$$\nabla X_t = (1 - B)X_t = \varepsilon_t$$

multiplies both sides by $(1 - B)$, which **annihilates the unit root**, leaving only white noise — a stationary process.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Differencing is the foundation of **ARIMA modeling for load forecasting** (electrical grids), **demand forecasting** (supply chains), **economic indicator smoothing** (CPI, GDP), and **signal preprocessing** in control engineering (removing DC drift from accelerometer data). Every modern forecasting library — *forecast*, *statsmodels*, *prophet* — begins its pipeline with an automatic unit-root test (ADF / KPSS) followed by differencing.

## 2.5 Diagnostic Tests for the Order of Differencing

| Test | Null Hypothesis ($H_0$) | Decision Rule |
|---|---|---|
| **Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF)** | Series has a unit root (non-stationary) | Reject $H_0 \Rightarrow$ stationary; do **not** difference |
| **KPSS Test** | Series is stationary | Reject $H_0 \Rightarrow$ non-stationary; **do** difference |
| **Phillips-Perron (PP)** | Series has a unit root | Non-parametric alternative to ADF |
| **ACF Inspection** | Slow, linear decay of ACF | Slow linear decay $\Rightarrow$ difference |
| **PACF Inspection** | First lag PACF $\approx 1$ | $PACF(1) \approx 1 \Rightarrow$ difference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on conflicting tests:** ADF and KPSS have *opposite* nulls. The *KTU-recommended practice* (per Box–Jenkins) is to first inspect the **ACF**: a slow, non-exponential decay (correlation remains large for many lags) signals a unit root and triggers differencing.
