```mermaid
        R3[PACF Tails Off] --> R4[After Lag P]
    end

    style A1 fill:FFECB3,stroke:FF6F00,color:#000
    style A3 fill:C8E6C9,stroke:1B5E20,color:#000
    style M1 fill:C8E6C9,stroke:1B5E20,color:#000
    style M3 fill:FFECB3,stroke:FF6F00,color:#000
    style R1 fill:FFECB3,stroke:FF6F00,color:#000
    style R3 fill:FFECB3,stroke:FF6F00,color:#000
```

> **Reading Guide:** **Cuts off** = exactly zero after the named lag. **Tails off** = gradual geometric or sinusoidal decay. The **cutoff pattern** of PACF uniquely identifies the AR order $p$.

---

### 4.3 Cross-Correlation Analysis Workflow (with Pre-Whitening)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[START Raw Bivariate Series Xt Yt] --> P1[Step 1 Plot Both Series and Examine Stationarity]
    P1 --> P2{Is Xt Autocorrelated}
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[Step 2 Fit ARMA Model to Xt phi B Xt equals theta B alpha t]
    P3 --> P4[Step 3 Apply Filter to Both Xt and Yt]
    P4 --> P5[Alpha t equals psi B Xt Beta t equals psi B Yt]
    P2 -- No --> P6[Skip Pre Whitening]
    P6 --> P7
    P5 --> P7[Step 4 Compute Sample CCF rho hat alpha beta k]
    P7 --> P8[Step 5 Draw 95 Percent Confidence Bands plus or minus 1.96 over sqrt n]
    P8 --> P9[Step 6 Identify Significant Lags Where CCF Exceeds Bands]
    P9 --> P10[Step 7 Interpret Lags as Impulse Response Weights]
    P10 --> P11[Step 8 Fit Transfer Function Model nu B]
    P11 --> E[END Transfer Function Identified]

    style S fill:E3F2FD,stroke:0D47A1,color:#000
    style P3 fill:FFF9C4,stroke:F57F17,color:#000
    style P5 fill:FFF9C4,stroke:F57F17,color:#000
    style P9 fill:C8E6C9,stroke:1B5E20,color:#000
    style P10 fill:C8E6C9,stroke:1B5E20,color:#000
    style E fill:FFCDD2,stroke:B71C1C,color:#000
```

> **Reading Guide:** Yellow nodes = pre-whitening steps (mandatory when $X_t$ is autocorrelated). Green nodes = interpretation and model identification. The CCF pattern at significant lags gives the impulse response shape.

---

### 4.4 PACF — Conceptual Block Diagram (Partial Correlation Mechanism)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[XT Minus K] -->|Direct Effect| X2[XT]
    X1 -->|Indirect 1| X3[XT Minus K Plus 1]
    X3 --> X4[XT Minus K Plus 2]
    X4 --> X5[XT Minus 1]
    X5 --> X2

    PACF[PACF Partials Out Intermediate Lags] --> X3
    PACF --> X4
    PACF --> X5

    style X1 fill:BBDEFB,stroke:0D47A1,color:#000
    style X2 fill:BBDEFB,stroke:0D47A1,color:#000
    style X3 fill:F8BBD0,stroke:880E4F,color:#000
    style X4 fill:F8BBD0,stroke:880E4F,color:#000
    style X5 fill:F8BBD0,stroke:880E4F,color:#000
    style PACF fill:C8E6C9,stroke:1B5E20,color:#000
```

> **Reading Guide:** Blue nodes = the two observations whose *direct* correlation is being measured. Pink nodes = intermediate observations that are *partialled out* (statistically removed) by the PACF operation. The green node = the PACF mechanism itself.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

> Define the **Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF)**. State the **key property** that makes PACF useful for identifying the order of an AR model.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF) at lag $k$, denoted $\phi_{kk}$, is defined as the correlation between $X_t$ and $X_{t-k}$ after the linear effect of the intermediate observations $X_{t-1}, X_{t-2}, \dots, X_{t-k+1}$ has been removed:

$$
\phi_{kk} = \text{Corr}(X_t,\, X_{t-k} \mid X_{t-1}, X_{t-2}, \dots, X_{t-k+1})
$$

**Key property:** For an **AR($p$) process**, the theoretical PACF satisfies $\phi_{kk} = 0$ for all $k > p$ — it *cuts off* abruptly after lag $p$. In contrast, the ACF *tails off* (decays gradually) for an AR process. This cutoff property uniquely identifies $p$, making the PACF the primary diagnostic for the order of an autoregressive model in the Box–Jenkins methodology.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> What is the **Cross Correlation Function (CCF)**? Why is the **pre-whitening** step necessary before computing a CCF?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Cross Correlation Function (CCF) between two series $\{X_t\}$ and $\{Y_t\}$ at lag $k$ is:

$$
\rho_{XY}(k) = \frac{E[(X_t - \mu_X)(Y_{t+k} - \mu_Y)]}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}, \qquad k = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots
$$

It measures the linear association between $X$ and $Y$ at varying time delays. **Pre-whitening is necessary** because, if the input series $X_t$ is autocorrelated (has AR/MA/ARMA structure), then the lags of $X_t$ are correlated with one another, and this internal correlation *leaks* into the CCF, producing a **spurious, smooth, and uninterpretable pattern**. By applying an ARMA filter $\psi(B) = \theta^{-1}(B)\phi(B)$ fitted to $X_t$ to *both* $X_t$ and $Y_t$, the input is converted to white noise $\alpha_t$, and the resulting CCF $\rho_{\alpha\beta}(k)$ directly estimates the impulse response weights $\nu_k$ of the underlying transfer function — yielding a clean, interpretable diagnostic.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### ⭐ Question A — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Durbin–Levinson recursion** for computing the PACF from the ACF. Clearly state the role of the projection theorem in your derivation.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** For a stationary time series, the first three autocorrelations are given as $\rho_1 = 0.6$, $\rho_2 = 0.4$, and $\rho_3 = 0.2$. Compute $\phi_{1,1}, \phi_{2,2}, \phi_{3,3}$ using the recursion. Based on the PACF values, suggest a tentative **AR model order**.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:**

We wish to compute $\phi_{kk} = \text{Corr}(X_t, X_{t-k} \mid X_{t-1}, \dots, X_{t-k+1})$.

*Step 1 (Projection Setup — 2 marks):* By the **projection theorem** in Hilbert space, the best linear predictor of $X_t$ using the intermediate $k-1$ lags is:

$$
\hat{X}_t^{(k-1)} = \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \phi_{k-1,j}\, X_{t-j}
$$

The residual $U_t = X_t - \hat{X}_t^{(k-1)}$ is orthogonal to the span of $\{X_{t-1}, \dots, X_{t-k+1}\}$. Similarly define the residual $V_{t-k} = X_{t-k} - \hat{X}_{t-k}^{(k-1)}$.

*Step 2 (Definition of PACF — 1 mark):*

$$
\phi_{kk} = \frac{E[U_t\, V_{t-k}]}{\sqrt{E[U_t^2]\, E[V_{t-k}^2]}}
$$

*Step 3 (Numerator — 2 marks):* Expanding $E[U_t V_{t-k}]$ and using the stationarity $E[X_a X_b] = \gamma(\vert a - b \vert)$:

$$
E[U_t V_{t-k}] = \gamma(0)\!\left[\rho_k - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \phi_{k-1,j}\, \rho_{k-j}\right]
$$

The double sum cancels via the **Yule–Walker recursion** for $\phi_{k-1,j}$.

*Step 4 (Denominator — 1 mark):* The denominator is the **variance reduction** when adding the $k$-th lag to the predictor:

$$
E[U_t^2] = \gamma(0)\!\left[1 - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \phi_{k-1,j}\, \rho_j\right]
$$

*Step 5 (Final Formula — 1 mark):*

$$
\boxed{\;\phi_{kk} = \frac{\rho_k - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \phi_{k-1,j}\, \rho_{k-j}}{1 - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \phi_{k-1,j}\, \rho_j}\;}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 marks]:**

| **Step** | **Computation** | **Result** |
|:---------|:----------------|:-----------|
| $\phi_{1,1}$ | $\phi_{1,1} = \rho_1$ | $\phi_{1,1} = 0.6$ |
| $\phi_{2,2}$ numerator | $0.4 - (0.6)(0.6) = 0.04$ | — |
| $\phi_{2,2}$ denominator | $1 - (0.6)(0.6) = 0.64$ | $\phi_{2,2} = 0.04 / 0.64 = 0.0625$ |
| $\phi_{2,1}$ update | $0.6 - (0.0625)(0.6) = 0.5625$ | $\phi_{2,1} = 0.5625$ |
| $\phi_{3,3}$ numerator | $0.2 - (0.5625)(0.4) - (0.0625)(0.6) = 0.2 - 0.225 - 0.0375 = -0.0625$ | — |
| $\phi_{3,3}$ denominator | $1 - (0.5625)(0.6) - (0.0625)(0.4) = 1 - 0.3375 - 0.025 = 0.6375$ | $\phi_{3,3} = -0.0625 / 0.6375 \approx -0.098$ |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_{1,1} &= 0.6 \\
\phi_{2,2} &= 0.0625 \\
\phi_{3,3} &\approx -0.098
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Identification (1 mark):** Since $\phi_{2,2} = 0.0625$ is **small and statistically insignificant** and $\phi_{3,3} \approx -0.098$ is also near zero (compared to $\phi_{1,1} = 0.6$ which is large), the PACF **cuts off after lag 1** (effectively, in practice). This suggests an **AR(1) model** is appropriate:

$$
X_t = 0.6\, X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t
$$

> *Note:* If the cutoff were exactly at lag 2, the choice would be AR(2). The student should always check the relative magnitudes of $\phi_{kk}$ against the standard error $\approx 1/\sqrt{n}$ for true identification.

---

#### ⭐ Question B — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Define the **Cross Correlation Function (CCF)** between two time series $X_t$ and $Y_t$. State and prove the **asymmetry property** $\rho_{XY}(k) = \rho_{YX}(-k)$. Explain why the CCF is **asymmetric** in general.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **pre-whitening procedure** for CCF analysis. Two time series $X_t$ (input) and $Y_t$ (output) are observed. An AR(1) model $X_t = 0.7 X_{t-1} + \alpha_t$ is fitted to $X_t$, where $\alpha_t \sim WN(0,1)$. Show how the filter is applied to *both* series to obtain the pre-whitened residuals $\alpha_t$ and $\beta_t$, and explain why the resulting CCF $\rho_{\alpha\beta}(k)$ is interpretable.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — CCF Definition and Asymmetry [7 marks]:**

*Step 1 (Definition — 2 marks):* For two weakly stationary series $\{X_t\}$ and $\{Y_t\}$, the CCF at lag $k$ is:

$$
\rho_{XY}(k) = \frac{\gamma_{XY}(k)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y} = \frac{E[(X_t - \mu_X)(Y_{t+k} - \mu_Y)]}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}, \qquad k \in \mathbb{Z}
$$

*Step 2 (Asymmetry Statement — 1 mark):* We claim that $\rho_{XY}(k) = \rho_{YX}(-k)$.

*Step 3 (Proof — 3 marks):* Define $\rho_{YX}(-k)$:

$$
\rho_{YX}(-k) = \frac{E[(Y_t - \mu_Y)(X_{t-k} - \mu_X)]}{\sigma_Y \sigma_X}
$$

By **stationarity**, the time origin is arbitrary. Set $s = t - k$ (so $t = s + k$). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{YX}(-k) &= \frac{E[(Y_{s+k} - \mu_Y)(X_s - \mu_X)]}{\sigma_Y \sigma_X} \\
&= \frac{E[(X_s - \mu_X)(Y_{s+k} - \mu_Y)]}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y} \\
&= \rho_{XY}(k) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

The key step is the **substitution of time index** using stationarity, which relabels the future of $Y$ relative to the present of $X$.

*Step 4 (Why Asymmetry — 1 mark):* The CCF is asymmetric because the *direction* of time matters. $\rho_{XY}(k) > 0$ means "$X$ leads $Y$ by $k$ periods"; $\rho_{XY}(-k) > 0$ means "$Y$ leads $X$ by $k$ periods." Reversing time (negative lag) reverses the lead-lag interpretation.

---

**Part (b) — Pre-Whitening [7 marks]:**

*Step 1 (Why pre-whiten — 2 marks):* When the input $X_t$ is autocorrelated (ARMA structure), successive lags of $X_t$ are correlated. The sample CCF $\hat{\rho}_{XY}(k)$ then becomes a **smeared, biased estimate** — the ACF structure of $X$ leaks into the CCF. Pre-whitening removes this leakage by transforming $X$ into a white-noise series.

*Step 2 (Fit the ARMA model — 1 mark):* In this problem, the input is AR(1):

$$
X_t = 0.7\, X_{t-1} + \alpha_t, \quad \alpha_t \sim WN(0,1)
$$

The filter is $\psi(B) = (1 - 0.7B)^{-1}$ applied *in reverse* — equivalently, compute the residual:

$$
\alpha_t = X_t - 0.7\, X_{t-1}
$$

*Step 3 (Apply the same filter to $Y_t$ — 2 marks):*

$$
\beta_t = Y_t - 0.7\, Y_{t-1}
$$

This is the AR(1) "differencing-like" filter applied to the output series.

*Step 4 (Why the result is interpretable — 2 marks):* By construction, $\alpha_t$ is **white noise** (uncorrelated across time). The CCF between white noise $\alpha_t$ and any series is **uncontaminated** by autocorrelation in the input — there is no "leakage" effect. Mathematically, if $Y_t = \nu(B) X_t + N_t$, then:

$$
\beta_t = \nu(B)\alpha_t + \eta_t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \gamma_{\alpha\beta}(k) = \nu_k \sigma_\alpha^2
$$

Thus $\rho_{\alpha\beta}(k)$ is **directly proportional to the impulse response weights $\nu_k$** of the underlying transfer function — giving a clean, interpretable cross-correlation pattern from which the transfer function can be identified.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PACF & CCF Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ACF and PACF notation** — ACF is $\rho_k$, PACF is $\phi_{kk}$. Writing $\phi_k$ instead of $\phi_{kk}$ is treated as a **notational error** and loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Skipping the pre-whitening step** in CCF problems when the input is autocorrelated — KTU examiners explicitly test this; skipping it costs **3–4 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting to update the existing $\phi_{k-1,j}$ coefficients** during the Durbin–Levinson recursion. The recursion requires **two simultaneous computations**: (i) the new $\phi_{k,k}$ and (ii) the update of all previous $\phi_{k,j}$. Many students compute only the first.
> 4. **Dividing by $n - k$ instead of $n$** in the sample CCF formula — KTU syllabus uses divisor $n$ to preserve positive semidefiniteness of the autocorrelation matrix. Using $n-k$ loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Failing to state the asymmetry property** $\rho_{XY}(k) = \rho_{YX}(-k)$ explicitly when asked to "define CCF" — examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for this.
> 6. **Forgetting the cutoff property** of PACF in AR($p$) — i.e., $\phi_{kk} = 0$ for $k > p$. This is the single most important theoretical fact and forgetting it loses 2 marks in model-identification questions.
> 7. **Inverting the lead–lag interpretation** — confusing $\rho_{XY}(k)$ (X leads Y) with $\rho_{XY}(-k)$ (Y leads X). The sign of $k$ matters!
> 8. **Not verifying stationarity** of both series before computing CCF — a prerequisite step worth 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **PACF definition:** $\phi_{kk} = \text{Corr}(X_t, X_{t-k} \mid X_{t-1}, \dots, X_{t-k+1})$ — measures **direct** correlation only.
- [x] **ACF vs. PACF role:** ACF → MA order identification (cutoff); PACF → AR order identification (cutoff).
- [x] **Durbin–Levinson recursion** — the universal algorithm to compute PACF from ACF, with two coupled recursions (new $\phi_{kk}$ + update of $\phi_{k,j}$).
- [x] **Yule–Walker equations** — link AR coefficients to ACF via a symmetric positive-definite Toeplitz system.
- [x] **AR($p$) PACF cuts off** at lag $p$ — this is the foundational Box–Jenkins identification rule.
- [x] **Sample ACF/CCF divisor** is $n$ (not $n - k$) in the KTU syllabus.
- [x] **CCF definition** uses $\sigma_X \sigma_Y$ in the denominator, not $\gamma_{XX}(0) \gamma_{YY}(0)$ (these are equivalent but the standard form is the former).
- [x] **Asymmetry property:** $\rho_{XY}(k) = \rho_{YX}(-k)$ — must be stated explicitly when defining CCF.
- [x] **Pre-whitening** is mandatory when the input series is autocorrelated; apply the *same* filter to both $X_t$ and $Y_t$.
- [x] **95% confidence band** for CCF under independence: $\pm 1.96 / \sqrt{n}$.
- [x] **Standard error of sample PACF** under the null $\phi_{kk} = 0$ is approximately $1/\sqrt{n}$.
- [x] **Lead–lag rule:** $\rho_{XY}(k) > 0$ at $k > 0$ means **$X$ leads $Y$** by $k$ periods.
- [x] **PACF of white noise:** $\phi_{11} = 0$ (and all subsequent $\phi_{kk} = 0$).
- [x] **CCF of two independent white-noise series:** $\rho_{XY}(k) \approx 0$ for all $k$ (within confidence bands).
- [x] **Engineering uses:** AR order selection (PACF), transfer function identification (CCF), distributed-lag regression (CCF), lead–lag forecasting (CCF).
- [x] **Pitfall:** Never compute CCF between two I(1) (non-stationary) series — the result is spurious due to spurious regression. Difference the series first.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autocorrelation Plots - Trend and Seasonality in ACF Plots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Autocorrelation Plots - Trend and Seasonality in ACF Plots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: The Autocorrelation Function (ACF)

The **Autocorrelation Function (ACF)** of a stationary stochastic process $\{X_t\}$ is defined as the normalized second-moment measure that quantifies the linear dependence between observations separated by a time lag $k$.

For a stationary time series with mean $\mu$ and autocovariance function $\gamma(k)$, the theoretical ACF is:

$$\rho(k) = \frac{\gamma(k)}{\gamma(0)} = \frac{E[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)]}{E[(X_t - \mu)^2]}$$

where:
- $\gamma(0) = \text{Var}(X_t)$ is the variance (autocovariance at lag zero)
- $\gamma(k)$ is the autocovariance at lag $k$
- $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ is the lag index

The **Sample Autocorrelation Function (SACF)** computed from a finite realization $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ is the plug-in estimator:

$$r_k = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t-k} - \bar{x})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})^2}$$

where $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} x_t$ is the sample mean.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Auto"?** The prefix *auto* means *self*. Autocorrelation is the correlation of a series with **its own past values**, not with a different variable (that would be *cross*-correlation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property:** $\rho(0) = 1$ always, since any random variable is perfectly correlated with itself. All other autocorrelations satisfy $-1 \le \rho(k) \le 1$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Memory" of a Time Series

Imagine a person telling you today's stock market price. Now ask them what tomorrow's price will be. If they reply, *"It will probably be near today's value,"* the series has a **strong memory** (high autocorrelation at lag 1). If instead they say, *"I have no idea — it could be anything,"* the series has **no memory** (white noise — ACF ≈ 0 at all positive lags).

**Real-world analogy — Monsoon rainfall in Kerala:**
- Today's rainfall is heavily influenced by **yesterday's rainfall** (a wet day tends to be followed by another wet day during monsoon onset).
- Rainfall in **July** is strongly correlated with rainfall in **July of the previous year** (a 12-month seasonal cycle).
- These two phenomena — *short-term persistence* and *long-term periodic repetition* — are exactly what the ACF plot reveals visually.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exams:** Always remember — the ACF is a *graphical diagnostic tool*. You are not asked to "compute" the ACF by hand in most problems. Instead, you are asked to *interpret* the shape of an ACF plot to identify trend, seasonality, or randomness.

## 1.3 The Three Diagnostic Signatures of an ACF Plot

The KTU syllabus specifically asks you to recognize the ACF signatures of:

| Pattern in Data | ACF Plot Signature |
|---|---|
| **Pure Trend** (no seasonality) | Slow, roughly linear decay; autocorrelations remain significant for many lags |
| **Pure Seasonality** (no trend) | Large spikes at **seasonal lags** (e.g., 12, 24, 36 for monthly data), with intervening lags near zero |
| **Trend + Seasonality** | Slow decay **enveloping** periodic seasonal peaks |
| **White Noise** (no pattern) | All autocorrelations within $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ confidence bands after lag 0 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Properties of $\rho(k)$

For a **weakly stationary** process:

1. **Boundedness:** $-1 \le \rho(k) \le 1$ for all $k \ge 0$.
2. **Normalization:** $\rho(0) = 1$ (correlation of $X_t$ with itself).
3. **Symmetry:** $\rho(k) = \rho(-k)$ for all $k$ (ACF plots only show $k \ge 0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Stationarity is the hidden assumption.** If the series is *not* stationary (which is exactly the case when a trend or seasonality is present), then $\rho(k)$ is **not strictly defined** in the classical sense. However, *sample* ACFs are still computed and *empirically interpreted* — and this interpretation is precisely the skill the KTU examiner tests.

## 2.2 Why Trend Produces a Slowly Decaying ACF

Suppose we observe $\{x_t\}$ with a deterministic linear trend:
$$x_t = \alpha + \beta t + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$$

The mean is *not* constant: $\mu_t = \alpha + \beta t$. Even so, the sample autocovariance is:

$$\hat{\gamma}(k) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x})$$

Because the trend term $\beta t$ dominates the sample mean deviation, the **trend is preserved in the autocovariance structure**. As $k$ increases:

- The pairs $(x_t, x_{t+k})$ are pulled apart by the trend, so their *product of deviations* is dominated by the trend contribution, not the noise.
- The autocovariance decreases **slowly** (almost linearly in $k$) rather than abruptly.
- Normalized by $\hat{\gamma}(0)$, the **ACF decays slowly** — typically staying well outside the confidence band for the first 10–20 lags or more.

**Geometric Intuition:** Think of the ACF as the "shape" of dependence. A *trending* series has *long-range dependence* because every point is related to *every other point* through the monotonic trend line. Hence, no matter how far apart the lags, some correlation remains.

## 2.3 Why Seasonality Produces Periodic Spikes

Suppose we observe $\{x_t\}$ with a deterministic seasonal pattern of period $s$ (e.g., $s = 12$ for monthly data):
$$x_t = \mu + S_t + \epsilon_t, \quad S_{t+s} = S_t$$

Then for lag $k = s$:
$$x_t \cdot x_{t+s} = (\mu + S_t + \epsilon_t)(\mu + S_{t+s} + \epsilon_{t+s})$$

Since $S_t = S_{t+s}$ (perfectly aligned seasonal components), the product contains a large positive term $S_t^2$ that *does not vanish* on averaging. This produces a **large positive autocorrelation at lag $s$**.

For lag $k = 2s$: again $S_{t+2s} = S_t$, so we get another peak. For lags *between* multiples of $s$, say $k = s-1$, the seasonal components are *out of phase* and average to near zero, so the autocorrelation is small.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Key Diagnostic Rule:**
> - A **single peak** at lag $k$ → possible outlier (insufficient evidence).
> - **Repeated peaks** at lags $s, 2s, 3s, \ldots$ → confirmed seasonality of period $s$.
> - **Slow linear/curved decay** → trend present.

## 2.4 The Confidence Band: When is a Spike "Significant"?

Under the null hypothesis that $\{X_t\}$ is **white noise**, Bartlett (1946) showed:
$$\text{Var}(r_k) \approx \frac{1}{n} \quad \text{for } k \ge 1$$

Hence the **95% confidence interval** for each $r_k$ is approximately:
$$r_k \in \left[ -\frac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}},\; +\frac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}} \right]$$

A spike **outside this band** is considered statistically significant evidence against white noise.

For larger lag blocks, the approximate two-standard-error bounds are:
$$\pm \frac{2}{\sqrt{n}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Shortcut for Confidence Band:**
> - $n = 100$: bounds are $\pm 0.196$
> - $n = 50$: bounds are $\pm 0.277$
> - $n = 25$: bounds are $\pm 0.392$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Autocovariance at lag $k$ | $\gamma(k) = E[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)]$ | Symmetric: $\gamma(k) = \gamma(-k)$ |
| 2 | Theoretical ACF | $\rho(k) = \gamma(k)/\gamma(0)$ | $-1 \le \rho(k) \le 1$, $\rho(0) = 1$ |
| 3 | Sample autocovariance | $\hat{\gamma}(k) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x})$ | Sum length shrinks as $k$ grows |
| 4 | Sample ACF (SACF) | $r_k = \hat{\gamma}(k)/\hat{\gamma}(0)$ | Plug-in estimator of $\rho(k)$ |
| 5 | Bartlett SE (white noise) | $SE(r_k) \approx 1/\sqrt{n}$ | For $k \ge 1$ under $H_0$: white noise |
| 6 | 95% CI for $r_k$ | $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ | Used to draw dashed lines on plot |
| 7 | 2-SE band (faster) | $\pm 2/\sqrt{n}$ | Rule-of-thumb for $n \ge 25$ |
| 8 | Ljung-Box statistic | $Q(m) = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m} r_k^2/(n-k)$ | Tests joint significance of first $m$ lags |
| 9 | Trend signature | Slow (linear/curved) decay of $r_k$ | Stays significant for many lags |
| 10 | Seasonality signature | Repeated peaks at $k = s, 2s, 3s, \ldots$ | Period $s$ identified by first major peak |
| 11 | White noise signature | All $r_k$ inside $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ for $k \ge 1$ | No pattern — random fluctuations only |
| 12 | Random walk (no trend) | $r_k \approx 1$ for all $k$ | ACF does not decay; non-stationary |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Demand forecasting in power systems:** Identifying yearly seasonality in electricity demand using ACF.
- **Structural health monitoring:** Detecting drifting trends in vibration sensor data.
- **Climate engineering:** Identifying periodicities in rainfall/temperature time series for Kerala.
- **Economics & finance:** ACF of stock returns vs. ACF of absolute returns (volatility clustering).
- **Process control:** Detecting autocorrelation in residuals to validate control chart assumptions.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Autocovariance to Sample ACF

We start from the population definition and derive the sample estimator.

**Step 1 — Population autocovariance (theoretical):**
$$\gamma(k) = E[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)]$$

**Step 2 — Replace expectations by sample averages (Method of Moments):**
$$E[Y] \approx \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} y_t$$

Applying this to $Y_t = (X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)$:
$$\hat{\gamma}(k) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(x_t - \mu)(x_{t+k} - \mu)$$

**Note on the upper limit:** for lag $k$, only $n-k$ valid pairs $(x_t, x_{t+k})$ exist, so the sum runs to $n-k$.

**Step 3 — Replace unknown mean $\mu$ by sample mean $\bar{x}$:**
$$\hat{\gamma}(k) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x})$$

**Step 4 — Normalize by $\hat{\gamma}(0)$ to get the sample ACF:**
$$r_k = \frac{\hat{\gamma}(k)}{\hat{\gamma}(0)} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})^2}$$

This is the standard estimator used in every statistical software (R, Python statsmodels, MATLAB).

## 3.2 Derivation: Bartlett's Standard Error under White Noise

Under the null hypothesis $H_0: \{X_t\} \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$:

**Step 1 — State the assumption.** Each $X_t$ is independent with $E[X_t] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(X_t) = \sigma^2$.

**Step 2 — Compute $E[r_k^2]$ for $k \ge 1$.** Since pairs $(X_t, X_{t+k})$ are independent, the numerator and denominator of $r_k$ involve different random products. A careful second-moment calculation (Bartlett, 1946) yields:

$$E[r_k^2] \approx \frac{1}{n} \quad (k \ge 1)$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root to get the standard error:**
$$SE(r_k) = \sqrt{E[r_k^2]} \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 4 — Construct the approximate 95% confidence interval** using a normal approximation:
$$r_k \in \left[ -\frac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}},\; \frac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}} \right]$$

This is the dashed horizontal line drawn on every standard ACF plot.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — A Trending Series

Consider the artificial series (8 observations): $x_1=2, x_2=4, x_3=5, x_4=7, x_5=9, x_6=10, x_7=12, x_8=14$.

**Step 1 — Compute the mean:**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{2+4+5+7+9+10+12+14}{8} = \frac{63}{8} = 7.875$$

**Step 2 — Compute deviations from mean:**
$$d_t = x_t - \bar{x} = [-5.875,\; -3.875,\; -2.875,\; -0.875,\; 1.125,\; 2.125,\; 4.125,\; 6.125]$$

**Step 3 — Sum of squares $\sum d_t^2$ (denominator):**
$$\sum d_t^2 = 34.516 + 15.016 + 8.266 + 0.766 + 1.266 + 4.516 + 17.016 + 37.516 = 118.875$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\hat{\gamma}(1)$ — sum of $d_t \cdot d_{t+1}$ for $t=1,\ldots,7$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_1 d_2 &= (-5.875)(-3.875) = 22.766 \\
d_2 d_3 &= (-3.875)(-2.875) = 11.141 \\
d_3 d_4 &= (-2.875)(-0.875) = 2.516 \\
d_4 d_5 &= (-0.875)(1.125) = -0.984 \\
d_5 d_6 &= (1.125)(2.125) = 2.391 \\
d_6 d_7 &= (2.125)(4.125) = 8.766 \\
d_7 d_8 &= (4.125)(6.125) = 25.266 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sum d_t d_{t+1} = 22.766 + 11.141 + 2.516 - 0.984 + 2.391 + 8.766 + 25.266 = 71.859$$

**Step 5 — Sample ACF at lag 1:**
$$r_1 = \frac{71.859}{118.875} \approx 0.605$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\hat{\gamma}(2)$ (sum over $t=1,\ldots,6$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_1 d_3 &= (-5.875)(-2.875) = 16.891 \\
d_2 d_4 &= (-3.875)(-0.875) = 3.391 \\
d_3 d_5 &= (-2.875)(1.125) = -3.234 \\
d_4 d_6 &= (-0.875)(2.125) = -1.859 \\
d_5 d_7 &= (1.125)(4.125) = 4.641 \\
d_6 d_8 &= (2.125)(6.125) = 13.016 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sum d_t d_{t+2} = 16.891 + 3.391 - 3.234 - 1.859 + 4.641 + 13.016 = 32.844$$

$$r_2 = \frac{32.844}{118.875} \approx 0.276$$

**Step 7 — Compute $r_3$ (sum over $t=1,\ldots,5$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_1 d_4 &= (-5.875)(-0.875) = 5.141 \\
d_2 d_5 &= (-3.875)(1.125) = -4.359 \\
d_3 d_6 &= (-2.875)(2.125) = -6.109 \\
d_4 d_7 &= (-0.875)(4.125) = -3.609 \\
d_5 d_8 &= (1.125)(6.125) = 6.891 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sum d_t d_{t+3} = 5.141 - 4.359 - 6.109 - 3.609 + 6.891 = -2.045$$

$$r_3 = \frac{-2.045}{118.875} \approx -0.017$$

**Step 8 — Confidence bound for $n=8$:**
$$\frac{1.96}{\sqrt{8}} \approx 0.693$$

**Step 9 — Interpretation:** $r_1 = 0.605$ is **inside** the band $\pm 0.693$ (just barely), $r_2 = 0.276$ is also inside. However, the ACF values are *positive and decaying* — this is the **slow-decay signature of a trend**. For larger $n$, the early lags would be clearly outside the band.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — A Seasonal Series (Conceptual Walk-Through)

Consider monthly sales data with period $s = 12$. The expected ACF pattern is:

- $r_{12}$ is large and positive (same month, one year apart).
- $r_{24}$ is large and positive.
- $r_{36}$ is large and positive.
- All non-multiples of 12 should be near zero (no short-term persistence, only yearly cycle).

The **plot would show a "wave" or "scallop" pattern** with peaks at $k = 12, 24, 36, \ldots$ and troughs at intermediate lags — the textbook signature of seasonality.

## 3.5 The Ljung-Box Statistic (Module 3 Cross-Reference)

To formally test whether the first $m$ autocorrelations are jointly zero:
$$Q(m) = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\frac{r_k^2}{n-k}$$

Under $H_0$ (white noise), $Q(m) \sim \chi^2_{(m)}$. Reject $H_0$ if $Q(m) > \chi^2_{1-\alpha, m}$.

- For a **trending series**: $Q(m)$ is large → reject $H_0$ → non-white-noise → trend confirmed.
- For a **seasonal series**: $Q(m)$ is large even at small $m$ if $m \ge s$ → reject $H_0$ → seasonality confirmed.
- For **white noise**: $Q(m)$ stays small → fail to reject.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ACF Interpretation Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Compute Sample ACF r_k for k = 1 to m"] --> B{"Are early lags r_1, r_2 positive and large?"}
    B -- "No, all small and inside +/- 1.96/sqrt n" --> C["Conclude: WHITE NOISE - no pattern"]
    B -- "Yes" --> D{"Do r_k values decay slowly to zero over many lags?"}
    D -- "Yes, slow linear/curved decay" --> E["Conclude: TREND present in series"]
    D -- "No, decay quickly to near zero" --> F{"Are there significant peaks at regular intervals k = s, 2s, 3s?"}
    F -- "Yes, repeated peaks at multiples of s" --> G["Conclude: SEASONALITY with period s"]
    F -- "No peaks" --> H["Conclude: ARMA type dependence (short memory)"]
    E --> I{"Are there ALSO peaks at seasonal lags?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Conclude: TREND + SEASONALITY both present"]
    I -- "No" --> K["Conclude: TREND only (no seasonality)"]
    G --> L{"Is there also a slow decay baseline?"}
    L -- "Yes" --> J
    L -- "No" --> M["Conclude: SEASONALITY only (stationary around seasonal mean)"]
```

## 4.2 ACF Signature Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TS["TIME SERIES TYPES"]
        TS1["White Noise X_t ~ N 0 sigma2"]
        TS2["Trending Series X_t = a + b t + noise"]
        TS3["Seasonal Series X_t = mu + S_t + noise"]
        TS4["Random Walk X_t = X_t-1 + noise"]
    end
    subgraph ACF["ACF PLOT SIGNATURES"]
        ACF1["All r_k within bands for k greater than or equal to 1"]
        ACF2["Slow positive linear/curved decay over 10 to 20+ lags"]

        ACF4["r_k approximately equal to 1 for all k, no decay"]
    end
    TS1 --> ACF1
    TS2 --> ACF2
    TS3 --> ACF3

```

## 4.3 ACF Plot Anatomy Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["ACF PLOT ANATOMY"] --> Q1["Vertical axis: r_k, range -1 to +1"]
    P --> Q2["Horizontal axis: lag k, from 0 to m"]
    P --> Q3["Solid bar at each lag showing r_k value"]
    P --> Q4["Dashed horizontal lines at +/- 1.96 over sqrt n: 95 percent confidence band"]
    P --> Q5["Reference line at r_0 = 1 always equal to 1"]
    Q3 --> R1["Bars OUTSIDE dashed lines: statistically significant"]
    Q3 --> R2["Bars INSIDE dashed lines: not significant at 5 percent level"]
    Q4 --> S1["Slow decay outside bands: TREND"]
    Q4 --> S2["Periodic spikes outside bands: SEASONALITY"]
    Q4 --> S3["All inside bands: WHITE NOISE"]
```

## 4.4 Trend vs. Seasonality vs. Combined — Visual Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Observed Series Pattern"] --> B{"ACF plot shape"}
    B -- "Smooth monotonic decay" --> C["TREND ONLY: Differencing d = 1 often removes"]
    B -- "Sharp peaks at regular multiples" --> D["SEASONALITY ONLY: Seasonal differencing D = 1 at lag s removes"]
    B -- "Decay envelope with periodic peaks inside" --> E["TREND + SEASONALITY: Apply both d = 1 then D = 1"]
    B -- "No pattern" --> F["WHITE NOISE: No transformation needed"]
    C --> G["Recommendation: ARIMA p d q with d = 1"]
    D --> H["Recommendation: SARIMA P D Q s with D = 1"]
    E --> I["Recommendation: SARIMA p d q x P D Q s"]
    F --> J["Recommendation: No model needed; or ARMA on residuals"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the sample autocorrelation function. Explain the significance of the value $\rho(0) = 1$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The sample autocorrelation function (SACF) of a series $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ at lag $k$ is defined as:
$$r_k = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})^2}$$

It measures the linear correlation between observations that are $k$ time units apart, after removing the sample mean.

**Significance of $\rho(0) = 1$:** At lag $k = 0$, every observation is perfectly correlated with itself. Formally, substituting $k = 0$ into the formula:
$$r_0 = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})^2}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})^2} = 1$$

This serves as a **reference baseline** for interpreting all other autocorrelations. The ACF plot always begins at 1.0 at lag 0, and all subsequent values $r_1, r_2, \ldots$ are interpreted as *deviations from this perfect self-correlation*. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — December 2023]
**What is the Bartlett standard error for the sample autocorrelation function? State the 95% confidence interval used to test whether a sample ACF value is significantly different from zero.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Under the null hypothesis $H_0$ that $\{X_t\}$ is a white noise process, Bartlett (1946) showed that the variance of the sample autocorrelation $r_k$ at lag $k \ge 1$ is approximately:
$$\text{Var}(r_k) \approx \frac{1}{n}$$

Hence the **standard error** is:
$$SE(r_k) \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$$

The **95% confidence interval** under the normal approximation is:
$$r_k \in \left[ -\frac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}},\; +\frac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}} \right]$$

If a sample ACF value $r_k$ falls **outside** this interval, it is statistically significant at the 5% level, providing evidence against white noise. The dashed horizontal lines drawn on a standard ACF plot correspond to these bounds. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. Students answer **ONE full question** out of the two alternatives provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch how an **Autocorrelation Function (ACF) plot** can be used to identify the presence of **trend** in a non-stationary time series. What is the underlying mathematical reason for the slow decay of autocorrelations in trending data?

**(b) [7 Marks)** Describe how the **ACF plot** reveals the presence of **seasonality** in a time series. How would you distinguish a pure seasonal pattern from a pure trend using the ACF alone? Illustrate with the typical shape of $r_k$ for a monthly series with period $s = 12$.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A trending series has a **monotonic, non-constant mean**. As a result, the sample autocovariance at lag $k$:
$$\hat{\gamma}(k) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x})$$

does not collapse quickly to zero as $k$ grows. The trend term $\beta t$ in the data contributes a positive product $(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x}) \approx \beta^2 \cdot t \cdot (t+k)$ for large $t$, which decreases only **linearly** with $k$, not exponentially.

**ACF plot signature:** A clear **slow, roughly linear decay** of $r_k$ from near 1 at $k = 1$ towards zero. The autocorrelations remain statistically significant (outside the $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ bands) for a large number of lags (often 10–20 or more). There is **no periodic peak pattern**.

**Mathematical reason:** The autocovariance of a deterministic linear trend $x_t = \alpha + \beta t$ is:
$$\gamma(k) = \beta^2 \left[ \frac{n^3}{3} - \frac{n^2 k}{2} + \frac{k^3}{6} \right] \cdot \frac{1}{n^2}$$

which decays only as a polynomial in $k$, leading to $\rho(k) \approx 1 - ck/n$ — a **linear decay**. **(7 marks: 3 marks signature, 2 marks math, 2 marks explanation)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

A seasonal series has a periodic component $S_t$ with period $s$, satisfying $S_{t+s} = S_t$. The autocovariance at multiples of $s$ is:
$$\gamma(s) = E[S_t \cdot S_{t+s}] = E[S_t^2] > 0$$

while for $k$ not a multiple of $s$, $E[S_t \cdot S_{t+k}] \approx 0$ (in-phase averaging cancels out-of-phase pairs).

**ACF plot signature:** Large, **discrete spikes** at lags $k = s, 2s, 3s, \ldots$ For monthly data with $s = 12$, the spikes appear at $k = 12, 24, 36, \ldots$ with intervening lags near zero (a "scallop" or "comb" pattern).

**Distinguishing pure trend from pure seasonality using ACF alone:**

| Feature | Pure Trend | Pure Seasonality |
|---|---|---|
| Decay pattern | Smooth, monotonic | Discrete spikes at multiples of $s$ |
| Number of significant lags | Many (10–20+) | Few but periodic |
| Slow-decay baseline? | Yes | No |
| Periodic peaks? | No | Yes |

The **decay envelope** test: if all $r_k$ values for $k = 1, 2, \ldots$ are *positive and slowly decreasing*, it is trend. If the values are *near zero* except at $k = s, 2s, 3s, \ldots$, it is seasonality. **(7 marks: 2 marks seasonal theory, 3 marks plot signature, 2 marks comparison table)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — December 2023, Module 3]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the **autocorrelation function** for a stationary time series. Derive the relationship $\rho(k) = \gamma(k)/\gamma(0)$ and explain why the bounds $-1 \le \rho(k) \le 1$ must hold for all $k$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following time series of length $n = 6$: $x = \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12\}$. Compute the sample ACF at lags $k = 1, 2, 3$. Plot the ACF and identify the dominant pattern (trend, seasonality, or white noise).

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

For a **weakly stationary** stochastic process $\{X_t\}$ with constant mean $\mu$ and autocovariance function $\gamma(k) = E[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)]$ that depends only on the lag $k$:

The **autocorrelation function** is the normalized autocovariance:
$$\rho(k) = \frac{\gamma(k)}{\gamma(0)} = \frac{E[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)]}{E[(X_t - \mu)^2]}$$

**Derivation of bounds (Cauchy–Schwarz inequality):** For any two random variables $U, V$ with finite second moments, the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality states:
$$(E[UV])^2 \le E[U^2] \cdot E[V^2]$$

Let $U = X_t - \mu$ and $V = X_{t+k} - \mu$. Then:
$$\left(E[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)]\right)^2 \le E[(X_t - \mu)^2] \cdot E[(X_{t+k} - \mu)^2]$$

Since stationarity gives $E[(X_{t+k} - \mu)^2] = E[(X_t - \mu)^2] = \gamma(0)$:
$$[\gamma(k)]^2 \le [\gamma(0)]^2 \implies \vert \gamma(k) \vert \le \gamma(0)$$

Dividing by $\gamma(0) > 0$:
$$-1 \le \rho(k) \le 1 \quad \text{for all } k$$

**Equality** holds if and only if $X_t$ and $X_{t+k}$ are perfectly linearly related (i.e., $X_{t+k} = aX_t + b$ almost surely). **(7 marks: 2 marks definition, 2 marks derivation, 3 marks bound proof)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Sample mean:**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{2+4+6+8+10+12}{6} = 7$$

**Step 2 — Deviations from mean:**
$$d_t = x_t - \bar{x} = [-5, -3, -1, 1, 3, 5]$$

**Step 3 — Sum of squares (denominator):**
$$\sum_{t=1}^{6} d_t^2 = 25 + 9 + 1 + 1 + 9 + 25 = 70$$

**Step 4 — Lag-1 numerator:** $\sum_{t=1}^{5} d_t d_{t+1}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
(-5)(-3) + (-3)(-1) + (-1)(1) + (1)(3) + (3)(5) \\
= 15 + 3 - 1 + 3 + 15 = 35
\end{aligned}$$

$$r_1 = \frac{35}{70} = 0.500$$

**Step 5 — Lag-2 numerator:** $\sum_{t=1}^{4} d_t d_{t+2}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
(-5)(-1) + (-3)(1) + (-1)(3) + (1)(5) = 5 - 3 - 3 + 5 = 4
\end{aligned}$$

$$r_2 = \frac{4}{70} \approx 0.057$$

**Step 6 — Lag-3 numerator:** $\sum_{t=1}^{3} d_t d_{t+3}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
(-5)(1) + (-3)(3) + (-1)(5) = -5 - 9 - 5 = -19
\end{aligned}$$

$$r_3 = \frac{-19}{70} \approx -0.271$$

**Step 7 — Confidence bound:** $1.96/\sqrt{6} \approx 0.800$.

**Step 8 — Plot interpretation:**

| Lag $k$ | $r_k$ | Inside $\pm 0.800$? |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.500 | Yes |
| 2 | 0.057 | Yes |
| 3 | -0.271 | Yes |

**Pattern identification:** The series is a **perfect linear trend** $x_t = 2t$. The sample ACF starts at 0.500 and decays, alternating in sign — the classic *slow alternating decay* of a linear trend. However, the small sample size ($n=6$) makes the lags fall inside the wide confidence band, so statistically the test is inconclusive. **Structurally**, however, the pattern is clearly **trend-dominated** (a single dominant positive $r_1$ followed by a sign-flipping decay). There is no evidence of seasonality. **(7 marks: 5 marks computation, 2 marks interpretation)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ACF with PACF** — The ACF measures total correlation at lag $k$ (including indirect effects through intermediate lags). The PACF measures *direct* correlation. KTU Module 3 focuses on ACF; do not mix the two.
> 2. **Forgetting to state that ACF is undefined for non-stationary series** — The classical $\rho(k)$ formula assumes stationarity. Always state the assumption, then proceed with sample-based *empirical* interpretation.
> 3. **Confusing trend decay with seasonality** — A slow linear decay indicates trend. A periodic *spike* pattern indicates seasonality. Many students write "the ACF decays" for both, losing the distinction marks.
> 4. **Wrong confidence interval formula** — Use $1.96/\sqrt{n}$, NOT $1/\sqrt{n}$ for the 95% bound. Use $1/\sqrt{n}$ only for the *standard error* itself.
> 5. **Not normalizing by $\hat{\gamma}(0)$** — Students sometimes report autocovariance values and call them "autocorrelations." Always check whether the value is between $-1$ and $+1$.
> 6. **Forgetting $r_0 = 1$** — The first bar in any ACF plot is always 1.0. If your plot shows otherwise, recompute.
> 7. **Sign-flip pattern** — A *linear trend* can produce an *alternating sign* ACF (especially for small samples), not a strictly positive one. Don't mistakenly call it a seasonal pattern just because the sign alternates.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ACF definition:** $\rho(k) = \gamma(k)/\gamma(0)$ — a normalized version of autocovariance.
- **$r_0 = 1$ always.** Plot interpretation starts at lag 1.
- **Bounds:** $-1 \le r_k \le 1$ for all $k$ (Cauchy–Schwarz).
- **Sample ACF formula:** $r_k = \sum(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x}) / \sum(x_t - \bar{x})^2$.
- **Bartlett SE under white noise:** $1/\sqrt{n}$.
- **95% confidence band:** $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ (or approximately $\pm 2/\sqrt{n}$).
- **Trend signature:** Slow, roughly linear decay of $r_k$, many significant lags.
- **Seasonality signature:** Discrete peaks at $k = s, 2s, 3s, \ldots$ with troughs between.
- **Trend + Seasonality signature:** Slow-decay envelope with periodic peaks embedded.
- **White noise signature:** All $r_k$ inside the $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ band for $k \ge 1$.
- **Random walk signature:** $r_k \approx 1$ for all $k$ (non-decaying — non-stationary).
- **Stationarity assumption:** Strictly required for theoretical $\rho(k)$, but sample ACFs are still computed and interpreted empirically.
- **Rule of thumb:** If the first few lags (1–5) decay slowly to zero → trend. If the first few lags decay *fast* but specific later lags spike → seasonality.
- **Practical check:** Always compute the confidence bounds first, then count how many $r_k$ lie outside — the *pattern of significant lags* is the diagnostic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autoregressive (AR) Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Autoregressive (AR) Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Autoregressive (AR) model** of order $p$, denoted **AR($p$)**, expresses the current value of a time series as a finite, linear combination of its own past $p$ values plus a stochastic shock (white noise). Mathematically, it is written as:

$$X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \dots + \phi_p X_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$$

where:
- $\phi_1, \phi_2, \dots, \phi_p$ are the **autoregressive coefficients** (model parameters to be estimated).
- $\{\varepsilon_t\}$ is a **white noise** process with mean $0$ and variance $\sigma_\varepsilon^2$, i.e., $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$.
- The series $\{X_t\}$ is assumed to be **weakly stationary**.

Using the **backshift operator** $B$ (where $B X_t = X_{t-1}$), the model is compactly written as:

$$\phi(B) X_t = \varepsilon_t, \quad \text{with} \quad \phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \dots - \phi_p B^p$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The AR($p$) process is the building block of the Box-Jenkins family. Its dual counterpart is the MA($q$) process. Every causal AR process corresponds to an infinite-order MA($\infty$) representation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are filling a **bathtub with water**:
- The water level **today** depends on the water level **yesterday, day before, ...** (memory).
- A small random **splash** ($\varepsilon_t$) is added each moment.
- If the previous levels were high, the current level tends to stay high; if they were low, it stays low — a **persistence** behavior.

This is exactly how an **AR process behaves**: each observation is a *weighted memory* of its past plus a fresh random innovation.

### Real-World Examples
| Domain | AR Interpretation |
|---|---|
| **Stock Returns** | Today's return depends on yesterday's return (momentum). |
| **Weather (Temperature)** | Today's temperature is highly correlated with yesterday's. |
| **Economics (GDP)** | Current GDP depends on its lagged values. |
| **Signal Processing** | Speech signals are predictable from past samples. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition**: AR models capture the **inertia** or **momentum** present in many natural and economic systems — the past "echoes" into the future.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Notation Glossary

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Notation |
|---|---|---|
| $p$ | Order of the AR model | Integer $\geq 1$ |
| $\phi_i$ | $i$-th AR coefficient | Real number |
| $\sigma_\varepsilon^2$ | White noise variance | Positive real |
| $\rho_k$ | Autocorrelation at lag $k$ | $\in [-1, 1]$ |
| $\gamma_k$ | Autocovariance at lag $k$ | Real number |

> [!TIP]
> Always remember: **AR models are linear regressions of a variable against its own past**. The coefficients measure the *strength of memory*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Structure & Operational Logic

An **AR($p$)** model operates under the following logical pillars:

1. **Linearity**: The dependence on past values is strictly linear.
2. **Stationarity**: The roots of the **characteristic polynomial** $\phi(z) = 1 - \phi_1 z - \dots - \phi_p z^p$ must all lie **outside the unit circle** in the complex plane (i.e., $|z_i| > 1$ for every root $z_i$).
3. **Invertibility (Automatic)**: A causal AR process is **always invertible** (no separate constraint needed).
4. **Memory Length**: The order $p$ determines the *effective memory* — values older than $p$ influence $X_t$ only indirectly through the recursion.

## 2.2 Special Cases

### AR(1) Model — First-Order
$$X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$
- Stationarity condition: $|\phi_1| < 1$, i.e., $\mathbf{-1 < \phi_1 < 1}$.
- If $\phi_1 > 0$ → positive autocorrelation (smooth series).
- If $\phi_1 < 0$ → alternating behavior (oscillatory series).

### AR(2) Model — Second-Order
$$X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$$
The **stationarity region** in the $(\phi_1, \phi_2)$ plane is a triangle bounded by:
- $\phi_1 + \phi_2 < 1$
- $\phi_2 - \phi_1 < 1$
- $\phi_2 > -1$

## 2.3 The Yule–Walker Equations (Heart of AR Models)

The **Yule–Walker equations** form a recursive linear system that connects the autocovariances $\gamma_k$ of an AR($p$) process to its coefficients.

For $k \geq 1$:
$$\gamma_k = \phi_1 \gamma_{k-1} + \phi_2 \gamma_{k-2} + \dots + \phi_p \gamma_{k-p}$$

Dividing both sides by $\gamma_0 = \text{Var}(X_t)$ yields the autocorrelation form:
$$\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2} + \dots + \phi_p \rho_{k-p}, \quad k \geq 1$$

For the variance equation ($k = 0$):
$$\gamma_0 = \phi_1 \gamma_1 + \phi_2 \gamma_2 + \dots + \phi_p \gamma_p + \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

In **matrix form**:
$$\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_p \boldsymbol{\Phi} = \boldsymbol{\gamma}_p$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_p$ is the $p \times p$ Toeplitz autocovariance matrix and $\boldsymbol{\Phi} = (\phi_1, \dots, \phi_p)^\top$.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why stationarity?** Because without it, the process has a time-varying mean/variance and the model is statistically non-identifiable.
- **Why Yule–Walker?** They allow O(·) estimation of $\phi_i$ from sample autocorrelations.
- **Why roots outside unit circle?** Ensures the impulse response decays — the process "forgets" the distant past.

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X_t = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i X_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$ | AR($p$) definition |
| 2 | $\phi(B) X_t = \varepsilon_t$ | Backshift form |
| 3 | $\gamma_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \gamma_{k-i}, \quad k \geq 1$ | Yule–Walker (autocovariance) |
| 4 | $\rho_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \rho_{k-i}, \quad k \geq 1$ | Yule–Walker (autocorrelation) |
| 5 | $\gamma_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \gamma_i + \sigma_\varepsilon^2$ | Variance equation |
| 6 | $\phi_1, \dots, \phi_p \;\text{s.t.}\; \phi(z) = 0 \Rightarrow \vert z_i \vert > 1$ | Stationarity condition |
| 7 | AR(1): $\rho_k = \phi_1^{k}$ | Geometric ACF decay |
| 8 | AR(1): $\gamma_0 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 / (1 - \phi_1^2)$ | Unconditional variance |
| 9 | AR(2): $\rho_1 = \phi_1 / (1 - \phi_2)$ | First-lag autocorrelation |
| 10 | PACF cuts off after lag $p$ | AR($p$) identification |
| 11 | ACF tails off (exponential / damped sine) | AR($p$) identification |
| 12 | $\hat{\phi} = \mathbf{R}_p^{-1} \mathbf{r}_p$ | Yule–Walker estimator (sample) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic**: For AR($p$) → **PACF cuts off after lag $p$**, while the ACF **tails off gradually**. This is the **single most tested identification rule** in KTU exams.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | AR Model Application |
|---|---|
| **Econometrics** | GDP, inflation, unemployment forecasting |
| **Signal Processing** | Speech modeling (LPC), noise cancellation |
| **Control Systems** | Identifying dynamics of linear systems |
| **Finance** | Volatility & return modeling (preliminary step before GARCH) |
| **Biomedical** | ECG, EEG, HRV signal analysis |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used below**: $\gamma_k$ denotes the autocovariance at lag $k$; $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$ is the autocorrelation; $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$ is white noise with $E[\varepsilon_t \varepsilon_s] = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \delta_{ts}$.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Yule–Walker Equations for AR(1)

**Given**: $X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, with $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$.

**Step 1** — Multiply both sides by $X_{t-k}$ and take expectation:
$$E[X_t X_{t-k}] = \phi_1 E[X_{t-1} X_{t-k}] + E[\varepsilon_t X_{t-k}]$$

This produces:
$$\gamma_k = \phi_1 \gamma_{k-1} + E[\varepsilon_t X_{t-k}]$$

**Step 2** — For $k \geq 1$, since $\varepsilon_t$ is uncorrelated with $X_{t-k}$ (white noise assumption), $E[\varepsilon_t X_{t-k}] = 0$:

$$\gamma_k = \phi_1 \gamma_{k-1}, \quad k \geq 1$$

**Step 3** — Divide by $\gamma_0$:

$$\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1}, \quad k \geq 1$$

**Step 4** — Apply recursively starting from $\rho_0 = 1$:

$$\rho_1 = \phi_1 \rho_0 = \phi_1$$
$$\rho_2 = \phi_1 \rho_1 = \phi_1^2$$
$$\rho_k = \phi_1^k$$

**Conclusion**: The ACF of an AR(1) process **decays geometrically** (exponentially) as $\phi_1^{\vert k \vert}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Unconditional Variance of AR(1)

**Step 1** — Square both sides:
$$X_t^2 = \phi_1^2 X_{t-1}^2 + 2 \phi_1 X_{t-1} \varepsilon_t + \varepsilon_t^2$$

**Step 2** — Take expectation:
$$\gamma_0 = \phi_1^2 \gamma_0 + 0 + \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

(The cross-term vanishes because $E[X_{t-1} \varepsilon_t] = 0$ under stationarity.)

**Step 3** — Solve:
$$\gamma_0 (1 - \phi_1^2) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$
$$\gamma_0 = \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$$

**Condition for finiteness**: $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$, recovering the **stationarity requirement**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Yule–Walker Equations for AR(2)

**Given**: $X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$.

**Step 1** — Multiply by $X_{t-k}$ and take expectation (for $k \geq 1$):

$$\gamma_k = \phi_1 \gamma_{k-1} + \phi_2 \gamma_{k-2}$$

**Step 2** — Apply at $k = 1$:
$$\gamma_1 = \phi_1 \gamma_0 + \phi_2 \gamma_{-1}$$

Using symmetry $\gamma_{-1} = \gamma_1$:
$$\gamma_1 = \phi_1 \gamma_0 + \phi_2 \gamma_1$$
$$\gamma_1 (1 - \phi_2) = \phi_1 \gamma_0$$
$$\rho_1 = \frac{\phi_1}{1 - \phi_2}$$

**Step 3** — Apply at $k = 2$:
$$\gamma_2 = \phi_1 \gamma_1 + \phi_2 \gamma_0$$

Dividing by $\gamma_0$:
$$\rho_2 = \phi_1 \rho_1 + \phi_2$$

**Step 4** — Apply at $k = 3, 4, \dots$:
$$\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2}, \quad k \geq 2$$

This is the **second-order linear difference equation** governing the ACF. Its solution is a **mixture of damped exponentials** (real roots) or **damped sinusoids** (complex roots).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Variance Equation for AR(2)

**Step 1** — Multiply $X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$ by $X_t$ and take expectation:

$$\gamma_0 = \phi_1 \gamma_1 + \phi_2 \gamma_2 + \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

**Step 2** — Substitute $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$ from above:

$$\gamma_0 = \phi_1 \cdot \frac{\phi_1 \gamma_0}{1 - \phi_2} + \phi_2 \cdot (\phi_1 \gamma_1 + \phi_2 \gamma_0) + \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

**Step 3** — Solve for $\gamma_0$:

$$\gamma_0 = \frac{(1 - \phi_2) \sigma_\varepsilon^2}{(1 + \phi_2)\left[(1 - \phi_2)^2 - \phi_1^2\right]}$$

This is the **unconditional variance** of a stationary AR(2) process.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Forecasting with an AR($p$) Model

The **$h$-step-ahead forecast** at origin $T$ is the **conditional expectation**:

$$\hat{X}_{T+h} = E[X_{T+h} \mid X_T, X_{T-1}, \dots]$$

Using the AR recursion:
$$\hat{X}_{T+h} = \phi_1 \hat{X}_{T+h-1} + \phi_2 \hat{X}_{T+h-2} + \dots + \phi_p \hat{X}_{T+h-p}$$

with the boundary conditions:
$$\hat{X}_{T+j} = X_{T+j}, \quad j \leq 0$$

The **forecast error variance** grows with horizon $h$:
$$\text{Var}(e_{T+h}) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \sum_{j=0}^{h-1} \psi_j^2$$

where $\psi_j$ are the MA($\infty$) coefficients obtained by expanding $\phi^{-1}(B)$.

---

## 3.6 Sample Estimation (Yule–Walker / Method of Moments)

Replace $\gamma_k$ with **sample autocovariances** $c_k$:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\Phi}} = \hat{\boldsymbol{\Gamma}}_p^{-1} \hat{\boldsymbol{\gamma}}_p$$

where $\hat{\boldsymbol{\Gamma}}_p$ is the $p \times p$ sample autocovariance matrix. For **large samples**, $\hat{\phi}_i$ is approximately **unbiased and normally distributed**, with standard errors obtainable from $\hat{\sigma}_\varepsilon^2 / n$.

---

## 3.7 📊 Analytical Decision Matrix — Identifying AR($p$)

| Behavior | AR($p$) Signature | Identification Step |
|---|---|---|
| ACF | Tails off (gradual decay) | Plot $\rho_k$ vs $k$ |
| PACF | **Cuts off after lag $p$** | Look for last significant spike |
| Order $p$ | Number of significant PACF spikes | Use AIC / BIC for refinement |
| Stationarity | Roots of $\phi(z) = 0$ lie outside unit circle | Verify via characteristic equation |
| Diagnostic | Residuals should resemble white noise | Apply Ljung–Box test |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an AR($p$) Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1[White Noise epsilon t] --> sumN[Summation Node]
    inp2[White Noise epsilon t-1] --> delay1[Delay B] --> sumN
    inp3[White Noise epsilon t-2] --> delay2[Delay B twice] --> sumN
    phi1[Phi 1] --> mul1[Multiplier] --> sumN
    phi2[Phi 2] --> mul2[Multiplier] --> sumN
    phiP[Phi p] --> mulP[Multiplier] --> sumN
    sumN --> outX[Output X t]
    outX --> fb1[Feedback X t-1] --> mul1
    outX --> fb2[Feedback X t-2] --> mul2
    outX --> fbP[Feedback X t-p] --> mulP
```

## 4.2 Stationarity Triangle for AR(2) in the ($\phi_1, \phi_2$) Plane

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    axisTitle["Stationarity Region for AR2"]
    origin["Origin 0,0"] --> axisX["phi1 axis"]
    origin --> axisY["phi2 axis"]
    axisX --> bound1["phi2 greater than minus 1"]
    axisX --> bound2["phi1 plus phi2 less than 1"]
    axisX --> bound3["phi2 minus phi1 less than 1"]
    bound1 --> region["TRIANGLE: Causal AR2 Region"]
    bound2 --> region
    bound3 --> region
    region --> inside["Inside this triangle: Process is stationary and causal"]
    region --> outside["Outside: Non-stationary explosive behavior"]
```

## 4.3 ACF and PACF Identification Pattern for AR($p$)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Examine Sample ACF and PACF Plots"]
    start --> q1{"ACF Tails off gradually?"}
    q1 -- NO --> notAR["Not AR. Consider MA or ARMA"]
    q1 -- YES --> q2{"PACF Cuts off sharply after lag p?"}
    q2 -- NO --> notAR
    q2 -- YES --> ident["Identified as AR order p"]
    ident --> fit["Estimate coefficients via Yule-Walker or MLE"]
    fit --> diag["Diagnostic Check on Residuals"]
    diag --> q3{"Residuals are White Noise?"}
    q3 -- NO --> refine["Refine p or consider ARMA"]
    q3 -- YES --> accept["Model Accepted. Use for Forecasting"]
```

## 4.4 Information Flow of AR($p$) Modeling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Raw Time Series Data] --> viz[Plot Series: Trend, Outliers]
    viz --> stat[Stationarity Tests: ADF, KPSS]
    stat --> diff[Differencing if Needed]
    diff --> id[Identify p via PACF]
    id --> est[Estimate Coefficients]
    est --> check[Check Stationarity of Estimated Model]
    check --> res[Residual Diagnostics]
    res --> fcst[Generate Forecasts]
    fcst --> conf[Confidence Intervals]
```

## 4.5 Conceptual Memory-Decay Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    now["X at time t"] --> lag1["X at time t-1"]
    now --> lag2["X at time t-2"]
    now --> lag3["X at time t-3"]
    now --> lagP["X at time t-p"]
    lag1 -- coefficient phi 1 --> now
    lag2 -- coefficient phi 2 --> now
    lag3 -- coefficient phi 3 --> now
    lagP -- coefficient phi p --> now
    eps["Innovation epsilon t"] --> now
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams**: Notice how the AR model resembles a **finite impulse response (FIR) filter** in signal processing — the past outputs are linearly combined with weights $\phi_i$ to produce the present.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an Autoregressive model of order $p$. Write the backshift operator form and state the stationarity condition.**

**Model Answer**:
An **Autoregressive model of order $p$**, denoted AR($p$), expresses a stationary time series $X_t$ as a linear combination of its own $p$ past values plus a white noise term:

$$X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \dots + \phi_p X_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$$

In **backshift operator** notation:

$$\phi(B) X_t = \varepsilon_t, \quad \text{where} \quad \phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \dots - \phi_p B^p$$

**Stationarity condition**: All roots of the characteristic polynomial $\phi(z) = 1 - \phi_1 z - \phi_2 z^2 - \dots - \phi_p z^p = 0$ must lie **outside the unit circle**, i.e., $\vert z_i \vert > 1$ for every root $z_i$. Equivalently, for AR(1): $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain how the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) and Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF) help in identifying an AR($p$) model.**

**Model Answer**:
- **ACF of AR($p$)**: Tails off **gradually** (mix of damped exponentials or damped sinusoids). It does not cut off at any specific lag.
- **PACF of AR($p$)**: **Cuts off sharply** after lag $p$. That is, $\text{PACF}(k) \approx 0$ for all $k > p$, and $\text{PACF}(p) = \phi_p$.
- This contrasting behavior — *ACF tails, PACF cuts off* — is the **signature pattern** used to identify AR($p$) models in Box-Jenkins methodology.
- The order $p$ is determined by the **last significant spike** in the PACF plot (often confirmed by AIC/BIC).

---

## 5.2 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note**: As per KTU ESE pattern, students answer **ONE** full question from a choice of two.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** For the AR(2) model $X_t = 0.5 X_{t-1} + 0.3 X_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$, determine whether the process is **stationary**. Justify your answer by examining the characteristic equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Yule–Walker equations** for the AR(2) process and compute the **first three autocorrelations** $\rho_1, \rho_2, \rho_3$. **(7 marks)**

---

### 📘 Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Stationarity Check (7 Marks)

**Step 1** — Write the characteristic polynomial:

$$\phi(z) = 1 - 0.5 z - 0.3 z^2 = 0$$

**Step 2** — Solve for the roots. Multiply by $-1$:

$$0.3 z^2 + 0.5 z - 1 = 0$$

**Step 3** — Apply the quadratic formula:

$$z = \frac{-0.5 \pm \sqrt{0.25 + 1.2}}{0.6} = \frac{-0.5 \pm \sqrt{1.45}}{0.6}$$

**Step 4** — Compute numerically:

$$z_1 = \frac{-0.5 + 1.2042}{0.6} = \frac{0.7042}{0.6} \approx 1.1736$$

$$z_2 = \frac{-0.5 - 1.2042}{0.6} = \frac{-1.7042}{0.6} \approx -2.8403$$

**Step 5** — Check the magnitudes:

$$\vert z_1 \vert \approx 1.1736 > 1 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\vert z_2 \vert \approx 2.8403 > 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6** — **Alternative verification** using the **stationarity triangle**:
- $\phi_1 + \phi_2 = 0.5 + 0.3 = 0.8 < 1$ ✓
- $\phi_2 - \phi_1 = 0.3 - 0.5 = -0.2 < 1$ ✓
- $\phi_2 = 0.3 > -1$ ✓

**Conclusion**: Both roots lie outside the unit circle, so the AR(2) process is **stationary and causal**. ✅

---

#### Part (b) — Yule–Walker Derivation and Autocorrelations (7 Marks)

**Step 1** — General Yule–Walker form for AR(2):

$$\gamma_k = \phi_1 \gamma_{k-1} + \phi_2 \gamma_{k-2}, \quad k \geq 1$$

**Step 2** — Convert to autocorrelation form (divide by $\gamma_0$):

$$\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2}, \quad k \geq 1$$

**Step 3** — Compute $\rho_1$ at $k = 1$:

$$\rho_1 = \phi_1 \rho_0 + \phi_2 \rho_{-1} = \phi_1 + \phi_2 \rho_1$$

(Since $\rho_0 = 1$ and $\rho_{-1} = \rho_1$.)

$$\rho_1 (1 - \phi_2) = \phi_1 \implies \rho_1 = \frac{\phi_1}{1 - \phi_2}$$

**Numerical computation**:

$$\rho_1 = \frac{0.5}{1 - 0.3} = \frac{0.5}{0.7} \approx 0.7143$$

**Step 4** — Compute $\rho_2$ at $k = 2$:

$$\rho_2 = \phi_1 \rho_1 + \phi_2 \rho_0 = 0.5 \cdot 0.7143 + 0.3 \cdot 1 = 0.3571 + 0.3 = 0.6571$$

**Step 5** — Compute $\rho_3$ at $k = 3$:

$$\rho_3 = \phi_1 \rho_2 + \phi_2 \rho_1 = 0.5 \cdot 0.6571 + 0.3 \cdot 0.7143 = 0.3286 + 0.2143 = 0.5429$$

**Final Results Table**:

| Lag $k$ | Autocorrelation $\rho_k$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 0.7143 |
| 2 | 0.6571 |
| 3 | 0.5429 |

The ACF exhibits **gradual decay**, confirming the AR(2) signature.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** For an AR(1) process $X_t = 0.7 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ with $\sigma_\varepsilon^2 = 4$, derive the **unconditional variance** $\gamma_0$ and the autocorrelation function $\rho_k$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given observations $X_1 = 2, X_2 = 3, X_3 = 4$, compute the **one-step-ahead** and **two-step-ahead** forecasts using the fitted AR(1) model with $\hat{\phi}_1 = 0.7$. Also write the forecast error variances. **(7 marks)**

---

### 📘 Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Variance and ACF of AR(1) (7 Marks)

**Step 1** — Derive the **unconditional variance** $\gamma_0$:

Square the model:
$$X_t^2 = 0.49 X_{t-1}^2 + 1.4 X_{t-1} \varepsilon_t + \varepsilon_t^2$$

Take expectation (cross-term vanishes due to independence):
$$\gamma_0 = 0.49 \gamma_0 + \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

$$\gamma_0 (1 - 0.49) = 4$$

$$\gamma_0 = \frac{4}{0.51} \approx 7.8431$$

**Step 2** — Derive the autocorrelation function:

From the Yule–Walker equation for AR(1):
$$\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1}, \quad k \geq 1$$

Iterating:
$$\rho_1 = 0.7, \quad \rho_2 = 0.49, \quad \rho_3 = 0.343, \quad \rho_k = 0.7^k$$

**Conclusion**: The ACF decays **geometrically** at rate $\phi_1 = 0.7$.

---

#### Part (b) — Forecasting (7 Marks)

**Step 1** — One-step-ahead forecast $\hat{X}_4$:

Using $X_t = 0.7 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ with $X_3 = 4$:
$$\hat{X}_4 = 0.7 \cdot X_3 = 0.7 \cdot 4 = 2.8$$

**Forecast error variance (one-step)**:
$$\text{Var}(e_4) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 = 4$$

**Step 2** — Two-step-ahead forecast $\hat{X}_5$:

The recursion gives:
$$\hat{X}_5 = 0.7 \hat{X}_4 = 0.7 \cdot 2.8 = 1.96$$

**Forecast error variance (two-step)**:
$$\text{Var}(e_5) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 (1 + \phi_1^2) = 4 (1 + 0.49) = 4 \cdot 1.49 = 5.96$$

**Step 3** — Three-step-ahead forecast (for completeness):

$$\hat{X}_6 = 0.7 \hat{X}_5 = 0.7 \cdot 1.96 = 1.372$$

$$\text{Var}(e_6) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 (1 + \phi_1^2 + \phi_1^4) = 4 (1 + 0.49 + 0.2401) = 6.9204$$

**Final Results**:

| Horizon $h$ | Forecast $\hat{X}_{3+h}$ | Variance $\text{Var}(e_{3+h})$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.800 | 4.000 |
| 2 | 1.960 | 5.960 |
| 3 | 1.372 | 6.920 |

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**:
> 1. **Stationarity verification**: Many students forget to verify $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$ or check the stationarity triangle. This is a **mandatory** step in KTU valuation and carries 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Cross-term omission**: When deriving $\gamma_0$, students often incorrectly retain the $2 \phi_1 X_{t-1} \varepsilon_t$ term. Always argue that $E[X_{t-1} \varepsilon_t] = 0$ for white noise.
> 3. **Forecast variance formula**: A very common error is writing $\text{Var}(e_{T+h}) = h \sigma_\varepsilon^2$ (which is only for a random walk). For AR, it is $\sigma_\varepsilon^2 \sum_{j=0}^{h-1} \psi_j^2$.
> 4. **Initial conditions**: Always anchor the recursion at observed values; do not invent boundary values.
> 5. **PACF vs ACF confusion**: Students frequently state "ACF cuts off after lag $p$" — this is **wrong**. It is the **PACF** that cuts off for AR models.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] AR($p$) models: $X_t = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i X_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$.
- [ ] **Stationarity** ⇔ all roots of $\phi(z) = 0$ satisfy $\vert z_i \vert > 1$.
- [ ] AR(1) stationary iff $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$.
- [ ] AR(2) stationary iff $(\phi_1, \phi_2)$ lies inside the **stationarity triangle**.
- [ ] **Yule–Walker equations**: $\gamma_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \gamma_{k-i}$ for $k \geq 1$.
- [ ] AR(1) ACF: $\rho_k = \phi_1^{\vert k \vert}$ — **geometric decay**.
- [ ] AR(1) variance: $\gamma_0 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 / (1 - \phi_1^2)$.
- [ ] AR(2) ACF: obeys recursion $\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2}$.
- [ ] **Identification rule**: ACF **tails off**, PACF **cuts off after lag $p$**.
- [ ] Estimation: **Yule–Walker** (method of moments) or **MLE / OLS**.
- [ ] Forecasting: recursive conditional expectation, with $\hat{X}_{T+j} = X_{T+j}$ for $j \leq 0$.
- [ ] Forecast error variance grows with horizon $h$ via $\sum \psi_j^2$.
- [ ] AR models are **always invertible** if stationary (no separate constraint).
- [ ] Diagnostic tool: residual ACF should resemble white noise (Ljung–Box test).

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Memory Hook**: *"AR(p) — PACF says stop at lag p, ACF keeps whispering forever."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moving Average (MA) Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Moving Average (MA) Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — MOVING AVERAGE (MA) MODELS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Moving Average Model of order q**, denoted as **MA(q)**, expresses the current value of a time series $X_t$ as a linear combination of the **current white-noise error term** $\epsilon_t$ and **q past white-noise error terms**, plus a constant mean $\mu$.

The mathematical representation is:

$$X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2} + \dots + \theta_q \epsilon_{t-q}$$

where:
- $\mu$ is the **constant mean** of the series
- $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\epsilon^2)$ is **white noise** with **zero mean** and **constant variance** $\sigma_\epsilon^2$
- $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_q$ are the **MA parameters** (real constants)
- $q$ is the **order** of the moving average process

The **backshift (lag) operator** representation is compactly written as:

$$X_t = \mu + \Theta(B)\,\epsilon_t$$

where $\Theta(B) = 1 + \theta_1 B + \theta_2 B^2 + \dots + \theta_q B^q$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology:** In all KTU 2024 scheme answers, explicitly state the model as "MA(q) of order q" and use $\mu$ for the mean, $\theta$ for the parameters, and $\sigma_\epsilon^2$ for the white-noise variance. Examiners award marks for **correct symbol usage**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Office Thermostat with Short Memory"**
> Imagine a smart thermostat that controls office temperature. The temperature *today* does **not** depend on temperatures from many days ago. Instead, it is determined by the **random door-opening shocks** (white noise) experienced today and on a few previous days. If a door was flung open yesterday, the thermostat reacted with a jolt, and the system is still "remembering" that jolt through today's correction. After a few days, the effect of that single shock **dies out completely**. This is the essence of an MA model — *finite memory of random shocks*.

In simple words: **An MA process has finite, short memory.** The autocorrelation function (ACF) becomes exactly zero after lag $q$, which is the **fingerprint** KTU examiners love to test.

### 1.3 Why MA Models Are Always Stationary

A remarkable property of any MA(q) process is that it is **always weakly (covariance) stationary**, **regardless of the parameter values** $\theta_1, \dots, \theta_q$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU:** Unlike AR models, MA models **do not require stationarity conditions** on parameters. The mean, variance, and autocovariances are **time-invariant** by construction because they depend only on white-noise terms which are themselves stationary.

### 1.4 Invertibility — The Hidden Constraint

While MA models are **always stationary**, they are not always **invertible**. An MA process is said to be **invertible** if it can be rewritten as an **infinite-order AR model** (AR($\infty$)).

For **MA(1): $X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}$**, the invertibility condition is:

$$\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$$

For a general **MA(q)** model, invertibility requires that **all roots** of the characteristic polynomial $\Theta(z) = 1 + \theta_1 z + \theta_2 z^2 + \dots + \theta_q z^q$ lie **outside the unit circle** in the complex plane.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Trick:** When KTU gives a numerical MA model, they will often ask "Is the model invertible?" — Your job is to check $\vert \theta \vert < 1$ for MA(1) or apply the root condition for MA(q).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Properties of MA(q) — Step-by-Step Logic

Let $x_t = X_t - \mu$ denote the **mean-corrected series**. Then $x_t$ is a zero-mean MA(q):

$$x_t = \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2} + \dots + \theta_q \epsilon_{t-q}$$

### Property 1: Mean

- The white-noise terms have $E[\epsilon_t] = 0$.
- Therefore, the mean of $X_t$ equals $\mu$, and the mean of $x_t$ equals $0$.

### Property 2: Variance

The variance is computed as the sum of squares of coefficients multiplied by $\sigma_\epsilon^2$:

$$\gamma_0 = Var(x_t) = \left(1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2 + \dots + \theta_q^2 \right)\sigma_\epsilon^2$$

- **Why this works:** Cross-terms like $E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-k}] = 0$ for $k \neq 0$ because white-noise is **uncorrelated**. Only squared terms survive.

### Property 3: Autocovariance Function (ACVF)

For an MA(q) process, the autocovariance at lag $k$ is:

$$\gamma_k = \sigma_\epsilon^2 \sum_{j=0}^{q-k} \theta_j \theta_{j+k}, \quad \text{for } k = 0, 1, \dots, q$$

$$\gamma_k = 0, \quad \text{for } k > q$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The MA "Cut-Off" Signature:** The ACF becomes **exactly zero** beyond lag $q$. This is the **diagnostic fingerprint** of an MA process used in the Box–Jenkins identification phase (along with a slowly decaying PACF).

### Property 4: Autocorrelation Function (ACF)

$$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0}$$

For $k > q$, $\rho_k = 0$. This is what makes MA models **identifiable from sample ACF plots**.

### Property 5: Invertibility

The MA(q) model can be rewritten as an AR($\infty$):

$$\epsilon_t = \pi(B) x_t = (1 - \pi_1 B - \pi_2 B^2 - \dots)(x_t)$$

This is possible **only if** all roots of $\Theta(z) = 0$ lie **outside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert > 1$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The table below contains the **most-tested formulas** for MA(1), MA(2), and general MA(q). Use these verbatim in your KTU 2024 exam answers.

| Property | MA(1): $x_t = \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}$ | MA(2): $x_t = \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2}$ | General MA(q) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mean** | $E[x_t] = 0$ | $E[x_t] = 0$ | $E[x_t] = 0$ |
| **Variance $\gamma_0$** | $(1 + \theta_1^2)\sigma_\epsilon^2$ | $(1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2)\sigma_\epsilon^2$ | $\left(\sum_{j=0}^{q} \theta_j^2\right)\sigma_\epsilon^2$ with $\theta_0 = 1$ |
| **ACVF $\gamma_1$** | $\theta_1 \sigma_\epsilon^2$ | $(\theta_1 + \theta_1 \theta_2)\sigma_\epsilon^2$ | $\sigma_\epsilon^2 \sum_{j=0}^{q-1} \theta_j \theta_{j+1}$ |
| **ACVF $\gamma_2$** | $0$ | $\theta_2 \sigma_\epsilon^2$ | $\sigma_\epsilon^2 \sum_{j=0}^{q-2} \theta_j \theta_{j+2}$ |
| **ACVF $\gamma_k$, $k>q$** | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| **ACF $\rho_1$** | $\dfrac{\theta_1}{1 + \theta_1^2}$ | $\dfrac{\theta_1 + \theta_1 \theta_2}{1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2}$ | $\dfrac{\sum_{j=0}^{q-1} \theta_j \theta_{j+1}}{\sum_{j=0}^{q} \theta_j^2}$ |
| **ACF $\rho_2$** | $0$ | $\dfrac{\theta_2}{1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2}$ | $\dfrac{\sum_{j=0}^{q-2} \theta_j \theta_{j+2}}{\sum_{j=0}^{q} \theta_j^2}$ |
| **Invertibility** | $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$ | All roots of $1 + \theta_1 z + \theta_2 z^2 = 0$ outside $\vert z \vert = 1$ | All roots of $\Theta(z) = 0$ outside $\vert z \vert = 1$ |
| **Stationarity** | Always | Always | Always |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ (used in $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$) is rendered as `\vert` in LaTeX. **Do not use the keyboard pipe `|`** — it breaks markdown table parsers.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

MA models are indispensable in:

- **Signal Processing:** Modelling finite-impulse-response (FIR) filters — a moving average of a white-noise input is mathematically identical to an MA(q) process.
- **Econometrics & Finance:** Capturing short-lived shocks like policy announcements, earnings surprises, or commodity price jumps.
- **Industrial Quality Control:** Analysing tool-wear effects that decay in a few production cycles.
- **Telecommunications:** Modelling error bursts in noisy channels.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Capturing the effect of a brief pollution event on air-quality readings.

## 2.4 Special Cases Frequently Asked in KTU

- **MA(1) with $\theta_1 = 1$:** The process becomes $x_t = \epsilon_t + \epsilon_{t-1}$, the **simplest non-invertible** case (a "random walk difference").
- **MA(1) with $\theta_1 = -1$:** The process is $x_t = \epsilon_t - \epsilon_{t-1}$, also **non-invertible** with $\vert \theta_1 \vert \not< 1$.
- **White noise** is a special case of MA(q) with $q = 0$ (no parameters).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section exhaustively derives the **mean**, **variance**, and **autocovariances** for the MA(1) process, then extends to MA(2). Every algebraic step is shown explicitly so you can reproduce it in the KTU exam.

## 3.1 MA(1) Model — Full Derivation

### Model Statement

$$X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}$$

with $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\epsilon^2)$, meaning:

$$E[\epsilon_t] = 0, \quad Var(\epsilon_t) = \sigma_\epsilon^2, \quad E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_s] = 0 \text{ for } t \neq s$$

### Step A — Mean of $X_t$

$$E[X_t] = E[\mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}]$$

Apply linearity of expectation:

$$E[X_t] = E[\mu] + E[\epsilon_t] + \theta_1 E[\epsilon_{t-1}]$$

Substitute white-noise properties $E[\epsilon_t] = 0$ and $E[\epsilon_{t-1}] = 0$:

$$E[X_t] = \mu + 0 + \theta_1 \cdot 0$$

$$\boxed{E[X_t] = \mu}$$

### Step B — Variance of $X_t$

Let $x_t = X_t - \mu = \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}$. Then:

$$Var(X_t) = Var(x_t) = E[x_t^2] - (E[x_t])^2 = E[x_t^2] - 0 = E[x_t^2]$$

Expand the square:

$$E[x_t^2] = E[(\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1})^2]$$

$$= E[\epsilon_t^2 + 2\theta_1 \epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_1^2 \epsilon_{t-1}^2]$$

Apply linearity:

$$= E[\epsilon_t^2] + 2\theta_1 E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-1}] + \theta_1^2 E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2]$$

Use white-noise: $E[\epsilon_t^2] = \sigma_\epsilon^2$, $E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2] = \sigma_\epsilon^2$, and $E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-1}] = 0$ (uncorrelated at different times):
