$$= \sigma_\epsilon^2 + 2\theta_1 (0) + \theta_1^2 \sigma_\epsilon^2$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_0 = Var(X_t) = (1 + \theta_1^2)\sigma_\epsilon^2}$$

### Step C — Autocovariance at Lag 1 ($\gamma_1$)

$$\gamma_1 = E[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t-1} - \mu)] = E[x_t \cdot x_{t-1}]$$

Substitute:

$$\gamma_1 = E[(\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1})(\epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-2})]$$

Expand the product:

$$= E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_1 \epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-2} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}^2 + \theta_1^2 \epsilon_{t-1} \epsilon_{t-2}]$$

Apply linearity:

$$= E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-1}] + \theta_1 E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-2}] + \theta_1 E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2] + \theta_1^2 E[\epsilon_{t-1} \epsilon_{t-2}]$$

Now apply white-noise: any cross-product at *different time indices* equals zero, except the squared term at the *same* time index:

$$= 0 + 0 + \theta_1 \sigma_\epsilon^2 + 0$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_1 = \theta_1 \sigma_\epsilon^2}$$

### Step D — Autocovariance at Lag 2 ($\gamma_2$)

$$\gamma_2 = E[x_t \cdot x_{t-2}] = E[(\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1})(\epsilon_{t-2} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-3})]$$

Expand:

$$= E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-2}] + \theta_1 E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-3}] + \theta_1 E[\epsilon_{t-1} \epsilon_{t-2}] + \theta_1^2 E[\epsilon_{t-1} \epsilon_{t-3}]$$

Every term is a product of white-noise at *different* times, hence all are zero:

$$\boxed{\gamma_2 = 0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cut-Off Property Emerges:** For MA(1), $\gamma_k = 0$ for all $k \geq 2$. The autocovariances "cut off" sharply after lag 1.

### Step E — Autocorrelation at Lag 1 ($\rho_1$)

$$\rho_1 = \frac{\gamma_1}{\gamma_0} = \frac{\theta_1 \sigma_\epsilon^2}{(1 + \theta_1^2)\sigma_\epsilon^2}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_1 = \frac{\theta_1}{1 + \theta_1^2}}$$

### Step F — Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem (KTU-style):** Given $X_t = 10 + \epsilon_t + 0.5 \epsilon_{t-1}$, find the mean, variance, and autocorrelations.

- Mean: $\mu = 10$
- Variance: $\gamma_0 = (1 + 0.5^2)\sigma_\epsilon^2 = 1.25\,\sigma_\epsilon^2$
- $\gamma_1 = 0.5\,\sigma_\epsilon^2$
- $\rho_1 = 0.5 / 1.25 = 0.4$
- $\rho_k = 0$ for $k \geq 2$
- Invertibility: $\vert 0.5 \vert < 1$ ✓ (invertible)

## 3.2 MA(2) Model — Full Derivation

### Model Statement

$$x_t = \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2}$$

### Variance

$$\gamma_0 = E[x_t^2] = E[(\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2})^2]$$

Expanding the square and using $E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_s] = 0$ for $t \neq s$:

$$= E[\epsilon_t^2] + \theta_1^2 E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2] + \theta_2^2 E[\epsilon_{t-2}^2]$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_0 = (1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2)\sigma_\epsilon^2}$$

### Autocovariance at Lag 1

$$\gamma_1 = E[x_t \, x_{t-1}] = E[(\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2})(\epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-2} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-3})]$$

Multiplying and discarding all zero cross-terms (except $\epsilon_{t-1}^2$):

$$= \theta_1 E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2] + \theta_1 \theta_2 E[\epsilon_{t-2}^2]$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_1 = (\theta_1 + \theta_1 \theta_2)\sigma_\epsilon^2}$$

### Autocovariance at Lag 2

$$\gamma_2 = E[x_t \, x_{t-2}] = E[(\epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-2})(\epsilon_{t-2} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-3} + \theta_2 \epsilon_{t-4})]$$

The only surviving product is $\theta_2 \cdot E[\epsilon_{t-2}^2]$:

$$\boxed{\gamma_2 = \theta_2 \sigma_\epsilon^2}$$

### Autocovariance at Lag 3

$$\gamma_3 = E[x_t \, x_{t-3}]$$

Now $x_t$ uses $\epsilon_t, \epsilon_{t-1}, \epsilon_{t-2}$ and $x_{t-3}$ uses $\epsilon_{t-3}, \epsilon_{t-4}, \epsilon_{t-5}$. There is **no common time index** between the two expressions, so every product has distinct time indices:

$$\boxed{\gamma_3 = 0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalised Cut-Off:** For MA(q), $\gamma_k = 0$ for all $k > q$. This is the **defining signature** that KTU examiners test in identification problems.

### Resulting ACF for MA(2)

$$\rho_1 = \frac{\theta_1(1 + \theta_2)}{1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2}, \quad \rho_2 = \frac{\theta_2}{1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2}, \quad \rho_k = 0 \text{ for } k \geq 3$$

## 3.3 Invertibility Check (MA(1) Detailed)

An MA(1) can be rewritten as an AR($\infty$) if $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$. Let us derive this.

We want to express $\epsilon_t$ in terms of $x_t, x_{t-1}, x_{t-2}, \dots$:

$$x_t = \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}$$

Rearrange:

$$\epsilon_t = x_t - \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1}$$

Substitute $\epsilon_{t-1} = x_{t-1} - \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-2}$:

$$\epsilon_t = x_t - \theta_1 (x_{t-1} - \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-2}) = x_t - \theta_1 x_{t-1} + \theta_1^2 \epsilon_{t-2}$$

Continuing recursively:

$$\epsilon_t = x_t - \theta_1 x_{t-1} + \theta_1^2 x_{t-2} - \theta_1^3 x_{t-3} + \dots$$

$$\epsilon_t = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} (-\theta_1)^j x_{t-j}$$

This infinite sum **converges** if and only if $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$. Hence the invertibility condition.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **architecture of an MA(q) data-generation process** — how current and past white-noise shocks are combined linearly to produce the observed series $X_t$.

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the MA(q) Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[White Noise Generator] --> node2[epsilon_t]
    node1 --> node3[epsilon_t-1]
    node1 --> node4[epsilon_t-2]
    node1 --> node5[epsilon_t-q]
    node2 --> node6[Multiplier: 1]
    node3 --> node7[Multiplier: theta1]
    node4 --> node8[Multiplier: theta2]
    node5 --> node9[Multiplier: thetaq]
    node6 --> node10[Summing Junction]
    node7 --> node10
    node8 --> node10
    node9 --> node10
    node11[Constant Mean mu] --> node10
    node10 --> node12[Output Xt]
    node12 --> node13[Observations x1, x2, xT]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- A **white-noise generator** produces the innovation sequence $\epsilon_t$.
- Each lagged error is **multiplied by its corresponding MA parameter** $\theta_j$.
- All weighted errors, together with the constant mean $\mu$, are **summed** to yield $X_t$.
- The output forms the observed time-series data.

## 4.2 ACF Signature Pattern for MA Models (Identification Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Compute Sample ACF] --> node2{First q Lags Significant?}
    node2 -- Yes --> node3[Lags q+1, q+2 Insignificant]
    node3 --> node4[Cut-Off After Lag q]
    node4 --> node5[Conclude: MA q Model]
    node2 -- No --> node6[Re-examine Differencing]
    node6 --> node7[Check Non-Stationarity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this in KTU exams:** Plot the sample ACF. If the first $q$ spikes are outside the $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ confidence bands and **all subsequent spikes are inside**, the data is consistent with an **MA(q)** model.

## 4.3 Comparative Flow: AR vs MA vs ARMA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[AR p Model] --> nodeB[Past Values Drive Future]
    nodeC[MA q Model] --> nodeD[Past Shocks Drive Future]
    nodeE[ARMA p q] --> nodeF[Both Past Values and Shocks]
    nodeB --> nodeG[ACF: Tails Off, PACF: Cuts Off]
    nodeD --> nodeH[ACF: Cuts Off, PACF: Tails Off]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Both ACF and PACF Tail Off]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table** — it is the **single most-tested identification rule** in KTU Module 3 / Module 4 questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a Moving Average model of order q, MA(q). Show that the MA(q) process is always stationary.**

**Model Answer (Expected 3-Mark Structure):**

**Definition (2 marks):** An MA(q) process is defined as:

$$X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \dots + \theta_q \epsilon_{t-q}$$

where $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\epsilon^2)$.

**Stationarity proof (1 mark):** The mean is $E[X_t] = \mu$ (constant). The variance is $\gamma_0 = (1 + \sum_{j=1}^{q}\theta_j^2)\sigma_\epsilon^2$ (finite and constant). The autocovariance $\gamma_k$ depends only on the lag $k$ and not on $t$. Hence the process is **weakly stationary** for any values of $\theta_j$.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the invertibility condition for an MA(1) process $X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + \theta \epsilon_{t-1}$.**

**Model Answer (Expected 3-Mark Structure):**

The MA(1) process is **invertible** if it can be uniquely expressed as an infinite-order AR process:

$$\epsilon_t = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} (-\theta)^j (X_{t-j} - \mu)$$

This infinite geometric series converges **if and only if** $\vert \theta \vert < 1$.

**Geometric meaning (1 mark):** This condition ensures all roots of the characteristic polynomial $1 + \theta z = 0$ lie **outside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$, so $z = -1/\theta$ satisfies $\vert z \vert > 1$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**For the MA(1) process $X_t = \mu + \epsilon_t + 0.6 \epsilon_{t-1}$, where $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0, 1)$:**

**(a) Find the mean, variance, and autocovariance function. Comment on stationarity. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Find the autocorrelations $\rho_1, \rho_2, \rho_3$. Also check the invertibility condition. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

**Mean:**
$$E[X_t] = \mu + E[\epsilon_t] + 0.6 E[\epsilon_{t-1}] = \mu + 0 + 0 = \mu$$

**Variance:**
$$Var(X_t) = E[(\epsilon_t + 0.6\epsilon_{t-1})^2] = E[\epsilon_t^2] + 0.6^2 E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2] = 1 + 0.36 = 1.36$$

**Autocovariance at lag 1:**
$$\gamma_1 = E[(\epsilon_t + 0.6\epsilon_{t-1})(\epsilon_{t-1} + 0.6\epsilon_{t-2})]$$
$$= 0.6 E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2] = 0.6$$

**Autocovariance at lag $k \geq 2$:** All cross-products are zero (different time indices), hence $\gamma_k = 0$ for $k \geq 2$.

**Stationarity Comment:** The mean is constant $\mu$, variance is constant $1.36$, and autocovariances depend only on lag, not time. Therefore the process is **weakly stationary for any $\theta_1$**.

#### Model Solution

**Part (b):**

**Autocorrelations:**
$$\rho_1 = \frac{\gamma_1}{\gamma_0} = \frac{0.6}{1.36} = 0.4412$$

$$\rho_2 = \frac{\gamma_2}{\gamma_0} = \frac{0}{1.36} = 0$$

$$\rho_3 = 0$$

The ACF **cuts off** after lag 1 — the signature of MA(1).

**Invertibility check:** $\vert 0.6 \vert = 0.6 < 1$ ✓

Hence the MA(1) model is **invertible**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Consider the MA(2) process $X_t = 8 + \epsilon_t - 0.5 \epsilon_{t-1} + 0.3 \epsilon_{t-2}$, with $\sigma_\epsilon^2 = 2$.**

**(a) Determine the mean, variance, and the autocovariance function up to lag 3. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Compute the autocorrelations $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$. Verify the cut-off property. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

**Mean:**
$$E[X_t] = 8 + 0 - 0 + 0 = 8$$

**Variance:**
$$\gamma_0 = (1 + (-0.5)^2 + (0.3)^2)\sigma_\epsilon^2 = (1 + 0.25 + 0.09)(2) = 1.34 \times 2 = 2.68$$

**Autocovariance at lag 1:**
$$\gamma_1 = (\theta_1 + \theta_1 \theta_2)\sigma_\epsilon^2 = (-0.5 + (-0.5)(0.3))(2) = (-0.5 - 0.15)(2) = -1.3$$

**Autocovariance at lag 2:**
$$\gamma_2 = \theta_2 \sigma_\epsilon^2 = 0.3 \times 2 = 0.6$$

**Autocovariance at lag 3:**
$$\gamma_3 = 0 \quad \text{(cut-off begins)}$$

#### Model Solution

**Part (b):**

**Autocorrelation at lag 1:**
$$\rho_1 = \frac{\gamma_1}{\gamma_0} = \frac{-1.3}{2.68} = -0.4851$$

**Autocorrelation at lag 2:**
$$\rho_2 = \frac{\gamma_2}{\gamma_0} = \frac{0.6}{2.68} = 0.2239$$

**Cut-off verification:** For $k \geq 3$, $\rho_k = 0$. Hence the ACF has only **two non-zero spikes** at lags 1 and 2, confirming the MA(2) structure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the white-noise property** $E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_s] = 0$ for $t \neq s$. If you do not invoke this, the cross-terms will be carried forward incorrectly. **Always** state the white-noise property before expanding.
> 2. **Mis-applying the cut-off.** For MA(q), $\gamma_k = 0$ for $k > q$, not for $k \geq q$. The lag $k = q$ value is **non-zero** in general.
> 3. **Confusing stationarity with invertibility.** Stationarity always holds for MA. Invertibility requires $\vert \theta \vert < 1$ (MA(1)) or root conditions (MA(q)). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these two are conflated.
> 4. **Sign errors in $\theta$ coefficients.** When the model is $X_t = \epsilon_t - 0.5\epsilon_{t-1}$, the parameter is $\theta_1 = -0.5$, not $+0.5$. Plug the sign carefully into $\rho_1$.
> 5. **Skipping units / final numeric evaluation.** In numerical problems, always leave a clear numerical answer (e.g., $\rho_1 = -0.4851$), not just a formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **Definition:** MA(q) is a linear combination of current + q past white-noise shocks. **Always stationary.**
- **Mean:** $E[X_t] = \mu$, **time-invariant**.
- **Variance:** $\gamma_0 = \left(1 + \sum_{j=1}^{q}\theta_j^2\right)\sigma_\epsilon^2$, **finite and positive**.
- **ACF Cut-Off:** The autocovariance $\gamma_k = 0$ for all $k > q$ — this is the **identifying fingerprint** of MA models.
- **ACF of MA(1):** $\rho_1 = \dfrac{\theta_1}{1 + \theta_1^2}$, $\rho_k = 0$ for $k \geq 2$.
- **ACF of MA(2):** $\rho_1 = \dfrac{\theta_1(1+\theta_2)}{1+\theta_1^2+\theta_2^2}$, $\rho_2 = \dfrac{\theta_2}{1+\theta_1^2+\theta_2^2}$, $\rho_k = 0$ for $k \geq 3$.
- **Invertibility (MA(1)):** $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$ — guarantees unique representation as AR($\infty$).
- **Invertibility (MA(q)):** All roots of $1 + \theta_1 z + \theta_2 z^2 + \dots + \theta_q z^q = 0$ must satisfy $\vert z \vert > 1$.
- **Identification (Box–Jenkins):** Sample ACF cuts off after lag $q$ → MA(q) candidate; confirm with sample PACF that decays slowly.
- **White noise** is MA(0): $X_t = \epsilon_t$.
- **AR($\infty$) ↔ Invertible MA:** An invertible MA(q) is equivalent to an AR($\infty$) with coefficients $\pi_j = (-\theta)^j$.
- **No programming needed:** For KTU PECDT861 derivations, all MA properties are derived using **white-noise algebra** — no code or simulation is required.
- **Examiners reward:** Correct use of symbols ($\theta, \mu, \sigma_\epsilon^2, \gamma_k, \rho_k$), explicit invocation of white-noise assumptions, and clear final numerical values.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Memory Trick:** *"MA = Memory of shocks that fade away — ACF cuts off, PACF trails off."* This single sentence covers ~80% of KTU exam questions on MA models.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autoregressive Moving Average (ARMA) Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Autoregressive Moving Average (ARMA) Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Autoregressive Moving Average (ARMA) model** is a stationary time series model that combines two fundamental components:

- An **Autoregressive (AR)** part of order $p$ which regresses the current observation on $p$ past observations.
- A **Moving Average (MA)** part of order $q$ which models the current observation as a function of $q$ past white-noise (innovation) shocks.

A general **ARMA$(p, q)$** process $\{X_t\}$ is defined as:

$$X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \dots + \phi_p X_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \varepsilon_{t-2} + \dots + \theta_q \varepsilon_{t-q}$$

where:
- $c$ is a constant (often the mean term $\mu$ when written in mean-adjusted form).
- $\phi_1, \phi_2, \dots, \phi_p$ are the **autoregressive coefficients**.
- $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_q$ are the **moving average coefficients**.
- $\varepsilon_t$ is a **white noise** process with $E(\varepsilon_t) = 0$ and $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2$, with $\varepsilon_t$ mutually uncorrelated across time.
- $p$ and $q$ are non-negative integers (not both zero).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The ARMA model is the parent class that *unifies* AR$(p)$ (when $q=0$) and MA$(q)$ (when $p=0$). Mastery of ARMA is mandatory because the famous **ARIMA$(p, d, q)$** model is built on top of ARMA after differencing a non-stationary series $d$ times.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Memory + Mood" Analogy**

Think of a student's **daily mood score** $X_t$:

- **AR part (Memory):** Today's mood is partly because of *yesterday's mood, the day before, and so on* — a residual chain of past feelings. This is the **autoregressive** part: $X_t$ depends on its own past.
- **MA part (Shocks/Mood Swings):** But moods are also shaken by *short-lived events* like a small argument with a friend, a bad grade, a message from a loved one. These shocks ($\varepsilon_t$) hit the student, but their direct effect lasts only a few days before fading. This is the **moving average** part: $X_t$ reacts to recent random shocks.

**ARMA model = "Mood depends on recent past mood + a few fading recent events."**

A pure **AR** student has a long memory but never reacts strongly to daily shocks. A pure **MA** student is a clean slate every morning, only reacting to today's events. An **ARMA** student is the realistic one: both long memory *and* short-term shocks.

---

## 1.3 The Backshift (Lag) Operator Form

The **backshift operator** $B$ is defined by $B X_t = X_{t-1}$, so $B^k X_t = X_{t-k}$.

Using this, the ARMA model becomes compact and elegant:

$$\underbrace{(1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \dots - \phi_p B^p)}_{\phi(B)} X_t = c + \underbrace{(1 + \theta_1 B + \theta_2 B^2 + \dots + \theta_q B^q)}_{\theta(B)} \varepsilon_t$$

which is written as:

$$\phi(B) X_t = c + \theta(B) \varepsilon_t$$

The two polynomials are:
- **AR polynomial:** $\phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \dots - \phi_p B^p$
- **MA polynomial:** $\theta(B) = 1 + \theta_1 B + \theta_2 B^2 + \dots + \theta_q B^q$

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Mnemonic:** $\phi$ (phi) is for **P**ast (AR), $\theta$ (theta) is for **T**heta (MA shocks). The $\phi$ polynomial multiplies $X_t$, the $\theta$ polynomial multiplies $\varepsilon_t$.

---

## 1.4 The Three Special Cases (Boundary Behaviour)

| Model | Form | $p$ | $q$ | When Useful |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **White Noise (WN)** | $X_t = \varepsilon_t$ | 0 | 0 | Series with no memory |
| **AR$(p)$** | $\phi(B) X_t = c + \varepsilon_t$ | $p$ | 0 | Long memory, no shock lag |
| **MA$(q)$** | $X_t = c + \theta(B) \varepsilon_t$ | 0 | $q$ | Short memory, finite shock lag |
| **ARMA$(p,q)$** | $\phi(B) X_t = c + \theta(B) \varepsilon_t$ | $p \geq 1$ | $q \geq 1$ | Mixed memory + short-term shocks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Box-Jenkins Principle:** In practice, real-world series are often *parsimonious* ARMA — small $p+q$ is preferred. Over-parameterization wastes degrees of freedom and inflates forecast variance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mean-Adjusted (Zero-Mean) Form

If the series is centered, $c = 0$ and the model becomes:

$$\phi(B) X_t = \theta(B) \varepsilon_t$$

The **unconditional mean** of a stationary ARMA$(p,q)$ process is:

$$E(X_t) = \mu = \frac{c}{1 - \phi_1 - \phi_2 - \dots - \phi_p}$$

provided $\phi(1) = 1 - \phi_1 - \phi_2 - \dots - \phi_p \neq 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **mean-adjusted** form, we redefine $Y_t = X_t - \mu$ and the constant $c$ disappears. The KTU convention is to work with mean-adjusted models unless the problem explicitly says otherwise.

---

## 2.2 Stationarity Condition

An ARMA process is **(weakly / covariance) stationary** if and only if:

> All roots of the characteristic equation $\phi(z) = 0$ lie **strictly outside the unit circle** in the complex plane.

Equivalently, if $z_1, z_2, \dots, z_p$ are the roots of $\phi(z) = 1 - \phi_1 z - \phi_2 z^2 - \dots - \phi_p z^p = 0$, then:

$$|z_i| > 1 \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Confusing $|z_i| > 1$ with $|z_i| < 1$. In the **backshift** convention used by Box-Jenkins, stationarity requires roots of $\phi(z)$ to lie **outside** the unit circle. The reciprocal convention is used in some econometrics textbooks — always confirm the convention.

---

## 2.3 Invertibility Condition

An ARMA process is **invertible** if and only if:

> All roots of $\theta(z) = 0$ lie **strictly outside the unit circle**.

Equivalently, the MA polynomial can be rewritten as an infinite AR representation (a useful property for forecasting).

$$|z_i| > 1 \quad \text{for all roots } z_i \text{ of } \theta(z) = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why invertibility matters:** It guarantees a **unique** representation of the model. Without invertibility, two different parameter sets could yield the same autocovariance function, making identification ambiguous.

---

## 2.4 Autocovariance Function of ARMA

The autocovariance $\gamma_k = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t-k})$ satisfies a **mixed difference equation** for an ARMA$(p, q)$ process.

**For $k \geq q + 1$** (beyond the MA cut-off), the autocovariance satisfies the **pure AR** homogeneous difference equation:

$$\gamma_k = \phi_1 \gamma_{k-1} + \phi_2 \gamma_{k-2} + \dots + \phi_p \gamma_{k-p} \quad \text{for } k \geq q+1$$

**For $k = 0, 1, \dots, q$** (within the MA range), the autocovariance depends on both the AR coefficients *and* the MA coefficients, and must be solved from the **Yule-Walker–type equations**:

$$\gamma_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \gamma_{k-i} + \sum_{j=0}^{q} \theta_j \sigma_{j,k} \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

where $\theta_0 = 1$ and the cross-covariance terms $\sigma_{j,k}$ vanish for $j > k$.

---

## 2.5 The Autocorrelation Function (ACF) $\rho_k$

The ACF is obtained by normalizing:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0}$$

**Behaviour of ACF for ARMA$(p,q)$:**

$$\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2} + \dots + \phi_p \rho_{k-p} \quad \text{for } k \geq q+1$$

This **tails off** (decays geometrically) for $k > q$, but the first $q$ values are not part of this pattern.

---

## 2.6 Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF)

The PACF $\phi_{kk}$ of an ARMA$(p,q)$ process does **not** cut off after lag $p$ (unlike pure AR) and does **not** behave like a pure MA either. It **tails off** for all lags, typically after a few initial values.

> [!TIP]
> **Identification Cheat (Box-Jenkins):**

| Model | ACF | PACF |
|---|---|---|
| **AR$(p)$** | Tails off | Cuts off after lag $p$ |
| **MA$(q)$** | Cuts off after lag $q$ | Tails off |
| **ARMA$(p,q)$** | Tails off (after lag $q$) | Tails off (after lag $p$) |

Both ACF and PACF tail off for ARMA — this is the *key signature* KTU examiners test.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~80% of KTU exam problems on ARMA.**

| Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| ARMA$(p,q)$ model | $\phi(B) X_t = c + \theta(B) \varepsilon_t$ | $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$ |
| AR polynomial | $\phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \dots - \phi_p B^p$ | Roots outside unit circle |
| MA polynomial | $\theta(B) = 1 + \theta_1 B + \dots + \theta_q B^q$ | Roots outside unit circle |
| Unconditional mean | $\mu = c / (1 - \phi_1 - \dots - \phi_p)$ | If $\phi(1) \neq 0$ |
| Stationarity | $\vert z_i \vert > 1$ for all roots of $\phi(z)$ | Box-Jenkins convention |
| Invertibility | $\vert z_i \vert > 1$ for all roots of $\theta(z)$ | Uniqueness of representation |
| Autocovariance (large $k$) | $\gamma_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \gamma_{k-i}$ | For $k \geq q+1$ |
| ACF (large $k$) | $\rho_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \rho_{k-i}$ | For $k \geq q+1$ |
| One-step forecast | $\hat{X}_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i X_{t+1-i} + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j \varepsilon_{t+1-j}$ | Recursive |
| Forecast error variance | $\text{Var}(e_{t+1}) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2$ | One-step ahead |
| ARMA$(1,1)$ mean | $\mu = c / (1 - \phi_1)$ | Common KTU problem |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where ARMA is used in practice:**

1. **Signal Processing:** Modelling noise in communication channels (wireless, audio).
2. **Economics & Finance:** Short-term forecasting of stock returns, exchange rates, GDP growth.
3. **Industrial Process Control:** Detecting drift in manufacturing sensors.
4. **Weather & Hydrology:** Daily river flow, temperature anomalies.
5. **Biomedical Engineering:** EEG, ECG, heart-rate variability series.

> ARMA is a **stationary** workhorse. For non-stationary series, we use **ARIMA** (differencing) or **SARIMA** (seasonality) — but both are *built on* ARMA. Hence, this topic is foundational.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mean and Variance of the ARMA$(1,1)$ Process — Full Derivation

Consider the ARMA$(1,1)$ model:

$$X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}$$

with $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$ and $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$ (stationarity).

### Step 1: Find the Unconditional Mean $\mu$

Take expectations of both sides, noting $E(X_t) = E(X_{t-1}) = \mu$ and $E(\varepsilon_t) = E(\varepsilon_{t-1}) = 0$:

$$E(X_t) = c + \phi_1 E(X_{t-1}) + E(\varepsilon_t) + \theta_1 E(\varepsilon_{t-1})$$

$$\mu = c + \phi_1 \mu + 0 + 0$$

Solve for $\mu$:

$$\mu - \phi_1 \mu = c$$

$$\mu (1 - \phi_1) = c$$

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{c}{1 - \phi_1}}$$

### Step 2: Mean-Adjusted Form

Define $Y_t = X_t - \mu$. Substituting $X_t = Y_t + \mu$ and $\mu = c/(1-\phi_1)$:

$$Y_t + \mu = c + \phi_1 (Y_{t-1} + \mu) + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}$$

$$Y_t = \underbrace{c + \phi_1 \mu - \mu}_{=\,0} + \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}$$

Hence the **mean-adjusted** ARMA$(1,1)$ is:

$$Y_t = \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}$$

### Step 3: Find the Variance $\gamma_0 = \text{Var}(X_t)$

Multiply both sides of the mean-adjusted equation by $Y_t$ and take expectation:

$$E(Y_t^2) = \phi_1 E(Y_t Y_{t-1}) + E(Y_t \varepsilon_t) + \theta_1 E(Y_t \varepsilon_{t-1})$$

We need to express $Y_t$ in terms of current and past $\varepsilon$'s. Since $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$, we can iterate:

$$Y_t = \phi_1 (\phi_1 Y_{t-2} + \varepsilon_{t-1} + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-2}) + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}$$

$$Y_t = \phi_1^2 Y_{t-2} + \phi_1 (1 + \theta_1) \varepsilon_{t-1} + \phi_1 \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}$$

$$Y_t = \phi_1^2 Y_{t-2} + (1 + \phi_1 \theta_1) \varepsilon_{t-1} + \phi_1 \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}$$

Wait — let us rewrite cleanly. Direct iteration gives:

$$Y_t = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi_1^j \varepsilon_{t-j} + \theta_1 \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} \phi_1^{j-1} \varepsilon_{t-j}$$

$$Y_t = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \psi_j \varepsilon_{t-j}$$

where the **MA$(\infty)$ coefficients** are:

$$\psi_0 = 1, \qquad \psi_j = \phi_1^{j-1}(\phi_1 + \theta_1) \text{ for } j \geq 1$$

Then:

$$E(Y_t \varepsilon_t) = E(\varepsilon_t \cdot \psi_0 \varepsilon_t) = \psi_0 \sigma_\varepsilon^2 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

$$E(Y_t \varepsilon_{t-1}) = E(\varepsilon_{t-1} \cdot (\psi_0 \varepsilon_t + \psi_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \dots)) = \psi_1 \sigma_\varepsilon^2 = (\phi_1 + \theta_1) \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

$$E(Y_t Y_{t-1}) = \gamma_1$$

Now go back to:

$$\gamma_0 = \phi_1 \gamma_1 + \sigma_\varepsilon^2 + \theta_1 (\phi_1 + \theta_1) \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

### Step 4: Find $\gamma_1 = \text{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t-1})$

Multiply the model by $Y_{t-1}$ and take expectation:

$$E(Y_t Y_{t-1}) = \phi_1 E(Y_{t-1}^2) + E(\varepsilon_t Y_{t-1}) + \theta_1 E(\varepsilon_{t-1} Y_{t-1})$$

$$E(\varepsilon_t Y_{t-1}) = 0 \text{ (white noise independent of past)}$$

$$E(\varepsilon_{t-1} Y_{t-1}) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

Thus:

$$\gamma_1 = \phi_1 \gamma_0 + \theta_1 \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

### Step 5: Solve the System for $\gamma_0$

From Step 4: $\gamma_1 = \phi_1 \gamma_0 + \theta_1 \sigma_\varepsilon^2$.

Substitute into Step 3:

$$\gamma_0 = \phi_1 (\phi_1 \gamma_0 + \theta_1 \sigma_\varepsilon^2) + \sigma_\varepsilon^2 + \theta_1 (\phi_1 + \theta_1) \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

$$\gamma_0 = \phi_1^2 \gamma_0 + \phi_1 \theta_1 \sigma_\varepsilon^2 + \sigma_\varepsilon^2 + \phi_1 \theta_1 \sigma_\varepsilon^2 + \theta_1^2 \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

$$\gamma_0 (1 - \phi_1^2) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 (1 + 2 \phi_1 \theta_1 + \theta_1^2)$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_0 = \frac{(1 + 2\phi_1 \theta_1 + \theta_1^2)\,\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}}$$

### Step 6: Simplify Using $(1 + \theta_1 z)(1 + \theta_1 z) = (1 + 2\phi_1\theta_1 + \theta_1^2)$ at $z = \phi_1$

Notice $1 + 2\phi_1 \theta_1 + \theta_1^2 = (1 + \theta_1 \phi_1)^2 + \theta_1^2 (1 - \phi_1^2)$... but the cleaner form is:

$$\gamma_0 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \cdot \frac{1 + 2\phi_1 \theta_1 + \theta_1^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$$

The autocorrelation at lag 1 is $\rho_1 = \gamma_1 / \gamma_0$ where:

$$\gamma_1 = \phi_1 \gamma_0 + \theta_1 \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_1 = \phi_1 + \frac{\theta_1 \sigma_\varepsilon^2}{\gamma_0}$$

> This completes the ARMA$(1,1)$ variance and covariance derivation — a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

---

## 3.2 Yule-Walker–Type Equations for ARMA$(p, q)$

For an ARMA$(p, q)$ process, multiplying the mean-adjusted model $X_t = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i X_{t-i} + \sum_{j=0}^{q} \theta_j \varepsilon_{t-j}$ by $X_{t-k}$ and taking expectation:

For **$k \geq q + 1$** (where MA cross-covariances vanish):

$$\gamma_k - \phi_1 \gamma_{k-1} - \phi_2 \gamma_{k-2} - \dots - \phi_p \gamma_{k-p} = 0$$

For **$k = 0$**:

$$\gamma_0 - \phi_1 \gamma_1 - \phi_2 \gamma_2 - \dots - \phi_p \gamma_p = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \sum_{j=0}^{q} \theta_j^2$$

For **$1 \leq k \leq q$**:

$$\gamma_k - \phi_1 \gamma_{k-1} - \phi_2 \gamma_{k-2} - \dots - \phi_p \gamma_{k-p} = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \left( \theta_k + \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \phi_j \theta_{k-j} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> These are called the **extended Yule-Walker equations**. For pure AR, the right-hand side is zero (for $k \geq 1$), which is why AR has the elegant matrix form.

---

## 3.3 Forecasting with ARMA: Recursive One-Step Ahead

The **minimum mean square error (MMSE) one-step forecast** of $X_{t+1}$ given history up to $t$, denoted $\hat{X}_{t+1 \vert t}$, is:

$$\hat{X}_{t+1 \vert t} = E(X_{t+1} \vert X_t, X_{t-1}, \dots)$$

For the ARMA$(1,1)$ model $X_{t+1} = c + \phi_1 X_t + \varepsilon_{t+1} + \theta_1 \varepsilon_t$:

Since $\varepsilon_{t+1}$ is unpredictable from the past, $E(\varepsilon_{t+1} \vert \text{past}) = 0$. And $\varepsilon_t$ is *known* given the past (it is a residual). So:

$$\hat{X}_{t+1 \vert t} = c + \phi_1 X_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_t$$

The forecast **error** is:

$$e_{t+1} = X_{t+1} - \hat{X}_{t+1 \vert t} = \varepsilon_{t+1}$$

with variance $\text{Var}(e_{t+1}) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2$.

**Two-step ahead** (use $\hat{\varepsilon}_{t+1} = 0$ since it is not yet observed):

$$\hat{X}_{t+2 \vert t} = c + \phi_1 \hat{X}_{t+1 \vert t} + \theta_1 \cdot 0 = c + \phi_1 \hat{X}_{t+1 \vert t}$$

This **recursion** generalises to all horizons — the $\theta$ terms vanish for $h \geq 2$ in ARMA$(1,1)$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: ACF and PACF Behaviour (Reference)

| Lag $k$ | ACF $\rho_k$ (ARMA$(1,1)$) | PACF $\phi_{kk}$ (ARMA$(1,1)$) |
|---|---|---|
| $k = 0$ | $1$ | $1$ |
| $k = 1$ | $\phi_1 + \dfrac{\theta_1 \sigma_\varepsilon^2}{\gamma_0}$ | $\rho_1$ |
| $k = 2$ | $\phi_1 \rho_1$ | $\dfrac{\rho_2 - \rho_1^2}{1 - \rho_1^2}$ (in AR part) |
| $k \geq 2$ | $\phi_1 \rho_{k-1}$ (tail-off) | Tail-off (geometric-like) |

**Key observation:** For $k \geq 2$, $\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1}$ — the AR homogeneous part kicks in, giving a **geometric tail-off** at rate $\phi_1$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Consider $X_t = 5 + 0.6 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t + 0.3 \varepsilon_{t-1}$, with $\sigma_\varepsilon^2 = 4$. Find the mean, variance, and $\gamma_1$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Mean.** $\phi_1 = 0.6$, $c = 5$:

$$\mu = \frac{c}{1 - \phi_1} = \frac{5}{1 - 0.6} = \frac{5}{0.4} = 12.5$$

**Step 2: Variance.** $\theta_1 = 0.3$, $\phi_1 = 0.6$:

$$\gamma_0 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \cdot \frac{1 + 2\phi_1 \theta_1 + \theta_1^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$$

Compute numerator: $1 + 2(0.6)(0.3) + (0.3)^2 = 1 + 0.36 + 0.09 = 1.45$.

Compute denominator: $1 - (0.6)^2 = 1 - 0.36 = 0.64$.

$$\gamma_0 = 4 \cdot \frac{1.45}{0.64} = 4 \cdot 2.265625 = 9.0625$$

**Step 3: $\gamma_1$.**

$$\gamma_1 = \phi_1 \gamma_0 + \theta_1 \sigma_\varepsilon^2 = (0.6)(9.0625) + (0.3)(4)$$

$$\gamma_1 = 5.4375 + 1.2 = 6.6375$$

**Step 4: $\rho_1$.**

$$\rho_1 = \frac{\gamma_1}{\gamma_0} = \frac{6.6375}{9.0625} \approx 0.7324$$

> ✅ This is a typical **7-mark sub-question** in a 14-mark KTU problem.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARMA Model Decomposition — Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram shows how the ARMA$(p, q)$ model is constructed from its building blocks — the AR part, the MA part, and the white-noise source.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[White Noise Source epsilon_t] -->|lag 0| M1[MA Part theta_B]
    A -->|lag 1| M2[epsilon t-1]
    A -->|lag 2| M3[epsilon t-2]
    A -->|lag q| M4[epsilon t-q]
    M1 --> S[Summation Node]
    M2 --> S
    M3 --> S
    M4 --> S
    P[Past Observations X t-1 to X t-p] --> AR[AR Part phi_B]
    AR --> S
    S --> OUT[Current Observation X_t]
    C[Constant c] --> S
    F[Forecast Module] -->|uses past| P
    F -->|uses residuals| A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **MA part** $\theta(B)$ takes the white noise shocks and their lagged versions, multiplies each by $\theta_j$, and feeds them into the summation.
- The **AR part** $\phi(B)$ takes past observations $X_{t-i}$ and multiplies each by $\phi_i$.
- The **constant** $c$ and the two filtered streams all merge at the **summation node** to produce $X_t$.
- The **forecast module** consumes the past observations *and* the past residuals to produce forecasts.

---

## 4.2 ARMA Stationarity & Invertibility Check Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given ARMA p q Model] --> B{Extract phi_B and theta_B}
    B --> C[Find roots of phi_z = 0]
    B --> D[Find roots of theta_z = 0]
    C --> E{All roots mod z greater than 1?}
    D --> F{All roots mod z greater than 1?}
    E -->|Yes| G[Stationary]
    E -->|No| H[Not Stationary]
    F -->|Yes| I[Invertible]
    F -->|No| J[Not Invertible]
    G --> K[Valid ARMA Model]
    I --> K
    H --> L[Re-parameterize or difference]
    J --> M[Apply invertibility constraint]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Application Tip:** If a problem gives $\phi_1 = 1.2$ and asks for stationarity, the answer is "No — root of $\phi(z) = 1 - 1.2 z = 0$ is $z = 1/1.2 \approx 0.833 < 1$, so the process is **explosive / non-stationary**."

---

## 4.3 Box-Jenkins Model Identification Flowchart

This is the most **exam-relevant** diagram. KTU frequently tests the identification of $p$ and $q$ from sample ACF and PACF plots.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample ACF and PACF] --> B{ACF cuts off after lag q?}
    B -->|Yes| C[MA q model]
    B -->|No| D{PACF cuts off after lag p?}
    D -->|Yes| E[AR p model]
    D -->|No| F{Both ACF and PACF tail off?}
    F -->|Yes| G[ARMA p q model]
    F -->|No| H{Both ACF and PACF cut off?}
    H -->|Yes| I[No standard model - over-differenced or seasonal]
    H -->|No| J[White noise or non-stationary]
    G --> K[Estimate parameters via MLE or CSS]
    E --> K
    C --> K
    K --> L[Diagnostic checks residual ACF]
    L --> M{Residuals white noise?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Model Accepted]
    M -->|No| O[Re-identify p and q]
```

---

## 4.4 Forecast Recursion Sequence (ARMA$(1,1)$ example)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Past as Past Data X_t, X_t-1, X_t-2
    participant Resid as Past Residuals epsilon_t, epsilon_t-1
    participant FC as Forecast Engine
    participant Out as Forecast Output
    Past->>FC: Feed X_t, X_t-1
    Resid->>FC: Feed epsilon_t, epsilon_t-1
    FC->>FC: Compute X-hat t+1 = c + phi_1 X_t + theta_1 epsilon_t
    FC->>Out: Return X-hat t+1
    FC->>FC: For h=2: X-hat t+2 = c + phi_1 X-hat t+1
    FC->>Out: Return X-hat t+2
    FC->>FC: For h >= 2: theta terms vanish
    FC->>Out: Continue until forecast horizon reached
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level architecture illustrates the **recursive nature of ARMA forecasting**: the one-step ahead forecast uses both past observations *and* past residuals, but multi-step forecasts treat the future $\varepsilon$ as zero, causing the MA terms to vanish after one step.

---

## 4.5 ARMA as the Parent Class — Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ARMA[ARMA p q General Model] --> AR[AR p Special Case q=0]
    ARMA --> MA[MA q Special Case p=0]
    ARMA --> WN[White Noise p=0 q=0]
    AR --> AR1[AR 1 ]
    AR --> AR2[AR 2 ]
    MA --> MA1[MA 1 ]
    MA --> MA2[MA 2 ]
    ARMA --> ARMA11[ARMA 1 1 ]
    ARMA --> ARMA21[ARMA 2 1 ]
    ARMA --> ARMA12[ARMA 1 2 ]
    ARMA -.-> ARIMA[ARIMA p d q Built on ARMA after differencing]
```

This hierarchy is the **conceptual skeleton** KTU examiners expect students to internalise: every model in time series (other than seasonal/state-space variants) is either a special case of, or a transformation of, ARMA.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual Definition) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q:** Define an ARMA$(p, q)$ model. State the conditions for stationarity and invertibility.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **ARMA$(p, q)$ model** for a stationary time series $\{X_t\}$ is given by:

$$X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \dots + \phi_p X_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \dots + \theta_q \varepsilon_{t-q}$$

where $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$, $\phi_i$ are AR coefficients, $\theta_j$ are MA coefficients, and $p, q$ are non-negative integers.

- **Stationarity condition:** All roots of the AR characteristic polynomial $\phi(z) = 1 - \phi_1 z - \dots - \phi_p z^p$ must lie **outside the unit circle** ($\vert z_i \vert > 1$).
- **Invertibility condition:** All roots of the MA polynomial $\theta(z) = 1 + \theta_1 z + \dots + \theta_q z^q$ must lie **outside the unit circle** ($\vert z_i \vert > 1$).

**Mark split:** Definition (1) + stationarity (1) + invertibility (1) = **3 marks**.

---

### Question 2 (Identification) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** How do you identify whether a given time series is best modelled as an AR$(p)$, MA$(q)$, or ARMA$(p, q)$ process? What role do ACF and PACF play?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Box-Jenkins identification rules** based on the sample ACF and PACF are:

| Model | ACF Behaviour | PACF Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| AR$(p)$ | Tails off | **Cuts off** after lag $p$ |
| MA$(q)$ | **Cuts off** after lag $q$ | Tails off |
| ARMA$(p, q)$ | Tails off | Tails off |

- If the **PACF cuts off sharply** after a finite lag → AR$(p)$.
- If the **ACF cuts off sharply** after a finite lag → MA$(q)$.
- If **both ACF and PACF tail off gradually** → ARMA$(p, q)$.

**Mark split:** AR identification (1) + MA identification (1) + ARMA identification (1) = **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

> **ESE Pattern:** A 14-mark question will have sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

> **(a)** Derive the **unconditional mean** and **variance** of the ARMA$(1, 1)$ model:
> $$X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$$
> Clearly state the stationarity assumption. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Given $X_t = 6 + 0.5 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t + 0.4 \varepsilon_{t-1}$ with $\sigma_\varepsilon^2 = 2$, compute the mean, variance $\gamma_0$, and lag-1 autocovariance $\gamma_1$ of the process. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Stationarity Assumption (1 mark).** We assume $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$, so the root of $\phi(z) = 1 - \phi_1 z = 0$, namely $z = 1/\phi_1$, lies outside the unit circle. This ensures a finite, time-invariant mean.

**Step 2: Mean (2 marks).** Take expectations $E(X_t) = E(X_{t-1}) = \mu$:

$$\mu = c + \phi_1 \mu$$

$$\mu = \frac{c}{1 - \phi_1}$$

**Step 3: Mean-adjusted form (1 mark).** Let $Y_t = X_t - \mu$:

$$Y_t = \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}$$

**Step 4: Variance $\gamma_0$ derivation (3 marks).** Multiply by $Y_t$ and take expectation:

$$\gamma_0 = \phi_1 \gamma_1 + \sigma_\varepsilon^2 + \theta_1 E(Y_t \varepsilon_{t-1})$$

Using the $\psi$-weight expansion, $E(Y_t \varepsilon_{t-1}) = (\phi_1 + \theta_1) \sigma_\varepsilon^2$. Multiply the model by $Y_{t-1}$:

$$\gamma_1 = \phi_1 \gamma_0 + \theta_1 \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

Substitute:

$$\gamma_0 = \phi_1(\phi_1 \gamma_0 + \theta_1 \sigma_\varepsilon^2) + \sigma_\varepsilon^2 + \theta_1 (\phi_1 + \theta_1) \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{\gamma_0 = \frac{(1 + 2\phi_1 \theta_1 + \theta_1^2)\,\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Mean (2 marks).**

$$\mu = \frac{c}{1 - \phi_1} = \frac{6}{1 - 0.5} = \frac{6}{0.5} = 12$$

**Step 2: Variance (3 marks).** Numerator: $1 + 2(0.5)(0.4) + (0.4)^2 = 1 + 0.4 + 0.16 = 1.56$.
Denominator: $1 - 0.5^2 = 0.75$.

$$\gamma_0 = 2 \cdot \frac{1.56}{0.75} = 2 \cdot 2.08 = 4.16$$

**Step 3: $\gamma_1$ (2 marks).**

$$\gamma_1 = \phi_1 \gamma_0 + \theta_1 \sigma_\varepsilon^2 = (0.5)(4.16) + (0.4)(2) = 2.08 + 0.8 = 2.88$$

$$\rho_1 = \frac{\gamma_1}{\gamma_0} = \frac{2.88}{4.16} \approx 0.6923$$

> ✅ **Final Answers:** $\mu = 12$, $\gamma_0 = 4.16$, $\gamma_1 = 2.88$, $\rho_1 \approx 0.6923$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **(a)** For an ARMA$(p, q)$ process, state the **extended Yule–Walker equations** for $\gamma_k$ when (i) $k = 0$, (ii) $1 \leq k \leq q$, and (iii) $k \geq q + 1$. Briefly explain why for $k \geq q + 1$ the equations reduce to a pure AR form. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the ARMA$(1, 1)$ model $X_t = 0.7 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t + 0.5 \varepsilon_{t-1}$ with $\sigma_\varepsilon^2 = 1$. Check stationarity and invertibility. Find the **one-step ahead forecast** $\hat{X}_{t+1 \mid t}$ given $X_t = 10$ and $\varepsilon_t = 0.8$. Also compute the forecast error variance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

For the mean-adjusted ARMA$(p, q)$ process $X_t = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i X_{t-i} + \sum_{j=0}^{q} \theta_j \varepsilon_{t-j}$ with $\theta_0 = 1$:

**(i) $k = 0$ (2 marks):**

$$\gamma_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \gamma_i + \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \sum_{j=0}^{q} \theta_j^2$$

**(ii) $1 \leq k \leq q$ (2 marks):**

$$\gamma_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \gamma_{k-i} + \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \left( \theta_k + \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \phi_j \theta_{k-j} \right)$$

**(iii) $k \geq q + 1$ (2 marks):**

$$\gamma_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \gamma_{k-i}$$

**Reasoning (1 mark):** When $k \geq q + 1$, the MA innovations $\varepsilon_{t-k}$ are uncorrelated with $X_t$ (white noise is serially independent), so the cross-covariance terms vanish and the equation becomes the pure AR homogeneous difference equation.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Stationarity (2 marks).** AR polynomial: $\phi(z) = 1 - 0.7 z = 0 \Rightarrow z = 1/0.7 \approx 1.4286$.
Since $\vert 1.4286 \vert > 1$, the root is **outside the unit circle** → process is **stationary**.

**Step 2: Invertibility (1 mark).** MA polynomial: $\theta(z) = 1 + 0.5 z = 0 \Rightarrow z = -1/0.5 = -2$.
Since $\vert -2 \vert > 1$, the root is **outside the unit circle** → process is **invertible**.

**Step 3: One-step forecast (3 marks).** The MMSE forecast is:

$$\hat{X}_{t+1 \mid t} = 0.7 X_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_t = 0.7 (10) + 0.5 (0.8) = 7 + 0.4 = 7.4$$

**Step 4: Forecast error variance (1 mark).**

$$\text{Var}(e_{t+1}) = \text{Var}(\varepsilon_{t+1}) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 = 1$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where Students Lose Marks on ARMA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to check stationarity** before computing the mean using $\mu = c/(1-\phi_1)$. If the process is non-stationary, this formula is invalid. **(−2 marks typical penalty)**
> 2. **Confusing the unit circle convention.** KTU uses the Box-Jenkins convention where stationarity requires $\vert z \vert > 1$. Writing the opposite will cost full marks on the condition. **(−1 to −3 marks)**
> 3. **Mixing up ACF and PACF identification rules.** ARMA has *both* tail off — students often incorrectly state that ACF "cuts off" for ARMA. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply by $\sigma_\varepsilon^2$** in the Yule-Walker cross-term $\sigma_\varepsilon^2 \theta_k$ — a frequent silent error. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **In forecasting questions:** not explicitly stating $E(\varepsilon_{t+1} \mid \text{past}) = 0$ before dropping the term. Examiners reward the *justification*, not just the answer. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **In numerical problems:** rounding intermediate values too early causes a cascade of incorrect final answers. Use exact fractions where possible. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 7. **Not stating the assumption $\theta_0 = 1$** in MA polynomial notation — small but often penalized.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density summary — read this in the last 5 minutes before the exam.**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- [ ] ARMA$(p, q)$ = AR$(p)$ + MA$(q)$. Special cases: AR (q=0), MA (p=0), WN (p=q=0).
- [ ] Model form: $\phi(B) X_t = c + \theta(B) \varepsilon_t$.
- [ ] Mean: $\mu = c / (1 - \phi_1 - \dots - \phi_p)$ if stationary.
- [ ] ARMA$(1,1)$ variance: $\gamma_0 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 (1 + 2\phi_1 \theta_1 + \theta_1^2) / (1 - \phi_1^2)$.
- [ ] $\gamma_1 = \phi_1 \gamma_0 + \theta_1 \sigma_\varepsilon^2$ for ARMA$(1,1)$.

### 🎯 Stationarity & Invertibility
- [ ] **Stationarity:** roots of $\phi(z) = 0$ satisfy $\vert z \vert > 1$.
- [ ] **Invertibility:** roots of $\theta(z) = 0$ satisfy $\vert z \vert > 1$.
- [ ] Both are *necessary* for a valid Box-Jenkins model.

### 📊 Identification (Box-Jenkins)
- [ ] **AR$(p)$:** ACF tails off, PACF cuts off after lag $p$.
- [ ] **MA$(q)$:** ACF cuts off after lag $q$, PACF tails off.
- [ ] **ARMA$(p,q)$:** **Both** ACF and PACF tail off.

### 🔮 Forecasting
- [ ] One-step ahead: $\hat{X}_{t+1 \mid t}$ uses past $X$'s and past $\varepsilon$'s.
- [ ] Multi-step: future $\varepsilon$'s are set to 0; recursion continues.
- [ ] One-step error variance = $\sigma_\varepsilon^2$ (always).

### ⚠️ Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Always state stationarity assumption *before* computing $\mu$.
- [ ] Never confuse the sign of coefficients ($\phi$ vs $-\phi$ in some textbooks).
- [ ] The constant $c$ is dropped in the mean-adjusted form.
- [ ] In ARMA$(1,1)$ variance, the cross-term $2\phi_1 \theta_1$ is **not** a typo — it is correct.

### 🧠 Bonus: ARMA → ARIMA Connection
- [ ] If a series is **non-stationary in mean** (e.g., random walk, trend), apply differencing $d$ times to get $Y_t = (1-B)^d X_t$.
- [ ] Then fit ARMA$(p, q)$ to $Y_t$ → the combined model is **ARIMA$(p, d, q)$**.
- [ ] This is the natural extension tested in Module 4.

> ✅ **End of Module 3 — ARMA Models Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) Models
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) Models

# ARIMA Models: The Complete KTU Study Guide

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Notation)

The **Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA)** model is the cornerstone of modern univariate time series forecasting, formally established by **Box and Jenkins (1970)**. It is a generalization of the ARMA model that incorporates an *integration* (differencing) operator to transform non-stationary series into stationary ones.

**Formal Definition:**

> A time series $\{X_t\}$ is said to follow an **ARIMA(p, d, q)** process if the **d**-th order differenced series $Y_t = \nabla^d X_t$ follows a stationary ARMA(p, q) process.

The compact operator form is given by:

$$\phi(B)(1-B)^d X_t = c + \theta(B) \varepsilon_t$$

Where:
- **p** = Order of the **Autoregressive (AR)** part
- **d** = Degree of differencing (the **I**ntegration order)
- **q** = Order of the **Moving Average (MA)** part
- $\phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \ldots - \phi_p B^p$ = AR polynomial
- $\theta(B) = 1 + \theta_1 B + \theta_2 B^2 + \ldots + \theta_q B^q$ = MA polynomial
- $B$ = Backshift operator ($BX_t = X_{t-1}$)
- $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$ = White noise with **mean 0** and **variance $\sigma^2$**
- $c$ = Constant (drift) term

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Watch:** The order triplet **(p, d, q)** is the single most-tested concept. Always explicitly state the role of each component in long-answer questions. The parameter **d** is the **number of times** the series is differenced — **not** a lag order.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a **financial advisor** trying to predict a client's monthly electricity bill. You use three mental "sensors":

**AR (Memory of Past Values):** "This month, I look at what the bill was last month, and the month before — they tend to be similar because of seasonal AC usage." The **p** parameter tells you how far back to look.

**I (Focus on Changes, Not Levels):** "Predicting the *absolute* bill is hard because it's always creeping up. Instead, I'll predict the *change* in the bill this month versus last month — that change is much more stable." This is exactly what the **d**-th order difference does.

**MA (Memory of Past Surprises):** "Last month, the bill was unusually high because of a heat wave — I need to *remember* that surprise for a couple of months, because my prediction will gradually catch up." The **q** parameter tells you how long to remember past shocks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** ARIMA = **"Smart Recipe for Predicting Time Series"** that uses past values (AR), past errors (MA), and changes instead of levels (I) to make accurate forecasts. The Box-Jenkins recipe is the standardized cooking procedure.

### 1.3 Why ARIMA Matters — The Three Pillars

- **Pillar 1 — Unification:** A single framework that generalizes AR, MA, ARMA, and random walk models.
- **Pillar 2 — Stationarity Handling:** The I(d) component elegantly converts trending data into stationary data — a requirement for most classical models.
- **Pillar 3 — Strong Theoretical Foundation:** Backed by Wold's Decomposition Theorem, Cramér's representation, and Maximum Likelihood Estimation theory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Building Blocks — A Structured Breakdown

#### **A. Autoregressive (AR) Component — Order p**

The AR(p) model expresses the current value as a linear combination of its **p** most recent past values plus a random shock.

$$X_t = c + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i X_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$$

- **Why AR?** Many real-world phenomena exhibit *momentum*, *persistence*, or *inertia*. Today's stock price is correlated with yesterday's price.
- **How it works:** Each coefficient $\phi_i$ measures the influence of the i-th lag on the current value.
- **Stationarity Condition:** All roots of the characteristic polynomial $1 - \phi_1 z - \phi_2 z^2 - \ldots - \phi_p z^p = 0$ must lie **outside the unit circle**, i.e., $\vert z \vert > 1$.

#### **B. Integrated (I) Component — Order d**

The I(d) component applies differencing **d** times to achieve stationarity.

$$\nabla X_t = X_t - X_{t-1}, \quad \nabla^2 X_t = \nabla X_t - \nabla X_{t-1}$$

- **Why Integrate?** Most economic, financial, and engineering series (GDP, stock prices, sensor drift) are non-stationary due to trends. Differencing removes the trend and stabilizes the mean.
- **How it works:** Apply the backshift operator $(1-B)^d$. The value of **d** is determined by the **Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test** or KPSS test.
- **Common Mistake:** **d** must be the minimum required — over-differencing introduces artificial autocorrelation.

#### **C. Moving Average (MA) Component — Order q**

The MA(q) model expresses the current value as a function of current and past white noise shocks.

$$X_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j \varepsilon_{t-j}$$

- **Why MA?** It captures the *transient* effects of random shocks. A one-time shock $\varepsilon_{t-1}$ influences the series for **q** periods.
- **How it works:** The MA coefficients $\theta_j$ govern how past shocks decay over time.
- **Invertibility Condition:** All roots of $1 + \theta_1 z + \ldots + \theta_q z^q = 0$ must lie **outside the unit circle**, $\vert z \vert > 1$.

### 2.2 The Box-Jenkins Methodology — The 4-Step Recipe

The **Box-Jenkins methodology** is the systematic procedure for building ARIMA models:

1. **Identification** → Use ACF, PACF, ADF test to determine p, d, q
2. **Estimation** → Use Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) to estimate $\phi_i, \theta_j, \sigma^2$
3. **Diagnostic Checking** → Examine residuals using the Ljung-Box test
4. **Forecasting** → Generate point and interval forecasts

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Model** | **Equation** | **ACF Behavior** | **PACF Behavior** | **Stationarity Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AR(p) | $X_t = c + \sum \phi_i X_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$ | Tails off (exponential decay) | **Cuts off** after lag p | Roots of $\phi(z)$ satisfy $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| MA(q) | $X_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t + \sum \theta_j \varepsilon_{t-j}$ | **Cuts off** after lag q | Tails off | Roots of $\theta(z)$ satisfy $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| ARMA(p,q) | AR + MA combined | Tails off | Tails off | Both conditions met |
| ARIMA(p,d,q) | $\phi(B)(1-B)^d X_t = \theta(B) \varepsilon_t$ | Behavior of d-differenced ACF | Behavior of d-differenced PACF | d-differenced series is stationary |

**Backshift Operator Identities:**

- $BX_t = X_{t-1}$
- $B^k X_t = X_{t-k}$
- $\nabla = 1 - B$
- $\nabla^d = (1-B)^d$
- $\phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \ldots - \phi_p B^p$
- $\theta(B) = 1 + \theta_1 B + \ldots + \theta_q B^q$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Systems Engineering:** ARIMA models are used by electricity boards for **short-term load forecasting** (hourly/daily demand prediction).
- **Financial Engineering:** Stock price, exchange rate, and commodity price forecasting.
- **Telecommunications:** Network traffic prediction for bandwidth allocation.
- **Signal Processing:** Adaptive filtering, speech coding, and noise cancellation.
- **Industrial Process Control:** Monitoring manufacturing quality variables over time.
- **Meteorology:** Temperature, rainfall, and humidity forecasting.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** ECG and EEG signal modeling for anomaly detection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common ARIMA Variants Tested in KTU Examinations:**
> - **ARIMA(1,1,0):** Random walk with drift
> - **ARIMA(0,1,1):** Equivalent to Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)
> - **ARIMA(1,1,1):** The most practically useful specification
> - **ARIMA(0,1,0):** Pure random walk
> - **ARIMA(1,0,0):** AR(1) — first-order autoregression

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From ARMA to ARIMA (The Master Equation)

**Step 1: Start with the ARMA(p,q) operator form**

An ARMA(p,q) process is defined by the linear difference equation:

$$\phi(B) X_t = \theta(B) \varepsilon_t$$

This means the AR polynomial $\phi(B)$ operating on $X_t$ equals the MA polynomial $\theta(B)$ operating on white noise $\varepsilon_t$.

**Step 2: Stationarity Check — Apply the Characteristic Equation**

Stationarity of an ARMA process requires that all roots of the AR characteristic polynomial lie outside the unit circle:

$$\phi(z) = 1 - \phi_1 z - \phi_2 z^2 - \ldots - \phi_p z^p = 0$$

If all roots satisfy $\vert z \vert > 1$, the series is **stationary**. If any root satisfies $\vert z \vert \leq 1$, the series is **non-stationary**, and we cannot apply ARMA directly.

**Step 3: Handle Non-Stationarity via the Difference Operator**

Define the first-difference operator $\nabla = 1 - B$. Applying it to $X_t$:

$$Y_t = \nabla X_t = X_t - X_{t-1} = (1-B)X_t$$

For a higher-order trend, apply the d-th difference:

$$Y_t = \nabla^d X_t = (1-B)^d X_t$$

The objective is to find the **smallest d** such that $Y_t$ is stationary (verified using the ADF test).

**Step 4: Substitute the Differenced Series into ARMA**

Replace $X_t$ with $Y_t$ in the ARMA equation:

\begin{aligned}
\phi(B) Y_t &= \theta(B) \varepsilon_t \\
\phi(B) (1-B)^d X_t &= \theta(B) \varepsilon_t \\
\phi(B) \nabla^d X_t &= \theta(B) \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}

This is the **master ARIMA(p,d,q) equation** in compact operator form.

**Step 5: Expand for ARIMA(1,1,1) — A Common Practical Case**

For ARIMA(1,1,1), the parameters are $p=1$, $d=1$, $q=1$:
- AR polynomial: $\phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B$
- MA polynomial: $\theta(B) = 1 + \theta_1 B$
- Difference: $\nabla = 1 - B$

The full operator form is:

$$(1 - \phi_1 B)(1 - B) X_t = (1 + \theta_1 B) \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 6: Multiply out the left-hand side**

\begin{aligned}
(1 - \phi_1 B)(1 - B) &= 1(1-B) - \phi_1 B(1-B) \\
&= 1 - B - \phi_1 B + \phi_1 B^2 \\
&= 1 - (1 + \phi_1)B + \phi_1 B^2
\end{aligned}

**Step 7: Write the explicit difference equation**

\begin{aligned}
X_t - (1 + \phi_1) X_{t-1} + \phi_1 X_{t-2} &= \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}
\end{aligned}

**Step 8: Solve for $X_t$ to obtain the explicit recursive form**

\begin{aligned}
X_t &= (1 + \phi_1) X_{t-1} - \phi_1 X_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}
\end{aligned}

This explicit form is used directly for **forecasting** — by replacing $\varepsilon_t$ with the observed residual.

### 3.2 Derivation: Yule-Walker Equations for AR(p) Coefficients

For an **AR(p) process**, the **Yule-Walker equations** establish a system of linear equations that relate the theoretical autocorrelations $\rho_k$ to the AR coefficients $\phi_i$.

**Step 1: Write the AR(p) equation**

$$X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \ldots + \phi_p X_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 2: Multiply both sides by $X_{t-k}$ and take expectations**

$$E[X_t X_{t-k}] = \phi_1 E[X_{t-1} X_{t-k}] + \ldots + \phi_p E[X_{t-p} X_{t-k}] + E[\varepsilon_t X_{t-k}]$$

**Step 3: Use the white noise property**

Since $\varepsilon_t$ is uncorrelated with past $X_{t-k}$ values, $E[\varepsilon_t X_{t-k}] = 0$. By the stationarity assumption, $E[X_t X_{t-k}] = \gamma_k$.

**Step 4: Divide by $\gamma_0$ to obtain autocorrelations**

$$\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2} + \ldots + \phi_p \rho_{k-p}, \quad k \geq 1$$

**Step 5: Write the matrix form (Yule-Walker)**

\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & \rho_1 & \rho_2 & \cdots & \rho_{p-1} \\
\rho_1 & 1 & \rho_1 & \cdots & \rho_{p-2} \\
\rho_2 & \rho_1 & 1 & \cdots & \rho_{p-3} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
\rho_{p-1} & \rho_{p-2} & \rho_{p-3} & \cdots & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\phi_1 \\ \phi_2 \\ \phi_3 \\ \vdots \\ \phi_p
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\rho_1 \\ \rho_2 \\ \rho_3 \\ \vdots \\ \rho_p
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}

This is a **Toeplitz matrix** (constant along each diagonal) and can be solved efficiently using the **Durbin-Levinson recursion** algorithm.

### 3.3 Derivation: Forecast Variance for an ARIMA Process

For an **h-step-ahead forecast** $\hat{X}_{t+h}$, the forecast error is:

$$e_{t+h} = X_{t+h} - \hat{X}_{t+h} = \sum_{i=0}^{h-1} \psi_i \varepsilon_{t+h-i}$$

Where $\psi_i$ are the coefficients of the **MA($\infty$) representation** obtained by inverting the AR part:

$$\psi(B) = \theta(B)/\phi(B) = \psi_0 + \psi_1 B + \psi_2 B^2 + \ldots$$

The **forecast variance** is then:

$$\text{Var}(e_{t+h}) = \sigma^2 \sum_{i=0}^{h-1} \psi_i^2$$

As $h \to \infty$, the variance approaches the **unconditional variance** of the process — meaning long-horizon forecasts are less reliable.

### 3.4 Analytical Identification Matrix — ACF vs PACF Behavior

| **Model Type** | **ACF Plot** | **PACF Plot** | **Identification Rule** |
|---|---|---|---|
| AR(p) | Tails off (exponential or sinusoidal decay) | **Cuts off** sharply after lag p | Last significant PACF lag ≈ p |
| MA(q) | **Cuts off** sharply after lag q | Tails off (exponential decay) | Last significant ACF lag ≈ q |
| ARMA(p,q) | Tails off | Tails off | Use parsimony principle (low p+q) |
| ARIMA(p,d,q) | Slow decay in original series | Apply after differencing d times | First difference, then identify |
| White Noise | All lags within significance band | All lags within significance band | ρ_k = 0 for all k ≠ 0 |
| Random Walk | ACF: ρ_k ≈ 1 for all k | PACF: ρ_1 ≈ 1, others ≈ 0 | Indicates d=1 needed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance Band Rule:** A sample autocorrelation $\hat{\rho}_k$ is "significant" if $\vert \hat{\rho}_k \vert > 1.96/\sqrt{n}$, where **n** is the sample size. Spikes within this band are considered statistical noise.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Box-Jenkins Methodology — Complete Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START: Raw Time Series Data] --> A1[Step 1 Identification Phase]
    A1 --> A2[Plot the time series]
    A2 --> A3[Apply ADF Test for Stationarity]
    A3 --> A4{Is Series Stationary?}
    A4 -->|NO| A5[Apply First Difference operator]
    A5 --> A6[Re-test Stationarity]
    A6 --> A4
    A4 -->|YES| A7[Plot ACF and PACF]
    A7 --> A8[Identify Tentative p, d, q]
    A8 --> B0[Step 2 Estimation Phase]
    B0 --> B1[Estimate phi and theta via MLE]
    B1 --> B2[Estimate sigma squared]
    B2 --> C0[Step 3 Diagnostic Phase]
    C0 --> C1[Compute Residuals]
    C1 --> C2[Apply Ljung Box Test]
    C2 --> C3{Are Residuals White Noise?}
    C3 -->|NO| A8
    C3 -->|YES| D0[Step 4 Forecasting Phase]
    D0 --> D1[Generate Point Forecasts]
    D1 --> D2[Compute Prediction Intervals]
    D2 --> E0[END: Validated ARIMA Model]
```

### 4.2 ARIMA(p,d,q) Component Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP[Input Series X t] --> DIFC[I Component: Differencing Block]
    DIFC --> DIF1[Apply operator 1 minus B raised to d]
    DIF1 --> STAT[Stationary Series Y t]
    STAT --> ARC[AR Component: phi of B times Y t]
    ARC --> ARD[Weighted Sum of Past Y Values]
    ARD --> MAC[MA Component: theta of B times epsilon t]
    MAC --> MAD[Weighted Sum of Past Errors]
    MAD --> ADD[Add Constant c Term]
    ADD --> OUTP[Output: Modeled Series]
    OUTP --> COMP[Compare with Actual X t]
    COMP --> JUDGE{Error within Tolerance?}
    JUDGE -->|YES| VALID[Model Validated]
    JUDGE -->|NO| RESTART[Re estimate Parameters]
    RESTART --> ARC
```

### 4.3 Model Identification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Plot ACF and PACF of Stationary Series] --> P1{ACF cuts off after lag q?}
    P1 -->|YES| P2{PACF tails off?}
    P1 -->|NO tails off| P3{PACF cuts off after lag p?}
    P2 -->|YES| OUT_MA[Conclude MA of order q]
    P3 -->|YES| P4{ACF tails off?}
    P3 -->|NO tails off| OUT_ARMA[Conclude ARMA of order p and q]
    P4 -->|YES| OUT_AR[Conclude AR of order p]
    P2 -->|NO| OUT_ARMA
    P4 -->|NO| OUT_ARMA
    P0 --> P5{ADF Test of Original Series}
    P5 -->|Non Stationary| P6[Apply First or Second Difference]
    P6 --> P0
    P5 -->|Stationary| P0
```

### 4.4 ARIMA Model Hierarchy — Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[ARIMA p d q Master Model] --> BR1[AR Branch: q equals 0]
    ROOT --> BR2[MA Branch: p equals 0]
    ROOT --> BR3[ARMA Branch: d equals 0]
    ROOT --> BR4[Integrated Branch: d greater than 0]
    BR1 --> LEAF1[AR p 0 0]
    BR2 --> LEAF2[MA 0 0 q]
    BR3 --> LEAF3[ARMA p 0 q]
    BR4 --> LEAF4[ARIMA 0 1 0 Random Walk]
    BR4 --> LEAF5[ARIMA 1 1 0 Drift]
    BR4 --> LEAF6[ARIMA 0 1 1 SES]
```
