## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the ARIMA(p,d,q) model. Explain the role of the **Integrated (I)** component with a suitable engineering example.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The **ARIMA(p, d, q)** model is a generalized time series forecasting model that combines three components: **Autoregression of order p**, **Integration of order d**, and **Moving Average of order q**. The compact operator form is:

$$\phi(B)(1-B)^d X_t = c + \theta(B) \varepsilon_t$$

**Role of the I Component (1 mark):** The I(d) component applies the difference operator d times to convert a **non-stationary** series into a **stationary** one. It removes deterministic and stochastic trends from the data.

**Engineering Example (0.5 mark):** Consider the **hourly power load** data of a city — this series exhibits a strong daily trend and is non-stationary. Applying the first difference $\nabla X_t = X_t - X_{t-1}$ gives the hour-to-hour change in load, which is approximately stationary. Hence **d = 1** is appropriate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Differentiate between **ACF** and **PACF**. How are they used in identifying the order of an ARIMA model?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | ACF (Autocorrelation Function) | PACF (Partial Autocorrelation Function) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Correlation between $X_t$ and $X_{t+k}$ directly | Correlation between $X_t$ and $X_{t+k}$ after removing effects of intermediate lags |
| Formula | $\rho_k = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t+k}) / \text{Var}(X_t)$ | Uses Yule-Walker recursion relations |
| AR(p) Behavior | Tails off (exponential decay) | **Cuts off** sharply after lag p |
| MA(q) Behavior | **Cuts off** sharply after lag q | Tails off |

**Identification Rule (1 mark):**
- If **PACF cuts off** after lag p and ACF tails off → **AR(p) model**
- If **ACF cuts off** after lag q and PACF tails off → **MA(q) model**
- If **both tail off** → **ARMA(p, q) model**

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Box-Jenkins methodology** for building ARIMA models. List and describe all four steps in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an **ARIMA(1,1,1)** model with parameters $\phi_1 = 0.6$ and $\theta_1 = 0.4$, derive the explicit forecasting equation for a one-step-ahead forecast. Given the observations $X_t = 100$, $X_{t-1} = 95$, and the last residual $\varepsilon_t = 2$, calculate the forecast. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Box-Jenkins Methodology (7 marks):**

The **Box-Jenkins methodology** (1970) is a five-stage iterative procedure for identifying, fitting, and validating ARIMA models. The four primary steps are:

**Step 1: Identification (2 marks)**
- **Plot** the time series to detect trend, seasonality, and outliers.
- Apply the **Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test** for stationarity. If non-stationary, apply differencing (first, second, or seasonal) and re-test.
- Plot the **ACF** and **PACF** of the stationary series to identify tentative values of **p** and **q**.
- Use **information criteria (AIC, BIC)** to compare candidate models.

**Step 2: Estimation (1.5 marks)**
- Estimate the AR coefficients $\phi_i$ and MA coefficients $\theta_j$ using:
  - **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** — most efficient
  - **Conditional Least Squares (CLS)**
  - **Yule-Walker equations** (for pure AR models)
- Use numerical optimization (e.g., **Gauss-Newton algorithm**, **Marquardt algorithm**).
- Estimate the noise variance $\sigma^2$.

**Step 3: Diagnostic Checking (2 marks)**
- Compute residuals: $\hat{\varepsilon}_t = X_t - \hat{X}_t$.
- Apply the **Ljung-Box (Portmanteau) test**:

$$Q(m) = n(n+2) \sum_{k=1}^{m} \frac{\hat{\rho}_k^2}{n-k} \sim \chi^2_{m-p-q}$$

- Test for residual normality using the **Jarque-Bera test**.
- If residuals are not white noise, return to **Step 1** and try alternative models.

**Step 4: Forecasting (1.5 marks)**
- Generate **point forecasts** $\hat{X}_{t+h}$ for horizon h.
- Compute **prediction intervals**: $\hat{X}_{t+h} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=0}^{h-1} \psi_i^2}$.
- The forecast horizon **h** is limited by model complexity; long horizons are unreliable.

---

**(b) ARIMA(1,1,1) Forecasting Equation Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Write the ARIMA(1,1,1) operator form (1 mark)**

$$(1 - \phi_1 B)(1 - B) X_t = (1 + \theta_1 B) \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 2: Expand the left-hand side (1 mark)**

\begin{aligned}
(1 - \phi_1 B)(1 - B) &= 1 - B - \phi_1 B + \phi_1 B^2 \\
&= 1 - (1 + \phi_1)B + \phi_1 B^2
\end{aligned}

**Step 3: Write the explicit difference equation (1 mark)**

\begin{aligned}
X_t - (1 + \phi_1) X_{t-1} + \phi_1 X_{t-2} &= \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}
\end{aligned}

**Step 4: Solve for $X_t$ (1 mark)**

\begin{aligned}
X_t &= (1 + \phi_1) X_{t-1} - \phi_1 X_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}
\end{aligned}

**Step 5: One-step-ahead forecast formula (1 mark)**

The h=1 forecast replaces $\varepsilon_{t+1}$ with its expectation (zero) and uses the last known residual $\varepsilon_t$:

$$\hat{X}_{t+1} = (1 + \phi_1) X_t - \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \theta_1 \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 6: Substitute numerical values (2 marks)**

\begin{aligned}
\hat{X}_{t+1} &= (1 + 0.6)(100) - (0.6)(95) + (0.4)(2) \\
&= 1.6 \times 100 - 0.6 \times 95 + 0.8 \\
&= 160 - 57 + 0.8 \\
&= \mathbf{103.8}
\end{aligned}

**Result:** The one-step-ahead forecast is $\hat{X}_{t+1} = 103.8$.

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define the **ACF** and **PACF** of a stationary time series. For an **AR(2) process** $X_t = 0.5 X_{t-1} + 0.3 X_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$, derive the **Yule-Walker equations** and find the autocorrelations $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Ljung-Box test**? How is it used for **model diagnostic checking** in ARIMA modeling? State the hypotheses and the test statistic formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ACF, PACF Definitions and Yule-Walker Derivation (7 marks):**

**Definitions (1.5 marks):**
- **Autocorrelation Function (ACF), $\rho_k$:** Measures the linear correlation between $X_t$ and $X_{t+k}$, defined as $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$ where $\gamma_k = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t-k})$.
- **Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF), $\phi_{kk}$:** Measures the correlation between $X_t$ and $X_{t-k}$ after removing the linear effects of all intermediate observations $X_{t-1}, X_{t-2}, \ldots, X_{t-k+1}$.

**Deriving the Yule-Walker Equations for AR(2) (3 marks):**

The given process is $X_t = 0.5 X_{t-1} + 0.3 X_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$.

**Step 1:** Multiply both sides by $X_{t-1}$ and take expectations:

\begin{aligned}
E[X_t X_{t-1}] &= 0.5 E[X_{t-1}^2] + 0.3 E[X_{t-1} X_{t-2}] + E[\varepsilon_t X_{t-1}] \\
\gamma_1 &= 0.5 \gamma_0 + 0.3 \gamma_1 + 0
\end{aligned}

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by $X_{t-2}$ and take expectations:

\begin{aligned}
E[X_t X_{t-2}] &= 0.5 E[X_{t-1} X_{t-2}] + 0.3 E[X_{t-2}^2] + E[\varepsilon_t X_{t-2}] \\
\gamma_2 &= 0.5 \gamma_1 + 0.3 \gamma_0 + 0
\end{aligned}

**Step 3:** Divide both equations by $\gamma_0$ to obtain autocorrelations:

\begin{aligned}
\rho_1 &= 0.5 + 0.3 \rho_1 \\
\rho_2 &= 0.5 \rho_1 + 0.3
\end{aligned}

**Solving the System (2.5 marks):**

From the first equation: $\rho_1 - 0.3 \rho_1 = 0.5 \Rightarrow 0.7 \rho_1 = 0.5$

$$\boxed{\rho_1 = \frac{0.5}{0.7} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.7143}$$

Substituting into the second equation:

\begin{aligned}
\rho_2 &= 0.5 \times 0.7143 + 0.3 \\
&= 0.3571 + 0.3 \\
&= \mathbf{0.6571}
\end{aligned}

$$\boxed{\rho_2 \approx 0.6571}$$

---

**(b) Ljung-Box Test (7 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The **Ljung-Box test** (1978) is a portmanteau (omnibus) statistical test that checks whether any group of autocorrelations of a residual series is significantly different from zero. It determines whether the residuals are jointly independent, i.e., resemble **white noise**.

**Hypotheses (2 marks):**
- **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** The residuals are independently distributed (white noise). That is, $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \ldots = \rho_m = 0$.
- **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$):** At least one autocorrelation is non-zero, i.e., the residuals still contain serial dependence.

**Test Statistic (2 marks):**

\begin{aligned}
Q(m) = n(n+2) \sum_{k=1}^{m} \frac{\hat{\rho}_k^2}{n-k}
\end{aligned}

Where:
- **n** = sample size
- **m** = number of lags being tested
- $\hat{\rho}_k$ = sample autocorrelation of residuals at lag k

Under $H_0$, $Q(m)$ follows a **chi-square distribution** with $(m - p - q)$ degrees of freedom.

**Decision Rule (1 mark):**
- If $Q(m) > \chi^2_{\alpha, m-p-q}$, **reject $H_0$** → the model is inadequate.
- If $Q(m) \leq \chi^2_{\alpha, m-p-q}$, **do not reject $H_0$** → the model is adequate.

**Application in ARIMA Modeling (1 mark):** If the Ljung-Box test rejects $H_0$ at significance level $\alpha = 0.05$, it indicates the model has failed to capture all the temporal structure. The analyst must return to the **identification phase** and consider alternative orders (p, d, q), additional seasonal terms, or include exogenous variables (ARIMAX).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common mistakes that cost marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the operator $(1-B)$ with $(B-1)$** — The correct difference operator is $\nabla = 1 - B$, NOT $B - 1$. The sign matters.
> 2. **Misinterpreting the 'd' parameter** — 'd' is the **number of differences** (degree of integration), **not** a lag order. Saying "d is the differencing lag" loses marks.
> 3. **Wrong degrees of freedom in Ljung-Box** — Use $m - p - q$, **not** just $m$. The parameters p and q must be subtracted.
> 4. **Mixing up ACF and PACF behavior** — Remember: **AR(p) → PACF cuts off, ACF tails off.** **MA(q) → ACF cuts off, PACF tails off.** This is reversed for many students.
> 5. **Forgetting the stationarity condition** — For AR(p), explicitly state that all roots of $\phi(z) = 0$ must satisfy $\vert z \vert > 1$.
> 6. **Skipping the integration step in the explicit equation** — When deriving the ARIMA(1,1,1) equation, do not forget to expand $(1 - \phi_1 B)(1 - B)$ before substituting numerical values.
> 7. **Not stating the white noise assumption** — Always explicitly state $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$ when defining ARIMA.
> 8. **Inverting ACF and PACF for AR vs MA** — This is the **#1** error in KTU valuation. Drill this distinction thoroughly.
> 9. **Omitting the iterative nature of Box-Jenkins** — Diagnostic checking may send you **back to identification**. The process is cyclic, not linear.
> 10. **Not mentioning AIC/BIC for model selection** — A complete 14-mark answer should include information criteria for comparing competing models.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **ARIMA(p, d, q)** = **A**utoregressive + **R**egression + **I**ntegration + **M**oving **A**verage — three components in one framework.
- The order triplet **(p, d, q)** is the most important concept tested in KTU exams.
- **Box-Jenkins 4-step procedure:** Identification → Estimation → Diagnostic Checking → Forecasting (cyclic, not linear).
- **Stationarity is the fundamental prerequisite** — verified using the ADF test, achieved through differencing.
- **ACF** = direct correlation at lag k; **PACF** = correlation after removing intermediate effects.
- **AR(p) identification:** PACF **cuts off** at lag p, ACF **tails off**.
- **MA(q) identification:** ACF **cuts off** at lag q, PACF **tails off**.
- **ARMA(p, q) identification:** Both ACF and PACF **tail off** — use parsimony principle.
- **Yule-Walker equations** are linear equations (Toeplitz matrix) that solve AR coefficients from autocorrelations.
- **Ljung-Box test statistic:** $Q(m) = n(n+2) \sum_{k=1}^{m} \hat{\rho}_k^2 / (n-k) \sim \chi^2_{m-p-q}$.
- **AIC** (Akaike Information Criterion) and **BIC** (Bayesian Information Criterion) are used for model selection — lower is better.
- The **operator form** of ARIMA is compact: $\phi(B)(1-B)^d X_t = c + \theta(B) \varepsilon_t$.
- **ARIMA(0,1,0)** = Random walk; **ARIMA(0,1,1)** = Simple Exponential Smoothing; **ARIMA(1,1,1)** = Most common practical choice.
- **Stationarity condition:** All roots of the AR polynomial must satisfy $\vert z \vert > 1$ (outside the unit circle).
- **Invertibility condition:** All roots of the MA polynomial must satisfy $\vert z \vert > 1$.
- **Forecast variance grows with horizon h:** $\text{Var}(e_{t+h}) = \sigma^2 \sum_{i=0}^{h-1} \psi_i^2$ — long-horizon forecasts are unreliable.
- **Parsimony principle:** Always choose the simplest model (lowest p + d + q) that adequately fits the data.
- **Residuals of a good ARIMA model must be white noise** — independent, zero-mean, constant variance.
- **Backshift operator identities:** $BX_t = X_{t-1}$, $B^k X_t = X_{t-k}$, $\nabla = 1 - B$.
- **Durbin-Levinson algorithm** efficiently solves the Yule-Walker Toeplitz system in $O(p^2)$ time.
- **Over-differencing** is dangerous — it introduces artificial negative autocorrelation and inflates MA parameter estimates.
- **Under-differencing** leaves residual trend in the data and produces non-stationary residuals.
- **ARIMA models are univariate** — they use only the past of one series. Multivariate extensions include VARMA, VARIMA, and ARIMAX.
- **Significance band** for ACF/PACF: $\pm 1.96 / \sqrt{n}$ for 95% confidence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seasonal ARIMA (SARIMA) Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Seasonal ARIMA (SARIMA) Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Seasonal ARIMA (SARIMA)** models, also known as **multiplicative seasonal ARIMA models**, are a generalization of the non-seasonal ARIMA framework designed to explicitly capture **periodic stochastic behaviour** embedded in a time series. Formally, a series $\{Z_t\}$ is said to follow a **SARIMA$(p,d,q)\times(P,D,Q)_s$ process** if it can be represented by the linear filter equation:

$$
\Phi_P(B^s)\,\phi_p(B)\,(1-B)^{d}\,(1-B^{s})^{D}\,Z_t \;=\; \Theta_Q(B^s)\,\theta_q(B)\,a_t
$$

where $a_t$ is a **white-noise process** with mean zero and constant variance $\sigma_a^{2}$, and $B$ denotes the **backshift (lag) operator** such that $B^k Z_t = Z_{t-k}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim line):** A time series exhibiting *non-stationary seasonal behaviour* is modelled by combining **regular (non-seasonal)** and **seasonal** AR/MA polynomials, with regular differencing of order $d$ and seasonal differencing of order $D$ applied to the observed series $Z_t$ prior to fitting the ARMA structure.

## 1.2 Component-Wise Breakdown of the SARIMA Notation

| Symbol | Order | Role in the Model |
|:------:|:-----:|:------------------|
| $p$ | Regular AR | Number of non-seasonal autoregressive lags |
| $d$ | Regular differencing | Degree of trend removal by $(1-B)^d$ |
| $q$ | Regular MA | Number of non-seasonal moving-average lags |
| $P$ | Seasonal AR | Number of seasonal autoregressive lags (at multiples of $s$) |
| $D$ | Seasonal differencing | Degree of seasonal differencing $(1-B^s)^D$ |
| $Q$ | Seasonal MA | Number of seasonal moving-average lags (at multiples of $s$) |
| $s$ | Seasonal period | Number of observations per year/cycle (**12** for monthly, **4** for quarterly, **7** for daily-weekly) |

The total parameter count is roughly $p+q+P+Q$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather Channel" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Two-Layer Orchestra:**
> Imagine a symphony orchestra where **two layers of musicians** are playing simultaneously:
> - 🎻 **The "Daily" Section (Non-Seasonal AR/MA)** — captures the **day-to-day mood swings** of the series (small, fast oscillations). This is governed by $\phi_p(B)$ and $\theta_q(B)$.
> - 🎺 **The "Yearly" Section (Seasonal AR/MA)** — captures the **annual rhythm** like rainfall every monsoon, retail sales every December, or temperature every summer. This is governed by $\Phi_P(B^s)$ and $\Theta_Q(B^s)$.
>
> Just as a conductor needs *both* sections to play in synchrony, the time series often needs *both* regular AND seasonal polynomials to be properly explained. Ignoring the seasonal section is like listening to only the violins — you hear *some* music but completely miss the seasonal melody.

The **seasonal differencing** operator $(1-B^s)$ is the "de-seasonalizer." It compares each observation with the *same season one period ago*, stripping away the recurring yearly pattern and leaving behind a series that can be handled by classical ARMA tools.

## 1.4 Why SARIMA is Essential — The KTU Motivation

Many real-world engineering and economic series exhibit **deterministic-looking seasonal patterns superimposed on stochastic noise**:

- 📈 Monthly electricity demand (peak in summer/winter)
- 🌧️ Quarterly agricultural production
- 🛒 Weekly e-commerce traffic
- ✈️ Daily airline passenger counts

Standard ARIMA **fails** on such series because the autocorrelation function (ACF) does not decay — it persists at **seasonal lags** (12, 24, 36 …) creating a "Christmas-tree" ACF pattern. SARIMA is the canonical fix.

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Seasonal Periods (must be memorized for KTU exams):**
> - Monthly data → $s = 12$
> - Quarterly data → $s = 4$
> - Daily data with weekly seasonality → $s = 7$
> - Hourly data with daily seasonality → $s = 24$
> - Half-hourly electricity data with daily seasonality → $s = 48$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of SARIMA — Layer-by-Layer Decomposition

The full SARIMA filter can be unrolled into **four independent polynomial layers** stacked multiplicatively. We dissect them one by one:

### 🔹 Layer 1: Regular (Non-Seasonal) AR Polynomial
$$
\phi_p(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \cdots - \phi_p B^p
$$
Captures **short-memory dependence** between adjacent observations.

### 🔹 Layer 2: Regular (Non-Seasonal) MA Polynomial
$$
\theta_q(B) = 1 - \theta_1 B - \theta_2 B^2 - \cdots - \theta_q B^q
$$
Captures **short-memory shocks** propagating from recent innovations.

### 🔹 Layer 3: Seasonal AR Polynomial
$$
\Phi_P(B^s) = 1 - \Phi_1 B^s - \Phi_2 B^{2s} - \cdots - \Phi_P B^{Ps}
$$
Captures dependence between observations **separated by exactly one full seasonal cycle** (e.g., January 2024 vs January 2023).

### 🔹 Layer 4: Seasonal MA Polynomial
$$
\Theta_Q(B^s) = 1 - \Theta_1 B^s - \Theta_2 B^{2s} - \cdots - \Theta_Q B^{Qs}
$$
Captures seasonal shocks from innovations exactly one period apart.

### 🔹 Differencing Stack
- **Regular differencing** $(1-B)^d$ removes polynomial trend.
- **Seasonal differencing** $(1-B^s)^D$ removes seasonal trend (a slowly-evolving seasonal pattern).

## 2.2 The Seasonal Backshift Trick

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The key insight is that **$B^s$ acts like a new "slow" backshift operator.** If we define the *seasonal backshift* $\tilde{B} \equiv B^s$, then $\tilde{B} Z_t = Z_{t-s}$, and the seasonal polynomials look identical in form to regular ARMA polynomials — but they operate at a *slower tempo*.

This allows us to apply the **Wold decomposition theorem** twice: once on the fast (non-seasonal) scale and once on the slow (seasonal) scale.

## 2.3 Why is the Model "Multiplicative"?

The word *multiplicative* refers to the **multiplication of the four polynomial factors** on each side of the equation. This is a modelling choice (not a property of the data) that says: *"the seasonal pattern and the non-seasonal pattern interact by being multiplied in the frequency domain,"* which corresponds to **convolution in the time domain**. This is the most parsimonious way to model seasonal data with a manageable number of parameters.

## 2.4 Identification: Reading the ACF and PACF

The **diagnostic signature** of a SARIMA process in the sample ACF and PACF is the single most important KTU-board-tested skill.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hallmark ACF/PACF patterns for SARIMA (must be memorized):**
> - The **non-seasonal** part behaves like an ARMA$(p,q)$ process at **low-order lags (1, 2, …, few)**.
> - The **seasonal** part shows up as a *spike* at the seasonal lag $s, 2s, 3s, \ldots$ with the corresponding pattern.
> - Between these two zones, the ACF typically **decays quickly to near-zero**.

### Identification Table (Killer KTU Cheat Sheet)

| Model | ACF Pattern | PACF Pattern |
|:------|:------------|:-------------|
| SARIMA$(0,0,0)(0,0,0)_s$ | No significant lags | No significant lags |
| AR part only at seasonal lag: $(0,0,0)(P,0,0)_s$ | Spikes decay at $s, 2s, 3s$ | Cuts off after $P$ spikes at $s, 2s, 3s$ |
| MA part only at seasonal lag: $(0,0,0)(0,0,Q)_s$ | Cuts off after $Q$ spikes at $s, 2s, 3s$ | Decays at $s, 2s, 3s$ |
| Mixed seasonal: $(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_s$ | Both patterns appear (fast + slow) | Both patterns appear (fast + slow) |

## 2.5 The Three-Stage Box–Jenkins Methodology for Seasonal Data

The classical **Box–Jenkins** paradigm extends naturally to SARIMA:

1. **Identification** — Use sample ACF, PACF, and unit-root tests (ADF, KPSS) to choose $p, d, q, P, D, Q, s$.
2. **Estimation** — Fit candidate models via Maximum Likelihood (or CSS) and compare via **AIC, BIC, AICc**.
3. **Diagnostic Checking** — Examine residuals $e_t = Z_t - \hat{Z}_t$ for residual autocorrelation (Ljung–Box test) and normality.

## 2.6 📋 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes |
|:-:|:------------------|:-----------|:------|
| 1 | **Backshift Operator** | $B^k Z_t = Z_{t-k}$ | Foundation of all polynomial operators |
| 2 | **Regular Differencing** | $\nabla Z_t = Z_t - Z_{t-1} = (1-B)Z_t$ | Removes linear trend |
| 3 | **Seasonal Differencing** | $\nabla_s Z_t = Z_t - Z_{t-s} = (1-B^s)Z_t$ | Removes seasonal trend |
| 4 | **Full SARIMA Filter** | $\Phi_P(B^s)\phi_p(B)(1-B)^d(1-B^s)^D Z_t = \Theta_Q(B^s)\theta_q(B) a_t$ | The master equation |
| 5 | **Regular AR Poly** | $\phi_p(B) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^p \phi_i B^i$ | Roots outside unit circle for stationarity |
| 6 | **Regular MA Poly** | $\theta_q(B) = 1 - \sum_{j=1}^q \theta_j B^j$ | Roots outside unit circle for invertibility |
| 7 | **Seasonal AR Poly** | $\Phi_P(B^s) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^P \Phi_i B^{is}$ | Same stationarity rule, applied at lag $s$ |
| 8 | **Seasonal MA Poly** | $\Theta_Q(B^s) = 1 - \sum_{j=1}^Q \Theta_j B^{js}$ | Same invertibility rule, applied at lag $s$ |
| 9 | **White Noise** | $a_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_a^2)$ | $\mathbb{E}[a_t]=0$, $\mathbb{V}[a_t]=\sigma_a^2$, $\mathbb{E}[a_t a_s]=0$ for $t \ne s$ |
| 10 | **Stationarity Condition** | All roots of $\phi_p(B) = 0$ and $\Phi_P(B^s) = 0$ lie **outside the unit circle** $\vert B \vert > 1$ | Both must hold |
| 11 | **Invertibility Condition** | All roots of $\theta_q(B) = 0$ and $\Theta_Q(B^s) = 0$ lie **outside the unit circle** $\vert B \vert > 1$ | Both must hold |
| 12 | **Ljung–Box Statistic** | $Q(K) = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{K}\frac{\hat{\rho}_k^2}{n-k} \sim \chi^2(K-p-q)$ | Test for residual autocorrelation |
| 13 | **AIC** | $\mathrm{AIC} = -2\ln(L) + 2k$ | Lower is better; $k$ = # parameters |
| 14 | **BIC** | $\mathrm{BIC} = -2\ln(L) + k\ln(n)$ | Stronger penalty for parameters |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Seasonal Period $s$ | Series Type |
|:-------------------|:-------------------:|:------------|
| **Power Load Forecasting** | 12 or 24 | Monthly/daily electricity demand |
| **Hydrology** | 12 | River flow, rainfall |
| **Transportation Engineering** | 7 or 12 | Daily commuter counts |
| **Manufacturing Quality Control** | 4 or 12 | Quarterly/monthly defect rates |
| **Telecom Traffic** | 24 or 168 | Hourly call volume |
| **Agricultural Economics** | 4 | Crop yield, fertilizer use |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Expanding the Seasonal Differencing Operator

We need to show explicitly how $(1-B^s)^D$ acts on $Z_t$ for $D=1$ and $D=2$.

### Case A: First-Order Seasonal Differencing ($D=1$)

Starting with the operator definition:

$$
(1-B^s)^1 Z_t = (1-B^s) Z_t
$$

Apply the backshift:

$$
(1-B^s) Z_t = Z_t - B^s Z_t
$$

Replace $B^s Z_t$ by $Z_{t-s}$:

$$
\boxed{\,(1-B^s) Z_t = Z_t - Z_{t-s}\,}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Each observation is now expressed as the difference between its current value and the value from exactly $s$ periods earlier. This is the **seasonal analog of simple differencing** used in trend removal.

### Case B: Second-Order Seasonal Differencing ($D=2$)

Apply the operator twice:

$$
(1-B^s)^2 Z_t = (1-B^s)\big[(1-B^s) Z_t\big]
$$

Substitute the result from Case A:

$$
(1-B^s)^2 Z_t = (1-B^s)(Z_t - Z_{t-s})
$$

Distribute:

$$
(1-B^s)^2 Z_t = Z_t - Z_{t-s} - B^s Z_t + B^s Z_{t-s}
$$

Apply the backshift to each term:

$$
(1-B^s)^2 Z_t = Z_t - Z_{t-s} - Z_{t-s} + Z_{t-2s}
$$

Combine like terms:

$$
\boxed{\,(1-B^s)^2 Z_t = Z_t - 2Z_{t-s} + Z_{t-2s}\,}
$$

> **Insight:** This is structurally identical to a *second-order difference* but at a *seasonal stride* of $s$. For monthly data with $s=12$, this compares January 2024 with January 2023 *and* January 2022.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Unrolling a Concrete SARIMA$(0,1,1)(0,1,1)_{12}$ Model

This is the canonical "airline" model famously fitted by Box & Jenkins to airline passenger data. We derive the explicit time-domain equation.

### Given Model

$$
(1-B)(1-B^{12}) Z_t = (1 - \theta_1 B)(1 - \Theta_1 B^{12})\, a_t
$$

### Step 1 — Expand the Right-Hand Side

Multiply the two MA polynomials:

$$
(1 - \theta_1 B)(1 - \Theta_1 B^{12}) = 1 - \theta_1 B - \Theta_1 B^{12} + \theta_1 \Theta_1 B^{13}
$$

The right side becomes:

$$
Z_t - Z_{t-1} - Z_{t-12} + Z_{t-13} = a_t - \theta_1 a_{t-1} - \Theta_1 a_{t-12} + \theta_1 \Theta_1 a_{t-13}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve Explicitly for $Z_t$

Rearrange to isolate $Z_t$:

$$
\boxed{\,Z_t = Z_{t-1} + Z_{t-12} - Z_{t-13} + a_t - \theta_1 a_{t-1} - \Theta_1 a_{t-12} + \theta_1 \Theta_1 a_{t-13}\,}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This single equation tells the entire story:
> - The first two terms $Z_{t-1} + Z_{t-12}$ add the **regular trend memory** and the **seasonal memory**.
> - The $Z_{t-13}$ correction term prevents double-counting (the interaction term).
> - The four $a$-terms are the noise structure with regular MA, seasonal MA, and their cross-product.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Unrolling a SARIMA$(1,0,0)(1,0,0)_{12}$ Model

This is a pure seasonal AR example (e.g., quarterly retail sales).

### Given Model

$$
(1 - \phi_1 B)(1 - \Phi_1 B^{12}) Z_t = a_t
$$

### Step 1 — Expand the Left-Hand Side

$$
(1 - \phi_1 B)(1 - \Phi_1 B^{12}) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \Phi_1 B^{12} + \phi_1 \Phi_1 B^{13}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply to $Z_t$

$$
Z_t - \phi_1 Z_{t-1} - \Phi_1 Z_{t-12} + \phi_1 \Phi_1 Z_{t-13} = a_t
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $Z_t$

$$
\boxed{\,Z_t = \phi_1 Z_{t-1} + \Phi_1 Z_{t-12} - \phi_1 \Phi_1 Z_{t-13} + a_t\,}
$$

> **Reading the equation:** The current value depends on the **previous observation** ($\phi_1$ term), the **same season last cycle** ($\Phi_1$ term), and a **correction** for the case where both refer to the same observation (the cross term).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Variance of a SARIMA$(0,0,1)(0,0,1)_s$ Process

This is a KTU favourite. We compute $\mathrm{Var}(Z_t)$ for the simplest seasonal-MA process.

### Given Model

$$
Z_t = (1 - \theta_1 B)(1 - \Theta_1 B^s) a_t = (1 - \theta_1 B - \Theta_1 B^s + \theta_1 \Theta_1 B^{s+1}) a_t
$$

### Step 1 — Write the MA($\infty$) Form

$$
Z_t = a_t - \theta_1 a_{t-1} - \Theta_1 a_{t-s} + \theta_1 \Theta_1 a_{t-s-1}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Variance

For a white noise process, $\mathrm{Var}(a_t a_{t-k}) = 0$ for $k \neq 0$. Therefore:

$$
\mathrm{Var}(Z_t) = \sigma_a^2 \left[1 + \theta_1^2 + \Theta_1^2 + (\theta_1 \Theta_1)^2\right]
$$

### Step 3 — Box the Result

$$
\boxed{\,\mathrm{Var}(Z_t) = \sigma_a^2 \left(1 + \theta_1^2 + \Theta_1^2 + \theta_1^2 \Theta_1^2\right)\,}
$$

> **Sanity check:** If $\theta_1 = 0$ and $\Theta_1 = 0$, the process reduces to white noise with variance $\sigma_a^2$. ✓

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Stationarity Condition for a Pure Seasonal AR$(1)_s$

We need to show when $Z_t = \Phi_1 Z_{t-s} + a_t$ is stationary.

### Step 1 — Take the Characteristic Equation

$$
\Phi_1(B^s) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 1 - \Phi_1 B^s = 0
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $B^s$

$$
B^s = \frac{1}{\Phi_1}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Stationarity Rule

For stationarity, all roots of the AR polynomial must lie **outside the unit circle** in the complex $B$-plane:

$$
\vert B \vert > 1
$$

Since $B^s$ and $B$ are related by the $s$-th power mapping, the condition becomes:

$$
\boxed{\,\left\vert \frac{1}{\Phi_1} \right\vert^{1/s} > 1 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \vert \Phi_1 \vert < 1\,}
$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** Even a pure seasonal AR(1) at lag $s$ has a much more lenient stationarity bound than a regular AR(1) because the $s$-th root dampens the constraint. For $s=12$, $\Phi_1$ can be as large as $\approx 0.99$ before the process becomes non-stationary.

## 3.6 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Board Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Problem (frequently tested in KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Consider the SARIMA$(1,1,1)(1,1,1)_{12}$ model applied to monthly retail sales data. Write the explicit expanded equation showing all terms, and identify which terms represent the trend memory, seasonal memory, regular noise, and seasonal noise.

### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

The model is:

$$
(1 - \phi_1 B)(1 - \Phi_1 B^{12})(1-B)(1-B^{12}) Z_t = (1 - \theta_1 B)(1 - \Theta_1 B^{12}) a_t
$$

**Expand the left-hand (AR × differencing) block:**

First, $(1 - \phi_1 B)(1 - \Phi_1 B^{12})$:

$$
1 - \phi_1 B - \Phi_1 B^{12} + \phi_1 \Phi_1 B^{13}
$$

Next, $(1-B)(1-B^{12})$:

$$
1 - B - B^{12} + B^{13}
$$

Multiplying these two gives the full LHS operator (4×4 = 16 terms; we list the unique lag powers):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS operator} =\;& 1 - (\phi_1 + 1) B + \phi_1 B^2 \;-\; (\Phi_1 + 1) B^{12} + (\Phi_1 \phi_1 + \phi_1 + 1) B^{13} \\
&- \phi_1 B^{14} \;-\; \Phi_1 B^{24} + \Phi_1 B^{25} \;+\; \phi_1 \Phi_1 B^{26} + B^{12} - B^{13} \;+\; B^{14} - B^{26}
\end{aligned}
$$

(For brevity in a board solution, students typically write the operator in factored form and only expand the right-hand side.)

**Expand the right-hand (MA) block:**

$$
(1 - \theta_1 B)(1 - \Theta_1 B^{12}) = 1 - \theta_1 B - \Theta_1 B^{12} + \theta_1 \Theta_1 B^{13}
$$

**Final Expanded Form:**

$$
\boxed{\,W_t = a_t - \theta_1 a_{t-1} - \Theta_1 a_{t-12} + \theta_1 \Theta_1 a_{t-13}\,}
$$

where $W_t = (1 - \phi_1 B)(1 - \Phi_1 B^{12})(1-B)(1-B^{12}) Z_t$ is the *fully-differenced-and-filtered* series.

**Component Identification:**

| Term | Represents |
|:-----|:-----------|
| $(1-B)$ | Trend memory (regular differencing) |
| $(1-B^{12})$ | Seasonal memory (seasonal differencing) |
| $(1 - \phi_1 B)$ | Regular AR (non-seasonal autoregression) |
| $(1 - \Phi_1 B^{12})$ | Seasonal AR (yearly autoregression) |
| $(1 - \theta_1 B)$ | Regular MA (non-seasonal moving average) |
| $(1 - \Theta_1 B^{12})$ | Seasonal MA (yearly moving average) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a SARIMA Model

The diagram below maps the complete information flow inside a SARIMA filter — from raw observation $Z_t$ through the differencing stack, AR/MA polynomials, and the white-noise innovation $a_t$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawSeries["Observed Series Zt with Trend and Seasonality"] --> regDiff["Regular Differencing: 1 minus B to the d"]
    regDiff --> seasDiff["Seasonal Differencing: 1 minus B to the s to the D"]
    seasDiff --> regAR["Regular AR Polynomial: phi p of B"]
    regAR --> seasAR["Seasonal AR Polynomial: Phi P of B to the s"]
    seasAR --> filterOut["Filtered Residuals at Output"]

    noiseInput["White Noise at"] --> regMA["Regular MA Polynomial: theta q of B"]
    regMA --> seasMA["Seasonal MA Polynomial: Theta Q of B to the s"]
    seasMA --> filterOut

    filterOut --> residual["Residual Series et"]
    residual --> ljungBox["Ljung Box Q Statistic on Residuals"]
    ljungBox --> decision{"Residuals White Noise?"}
    decision -- Yes --> forecastOk["Model Accepted: Forecast Zt plus h"]
    decision -- No --> loopBack["Re identify p d q P D Q and Refit"]
    loopBack --> regDiff

    forecastOk --> aicCompare["Compare AIC BIC with Alternative Models"]
    aicCompare --> finalModel["Final Selected SARIMA Model"]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree for Choosing $p, d, q, P, D, Q$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start with Observed Seasonal Series Zt"] --> trendQ{"Is there a Trend? ADF and KPSS tests"}
    trendQ -- Yes --> d1["Set d equals 1 or 2 after ADF check"]
    trendQ -- No --> d0["Keep d equals 0"]
    d1 --> seasQ{"Is there a Seasonal Pattern? ACF spikes at lag s?"}
    d0 --> seasQ
    seasQ -- Yes --> D1["Apply Seasonal Differencing: set D equals 1"]
    seasQ -- No --> D0["Set D equals 0"]
    D1 --> acfPACF["Inspect ACF and PACF at low lags 1 to p and at seasonal lags s 2s 3s"]
    D0 --> acfPACF
    acfPACF --> chooseP["Choose P from seasonal PACF cut off"]
    acfPACF --> chooseQ["Choose Q from seasonal ACF cut off"]
    acfPACF --> choosep["Choose p from regular PACF cut off"]
    acfPACF --> chooseq["Choose q from regular ACF cut off"]
    chooseP --> fit["Fit Candidate Model via Maximum Likelihood"]
    chooseQ --> fit
    choosep --> fit
    chooseq --> fit
    fit --> infoCrit{"Lowest AIC BIC?"}
    infoCrit -- Yes --> diag["Residual Diagnostics: Ljung Box Normality"]
    infoCrit -- No --> reject["Discard and try other p d q P D Q"]
    reject --> acfPACF
    diag --> final["Final SARIMA Model Selected"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram of the SARIMA$(1,1,1)(1,1,1)_{12}$ Airline Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    zt["Zt: Monthly Passengers"] --> diff1["1 minus B: Remove Trend"]
    diff1 --> diff12["1 minus B to 12: Remove Seasonality"]
    diff12 --> ar1["1 minus phi 1 B: Regular AR"]
    ar1 --> ar12["1 minus Phi 1 B to 12: Seasonal AR"]
    ar12 --> out1["Output: Filtered Series"]

    a1["a t minus 1"] --> ma1["1 minus theta 1 B: Regular MA"]
    a12["a t minus 12"] --> ma12["1 minus Theta 1 B to 12: Seasonal MA"]
    a13["a t minus 13"] --> crossTerm["Cross term: theta 1 Theta 1 B to 13"]
    ma1 --> out2["Combine"]
    ma12 --> out2
    crossTerm --> out2
    out2 --> out1

    out1 --> resid["Residual e t"]
    resid --> check["Check White Noise: Ljung Box Q Statistic"]
    check --> ok{"All p values above 0.05"}
    ok -- Yes --> forecast["Forecast Z t plus h for h equals 1 to 12"]
    ok -- No --> refit["Refit with Different Orders"]
    refit --> diff1
```

## 4.4 ACF/PACF Signature Map for SARIMA Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Examine Sample ACF and PACF"] --> B{"Pattern of Lags 1 2 3"}
    B -- Spikes cut off --> C["AR signature at low lags: p is positive"]
    B -- Decay --> D["MA signature at low lags: q is positive"]

    A --> E{"Pattern of Lags s 2s 3s"}
    E -- Spikes cut off --> F["Seasonal AR: P is positive"]
    E -- Decay --> G["Seasonal MA: Q is positive"]
    E -- No pattern --> H["Set P equals 0 and Q equals 0"]

    A --> I{"ADF and KPSS Tests"}
    I -- Non Stationary --> J["Apply regular differencing: d is positive"]
    I -- Stationary --> K["Set d equals 0"]

    A --> L{"CanTest or CH test for Seasonal Unit Root"}
    L -- Significant --> M["Apply seasonal differencing: D is positive"]
    L -- Insignificant --> N["Set D equals 0"]

    C --> final["Candidate SARIMA p d q P D Q to the s"]
    D --> final
    F --> final
    G --> final
    H --> final
    J --> final
    K --> final
    M --> final
    N --> final
```
