# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define a Vector Autoregressive VAR(p) model. State the assumptions on the error vector $\mathbf{u}_t$.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Vector Autoregressive (VAR) model of order $p$**, denoted **VAR(p)**, is a multivariate time series model in which a $K$-dimensional vector $\mathbf{Y}_t = (Y_{1t}, Y_{2t}, \ldots, Y_{Kt})'$ is expressed as a linear combination of its own $p$ past values and the past values of all other variables in the system, plus a stochastic error term.

Mathematically:

$$\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{A}_2 \mathbf{Y}_{t-2} + \cdots + \mathbf{A}_p \mathbf{Y}_{t-p} + \mathbf{u}_t$$

**Assumptions on $\mathbf{u}_t$:**
1. **Zero mean:** $\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{u}_t) = \mathbf{0}$.
2. **No serial correlation:** $\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{u}_t \mathbf{u}_s') = \mathbf{0}$ for $t \neq s$ (white noise).
3. **Constant positive-definite covariance:** $\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{u}_t \mathbf{u}_t') = \mathbf{\Sigma}$ with $\mathbf{\Sigma} > 0$.
4. **Normality (for inference):** $\mathbf{u}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{\Sigma})$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q2. What are Impulse Response Functions (IRF) in a VAR model? Why are orthogonalized IRFs often preferred over simple IRFs?**

**Model Answer:**

An **Impulse Response Function (IRF)** traces the effect of a one-time shock (innovation) to one of the innovations $\mathbf{u}_t$ on the current and future values of all variables in the VAR system. The IRF coefficients $\mathbf{\Phi}_j$ are the matrices multiplying the lagged shocks in the **MA(∞) representation** $\mathbf{Y}_t = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \mathbf{\Phi}_j \mathbf{u}_{t-j}$.

**Why orthogonalized IRFs are preferred:**

In a reduced-form VAR, the innovations $\mathbf{u}_t$ are generally **contemporaneously correlated** (off-diagonal elements of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ are non-zero). Thus, a shock in $u_{1t}$ cannot be cleanly separated from a shock in $u_{2t}$ occurring at the same time. To interpret shocks as if they were independent, we use the **Cholesky decomposition** $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{P}\mathbf{P}'$ to define orthogonalized shocks $\mathbf{e}_t = \mathbf{P}^{-1}\mathbf{u}_t$ with $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{e}_t) = \mathbf{I}$. The orthogonalized IRF is $\mathbf{\Psi}_j = \mathbf{\Phi}_j \mathbf{P}$, and depends on the **ordering** of variables (chosen by economic theory or domain knowledge).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** For a bivariate VAR(1) model $\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{u}_t$, with $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.4 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 0.3 \\ 0.3 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}$, **derive the stability condition and check whether the system is stationary**. Compute the **first two impulse response matrices** $\mathbf{\Phi}_1$ and $\mathbf{\Phi}_2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Forecast Error Variance Decomposition (FEVD)**. For the same bivariate VAR(1) above, compute the **2-step-ahead FEVD matrix** using the orthogonalized IRFs obtained from the Cholesky decomposition of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) Solution

**Stability Check (Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$):**

The characteristic polynomial is $\det(\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$:

$$\det\begin{bmatrix} 0.5 - \lambda & 0.4 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = (0.5 - \lambda)(0.6 - \lambda) - 0.08 = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \lambda^2 - 1.1\lambda + 0.30 - 0.08 = \lambda^2 - 1.1\lambda + 0.22 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{1.1 \pm \sqrt{1.21 - 0.88}}{2} = \frac{1.1 \pm \sqrt{0.33}}{2} = \frac{1.1 \pm 0.5745}{2}$$

$$\Rightarrow \lambda_1 \approx 0.8372, \quad \lambda_2 \approx 0.2628$$

Since $\vert \lambda_1 \vert = 0.8372 < 1$ and $\vert \lambda_2 \vert = 0.2628 < 1$, **both eigenvalues lie inside the unit circle**. The system is **covariance-stationary**.

**Impulse Response $\mathbf{\Phi}_1$:**

By definition for VAR(1), $\mathbf{\Phi}_1 = \mathbf{A}$:

$$\mathbf{\Phi}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.4 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Impulse Response $\mathbf{\Phi}_2$:**

For VAR(1), $\mathbf{\Phi}_2 = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{\Phi}_1 = \mathbf{A}^2$:

$$\mathbf{A}^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.4 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.4 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (0.5)(0.5) + (0.4)(0.2) & (0.5)(0.4) + (0.4)(0.6) \\ (0.2)(0.5) + (0.6)(0.2) & (0.2)(0.4) + (0.6)(0.6) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 + 0.08 & 0.20 + 0.24 \\ 0.10 + 0.12 & 0.08 + 0.36 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.33 & 0.44 \\ 0.22 & 0.44 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Interpretation:** A unit shock to $u_{1t}$ increases $Y_{1,t+2}$ by $0.33$ and $Y_{2,t+2}$ by $0.22$ (in expectation). $\blacksquare$

##### Part (b) Solution

**FEVD Conceptual Explanation:**

FEVD answers the question: *"Of the $h$-step-ahead forecast error variance of variable $i$, what fraction is attributable to shocks originating in variable $j$?"*

It uses **orthogonalized IRFs** to ensure shocks are mutually independent.

**Step 1 — Cholesky Decomposition of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$:**

Find lower triangular $\mathbf{P}$ such that $\mathbf{P}\mathbf{P}' = \mathbf{\Sigma}$:

$$p_{11}^2 = 1.0 \Rightarrow p_{11} = 1.0$$
$$p_{21} p_{11} = 0.3 \Rightarrow p_{21} = 0.3$$
$$p_{21}^2 + p_{22}^2 = 1.0 \Rightarrow 0.09 + p_{22}^2 = 1.0 \Rightarrow p_{22} = \sqrt{0.91} \approx 0.9539$$

$$\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 0 \\ 0.3 & 0.9539 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Orthogonalized IRFs $\mathbf{\Psi}_j = \mathbf{\Phi}_j \mathbf{P}$:**

$$\mathbf{\Psi}_1 = \mathbf{\Phi}_1 \mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.4 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 0 \\ 0.3 & 0.9539 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 + 0.12 & 0 + 0.3816 \\ 0.2 + 0.18 & 0 + 0.5723 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.62 & 0.3816 \\ 0.38 & 0.5723 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{\Psi}_2 = \mathbf{\Phi}_2 \mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.33 & 0.44 \\ 0.22 & 0.44 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 0 \\ 0.3 & 0.9539 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.462 & 0.4197 \\ 0.352 & 0.4197 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — FEVD Formula (h=2):**

For variable $i$, shock $j$:

$$\omega_{ij}(2) = \frac{\sum_{s=0}^{1} \left(\mathbf{e}_i' \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{e}_j\right)^2}{\sum_{s=0}^{1} \sum_{k=1}^{2} \left(\mathbf{e}_i' \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{e}_k\right)^2}$$

**Computing $\omega_{11}(2)$ — contribution of shock 1 to variable 1's 2-step error variance:**

Numerator: $(\mathbf{e}_1' \mathbf{\Psi}_0 \mathbf{e}_1)^2 + (\mathbf{e}_1' \mathbf{\Psi}_1 \mathbf{e}_1)^2$
- $\mathbf{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 0 \\ 0.3 & 0.9539 \end{bmatrix}$
- $\mathbf{e}_1' \mathbf{\Psi}_0 \mathbf{e}_1 = 1.0$
- $\mathbf{e}_1' \mathbf{\Psi}_1 \mathbf{e}_1 = 0.62$
- Numerator: $1.0^2 + 0.62^2 = 1.0 + 0.3844 = 1.3844$

Denominator: sum over $k=1,2$ of $(\mathbf{e}_1' \mathbf{\Psi}_0 \mathbf{e}_k)^2 + (\mathbf{e}_1' \mathbf{\Psi}_1 \mathbf{e}_k)^2$
- $k=1$: $1.0^2 + 0.62^2 = 1.3844$
- $k=2$: $0^2 + 0.3816^2 = 0.1456$
- Denominator: $1.3844 + 0.1456 = 1.5300$

$$\omega_{11}(2) = \frac{1.3844}{1.5300} \approx 0.905$$

**Computing $\omega_{12}(2) = 1 - \omega_{11}(2) \approx 0.095$** (by row normalization).

**Computing $\omega_{21}(2)$ — contribution of shock 1 to variable 2's 2-step error variance:**

Numerator: $(\mathbf{e}_2' \mathbf{\Psi}_0 \mathbf{e}_1)^2 + (\mathbf{e}_2' \mathbf{\Psi}_1 \mathbf{e}_1)^2 = 0.3^2 + 0.38^2 = 0.09 + 0.1444 = 0.2344$

Denominator: sum over $k$
- $k=1$: $0.2344$
- $k=2$: $0.9539^2 + 0.5723^2 = 0.9099 + 0.3275 = 1.2374$
- Total: $0.2344 + 1.2374 = 1.4718$

$$\omega_{21}(2) = \frac{0.2344}{1.4718} \approx 0.159$$

**Computing $\omega_{22}(2) = 1 - 0.159 \approx 0.841$.**

**Final FEVD Matrix (h=2):**

$$\mathbf{\Omega}(2) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.905 & 0.095 \\ 0.159 & 0.841 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Interpretation:** About 90.5% of the 2-step-ahead forecast error variance of $Y_{1t}$ is due to its own shocks, and 9.5% due to shocks in $Y_{2t}$. The matrix rows sum to 1, confirming the decomposition. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What is the **Granger causality test**? For a bivariate VAR(1), state the restricted and unrestricted models, and derive the **F-statistic formula**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the bivariate VAR(1) with parameters from Question A(a), you are given the residual sum of squares (RSS) for the restricted (univariate AR(1)) models:
- RSS for $Y_1$ restricted: $95.4$ with $T = 50$.
- RSS for $Y_2$ restricted: $88.2$.
- RSS for $Y_1$ equation in unrestricted VAR(1): $80.1$.
- RSS for $Y_2$ equation in unrestricted VAR(1): $72.6$.

**Test whether $Y_2$ Granger-causes $Y_1$** at the 5% significance level. State the null and alternative hypotheses. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) Solution

**Conceptual Definition:**

**Granger causality** (more accurately, "Granger-predictive causality") is a statistical concept introduced by Clive Granger (1969). A variable $X$ is said to *Granger-cause* $Y$ if the past values of $X$ contain information that helps predict future values of $Y$ *beyond* what is contained in the past values of $Y$ alone. It is a notion of **predictive precedence**, not true causation.

**Restricted Model (for testing "$X$ does not Granger-cause $Y$"):**

$$Y_t = \alpha + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \beta_i Y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t \quad \text{(Y regressed on its own lags only)}$$

**Unrestricted Model (allowing $X$ to Granger-cause $Y$):**

$$\begin{bmatrix} Y_t \\ X_t \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \begin{bmatrix} Y_{t-1} \\ X_{t-1} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_{1t} \\ \varepsilon_{2t} \end{bmatrix}$$

The equation for $Y$:

$$Y_t = c_1 + \beta_1 Y_{t-1} + \gamma_1 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_{1t}$$

**F-Statistic Derivation:**

The null hypothesis $H_0: \gamma_1 = \gamma_2 = \cdots = \gamma_m = 0$ (i.e., $X$'s lags do not help predict $Y$).

The test statistic is:

$$F = \frac{(RSS_R - RSS_{UR})/m}{RSS_{UR}/(T - d)}$$

where:
- $m$ = number of restrictions = number of lagged $X$ coefficients being tested.
- $RSS_R$ = residual sum of squares from restricted model.
- $RSS_{UR}$ = residual sum of squares from unrestricted model.
- $T$ = number of usable observations.
- $d$ = number of parameters estimated in the unrestricted model.
- $F \sim F(m, T - d)$ under $H_0$. $\blacksquare$

##### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — State Hypotheses:**

- $H_0$: $Y_2$ does **not** Granger-cause $Y_1$ (the coefficient of $Y_{2,t-1}$ in the $Y_1$ equation is zero).
- $H_1$: $Y_2$ Granger-causes $Y_1$ (the coefficient of $Y_{2,t-1}$ is non-zero).

**Step 2 — Identify Parameters:**

- $m = 1$ (one restriction: the coefficient on $Y_{2,t-1}$).
- $T = 50$ (sample size).
- $d = K p + K + 1 = 2(1) + 1 = 4$ (intercept + two AR(1) coefficients in the unrestricted $Y_1$ equation: $c_1, \beta_1, \gamma_1$; for this equation alone, $d = 3$; we use $d = 3$ for the per-equation F-test).

**Step 3 — Compute the F-statistic:**

$$F = \frac{(RSS_R - RSS_{UR})/m}{RSS_{UR}/(T - d)} = \frac{(95.4 - 80.1)/1}{80.1/(50 - 3)} = \frac{15.3}{80.1/47} = \frac{15.3}{1.7043} \approx 8.978$$

**Step 4 — Critical Value:**

At 5% significance, with $F(1, 47)$ distribution: $F_{\text{crit}} \approx 4.08$ (from F-tables).

**Step 5 — Decision:**

Since $F_{\text{computed}} = 8.978 > F_{\text{crit}} = 4.08$, we **reject $H_0$** at the 5% level.

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**

There is strong statistical evidence that **$Y_2$ Granger-causes $Y_1$**. Lagged values of $Y_2$ provide significant additional predictive power for $Y_1$ beyond $Y_1$'s own past. $\blacksquare$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Pattern)**
> 1. **Stability check is mandatory** — Many students compute coefficients and forecasts but forget to verify that eigenvalues lie inside the unit circle. *Loss: 2–3 marks per question.*
> 2. **Cholesky ordering assumption** must be stated explicitly. The orthogonalized IRF and FEVD are *not* unique; they depend on the ordering of variables.
> 3. **Do not confuse Granger causality with structural causality.** KTU specifically awards partial credit for the correct *interpretation* — "predictive precedence" rather than "true cause."
> 4. **Granger F-test degrees of freedom** — Students frequently use $T - K(mp+1) - 1$ formula incorrectly. Always state the formula before substituting.
> 5. **FEVD rows must sum to 1** — A row sum not equal to 1 indicates a computational error; examiners check this.
> 6. **Residual diagnostic tests** (Ljung-Box, Jarque-Bera, ARCH-LM) must be *run and reported*; describing them without applying them yields zero marks.
> 7. **Forecast confidence intervals** require the forecast error covariance $\mathbf{\Sigma}(h)$ — students often present point forecasts only.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — VAR Models (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** A VAR(p) regresses each of $K$ variables on $p$ lags of itself and all other variables. **Coefficient matrices** are $K \times K$.
- **Stationarity Condition:** All eigenvalues of the **companion matrix** $\mathbf{F}$ must lie strictly **inside the unit circle** (modulus < 1). Equivalently, the spectral radius $\rho(\mathbf{F}) < 1$.
- **Estimation:** For Gaussian VARs, **OLS equation-by-equation = Maximum Likelihood** (Lütkepohl's theorem). Estimator is $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}$.
- **Lag Selection:** Use **AIC** (overfits), **BIC** (consistent, preferred for large $T$), **HQIC** (intermediate). Choose the lag that **minimizes** the criterion.
- **Stability in Companion Form:** $\det(\mathbf{I}_{Kp} - \mathbf{F}z) \neq 0$ for all $\vert z \vert \leq 1$.
- **Impulse Response Function (IRF):** $\mathbf{\Phi}_s = \sum_{i=1}^{\min(s,p)} \mathbf{A}_i \mathbf{\Phi}_{s-i}$ with $\mathbf{\Phi}_0 = \mathbf{I}_K$. Trace the response of all variables to a shock in one variable.
- **Orthogonalized IRF:** $\mathbf{\Psi}_j = \mathbf{\Phi}_j \mathbf{P}$ where $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{P}\mathbf{P}'$ is the Cholesky decomposition. Depends on variable ordering.
- **Forecast Error Variance Decomposition (FEVD):** Decomposes the $h$-step-ahead forecast error variance of each variable by source shock. Rows sum to 1.
- **Granger Causality:** Test $H_0: \gamma_1 = \cdots = \gamma_m = 0$ using an $F$-test. *Predictive precedence*, not true causation.
- **Forecasting:** $h$-step-ahead forecast obtained by **iterative substitution**. Forecast error variance grows with $h$ and converges to the unconditional variance.
- **Diagnostic Checks:** (i) Multivariate Ljung-Box for residual autocorrelation, (ii) Jarque-Bera for normality, (iii) ARCH-LM for heteroskedasticity, (iv) Stability check.
- **Assumptions:** Stationarity, no serial correlation, homoskedasticity, zero-mean white-noise errors, optional Gaussianity for inference.
- **Structural vs. Reduced Form:** Reduced form uses $\mathbf{u}_t$ (freely correlated). Structural form uses $\mathbf{e}_t$ (orthogonal) via Cholesky or other identification.
- **When to Use VAR:** When multiple time series are **simultaneously endogenous** with bidirectional dynamic relationships; **not** for purely exogenous predictors (use ARIMAX instead).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vector ARMA Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vector ARMA Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Vector ARMA Models — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Vector Autoregressive Moving Average (VARMA)** model is the natural multivariate extension of the scalar ARMA model to a **$K$-dimensional stationary vector time series** $\mathbf{Y}_t = (Y_{1t}, Y_{2t}, \ldots, Y_{Kt})'$. It describes the joint dynamic behaviour of $K$ interrelated time series by allowing each series to be explained by its own past values **and** the past values of all the other series in the system.

The general **VARMA$(p,q)$** process is written as:

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu} = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \, (\mathbf{Y}_{t-i} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \boldsymbol{\Theta}_j \, \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{t-j} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{Y}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$ is the $K \times 1$ observation vector at time $t$.
- $\boldsymbol{\mu} \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$ is the **mean vector**.
- $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_i$ are $K \times K$ **autoregressive coefficient matrices** (for $i=1,\ldots,p$).
- $\boldsymbol{\Theta}_j$ are $K \times K$ **moving-average coefficient matrices** (for $j=1,\ldots,q$).
- $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$ is a white-noise vector with $\mathbb{E}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t) = \mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbb{E}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t') = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\varepsilon}$ (a positive-definite $K \times K$ covariance matrix), and $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t$ is serially uncorrelated.

Special cases:
- **VAR$(p)$** model: $q = 0$.
- **VMA$(q)$** model: $p = 0$.
- **VAR$(1)$** (simplest VARMA): $p=1, q=0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of *PECDT861 — Time Series Modeling & Analysis* treats Vector ARMA models as the unifying framework for analysing **jointly dependent** time series — a single equation for one series is almost always inadequate when variables are dynamically correlated (e.g., GDP & inflation, temperature & humidity, stock index & interest rate).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Why Multivariate?

**Intuition (Plain English):**
Imagine you are monitoring a patient's health with **two sensors**: heart rate and blood pressure. A univariate model for heart rate alone would only use its own past. But in reality, a sudden drop in blood pressure *causes* the heart rate to rise a few seconds later. A **Vector ARMA model** is like having a *control panel that listens to every sensor simultaneously* — it allows the past of one series to influence the future of the other. In other words, the past of the entire *vector* drives the present of the entire vector.

**Single-Variable Analogy → Vector Analogy:**

| Univariate Concept | Vector Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Scalar $y_t$ | Vector $\mathbf{Y}_t$ |
| Coefficient $\phi$ | Matrix $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ |
| White noise $\varepsilon_t$ | White-noise vector $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t$ |
| Variance $\sigma^2$ | Covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\varepsilon}$ |
| Autocorrelation $\rho_k$ | Cross-correlation matrix (CCM) $\boldsymbol{\rho}_k$ |
| Yule–Walker equation | Multivariate Yule–Walker equation |

> [!NOTE]
> A scalar ARMA model fitted separately to each component series **ignores cross-variable dynamics entirely**. Vector ARMA explicitly models **lead–lag relationships** between variables.

---

## 1.3 Why We Need Vector ARMA in Engineering

- **Macroeconomic forecasting** (GDP, inflation, unemployment simultaneously).
- **Signal processing:** joint modelling of multi-channel sensor signals.
- **Control systems:** state-space representation of multiple-input–multiple-output (MIMO) systems.
- **Finance:** portfolio risk, asset-return dynamics, volatility transmission.
- **Biomedical engineering:** EEG, ECG, EMG channel interactions.
- **Climatology:** temperature, pressure, humidity, wind-speed interactions.

> [!TIP]
> Whenever your **KTU 2024 Scheme** problem statement mentions *"two or more related time series"* or *"joint forecasting of multiple variables"*, the answer almost always lives in the VARMA family.

---

## 1.4 Dimensions & Common Symbols (Reference Card)

| Symbol | Meaning | Dimension |
|---|---|---|
| $K$ | Number of component series | scalar |
| $p$ | AR order | scalar |
| $q$ | MA order | scalar |
| $\mathbf{Y}_t$ | Observation vector | $K \times 1$ |
| $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ | Mean vector | $K \times 1$ |
| $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_i$ | AR coefficient matrix | $K \times K$ |
| $\boldsymbol{\Theta}_j$ | MA coefficient matrix | $K \times K$ |
| $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\varepsilon}$ | Innovation covariance | $K \times K$ |
| $\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_k$ | Autocovariance matrix at lag $k$ | $K \times K$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Sub-Families

### 2.1.1 Vector Autoregressive — VAR$(p)$

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \mathbf{Y}_{t-i} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t
$$

- $\mathbf{c} = (I_K - \sum_{i=1}^{p}\boldsymbol{\Phi}_i)\boldsymbol{\mu}$ is a constant intercept vector.
- VAR$(p)$ is the **workhorse** of applied multivariate time series — VMA and full VARMA are rarely estimated directly.

### 2.1.2 Vector Moving Average — VMA$(q)$

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \boldsymbol{\Theta}_j \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{t-j}
$$

### 2.1.3 Vector ARMA — VARMA$(p,q)$

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \mathbf{Y}_{t-i} + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \boldsymbol{\Theta}_j \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{t-j} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t
$$

---

## 2.2 The Lag Operator (Backshift) Form

Let $L$ be the backshift operator with $L\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{Y}_{t-1}$. Define the matrix polynomials:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Phi}(L) = \mathbf{I}_K - \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 L - \boldsymbol{\Phi}_2 L^2 - \cdots - \boldsymbol{\Phi}_p L^p
$$

$$
\boldsymbol{\Theta}(L) = \mathbf{I}_K + \boldsymbol{\Theta}_1 L + \boldsymbol{\Theta}_2 L^2 + \cdots + \boldsymbol{\Theta}_q L^q
$$

The compact form is:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Phi}(L) \, (\mathbf{Y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \boldsymbol{\Theta}(L) \, \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t
$$

---

## 2.3 Stationarity Condition (KTU Favourite)

A VARMA process is **weakly (covariance) stationary** iff:

$$
\det \bigl[ \boldsymbol{\Phi}(z) \bigr] = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \vert z \vert > 1
$$

That is, **all roots of $\det \boldsymbol{\Phi}(z) = 0$ must lie strictly outside the unit circle in the complex plane**.

For a VAR$(1)$ process $\mathbf{Y}_t = \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t$, stationarity requires:

$$
\det(\mathbf{I}_K - \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 z) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \vert z \vert > 1
$$

Equivalently, **all eigenvalues of $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_1$ must lie inside the unit circle**, i.e., $\vert \lambda_i(\boldsymbol{\Phi}_1) \vert < 1$ for $i=1,\ldots,K$.

> [!NOTE]
> Invertibility requires the same condition applied to $\boldsymbol{\Theta}(z)$: $\det \boldsymbol{\Theta}(z) = 0$ must have all roots **outside the unit circle**.

---

## 2.4 Mean of a Stationary VARMA

Taking expectations on both sides of the VARMA equation, stationarity ($\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{Y}_{t-i}) = \mathbb{E}(\mathbf{Y}_t) = \boldsymbol{\mu}$ for all $i$) gives:

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \boldsymbol{\mu}
$$

Solving:

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \bigl( \mathbf{I}_K - \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 - \boldsymbol{\Phi}_2 - \cdots - \boldsymbol{\Phi}_p \bigr)^{-1} \mathbf{c}
$$

provided the inverse exists (which it does exactly when the stationarity condition holds).

---

## 2.5 The Multivariate Yule–Walker Equations

Let $\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_k = \mathbb{E}\bigl[(\mathbf{Y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{Y}_{t-k} - \boldsymbol{\mu})'\bigr]$ be the lag-$k$ autocovariance matrix. Pre-multiplying the VAR$(p)$ equation by $(\mathbf{Y}_{t-k}-\boldsymbol{\mu})'$ and taking expectations gives:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \, \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{k-i} + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\varepsilon} \cdot \mathbf{1}_{\{k=0\}}, \quad k \geq 0
$$

For $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ this is a system of matrix Riccati-like equations that uniquely determine the $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_i$ given $\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_k$.

---

## 2.6 Forecast Formula (KTU Derivative Favourite)

For a stationary VARMA, the **$h$-step-ahead point forecast** $\widehat{\mathbf{Y}}_{t+h \vert t}$ is the conditional expectation of $\mathbf{Y}_{t+h}$ given information up to time $t$, recursively obtained by iterating the model forward and replacing future unknown $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$s by **zero**:

$$
\widehat{\mathbf{Y}}_{t+h \vert t} = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \, \widehat{\mathbf{Y}}_{t+h-i \vert t} + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \boldsymbol{\Theta}_j \, \widehat{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}_{t+h-j \vert t}
$$

with the convention that $\widehat{\mathbf{Y}}_{t+k \vert t} = \mathbf{Y}_{t+k}$ for $k \leq 0$, and $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}_{t+k \vert t} = \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{t+k}$ for $k \leq 0$, and $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}_{t+k \vert t} = \mathbf{0}$ for $k > 0$.

The forecast-error covariance matrix is:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}(h) = \mathbb{E}\bigl[(\mathbf{Y}_{t+h} - \widehat{\mathbf{Y}}_{t+h \vert t})(\mathbf{Y}_{t+h} - \widehat{\mathbf{Y}}_{t+h \vert t})'\bigr]
$$

which grows monotonically toward the unconditional covariance as $h \to \infty$.

---

## 2.7 Granger Causality (Quick Test for Cross-Variable Effects)

For a VAR$(p)$, the $i$-th component of $\mathbf{Y}_t$ is said to **Granger-cause** the $j$-th component if the past of $Y_{it}$ improves the forecast of $Y_{jt}$. Statistically:

$$
H_0: \text{All entries in row $i$ of } \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1, \ldots, \boldsymbol{\Phi}_p \text{ corresponding to column $i$} = 0
$$

This is tested using a **Wald (or LR) test** that follows $\chi^2$ with $p$ degrees of freedom.

---

## 2.8 The MA$(\infty)$ Representation & Impulse Response

A stationary VARMA can be written as:

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \sum_{s=0}^{\infty} \boldsymbol{\Psi}_s \, \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{t-s}
$$

where the **MA-coefficient matrices** $\boldsymbol{\Psi}_s$ satisfy the recursion:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{I}_K, \quad \boldsymbol{\Psi}_s = \sum_{i=1}^{\min(s,p)} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \, \boldsymbol{\Psi}_{s-i} - \boldsymbol{\Theta}_s \cdot \mathbf{1}_{\{s \leq q\}}
$$

The sequence $\{\boldsymbol{\Psi}_s\}$ is called the **impulse response function (IRF)** of the system — it traces how a one-time shock in component $i$ propagates through all components over time.

---

## 2.9 Forecast-Error Variance Decomposition (FEVD)

The $h$-step-ahead forecast error of $Y_{jt}$ can be decomposed into contributions from each shock $\varepsilon_{kt}$:

$$
\text{FEVD}_{jk}(h) = \frac{\sum_{s=0}^{h-1} (\mathbf{e}_j' \boldsymbol{\Psi}_s \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\varepsilon} \mathbf{e}_k)^2}{\bigl[\boldsymbol{\Sigma}(h)\bigr]_{jj}}
$$

where $\mathbf{e}_k$ is the $k$-th unit vector. FEVD answers: *"What fraction of the $h$-step forecast error in $Y_j$ is due to shocks in $Y_k$?"*

---

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-glance summary** — these are the equations you must memorise for KTU 2024 ESE questions on Vector ARMA.

| Concept | Equation / Condition | Dimension |
|---|---|---|
| VARMA$(p,q)$ model | $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(L)(\mathbf{Y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \boldsymbol{\Theta}(L)\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t$ | $K$-vector |
| Stationarity | $\det \boldsymbol{\Phi}(z) = 0 \Rightarrow \vert z \vert > 1$ | scalar condition |
| Invertibility | $\det \boldsymbol{\Theta}(z) = 0 \Rightarrow \vert z \vert > 1$ | scalar condition |
| Mean | $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mathbf{I}_K - \sum \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i)^{-1} \mathbf{c}$ | $K \times 1$ |
| Yule–Walker | $\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{k-i}$ for $k \geq 1$ | $K \times K$ |
| MA$(\infty)$ form | $\mathbf{Y}_t = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \sum_{s=0}^{\infty} \boldsymbol{\Psi}_s \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{t-s}$ | $K \times 1$ |
| IRF recursion | $\boldsymbol{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{I}$, $\boldsymbol{\Psi}_s = \sum_{i=1}^{s} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \boldsymbol{\Psi}_{s-i} - \boldsymbol{\Theta}_s$ | $K \times K$ |
| $h$-step forecast | $\widehat{\mathbf{Y}}_{t+h\vert t} = \mathbf{c} + \sum \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \widehat{\mathbf{Y}}_{t+h-i \vert t} + \sum \boldsymbol{\Theta}_j \widehat{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}_{t+h-j \vert t}$ | $K \times 1$ |
| Granger $F$-test | $F = \frac{(\text{SSR}_{\text{restricted}} - \text{SSR}_{\text{unrestricted}}) / p}{\text{SSR}_{\text{unrestricted}} / (T - 2p - 1)} \sim F_{p, T-2p-1}$ | scalar |
| FEVD (relative) | $\omega_{jk}(h) = \frac{\sigma_{kk}^{-1} \sum_{s=0}^{h-1} (\mathbf{e}_j' \boldsymbol{\Psi}_s \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\varepsilon} \mathbf{e}_k)^2}{\sum_{k=1}^{K}\omega_{jk}(h)}$ | fraction in $[0,1]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why each is needed:** Stationarity/Invertibility are exam-favourite 7-mark derivations. Yule–Walker appears in identification and estimation. IRF/FEVD appear in interpretation (Granger-causality) — a typical "explain with diagram" 3-mark question.

---

## 2.11 Model-Building Workflow (KTU Module-Mapping)

1. **Plot & Transform** the $K$ series; difference to induce stationarity (ADF / KPSS tests).
2. **Identification** — use **VAR-order selection** via AIC, BIC, HQ, FPE.
3. **Estimation** — OLS equation-by-equation (feasible because each equation has the *same* regressors) or Maximum Likelihood.
4. **Diagnostic checking** — residual cross-correlation matrices (Portmanteau/Ljung–Box type tests), residual normality (Jarque–Bera).
5. **Forecasting** — recursive $h$-step forecast + forecast-interval construction.
6. **Structural analysis** — IRF, FEVD, Granger-causality tests.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic transition below is shown explicitly — *no step is skipped* — to match the depth expected of a **KTU 2024 ESE Part-B 14-mark question**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mean Vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ for a Stationary VAR$(p)$

**Start with the model (with intercept $\mathbf{c}$):**

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_2 \mathbf{Y}_{t-2} + \cdots + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_p \mathbf{Y}_{t-p} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t
$$

**Step 1 — Take unconditional expectations of both sides.** By stationarity $\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{Y}_t) = \mathbb{E}(\mathbf{Y}_{t-i}) = \boldsymbol{\mu}$ and $\mathbb{E}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t) = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{Y}_t) = \mathbf{c} + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \mathbb{E}(\mathbf{Y}_{t-1}) + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_2 \mathbb{E}(\mathbf{Y}_{t-2}) + \cdots + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_p \mathbb{E}(\mathbf{Y}_{t-p}) + \mathbb{E}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ for every expectation:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c} + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \boldsymbol{\mu} + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_2 \boldsymbol{\mu} + \cdots + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_p \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ on the right-hand side:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c} + \bigl(\boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_2 + \cdots + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_p\bigr) \boldsymbol{\mu}
$$

**Step 4 — Move the matrix-product term to the LHS:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} - \bigl(\boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_2 + \cdots + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_p\bigr) \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c}
$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ on the LHS (distributive property of matrix multiplication):**

$$
\bigl(\mathbf{I}_K - \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 - \boldsymbol{\Phi}_2 - \cdots - \boldsymbol{\Phi}_p\bigr) \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c}
$$

**Step 6 — Left-multiply by the inverse matrix, which exists because the stationarity condition guarantees $\det(\mathbf{I}_K - \sum \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i) \neq 0$:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \bigl(\mathbf{I}_K - \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 - \boldsymbol{\Phi}_2 - \cdots - \boldsymbol{\Phi}_p\bigr)^{-1} \mathbf{c}
$$

**Result:** This is the **mean vector** of a stationary VAR$(p)$ process. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Stationarity Condition for VAR$(1)$

**Start with the simplest VAR:**

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t
$$

**Step 1 — Deviation-from-mean form:** Let $\mathbf{Z}_t = \mathbf{Y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu}$. Then the model simplifies to:

$$
\mathbf{Z}_t = \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \mathbf{Z}_{t-1} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t
$$

**Step 2 — Iterate backwards $s$ steps:**

$$
\mathbf{Z}_{t-1} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \mathbf{Z}_{t-2} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{t-1} \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{Z}_{t-2} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \mathbf{Z}_{t-3} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{t-2} \;\Rightarrow\; \cdots
$$

Unrolling:

$$
\mathbf{Z}_t = \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1^s \mathbf{Z}_{t-s} + \sum_{j=0}^{s-1} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1^j \, \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{t-j}
$$

**Step 3 — As $s \to \infty$, the term $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_1^s \mathbf{Z}_{t-s}$ must vanish (otherwise $\mathbf{Y}_t$ would not be covariance-stationary).** The matrix $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_1^s \to \mathbf{0}$ iff all eigenvalues of $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_1$ lie strictly inside the unit disc, i.e., $\vert \lambda_i(\boldsymbol{\Phi}_1) \vert < 1$ for $i = 1, \ldots, K$.

**Step 4 — Equivalent statement in terms of the characteristic polynomial:** Eigenvalues of $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_1$ are the roots of $\det(\boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 - \lambda \mathbf{I}_K) = 0$. By the change of variable $z = 1/\lambda$, the condition $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$ becomes $\vert z \vert > 1$. The characteristic polynomial of $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_1$ in the $z$-domain is $\det(\mathbf{I}_K - \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 z) = 0$.

**Result:** Stationarity of VAR$(1)$ requires **$\det(\mathbf{I}_K - \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 z) = 0$ to have all roots outside the unit circle**, equivalently **all eigenvalues of $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_1$ inside the unit circle**. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> For higher-order VAR$(p)$, the condition generalises naturally: $\det \boldsymbol{\Phi}(z) = \det \bigl(\mathbf{I}_K - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i z^i\bigr) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \vert z \vert > 1$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Multivariate Yule–Walker Equations for VAR$(p)$

**Start with deviation-from-mean VAR$(p)$:**

$$
\mathbf{Z}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \mathbf{Z}_{t-i} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t
$$

**Step 1 — Right-multiply both sides by $\mathbf{Z}_{t-k}'$ (a $1 \times K$ row vector) for $k \geq 0$:**

$$
\mathbf{Z}_t \mathbf{Z}_{t-k}' = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \, \mathbf{Z}_{t-i} \mathbf{Z}_{t-k}' + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t \mathbf{Z}_{t-k}'
$$

**Step 2 — Take expectations.** Use the white-noise property $\mathbb{E}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t \mathbf{Z}_{t-k}') = \mathbf{0}$ for $k \geq 1$ (uncorrelated with past), and $\mathbb{E}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t') = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\varepsilon}$. The result is the lag-$k$ autocovariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_k = \mathbb{E}(\mathbf{Z}_t \mathbf{Z}_{t-k}')$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \, \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{k-i} + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\varepsilon} \cdot \delta_{k,0}
$$

where $\delta_{k,0} = 1$ if $k=0$ and $0$ otherwise (the Kronecker delta).

**Step 3 — Separate the $k=0$ and $k \geq 1$ cases:**

For $k = 0$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \, \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{-i} + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\varepsilon}
$$

Using $\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{-i} = \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_i'$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \, \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_i' + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\varepsilon}
$$

For $k \geq 1$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \, \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{k-i}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve recursively for the $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_i$:** For $k=1,2,\ldots,p$ we obtain $p$ matrix equations in the unknown $\{\boldsymbol{\Phi}_1, \ldots, \boldsymbol{\Phi}_p\}$. Stacking them vertically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_1 &= \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_0 + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_2 \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{-1} + \cdots + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_p \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{-(p-1)} \\
\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_2 &= \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_1 + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_2 \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_0 + \cdots + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_p \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{-(p-2)} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_p &= \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{p-1} + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_2 \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{p-2} + \cdots + \boldsymbol{\Phi}_p \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_0
\end{aligned}
$$

Define the block matrix:

$$
\mathbf{G} = \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_1 \\ \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_p \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_0 & \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{-1} & \cdots & \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{-(p-1)} \\ \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_1 & \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_0 & \cdots & \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{-(p-2)} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{p-1} & \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{p-2} & \cdots & \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\boldsymbol{\Phi} = \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \\ \boldsymbol{\Phi}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \boldsymbol{\Phi}_p \end{bmatrix}
$$

Then:

$$
\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{A} \, \boldsymbol{\Phi} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boldsymbol{\Phi} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} \mathbf{G}
$$

provided $\mathbf{A}$ is non-singular (which holds for a stationary process). This is the **multivariate Yule–Walker estimator** for VAR coefficients. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the MA$(\infty)$ / Impulse-Response Recursion

**Step 1 — Start from the lag-operator form:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\Phi}(L) \, \mathbf{Z}_t = \boldsymbol{\Theta}(L) \, \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t
$$

Formally invert $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(L)$ as a matrix power series in $L$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Phi}(L)^{-1} = \sum_{s=0}^{\infty} \boldsymbol{\Psi}_s L^s
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the inverse to both sides:**

$$
\mathbf{Z}_t = \boldsymbol{\Phi}(L)^{-1} \boldsymbol{\Theta}(L) \, \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t = \sum_{s=0}^{\infty} \boldsymbol{\Psi}_s \, \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{t-s}
$$

**Step 3 — Recursion for $\boldsymbol{\Psi}_s$.** Multiply both sides of $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(L) \sum \boldsymbol{\Psi}_s L^s = \boldsymbol{\Theta}(L)$ and compare coefficients of $L^s$:

For $s = 0$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{I}_K
$$

For $s \geq 1$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Psi}_s - \sum_{i=1}^{\min(s,p)} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \boldsymbol{\Psi}_{s-i} = \boldsymbol{\Theta}_s \cdot \mathbf{1}_{\{s \leq q\}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Psi}_s = \sum_{i=1}^{\min(s,p)} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_i \boldsymbol{\Psi}_{s-i} + \boldsymbol{\Theta}_s \cdot \mathbf{1}_{\{s \leq q\}}
$$

**Step 4 — IRF interpretation:** The $(j,k)$-th element of $\boldsymbol{\Psi}_s$ measures the response of $Z_{jt}$ (component $j$) at horizon $s$ to a unit shock in $\varepsilon_{kt}$ (component $k$) at time $t$, holding all else constant. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

Suppose $K=2$ and we have a VAR$(1)$ model:

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t
$$

**Task:** Test for stationarity.

**Step 1 — Compute the eigenvalues of $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_1$:** The characteristic equation is:

$$
\det(\boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 - \lambda \mathbf{I}_2) = (0.4 - \lambda)(0.6 - \lambda) - (0.1)(0.2) = 0
$$

Expanding:

$$
0.24 - 0.4\lambda - 0.6\lambda + \lambda^2 - 0.02 = \lambda^2 - \lambda + 0.22 = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the quadratic:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 4(0.22)}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{0.12}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm 0.3464}{2}
$$

So $\lambda_1 \approx 0.6732$ and $\lambda_2 \approx 0.3268$.

**Step 3 — Check magnitudes:** $\vert \lambda_1 \vert = 0.6732 < 1$ and $\vert \lambda_2 \vert = 0.3268 < 1$.

**Conclusion:** Both eigenvalues lie inside the unit circle, so the VAR$(1)$ process is **stationary**. $\checkmark$

---

## 3.6 Forecast-Mean-Square-Error (FMSE) Matrix

For a stationary VAR$(1)$ with $\mathbf{Z}_t = \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \mathbf{Z}_{t-1} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t$, the **1-step-ahead** forecast error is $\mathbf{e}_t(1) = \mathbf{Z}_t - \widehat{\mathbf{Z}}_{t \vert t-1} = \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t$, so the FMSE matrix is:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}(1) = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{e}_t(1) \mathbf{e}_t(1)'] = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\varepsilon}
$$

The **2-step-ahead** forecast error:

$$
\mathbf{e}_t(2) = \mathbf{Z}_{t+2} - \widehat{\mathbf{Z}}_{t+2 \vert t} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{t+1} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{t+2}
$$

Its covariance:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}(2) = \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\varepsilon} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1' + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\varepsilon}
$$

The general recursion:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}(h) = \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1 \boldsymbol{\Sigma}(h-1) \boldsymbol{\Phi}_1' + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\varepsilon}, \quad h = 1, 2, \ldots
$$

and as $h \to \infty$, $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}(h) \to \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_0$ (the unconditional covariance). $\blacksquare$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Vector ARMA Modelling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Collect K-variate time series Yt = Y1t, Y2t, ..., YKt"] --> B["Plot & inspect each series"]
    B --> C["Test stationarity ADF and KPSS per series"]
    C --> D{"Stationary?"}
    D -- "No" --> E["Apply differencing or log transform"]
    E --> C
    D -- "Yes" --> F["Identify orders p and q via AIC, BIC, HQ"]
    F --> G["Fit VARMA p q or VAR p model"]
    G --> H["Estimate coefficients by OLS or MLE"]
    H --> I["Diagnostic check residuals: portmanteau, normality"]
    I --> J{"Residuals white noise?"}
    J -- "No" --> F
    J -- "Yes" --> K["Forecasting h-step ahead"]
    K --> L["Structural analysis: IRF, FEVD, Granger causality"]
    L --> M["Interpretation and reporting"]
```

        Phi1["Phi-1 matrix KxK"]

    end

    subgraph ThetaLayer["MA Coefficient Matrices"]
        Theta1["Theta-1 matrix KxK"]
        ThetaQ["Theta-q matrix KxK"]
    end

    subgraph Noise["Innovation Process"]

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Causal / Dynamic Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1["Y1t-1"] -->|"Phi-11, Phi-12, ..."| Z["Yt K-vector output"]
    X2["Y2t-1"] --> Z
    X3["YKt-1"] --> Z
    E["Epsilont innovation"] --> Z
    H1["Y1t-2"] -->|"Phi-21 entries"| Z
    H2["Y2t-2"] --> Z
    M1["Epsilont-1"] -->|"Theta-1 entries"| Z
    M2["Epsilont-q"] -->|"Theta-q entries"| Z
```

> [!TIP]
> The block diagram highlights the **fundamental multivariate feature**: every lagged value of *every* component can directly affect *every* component of $\mathbf{Y}_t$. This is impossible in a univariate framework and is the **principal strength of vector models**.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Impulse Response Function Trace

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0["Shock at t = 0"] --> S1["t = 1: Psi-1 response"]
    S1 --> S2["t = 2: Psi-2 response"]
    S2 --> S3["t = 3: Psi-3 response"]
    S3 --> S4["t = h: Psi-h response"]
    S4 --> S5["t equals infinity: 0 if stationary"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Stationarity of the VARMA guarantees that the IRF sequence $\{\boldsymbol{\Psi}_s\}$ **decays geometrically to zero** as $s \to \infty$. This is the time-domain expression of the same stationarity condition we derived in §3.2.
