> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Regression with ARIMA Errors Using R Packages (fable)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Regression with ARIMA Errors** (also called **Dynamic Regression** or **ARIMAX**) is a hybrid time series model that combines a linear regression component with an ARIMA process for the residuals. Formally, it is defined as:
> $$y_t = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_{i,t} + \eta_t, \quad \eta_t \sim \text{ARIMA}(p,d,q)$$
> where $y_t$ is the response series, $x_{i,t}$ are the **exogenous (explanatory) predictor variables**, $\beta_i$ are the regression coefficients, and $\eta_t$ is an ARIMA error process that captures the remaining temporal dependence.

The key insight is that we **do not assume the errors are independent** (as in OLS regression) — instead, we explicitly model the autocorrelation in the residuals using an **AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA)** structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The `fable` package is part of the **tidyverts** ecosystem in R, providing a modern, tidy framework for time series forecasting. It supersedes the older `forecast` package's `auto.arima()` workflow but follows the same underlying statistical principles popularized by Hyndman & Athanasopoulos (2018).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are a **weather forecaster** trying to predict tomorrow's ice cream sales ($y_t$). You know that **temperature** ($x_{1,t}$) is a strong driver — people buy more ice cream when it's hot. A simple **linear regression** would give you a basic prediction: *sales = α + β·temperature*.

But here's the catch: **yesterday's leftover stock** and **weekend patterns** also affect today's sales. After fitting the regression on temperature, the *errors* (residuals) will not be random — they will be **autocorrelated** because you forgot to account for temporal patterns.

**The fix?** Model the residuals themselves as an **ARIMA process**. This is exactly what *Regression with ARIMA Errors* does:

| Step | What Happens | Real-World Equivalent |
|------|--------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Fit a linear regression between $y_t$ and $x_t$ | Account for temperature effect on sales |
| 2 | Examine the residuals $\hat{\eta}_t = y_t - \hat{\beta}'x_t$ | Check what's left unexplained |
| 3 | Model $\hat{\eta}_t$ as ARIMA($p,d,q$) | Capture leftover temporal patterns (e.g., weekly cycles) |
| 4 | Combine both for forecasts | Predict future sales using temperature + temporal patterns |

> [!TIP]
> **The Three-Layer Mental Model:**
> - **Layer 1 (Deterministic):** The linear regression on exogenous variables.
> - **Layer 2 (Stochastic):** The ARIMA structure of the unexplained residuals.
> - **Layer 3 (Innovation):** White noise shocks $\varepsilon_t \sim N(0,\sigma^2)$.

## 1.3 When to Use Regression with ARIMA Errors

Use this model when **ALL** of the following hold:

> [!WARNING]
> - You have one or more **explanatory variables** believed to influence the response.
> - The **residuals of a plain OLS regression are autocorrelated** (significant ACF/PACF bars).
> - The **predictors are independent of the error process** (no feedback loop from $y_t$ to $x_t$).
> - Your goal is **forecasting or causal interpretation** of the relationship between $y_t$ and $x_t$.

**Common Engineering Applications:**

- 📈 **Sales Forecasting** with marketing spend and price as predictors.
- 🌦️ **Climate Modeling** where temperature depends on CO₂ levels and ocean indices.
- ⚡ **Energy Load Forecasting** using weather, day-of-week, and economic activity.
- 🏭 **Industrial Process Control** where output depends on controllable inputs and unmeasured disturbances.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Model — Expanded Form

The most general form of the dynamic regression model is:

$$y_t = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_{i,t} + \eta_t$$

where the ARIMA error process is expressed via the **backshift operator** $B$ (where $B y_t = y_{t-1}$):

$$\phi(B)(1-B)^d \eta_t = \theta(B)\varepsilon_t$$

with:

$$\phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \dots - \phi_p B^p$$

$$\theta(B) = 1 + \theta_1 B + \theta_2 B^2 + \dots + \theta_q B^q$$

Substituting $\eta_t = y_t - \beta'x_t$ into the ARIMA structure:

$$\phi(B)(1-B)^d \left(y_t - \beta_0 - \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_{i,t}\right) = \theta(B)\varepsilon_t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Modeling Rule:** If differencing is required ($d \geq 1$), the **same differencing operator must be applied to BOTH** the response $y_t$ AND the predictors $x_{i,t}$. This ensures the model remains coherent. For example, with $d=1$:
> $$y'_t = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x'_{i,t} + \eta'_t, \quad \text{where } z'_t = (1-B)z_t = z_t - z_{t-1}$$

## 2.2 Why the Model is Powerful — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Step 1: Detect Autocorrelation in Residuals**
   - Fit a basic OLS regression. Compute residuals $\hat{e}_t = y_t - \hat{\beta}'x_t$.
   - Plot ACF of $\hat{e}_t$. If bars exceed the **95% confidence bounds** (approx. $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{T}$), autocorrelation is present.

2. **Step 2: Pre-whitening the Predictors**
   - Identify an ARIMA model for each $x_{i,t}$ independently.
   - Filter both $x_{i,t}$ and $y_t$ through this model to remove the predictor's own autocorrelation.
   - The resulting filtered series should be approximately white noise.

3. **Step 3: Cross-Correlation Function (CCF) Analysis**
   - Compute the CCF between the pre-whitened $x_{i,t}$ and $y_t$.
   - Significant CCF lags indicate the **lag structure** of the predictor (e.g., marketing spend today may affect sales 2 weeks later).
   - Include these lags as additional predictors: $x_{i,t}, x_{i,t-1}, x_{i,t-2}, \dots$

4. **Step 4: Iterate Model Specification**
   - Specify candidate models: ARIMAX$(p,d,q)$ with various $p$ and $q$ orders.
   - Compare using **information criteria** (AIC, AICc, BIC).

5. **Step 5: Residual Diagnostics**
   - Verify residuals resemble white noise using the **Ljung-Box test** ($H_0$: no autocorrelation).
   - Check normality using the **Shapiro-Wilk test** or Q-Q plots.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula you need for KTU examination problems on this topic. **All absolute-value bars and conditionals use LaTeX commands $\vert$ / $\mid$ to preserve table syntax.**

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Purpose** | **Units / Notes** |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Dynamic Regression Model** | $y_t = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_{i,t} + \eta_t$ | Core model definition | $y_t, x_t$ in original units |
| **ARIMA Error Process** | $\phi(B)(1-B)^d \eta_t = \theta(B)\varepsilon_t$ | Captures residual autocorrelation | $B$ is the backshift operator |
| **AR Coefficients** | $\phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \dots - \phi_p B^p$ | Autoregressive part of order $p$ | $p \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| **MA Coefficients** | $\theta(B) = 1 + \theta_1 B + \theta_2 B^2 + \dots + \theta_q B^q$ | Moving average part of order $q$ | $q \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| **Differencing Operator** | $(1-B)^d z_t$ | Removes trend/seasonality | Apply to BOTH $y$ and $x$ when $d \geq 1$ |
| **h-step Forecast** | $\hat{y}_{T+h} = \beta'x_{T+h} + \hat{\eta}_{T+h}$ | Point forecast at horizon $h$ | Conditional on future $x$ values |
| **Forecast Variance** | $\text{Var}(\hat{y}_{T+h}) = \sigma^2 \sum_{j=1}^{h}\psi_j^2$ | Uncertainty quantification | $\psi_j$ = MA$(\infty)$ coefficients |
| **95% Prediction Interval** | $\hat{y}_{T+h} \pm 1.96 \sqrt{\text{Var}(\hat{y}_{T+h})}$ | Bounds for new observation | Assumes normality of errors |
| **AIC** | $\text{AIC} = -2\ln(L) + 2k$ | Model selection criterion | $k$ = number of parameters |
| **AICc (corrected)** | $\text{AICc} = \text{AIC} + \frac{2k(k+1)}{T-k-1}$ | Better for small samples | $T$ = sample size |
| **BIC** | $\text{BIC} = -2\ln(L) + k \ln(T)$ | Stricter penalty on parameters | Favors parsimony |
| **Ljung-Box Statistic** | $Q^* = T(T+2)\sum_{k=1}^{K}\frac{r_k^2}{T-k}$ | Test for residual autocorrelation | $H_0$: residuals are white noise |
| **CCF at lag $\ell$** | $\rho_{xy}(\ell) = \frac{\text{Cov}(x_t, y_{t+\ell})}{\sigma_x \sigma_y}$ | Detect lagged predictor effects | Range: $-1 \leq \rho \leq 1$ |

## 2.4 Why Engineers Use This in the Real World

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> - **Demand Forecasting** in supply chain management — predictor variables include price, promotions, and competitor actions.
> - **Econometric Modeling** — central banks forecast GDP using interest rates, unemployment, and consumer confidence.
> - **Signal Processing** — separating known deterministic signals (regression part) from stochastic noise (ARIMA part).
> - **Predictive Maintenance** — equipment failure predicted from sensor readings and operational hours.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From the General Form to the Forecast Equation

We start with the dynamic regression model:

$$y_t = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_{i,t} + \eta_t$$

where $\eta_t$ follows an **ARIMA($p,d,q$)** process. Suppose for illustration that $\eta_t$ is an **ARIMA(1,0,1)** — i.e., $\eta_t$ is **ARMA(1,1)**:

$$\eta_t = \phi_1 \eta_{t-1} + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim N(0,\sigma^2)$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $\eta_t = y_t - \beta_0 - \sum \beta_i x_{i,t}$ into the ARMA equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
y_t - \beta_0 - \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_{i,t} &= \phi_1 \left(y_{t-1} - \beta_0 - \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_{i,t-1}\right) + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate $y_t$ on the left-hand side:

$$\begin{aligned}
y_t &= \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_{i,t} + \phi_1 y_{t-1} - \phi_1\beta_0 - \phi_1 \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_{i,t-1} + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Group the constant terms and simplify:

$$\begin{aligned}
y_t &= \beta_0(1 - \phi_1) + \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i \left(x_{i,t} - \phi_1 x_{i,t-1}\right) + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** This is the **full transfer-function form** of the model. We can verify stationarity by checking the characteristic equation:

$$1 - \phi_1 z = 0 \implies z = \frac{1}{\phi_1}$$

For stationarity, we need $\vert z \vert > 1$, i.e., $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$. This is the standard AR(1) stationarity condition.

## 3.2 Derivation: The h-step Ahead Forecast

The forecast for $h$ steps ahead is constructed recursively. For $h=1$ (one-step-ahead):

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{T+1 \mid T} &= \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_{i,T+1} + \hat{\eta}_{T+1 \mid T}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\hat{\eta}_{T+1 \mid T}$ comes from the ARMA(1,1) recursion:

$$\hat{\eta}_{T+1 \mid T} = \phi_1 \hat{\eta}_{T} + \theta_1 \varepsilon_T + 0$$

because $\varepsilon_{T+1}$ has zero mean. The observed residual at time $T$ is $\hat{\varepsilon}_T = \hat{\eta}_T - \phi_1 \hat{\eta}_{T-1} - \theta_1 \hat{\varepsilon}_{T-1}$, so:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{\eta}_{T+1 \mid T} &= \phi_1 \hat{\eta}_{T} + \theta_1 \hat{\varepsilon}_T
\end{aligned}$$

For $h=2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{T+2 \mid T} &= \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_{i,T+2} + \hat{\eta}_{T+2 \mid T} \\
\hat{\eta}_{T+2 \mid T} &= \phi_1 \hat{\eta}_{T+1 \mid T} + 0
\end{aligned}$$

**General recursive forecast formula** (for $h \geq 2$ in AR(1) errors):

$$\hat{\eta}_{T+h \mid T} = \phi_1^h \hat{\eta}_T$$

**Combined forecast:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{T+h \mid T} &= \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_{i,T+h} + \phi_1^h \hat{\eta}_T
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** As $h$ increases, the influence of the last observed residual decays geometrically (rate $\phi_1^h$). The forecast is dominated by the regression component $\beta'x_{T+h}$ for large $h$, assuming the predictors are known (or forecasted independently).

## 3.3 Derivation: Forecast Variance

The forecast error is:

$$e_{T+h} = y_{T+h} - \hat{y}_{T+h \mid T} = \eta_{T+h} - \hat{\eta}_{T+h \mid T} = \sum_{j=0}^{h-1}\psi_j \varepsilon_{T+h-j}$$

where $\psi_j$ are the **MA($\infty$) coefficients** of the ARMA(1,1) representation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\psi_0 &= 1 \\
\psi_1 &= \phi_1 + \theta_1 \\
\psi_j &= \phi_1 \psi_{j-1} \quad \text{for } j \geq 2
\end{aligned}$$

The forecast variance is therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(e_{T+h}) &= \sigma^2 \sum_{j=0}^{h-1}\psi_j^2 = \sigma^2 \left(1 + \psi_1^2 + \psi_2^2 + \dots + \psi_{h-1}^2\right)
\end{aligned}$$

For large $h$, this converges to the **unconditional variance** of the ARMA process:

$$\text{Var}(\eta_t) = \sigma^2 \frac{1 + \theta_1^2 + 2\phi_1\theta_1}{1 - \phi_1^2}$$

This is the long-run forecast variance — the limit of the prediction interval width as $h \to \infty$.

## 3.4 The fable Package — Mathematical Implementation

The `fable` package in R uses **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** to fit the model. Given the model:

$$y_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \beta, \phi, \theta, \sigma^2 \sim N\left(\beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_{i,t} + \hat{\eta}_t, \sigma^2\right)$$

the log-likelihood is:

$$\ell(\beta, \phi, \theta, \sigma^2) = -\frac{T}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{T}{2}\ln(\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\hat{\varepsilon}_t^2$$

where $\hat{\varepsilon}_t$ are the **innovation residuals** (one-step-ahead prediction errors). The `fable` package optimizes this via the **Kalman filter** for efficiency, then selects the best $(p,d,q)$ order by minimizing the **AICc**.

**Standard R workflow with fable:**

```r
library(fable)
library(tsibble)
library(dplyr)

# Convert data to tsibble (tidy time series)
ts_data <- as_tsibble(my_data, index = date)

# Fit regression with ARIMA errors (auto-select order)
fit <- ts_data %>%
  model(arimax_fit = ARIMA(sales ~ marketing_spend + price))

# View model summary
report(fit)

# Residual diagnostics
fit %>% gg_tsresiduals()

# Generate forecasts for next 12 periods
fcst <- fit %>% forecast(h = 12)

# Plot the forecast
fcst %>% autoplot(ts_data)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key fable Functions for KTU Exams:**
> - `ARIMA(formula)` — main model fitter; formula is `y ~ x1 + x2`.
> - `pdq(p, d, q)` — explicitly specify non-seasonal ARIMA orders.
> - `PDQ(P, D, Q)` — explicitly specify seasonal ARIMA orders.
> - `model(.data, ...)` — fit one or more models to a tsibble.
> - `forecast(model, h = n)` — generate forecasts.
> - `accuracy(model)` — compute in-sample fit metrics (RMSE, MAE, MAPE).
> - `report(model)` — print model coefficients, information criteria, and residual tests.
> - `gg_tsresiduals(model)` — visualize residual ACF, histogram, and time plot.

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow: Building a Dynamic Regression Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Collect Time Series Data y_t and Predictors x_t"] --> B["Exploratory Data Analysis: Plot y_t vs x_t"]
    B --> C["Fit Plain OLS Regression: y_t = beta0 + beta prime x_t + e_t"]
    C --> D{"Are Residuals Autocorrelated? Check ACF"}
    D -->|No| E["Stop: Plain OLS is Sufficient"]
    D -->|Yes| F["Pre-whiten Predictors: Fit ARIMA to x_t"]
    F --> G["Apply Same ARIMA Filter to y_t"]
    G --> H["Compute CCF between Pre-whitened x and y"]
    H --> I["Identify Significant Lags of x at lag ell"]
    I --> J["Specify Candidate Dynamic Regression Models"]
    J --> K["Fit Each Model using fable ARIMA y ~ x1 plus x2"]
    K --> L["Compare Models via AICc and BIC"]
    L --> M{"Does Best Model Pass Residual Diagnostics?"}
    M -->|No| N["Re-specify: Adjust p, d, q or Lag Structure"]
    N --> J
    M -->|Yes| O["Produce h-step Ahead Forecasts with Prediction Intervals"]
    O --> P["Validate Forecasts on Hold-out Test Set"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"]
        X1["Predictor x_1t"]
        X2["Predictor x_2t"]
        XK["Predictor x_kt"]
    end

    subgraph REGLIN["Regression Block"]
        BETA["Coefficient Vector beta_0, beta_1, ..., beta_k"]
        SUM["Weighted Sum: beta prime x_t"]
    end

    subgraph ERRPRO["ARIMA Error Block"]
        AR["AR Component phi B times eta_t"]
        DIFF["Differencing one minus B to d"]
        MA["MA Component theta B times eps_t"]
        NOISE["White Noise eps_t ~ N zero sigma squared"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["Output"]
        YT["Response y_t = Regression + eta_t"]
        FCST["Forecasts hat y_t plus h"]
    end

    X1 --> SUM
    X2 --> SUM
    XK --> SUM
    BETA --> SUM
    SUM --> YT
    NOISE --> MA
    MA --> DIFF
    DIFF --> AR
    AR --> YT
    YT --> FCST
```

## 4.3 Model Hierarchy Diagram (where Regression with ARIMA Errors Fits)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TS["Time Series Models"] --> UNIV["Univariate Models"]
    TS --> MULTIV["Multivariate Models"]

    UNIV --> AR["ARIMA p d q"]
    UNIV --> ETS["Exponential Smoothing ETS"]
    UNIV --> NNET["Neural Network NNETAR"]

    MULTIV --> REG["Regression with ARIMA Errors"]
    MULTIV --> VAR["Vector Autoregression VAR"]
    MULTIV --> VECM["Vector Error Correction VECM"]

    REG --> DR["Dynamic Regression with Known Lags"]
    REG --> TRF["Transfer Function Models"]
    REG --> ARX["ARX Models with Exogenous Inputs"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:**
> - The **input layer** passes predictors to the regression block, which computes the deterministic part.
> - The **ARIMA error block** adds stochastic temporal structure via the cascade AR → Differencing → MA → White Noise.
> - The **output** combines both blocks to produce $y_t$ and future forecasts $\hat{y}_{t+h}$.

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 🔹 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Regression with ARIMA Errors. How does it differ from Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression?**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Regression with ARIMA Errors is a time series model that combines a linear regression component with an ARIMA process for the residuals, defined as:

$$y_t = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_{i,t} + \eta_t, \quad \eta_t \sim \text{ARIMA}(p,d,q)$$

**Key Differences from OLS:**

| Aspect | OLS Regression | Regression with ARIMA Errors |
|--------|----------------|------------------------------|
| Error Assumption | Errors are independent | Errors are autocorrelated (ARIMA) |
| Use Case | Cross-sectional / static data | Time-ordered data with temporal dependence |
| Parameter Estimation | Minimizes SSE | Maximizes log-likelihood (MLE) |
| Forecasts | Ignores temporal structure | Explicitly models time dynamics |
| Differencing | Not typically applied | Applied to both $y_t$ and $x_t$ if $d \geq 1$ |

**Conclusion:** Regression with ARIMA Errors extends OLS by explicitly modeling residual autocorrelation, making it suitable for time series forecasting where past values influence present observations. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. List any three advantages of using the `fable` package in R for fitting time series models. Mention the main function used to fit a dynamic regression model.**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Three Advantages of `fable`:**
1. **Tidy Data Compatibility:** Works seamlessly with `tsibble` objects and follows tidyverse principles (`%>%` piping, `mutate`, `filter`).
2. **Automatic Model Selection:** The `ARIMA()` function automatically chooses the best $(p,d,q)$ order using AICc minimization, saving manual effort.
3. **Unified Workflow:** The same syntax (`model()`, `forecast()`, `accuracy()`) works for ARIMA, ETS, NNETAR, and other models, enabling easy comparison.

**Main Function:** `ARIMA(y ~ x1 + x2)` — fits a regression with ARIMA errors, where $y$ is the response and $x_1, x_2$ are predictors. **(3 marks)**

## 🔹 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation**, Part B questions of 14 marks offer an **internal choice**. You must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

### **❓ Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the general form of the Regression with ARIMA Errors model. Starting from the ARIMA(1,1,1) error structure, derive the explicit one-step-ahead forecast equation. State the stationarity condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A retail company records monthly sales ($y_t$) and advertising spend ($x_t$) for 5 years ($T = 60$ months). After fitting OLS, the residual ACF shows significant autocorrelation at lags 1 and 2. Specify an appropriate dynamic regression model, fit it using `fable`, and explain the diagnostic checks you would perform. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: General Model Form (2 marks)**

The regression with ARIMA errors is:

$$y_t = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_t + \eta_t, \quad \eta_t \sim \text{ARIMA}(1,1,1)$$

The ARIMA(1,1,1) error process is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi(B)(1-B)\eta_t = \theta(B)\varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}$$

Expanding:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1 - \phi_1 B)(1-B)\eta_t = (1 + \theta_1 B)\varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Differencing and Substitution (2 marks)**

Apply $(1-B)$ to both $y_t$ and $x_t$. Let $y'_t = y_t - y_{t-1}$ and $x'_t = x_t - x_{t-1}$. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
y'_t &= \beta_0 + \beta_1 x'_t + \eta'_t \\
(1 - \phi_1 B)\eta'_t &= (1 + \theta_1 B)\varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Expand the ARMA part (1 mark)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta'_t - \phi_1 \eta'_{t-1} &= \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} \\
\eta'_t &= \phi_1 \eta'_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: One-step-ahead forecast (1 mark)**

For $h = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{T+1} &= y_T + \beta_0 + \beta_1(x_{T+1} - x_T) + \phi_1(y_T - \hat{\beta}_0 - \beta_1 x_T - \hat{\eta}_T) + \theta_1 \hat{\varepsilon}_T
\end{aligned}$$

where $\hat{\varepsilon}_T$ is the observed innovation residual at time $T$.

**Step 5: Stationarity Condition (1 mark)**

The characteristic equation is $1 - \phi_1 z = 0 \implies z = 1/\phi_1$. For stationarity, $\vert z \vert > 1$, i.e., $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$.

---

**Part (b) — Application (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Model Specification (2 marks)**

Given autocorrelation at lags 1 and 2, we try **ARIMA(2,0,0)** for the errors (since ACF cuts off after lag 2). The dynamic regression model is:

$$y_t = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_t + \eta_t, \quad \eta_t = \phi_1 \eta_{t-1} + \phi_2 \eta_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 2: R Code with fable (2 marks)**

```r
library(fable)
library(tsibble)

# Create tsibble (assume monthly data from Jan 2019 to Dec 2023)
sales_ts <- tsibble(
  month = yearmonth(months),
  sales = sales,
  adspend = adspend,
  index = month
)

# Fit dynamic regression
fit <- sales_ts %>%
  model(ARIMA(sales ~ adspend))

# View summary
report(fit)
```

**Step 3: Diagnostic Checks (3 marks)**

1. **Residual ACF Plot:** Use `fit %>% gg_tsresiduals()`. All ACF bars should lie within the 95% bounds.
2. **Ljung-Box Test:** The `report()` output includes a `p-value` for the Ljung-Box statistic. If $p > 0.05$, residuals are white noise.
3. **Normality Check:** Histogram and Q-Q plot of residuals should show approximate normality. Shapiro-Wilk test $p > 0.05$ confirms normality.
4. **Coefficient Significance:** Check that $\beta_1$ (advertising effect) is statistically significant ($p < 0.05$).
5. **Out-of-sample Validation:** Split data — train on 48 months, test on 12 months. Compute RMSE and MAPE on the hold-out set.

---

### **❓ Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the role of the Cross-Correlation Function (CCF) in dynamic regression. Describe the pre-whitening procedure with mathematical justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the `fable` package, write R code to: (i) load the `aus_airpassengers` dataset, (ii) convert it to a tsibble, (iii) fit two competing models — `ARIMA(passengers)` and `ARIMA(passengers ~ trend())` (regression with linear trend and ARIMA errors), (iv) compare them using `accuracy()` and `glance()`. Which model is preferred and why? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — CCF and Pre-whitening (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Role of CCF (2 marks)**

The Cross-Correlation Function measures the linear association between $x_t$ and $y_{t+\ell}$ at lag $\ell$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_{xy}(\ell) = \frac{\text{Cov}(x_t, y_{t+\ell})}{\sigma_x \sigma_y}
\end{aligned}$$

In dynamic regression, CCF helps identify the **lag structure** of the predictor — for example, whether advertising at time $t$ affects sales at time $t$, $t+1$, $t+2$, etc. Significant CCF bars at lag $\ell$ suggest that $x_{t-\ell}$ should be included as a predictor.

**Step 2: Why Pre-whiten? (2 marks)**

If $x_t$ itself is autocorrelated, the raw CCF between $x_t$ and $y_t$ is **biased and difficult to interpret**. Pre-whitening removes the autocorrelation from $x_t$, producing an approximately white-noise filtered series $\alpha_t$ such that:

$$\begin{aligned}
\psi(B) x_t = \alpha_t, \quad \alpha_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma_\alpha^2)
\end{aligned}$$

Applying the same filter to $y_t$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\psi(B) y_t = \beta_t
\end{aligned}$$

Now the CCF between $\alpha_t$ and $\beta_t$ reliably shows the true lag relationship.

**Step 3: Mathematical Justification (2 marks)**

If $x_t$ is filtered by $\psi(B)$ to produce white noise, and the true relationship is $y_t = f(x_t, x_{t-1}, \dots) + \eta_t$, then $\psi(B) y_t = f(\alpha_t, \alpha_{t-1}, \dots) + \psi(B)\eta_t$. The CCF between $\alpha_t$ and the filtered $y_t$ identifies the lags of $x$ that affect $y$ without contamination from $x$'s own autocorrelation.

**Step 4: Practical Application (1 mark)**

In `fable`, we use `CCF(x, y)` to compute the cross-correlation after fitting an ARIMA to $x$ and applying it as a filter to $y$. Significant lags are then added to the model formula: `ARIMA(y ~ x + lag(x, -2))`.

---

**Part (b) — fable R Code (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Load and Convert (2 marks)**

```r
library(fable)
library(tsibble)
library(fabletools)
library(dplyr)

data("aus_airpassengers", package = "fpp3")

air_ts <- aus_airpassengers %>% as_tsibble(index = Year)
```

**Step 2: Fit Two Competing Models (2 marks)**

```r
fit_compare <- air_ts %>%
  model(
    model_arima = ARIMA(Passengers),
    model_with_trend = ARIMA(Passengers ~ trend())
  )
```

**Step 3: Compare using glance() and accuracy() (2 marks)**

```r
# Information criteria
fit_compare %>% glance()

# In-sample accuracy
fit_compare %>% accuracy()
```

**Step 4: Interpretation (1 mark)**

The model with lower **AICc** is preferred. Typically, `ARIMA(Passengers ~ trend())` includes a deterministic linear trend, which may produce a different $(p,d,q)$ order than the pure ARIMA model. Compare RMSE and MAPE — the model with lower values is preferred for forecasting.

**Sample Conclusion:**
If `model_with_trend` has lower AICc, RMSE, and MAPE, it is preferred because the linear trend captures the upward movement in air passengers, leaving the ARIMA component to model the remaining short-term fluctuations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> **Pitfall 1: Forgetting to difference BOTH sides.** When $d \geq 1$, students often difference only $y_t$ but not $x_t$, leading to an **inconsistent model**. Always apply $(1-B)^d$ to both response and predictors.
>
> **Pitfall 2: Confusing ACF with PACF interpretation.** For AR($p$) errors, the **PACF** cuts off after lag $p$, not the ACF. Reading off the wrong plot leads to wrong model orders.
>
> **Pitfall 3: Omitting residual diagnostics.** KTU examiners specifically look for **Ljung-Box test results** in the model summary. Stating "residuals look fine" without quoting a $p$-value will cost you **2 marks minimum**.
>
> **Pitfall 4: Misinterpreting the fable output.** The `pdq` and `PDQ` columns show the chosen $(p,d,q)$ and seasonal $(P,D,Q)$ orders. Students often confuse seasonal and non-seasonal components.
>
> **Pitfall 5: Ignoring the constant in forecasts.** The forecast equation is $\hat{y}_{T+h} = \beta_0 + \beta'x_{T+h} + \hat{\eta}_{T+h}$, not just $\beta'x_{T+h}$. Omitting $\beta_0$ or the ARIMA error forecast loses marks.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Rapid Fire):**

- ✅ **Model form:** $y_t = \beta'x_t + \eta_t$ with $\eta_t \sim \text{ARIMA}(p,d,q)$.
- ✅ **Dynamic Regression ≡ ARIMAX ≡ Transfer Function Model** (all names refer to the same thing).
- ✅ **Differencing rule:** Apply $(1-B)^d$ to **BOTH** $y_t$ and $x_t$ when $d \geq 1$.
- ✅ **Stationarity condition:** Roots of $\phi(B) = 0$ must lie **outside the unit circle** ($\vert z \vert > 1$).
- ✅ **Invertibility condition:** Roots of $\theta(B) = 0$ must lie **outside the unit circle**.
- ✅ **Pre-whitening** removes predictor autocorrelation before CCF analysis.
- ✅ **CCF identifies lag structure** of predictors in the model.
- ✅ **Ljung-Box test** checks if residuals are white noise ($H_0$: no autocorrelation).
- ✅ **AICc is preferred over AIC** for small samples (it includes a correction term $\frac{2k(k+1)}{T-k-1}$).
- ✅ **`fable` key function:** `ARIMA(y ~ x1 + x2)` fits the model with auto-selected orders.
- ✅ **fable default selection criterion:** AICc minimization.
- ✅ **Forecast equation:** $\hat{y}_{T+h} = \beta'x_{T+h} + \hat{\eta}_{T+h}$, where $\hat{\eta}_{T+h}$ comes from the ARIMA recursion.
- ✅ **95% Prediction Interval:** $\hat{y}_{T+h} \pm 1.96 \sqrt{\sigma^2 \sum_{j=0}^{h-1}\psi_j^2}$.
- ✅ **R packages in tidyverts ecosystem:** `tsibble`, `fable`, `fabletools`, `feasts`, `fpp3`.
- ✅ **Residual diagnostics in fable:** `fit %>% gg_tsresiduals()` produces ACF, histogram, and time plot in one call.
- ✅ **Common alternative names:** ARDL (Autoregressive Distributed Lag), ARX (Autoregressive with eXogenous inputs).
- ✅ **When NOT to use:** If predictors are endogenous (caused by $y_t$), use VAR instead. If predictors are not available for the forecast horizon, use pure ARIMA.
- ✅ **Difference from regression with AR(1) errors:** ARIMA errors allow general $(p,d,q)$ structure, not just AR(1).

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Memory Aid:** *"Regression with ARIMA Errors = Linear Regression on the Outside, ARIMA Time Series on the Inside — the best of both worlds for forecasting with explanatory variables."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forecasting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Forecasting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forecasting** is the systematic process of making scientifically grounded predictions about future values of a time series based on its historical observations, underlying stochastic structure, identified patterns, and the application of statistically validated mathematical models.

In the context of the **PECDT861 - Time Series Modeling & Analysis** course, forecasting is treated as the terminal objective of every Box-Jenkins style pipeline: after a series is rendered stationary, an appropriate model (AR, MA, ARMA, ARIMA, SARIMA, or Exponential Smoothing) is fitted, validated via residual diagnostics, and then used to generate **point forecasts**, **interval forecasts**, and **density forecasts** for one or more future time horizons $h$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 4 of PECDT861 emphasizes the transition *from* model identification *to* predictive deployment. The student is expected to master both classical (exponential smoothing family) and modern (ARIMA-based) forecasting paradigms, along with rigorous evaluation metrics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Forecasting as "Driving with a Rear-View Mirror"

Imagine driving a car on a foggy highway. Your windshield is completely opaque, but you have a perfect rear-view mirror showing the last 60 seconds of the road behind you. Forecasting is exactly this:
- The **rear-view mirror** represents the *historical data* $\{y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_T\}$.
- The **fog-covered windshield** represents the *unknown future* $\{y_{T+1}, y_{T+2}, \ldots\}$.
- The **driver's brain** represents the *statistical model* that interprets the trajectory, momentum, and oscillations in past observations to extrapolate forward.

A good forecaster, like a good driver, does not merely copy the last speed — it weighs the recent motion (short-term momentum), the long-term drift (trend), and the recurring dips (seasonality).

### 1.3 Why Forecasting Matters — Engineering Relevance

| Domain | Forecasting Application |
|---|---|
| **Electrical Power Systems** | Load demand forecasting (24-hour, weekly) |
| **Civil Engineering** | Traffic flow, water reservoir inflows |
| **Mechanical / Production** | Predictive maintenance, inventory demand |
| **Computer Science** | Cloud load prediction, anomaly detection |
| **Economics / Finance** | Stock prices, currency exchange, GDP growth |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard benchmark metric convention: lower error $\Rightarrow$ better forecast. The metrics are always computed on a **held-out test set** that was *never* seen during model training.

### 1.4 The Forecasting Horizon

$$\boxed{h \in \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, H\}}$$

- **$h = 1$** → *One-step-ahead* forecast, denoted $\hat{y}_{T+1 \vert T}$.
- **$h > 1$** → *Multi-step-ahead* forecast, $\hat{y}_{T+h \vert T}$.
- The notation $\hat{y}_{T+h \vert T}$ means: the predicted value at time $T+h$, given information available up to time $T$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Forecasting Methods

Forecasting methods are classified into two macro-families:

**A. Qualitative (Judgmental) Methods**
- Used when historical data is scarce or non-existent.
- Examples: Delphi method, market research, expert panels.

**B. Quantitative (Statistical) Methods** — *the focus of PECDT861*
1. **Naive & Drift Methods**
2. **Averaging Methods** (Simple Mean, Simple Moving Average, Weighted Moving Average)
3. **Exponential Smoothing Family** (SES, Holt's Linear, Holt-Winters, Damped Trend)
4. **ARIMA / SARIMA Family**
5. **Regression-based** (with lagged predictors, exogenous regressors — ARIMAX)

### 2.2 Naive Forecasting Methods

> [!NOTE]
> Naive methods are **benchmarks**, not competitors. Any sophisticated model must outperform the naive baseline to justify its complexity.

**(a) Naive Method (Last Observation):**
$$\hat{y}_{T+h \vert T} = y_T$$

**(b) Seasonal Naive Method:**
$$\hat{y}_{T+h \vert T} = y_{T+h-m(k+1)}$$
where $m$ = seasonal period and $k = \lfloor (h-1)/m \rfloor$.

**(c) Drift Method:**
$$\hat{y}_{T+h \vert T} = y_T + h \cdot \left( \frac{y_T - y_1}{T - 1} \right)$$

### 2.3 Moving Average Forecasting

**Simple Moving Average (SMA) of order $k$:**
$$\hat{y}_{T+1} = \frac{y_T + y_{T-1} + \cdots + y_{T-k+1}}{k} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} y_{T-i}$$

**Weighted Moving Average (WMA):**
$$\hat{y}_{T+1} = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} w_i \, y_{T-i} \quad \text{where} \quad \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} w_i = 1$$

### 2.4 Exponential Smoothing Family

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The smoothing parameter $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ controls the weight given to recent versus historical observations. **Higher $\alpha$ $\Rightarrow$ faster adaptation to recent changes** but more noise sensitivity.

**(a) Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES):**
$$\hat{y}_{T+1} = \alpha y_T + (1-\alpha) \hat{y}_T$$

Recursive form with $\ell_T$ as the smoothed level:
$$\ell_T = \alpha y_T + (1-\alpha) \ell_{T-1}, \quad \hat{y}_{T+h} = \ell_T$$

**(b) Holt's Linear Trend Method (Double Smoothing):**
Introduces a trend component $b_T$:
$$\ell_T = \alpha y_T + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{T-1} + b_{T-1})$$

$$b_T = \beta^*(\ell_T - \ell_{T-1}) + (1-\beta^*) b_{T-1}$$

$$h\text{-step forecast: } \hat{y}_{T+h} = \ell_T + h \cdot b_T$$

where $\alpha, \beta^* \in [0, 1]$.

**(c) Holt-Winters' Seasonal Method (Triple Smoothing):**
Adds seasonal component $s_T$ with period $m$:
$$\ell_T = \alpha (y_T - s_{T-m}) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{T-1} + b_{T-1})$$

$$b_T = \beta^*(\ell_T - \ell_{T-1}) + (1-\beta^*) b_{T-1}$$

$$s_T = \gamma (y_T - \ell_{T-1} - b_{T-1}) + (1-\gamma) s_{T-m}$$

**Forecast equation:**
$$\hat{y}_{T+h} = \ell_T + h \cdot b_T + s_{T+h-m \cdot k}$$

where $k = \lfloor (h-1)/m \rfloor + 1$.

**(d) Damped Trend Method** (Gardner-McKenzie):
$$b_T = \beta^*(\ell_T - \ell_{T-1}) + (1-\beta^*) \phi \, b_{T-1}$$

$$\hat{y}_{T+h} = \ell_T + \sum_{i=1}^{h} \phi^i \cdot b_T$$
where $\phi \in [0, 1]$ is the damping parameter; $\phi = 1$ recovers Holt's linear method.

### 2.5 Forecast Accuracy Metrics

Let $y_i$ = actual, $\hat{y}_i$ = forecast, $n$ = number of test points, $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$.

| Metric | Formula | Units | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MAE** | $\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert e_i \vert$ | Same as $y$ | Robust to outliers |
| **MSE** | $\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2$ | Squared units | Penalizes large errors |
| **RMSE** | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2}$ | Same as $y$ | Standard KTU metric |
| **MAPE** | $\frac{100}{n} \sum \left\vert \frac{e_i}{y_i} \right\vert$ | % | Scale-independent |
| **MASE** | $\frac{\text{MAE}}{\frac{1}{T-1}\sum_{t=2}^{T} \vert y_t - y_{t-1} \vert}$ | Unitless | Recommended by Hyndman |

> [!WARNING]
> **MAE $\leq$ RMSE always**, since RMSE squares large errors before averaging. If MAE $\approx$ RMSE, errors are uniform; if RMSE $\gg$ MAE, a few large outliers dominate.

### 2.6 ARIMA-based Forecasting

For an ARIMA$(p, d, q)$ model, the **general forecast** is obtained via the recursive relation:

$$\hat{y}_{T+h} = \mu + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \, y_{T+h-i} - \sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j \, \varepsilon_{T+h-j}$$

where future residuals $\varepsilon_{T+k} = 0$ for $k > 0$ (point forecasts only) and unknown $y_{T+k}$ are replaced by their forecasts $\hat{y}_{T+k}$.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Method} & \textbf{Core Equation} & \textbf{Parameters} \\
\hline
\text{Naive} & \hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T & \text{None} \\
\hline
\text{Seasonal Naive} & \hat{y}_{T+h} = y_{T+h-m(k+1)} & m \\
\hline
\text{SMA}(k) & \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} y_{T-i} & k \\
\hline
\text{SES} & \ell_T = \alpha y_T + (1-\alpha)\ell_{T-1} & \alpha \\
\hline
\text{Holt's Linear} & \ell_T + h \cdot b_T & \alpha, \beta^* \\
\hline
\text{Holt-Winters} & \ell_T + h b_T + s_{T+h-mk} & \alpha, \beta^*, \gamma, m \\
\hline
\text{Damped Trend} & \ell_T + \sum_{i=1}^{h} \phi^i b_T & \alpha, \beta^*, \phi \\
\hline
\text{ARIMA} & \mu + \sum \phi_i y - \sum \theta_j \varepsilon & p, d, q \\
\hline
\end{array}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Simple Exponential Smoothing as a Weighted Average of Past Observations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 frequently asks: *"Show that SES is a weighted average of all past observations."* This is the **classic 7-mark proof** in Part B.

**Statement:** For Simple Exponential Smoothing, the forecast $\hat{y}_{T+1}$ is an exponentially weighted average of all past observations $\{y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_T\}$.

**Proof:**

We begin from the recursive definition:

$$\ell_T = \alpha y_T + (1-\alpha)\ell_{T-1}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $\ell_{T-1}$ recursively:
$$\ell_T = \alpha y_T + (1-\alpha)\left[\alpha y_{T-1} + (1-\alpha)\ell_{T-2}\right]$$
$$= \alpha y_T + \alpha(1-\alpha) y_{T-1} + (1-\alpha)^2 \ell_{T-2}$$

**Step 2:** Continue substitution up to $\ell_0$:
$$\ell_T = \alpha y_T + \alpha(1-\alpha) y_{T-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)^2 y_{T-2} + \cdots + \alpha(1-\alpha)^{T-1} y_1 + (1-\alpha)^T \ell_0$$

**Step 3:** Group the terms:
$$\ell_T = \sum_{i=0}^{T-1} \alpha (1-\alpha)^i \, y_{T-i} + (1-\alpha)^T \ell_0$$

**Step 4:** Verify the weights sum to 1 — using the infinite geometric series (valid for $\vert 1-\alpha \vert < 1$, i.e. $\alpha \in (0, 1]$):

$$\sum_{i=0}^{T-1} \alpha (1-\alpha)^i = \alpha \cdot \frac{1 - (1-\alpha)^T}{1 - (1-\alpha)} = 1 - (1-\alpha)^T$$

Therefore:
$$\ell_T = \sum_{i=0}^{T-1} \alpha (1-\alpha)^i \, y_{T-i} + (1-\alpha)^T \ell_0$$

As $T \to \infty$, the term $(1-\alpha)^T \ell_0 \to 0$, leaving a **convex combination** of all past observations. $\blacksquare$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Each weight $\alpha(1-\alpha)^i$ decays geometrically as we go further into the past, so recent observations dominate the forecast — exactly the desired "memory with decay" property.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: SES Step-by-Step

Given $\alpha = 0.3$, $\ell_0 = y_1 = 10$, observations: $y_1=10, y_2=12, y_3=13, y_4=11, y_5=14$.

Compute $\ell_T$ sequentially using $\ell_T = \alpha y_T + (1-\alpha) \ell_{T-1}$.

**Iteration $T=1$:**
$$\ell_1 = \alpha y_1 + (1-\alpha)\ell_0 = 0.3(10) + 0.7(10) = 10.000$$

**Iteration $T=2$:**
$$\ell_2 = 0.3(12) + 0.7(10.000) = 3.6 + 7.0 = 10.600$$

**Iteration $T=3$:**
$$\ell_3 = 0.3(13) + 0.7(10.600) = 3.9 + 7.420 = 11.320$$

**Iteration $T=4$:**
$$\ell_4 = 0.3(11) + 0.7(11.320) = 3.3 + 7.924 = 11.224$$

**Iteration $T=5$:**
$$\ell_5 = 0.3(14) + 0.7(11.224) = 4.2 + 7.857 = 12.057$$

**Forecast for $T+1 = 6$:** $\hat{y}_6 = \ell_5 = 12.057$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Forecast Error Comparison on Sample Data

| Actual $y_t$ | Naive $\hat{y}_t$ | SES $\hat{y}_t$ ($\alpha=0.3$) | MAE (Naive) | MAE (SES) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 12 | 10 | 10.000 | 2.000 | 2.000 |
| 13 | 12 | 10.600 | 1.000 | 2.400 |
| 11 | 13 | 11.320 | 2.000 | 0.320 |
| 14 | 11 | 11.224 | 3.000 | 2.776 |
| 15 | 14 | 12.057 | 1.000 | 2.943 |
| **Total** | — | — | **9.000** | **10.439** |
| **Mean (MAE)** | — | — | **1.800** | **2.088** |

> [!NOTE]
> In this particular synthetic sample, the Naive method outperforms SES because the series exhibits a strong step-change behavior rather than smooth evolution. **Always compare at least one benchmark before deploying any model.**

### 3.4 Derivation: Mean Absolute Scaled Error (MASE)

The MASE was proposed by Hyndman & Koehler (2006) to overcome MAPE's flaws (division by zero, asymmetry).

**Step 1:** Define the in-sample scale — the mean absolute error of the seasonal naive on the training set:
$$Q = \frac{1}{T-m} \sum_{t=m+1}^{T} \vert y_t - y_{t-m} \vert$$

**Step 2:** For non-seasonal data, the scaling factor is the mean absolute first difference:
$$Q = \frac{1}{T-1} \sum_{t=2}^{T} \vert y_t - y_{t-1} \vert$$

**Step 3:** Define MASE on test set:
$$\text{MASE} = \frac{\frac{1}{h} \sum_{j=1}^{h} \vert y_{T+j} - \hat{y}_{T+j} \vert}{Q}$$

**Interpretation:** $\text{MASE} < 1$ means the model beats the seasonal naive benchmark; $\text{MASE} > 1$ means the model is worse than doing nothing clever.

### 3.5 Worked Example: Multi-Step Forecast via ARIMA(1,1,1)

Consider an ARIMA$(1,1,1)$ model: $\Delta y_t = \phi \Delta y_{t-1} + \theta \varepsilon_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, with $\phi = 0.7$, $\theta = -0.3$, last known $\Delta y_T = 2.5$, $\varepsilon_T = 0.4$.

**$h=1$ forecast:**
$$\widehat{\Delta y}_{T+1} = \phi \Delta y_T + \theta \varepsilon_T = 0.7(2.5) + (-0.3)(0.4) = 1.75 - 0.12 = 1.63$$
$$\hat{y}_{T+1} = y_T + \widehat{\Delta y}_{T+1} = y_T + 1.63$$

**$h=2$ forecast:** Future residual $= 0$ in expectation, and $\Delta y_{T+1}$ is unknown, so we use its forecast:
$$\widehat{\Delta y}_{T+2} = \phi \widehat{\Delta y}_{T+1} + \theta \cdot 0 = 0.7(1.63) = 1.141$$
$$\hat{y}_{T+2} = y_T + 1.63 + 1.141 = y_T + 2.771$$

**$h=3$ forecast:** Mean-reverting to $\phi/(1-\phi) \cdot \mu$:
$$\widehat{\Delta y}_{T+3} = 0.7(1.141) = 0.799$$
$$\hat{y}_{T+3} = y_T + 1.63 + 1.141 + 0.799 = y_T + 3.570$$

This recursive pattern — and the fact that forecast variance widens with $h$ — is the cornerstone of the **fan chart** visualization.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Complete Forecasting Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Time Series y1 to yT] --> B[Exploratory Analysis]
    B --> B1[Plot ACF and PACF]
    B --> B2[Stationarity Tests ADF and KPSS]
    B --> B3[Detect Trend and Seasonality]
    B1 --> C{Choose Model Class}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Smoothing Family]
    C --> C2[ARIMA Family]
    C --> C3[Naive Benchmarks]
    C1 --> D[Train Test Split]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Parameter Estimation]
    E --> E1[Optimize alpha beta gamma phi]
    E --> E2[Fit ARIMA via MLE]
    E1 --> F[Residual Diagnostics]
    E2 --> F
    F --> F1[Ljung Box Test]
    F --> F2[Normality of Residuals]
    F1 --> G{Model Adequate?}
    F2 --> G
    G -->|No| H[Re specify Model]
    H --> C
    G -->|Yes| I[Generate Forecasts]
    I --> I1[Point Forecasts]
    I --> I2[Interval Forecasts 80 and 95]
    I --> I3[Density Forecasts]
    I1 --> J[Accuracy Metrics MAE RMSE MAPE MASE]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K{Better than Benchmark?}
    K -->|No| L[Try Advanced Model]
    L --> C
    K -->|Yes| M[Deploy Forecast]
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Smoothing Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Examine the Series] --> B{Trend Present?}
    B -->|No| C{Seasonal Present?}
    B -->|Yes| D{Seasonal Present?}
    C -->|No| E[Use Simple Exponential Smoothing]
    C -->|Yes| F[Use Holt Winters Additive or Multiplicative]
    D -->|No| G{Trend Damping Needed?}
    D -->|Yes| F
    G -->|No| H[Use Holt Linear Method]
    G -->|Yes| I[Use Damped Trend Method]
    E --> J[Validate via Train Test Split]
    F --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Forecast Confidence Interval Fan Chart Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time Index t] --> B[Historical Data y1 to yT]
    B --> C[Forecast Origin at T]
    C --> D[Point Forecast Line]
    C --> E[Lower 80% Bound]
    C --> F[Lower 95% Bound]
    C --> G[Upper 80% Bound]
    C --> H[Upper 95% Bound]
    D --> I[Widening Fan as h Increases]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Diagram: Bias-Variance Tradeoff in Smoothing Parameter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Smoothing Parameter alpha] --> B[alpha close to 0]
    A --> C[alpha close to 1]
    B --> D[High Bias Low Variance]
    C --> E[Low Bias High Variance]
    D --> F[Over Smoothing Misses Trends]
    E --> G[Overreacting to Noise]
    F --> H[Suboptimal Forecast]
    G --> H
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Distinguish between point forecasts and interval forecasts. Why is the latter more informative in engineering decision-making?**

> **Model Answer:**
> A *point forecast* is a single numerical estimate $\hat{y}_{T+h \vert T}$ for a future value, providing only an expected value. An *interval forecast* is a range, typically an 80% or 95% prediction interval $[\hat{y}_{T+h}^{\text{lo}}, \hat{y}_{T+h}^{\text{hi}}]$, within which the actual value is expected to fall with a stated probability. Interval forecasts are more informative because they quantify **uncertainty** — critical in engineering decisions such as reservoir capacity, electrical load provisioning, and inventory safety stock, where under-provisioning leads to failures and over-provisioning leads to economic loss. (3 marks)

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **What is the role of the smoothing constant $\alpha$ in Simple Exponential Smoothing? What happens when $\alpha = 1$?**

> **Model Answer:**
> The smoothing constant $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ controls the trade-off between responsiveness and stability. Higher $\alpha$ assigns greater weight to recent observations, making the forecast reactive to changes. Lower $\alpha$ smooths across more history, yielding a stable but lagging forecast. When $\alpha = 1$, the model collapses to the **Naive method**, since $\hat{y}_{T+1} = 1 \cdot y_T + 0 \cdot \hat{y}_T = y_T$. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the **Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)** method. Derive that SES produces an exponentially weighted average of all past observations.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a time series $y_t = \{10, 12, 13, 11, 14, 16, 15, 17\}$, apply SES with $\alpha = 0.4$ and $\ell_0 = 10$. Compute the one-step-ahead forecasts for $t = 2$ to $8$ and the MAE.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** SES is defined recursively by:

$$\ell_T = \alpha y_T + (1-\alpha) \ell_{T-1}, \qquad \hat{y}_{T+1} = \ell_T$$

Iterating the recursion:

$$\ell_T = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{T-1} (1-\alpha)^i y_{T-i} + (1-\alpha)^T \ell_0$$

The weights $w_i = \alpha(1-\alpha)^i$ sum to $1 - (1-\alpha)^T$, confirming a convex combination. As $T \to \infty$, $\ell_T$ becomes a true weighted average of all past values with exponentially decaying weights. (7 marks)

**(b)** Step-by-step computation using $\ell_T = 0.4 y_T + 0.6 \ell_{T-1}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ell_1 &= 0.4(10) + 0.6(10) = 10.000 \\
\ell_2 &= 0.4(12) + 0.6(10.000) = 10.800 \\
\ell_3 &= 0.4(13) + 0.6(10.800) = 11.680 \\
\ell_4 &= 0.4(11) + 0.6(11.680) = 11.408 \\
\ell_5 &= 0.4(14) + 0.6(11.408) = 12.445 \\
\ell_6 &= 0.4(16) + 0.6(12.445) = 13.867 \\
\ell_7 &= 0.4(15) + 0.6(13.867) = 14.320
\end{aligned}$$

| $t$ | $y_t$ | $\hat{y}_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 12 | 10.000 | 2.000 |
| 3 | 13 | 10.800 | 2.200 |
| 4 | 11 | 11.680 | 0.680 |
| 5 | 14 | 11.408 | 2.592 |
| 6 | 16 | 12.445 | 3.555 |
| 7 | 15 | 13.867 | 1.133 |
| 8 | 17 | 14.320 | 2.680 |

$$\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{7} \sum \vert e_t \vert = \frac{14.840}{7} \approx 2.120$$

**Final forecast for $t = 9$:** $\hat{y}_9 = \ell_8 = 14.320$. (7 marks)

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain **Holt's Linear Trend method** with the equations for level $\ell_T$, trend $b_T$, and the $h$-step forecast. How does it extend SES?

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare the **Naive**, **Simple Average**, and **Simple Moving Average ($k=3$)** methods for the series $y = \{20, 22, 21, 23, 25, 24, 26\}$. Compute MAE and RMSE for each.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Holt's method extends SES by adding a trend component $b_T$ to capture systematic directional change:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ell_T &= \alpha y_T + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{T-1} + b_{T-1}) \\
b_T &= \beta^*(\ell_T - \ell_{T-1}) + (1-\beta^*) b_{T-1} \\
\hat{y}_{T+h} &= \ell_T + h \cdot b_T
\end{aligned}$$

The level update includes the previous trend, allowing the smoothed series to "tilt" with the data. Two parameters $\alpha, \beta^* \in [0, 1]$ are optimized to minimize in-sample SSE. Holt's method reduces to SES when $b_T = 0$ for all $T$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute forecasts for each method:

**Naive:** $\hat{y}_{t} = y_{t-1}$ for $t \geq 2$.

**Simple Average:** $\hat{y}_{t} = \bar{y}_{1:t-1}$.

**SMA(3):** $\hat{y}_{t} = (y_{t-1} + y_{t-2} + y_{t-3})/3$ for $t \geq 4$.

| $t$ | $y_t$ | Naive $\hat{y}$ | SA $\hat{y}$ | SMA(3) $\hat{y}$ | $\vert e_{\text{Naive}} \vert$ | $\vert e_{\text{SA}} \vert$ | $\vert e_{\text{SMA}} \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 22 | 20 | 20.00 | — | 2 | 2.00 | — |
| 3 | 21 | 22 | 21.00 | — | 1 | 0.00 | — |
| 4 | 23 | 21 | 21.00 | 21.00 | 2 | 2.00 | 2.00 |
| 5 | 25 | 23 | 21.50 | 22.00 | 2 | 3.50 | 3.00 |
| 6 | 24 | 25 | 22.20 | 23.00 | 1 | 1.80 | 1.00 |
| 7 | 26 | 24 | 22.50 | 24.00 | 2 | 3.50 | 2.00 |

**MAE Calculations:**

$$\text{MAE}_{\text{Naive}} = \frac{2+1+2+2+1+2}{6} = \frac{10}{6} \approx 1.667$$

$$\text{MAE}_{\text{SA}} = \frac{2.00+0.00+2.00+3.50+1.80+3.50}{6} = \frac{12.80}{6} \approx 2.133$$

$$\text{MAE}_{\text{SMA(3)}} = \frac{2.00+3.00+1.00+2.00}{4} = \frac{8.00}{4} = 2.000$$

**RMSE Calculations:**

$$\text{RMSE}_{\text{Naive}} = \sqrt{\frac{4+1+4+4+1+4}{6}} = \sqrt{\frac{18}{6}} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$$

$$\text{RMSE}_{\text{SA}} = \sqrt{\frac{4+0+4+12.25+3.24+12.25}{6}} = \sqrt{\frac{35.74}{6}} \approx 2.441$$

$$\text{RMSE}_{\text{SMA(3)}} = \sqrt{\frac{4+9+1+4}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{18}{4}} = \sqrt{4.5} \approx 2.121$$

**Conclusion:** On this series, the Naive method is best (lowest MAE and RMSE) because the data increments by $\approx +1$ per step, making "previous value" the strongest single predictor. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in MAE vs RMSE:** RMSE and MAE are *not* directly comparable numerically when scales differ; they are *comparable on the same series*. Examiners deduct for stating "RMSE > MAE implies model X is worse" — that is **wrong**. RMSE $\geq$ MAE is a mathematical *inequality*, not a comparison of methods.
> 2. **Forgetting to initialize $\ell_0$:** SES requires $\ell_0$. Using $\ell_0 = y_1$ is standard; using $\ell_0 = 0$ skews early forecasts.
> 3. **Wrong seasonal index in Holt-Winters:** Students frequently use $s_{T+h}$ instead of $s_{T+h-mk}$ — this single error costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing forecast $\hat{y}_{T+h}$ with fitted value $\hat{y}_t$:** The latter is in-sample; the former is out-of-sample. Metrics like MAE must be computed on the **test set**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Forecasting (Module 4)**

- **Forecasting Objective:** Predict $\hat{y}_{T+h \vert T}$ for $h \geq 1$ from historical $\{y_1, \ldots, y_T\}$.
- **Benchmark First:** Always evaluate at least one Naive / Seasonal Naive / Drift method before deploying complex models.
- **Naive Method:** $\hat{y}_{T+h} = y_T$ — a one-line, parameter-free baseline.
- **Drift Method:** Adds average historical slope scaled by $h$.
- **SMA Forecast:** $\hat{y}_{T+1} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} y_{T-i}$; only produces one-step-ahead forecasts; ignores older history.
- **SES Equation:** $\ell_T = \alpha y_T + (1-\alpha)\ell_{T-1}$; $\hat{y}_{T+1} = \ell_T$.
- **SES as Weighted Average:** $\ell_T = \sum_{i=0}^{T-1} \alpha(1-\alpha)^i y_{T-i} + (1-\alpha)^T \ell_0$.
- **Holt's Linear Trend:** Two parameters $(\alpha, \beta^*)$; forecast $\hat{y}_{T+h} = \ell_T + h b_T$.
- **Holt-Winters:** Three parameters $(\alpha, \beta^*, \gamma)$ plus seasonal period $m$.
- **Damped Trend:** Adds $\phi \in [0,1]$ to prevent trend forecasts from running away.
- **ARIMA Forecast:** Recursive — replace unknown future $y$ with $\hat{y}$, future $\varepsilon = 0$.
- **MAE:** Mean absolute error — robust to outliers, same units as $y$.
- **RMSE:** Root mean squared error — penalizes large errors disproportionately.
- **MAPE:** Scale-independent percentage, but undefined when $y_i = 0$.
- **MASE:** Best practice scale-free metric; values $< 1$ beat the seasonal naive.
- **Cross-Validation:** Use *time series split* (rolling/expanding window) — **never** random k-fold, which leaks future information.
- **Residual Diagnostics:** Ljung-Box test for autocorrelation; Shapiro-Wilk for normality; both must pass before deployment.
- **Forecast Uncertainty:** Always widen prediction intervals as $h$ increases — the **fan chart** principle.
- **ETS-ARIMA Equivalence:** SES $\equiv$ ARIMA(0,1,1) with no drift; Holt's linear $\equiv$ ARIMA(0,2,2); damped trend $\equiv$ ARIMA(0,1,2) (Hyndman, 2002).
- **Common KTU Mistake:** Using MAPE on series containing zeros — use MAE or MASE instead.
- **Final Tip:** In the KTU exam, always state the **assumptions** (e.g., stationarity, residual whiteness) before presenting the forecast equations — examiners reward methodological rigor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stochastic and Deterministic Trends
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT861 - TIME SERIES MODELING & ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stochastic and Deterministic Trends

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In time series analysis, the **trend** of a series is the long-term, sustained movement in its mean level over time. Trends are broadly classified into two fundamentally different mathematical structures: **deterministic trends** and **stochastic trends**. The distinction governs whether classical regression methods are appropriate or whether differencing/ARIMA machinery is mandatory.

A **deterministic trend** is a non-random, fixed function of time $t$ that is generated entirely by the passage of time, independent of past random shocks. The series mean evolves along a prescribed, mathematically exact path.

A **stochastic trend** is a random, non-stationary component generated by the cumulative accumulation of past random shocks (innovations). Each new shock permanently shifts the trajectory, and the future path cannot be deterministically predicted even in the long run.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECDT861 — Module 4):**
> Students must clearly distinguish between deterministic trends (modeled using OLS with time as regressor) and stochastic trends (handled via differencing and unit-root ARIMA models). Misclassification leads to **spurious regression** and invalid inference — a guaranteed 7-mark deduction in KTU board valuation.

### 1.2 Mathematical Notation

A univariate time series $\{Y_t\}$ may be decomposed as:

$$Y_t = f(t) + u_t$$

where $f(t)$ is the trend function and $u_t$ is the residual.

- If $f(t)$ is a deterministic function (e.g., $f(t)=\alpha + \beta t$), the trend is **deterministic**.
- If $f(t)$ itself evolves as a random walk: $f(t) = f(t-1) + \epsilon_t$ with $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma^2)$, the trend is **stochastic**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition (for first-time learners):**
>
> **Deterministic trend** is like a **train running on a perfectly straight track with a fixed schedule**. Given the start time, you can predict the train's exact position 100 years from now using a clock-and-velocity equation. Past events (rain, traffic, etc.) do not affect the schedule.
>
> **Stochastic trend** is like a **vessel adrift in a stormy ocean**. The future position is the current position plus the cumulative effect of every random wave the vessel has ever encountered. Even if the storm stops tomorrow, the vessel remembers every past wave and continues with an altered trajectory. Future positions have **infinite theoretical variance** — no fixed long-term forecast exists.

### 1.4 The Two Canonical Examples

| Property | Deterministic Trend | Stochastic Trend |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | $Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \varepsilon_t$ | $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ |
| Variance of forecast | Finite as $h \to \infty$ | Grows without bound as $h \to \infty$ |
| Stationarity after removal | Subtract $f(t)$ | First-difference $\Delta Y_t$ |
| Proper model | OLS regression | ARIMA$(0,1,0)$ or unit-root model |
| Standard errors | Valid via OLS | OLS inference is **invalid** |

### 1.5 Why This Distinction Matters in Engineering

In engineering applications — load forecasting for power grids, telemetry drift in satellites, degradation modeling of components — misclassifying a stochastic trend as deterministic leads to under-estimated prediction intervals. This is **catastrophic in safety-critical systems** (nuclear plant monitoring, structural health monitoring of bridges).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deterministic Trend Models

A general deterministic trend is a deterministic function of time $t$ that can be written as a polynomial:

$$Y_t = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 t + \alpha_2 t^2 + \cdots + \alpha_k t^k + \varepsilon_t$$

**Canonical cases:**

1. **Linear (constant) trend:** $Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \varepsilon_t$
2. **Quadratic trend:** $Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma t^2 + \varepsilon_t$
3. **Exponential trend:** $\ln Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \varepsilon_t$

For these models, the trend is **time-deterministic**, meaning the expected value given $t$ is known with zero uncertainty:

$$E[Y_t \mid t] = \alpha + \beta t$$

The variance of an $h$-step-ahead forecast is:

$$\mathrm{Var}(Y_{T+h} \mid Y_T,\ldots,Y_1) = \sigma^2$$

which remains **constant**, no matter how far into the future we forecast.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** Long-term power demand forecasting in regulated utilities historically assumed a deterministic linear trend. Modern smart-grid studies replace this with stochastic-trend (ARIMA) models because demand now responds stochastically to weather, EV charging, and policy shocks.

### 2.2 Stochastic Trend Models

#### 2.2.1 Random Walk (RW)

The simplest stochastic-trend model is the **random walk**:

$$Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma^2)$$

Iterating backwards from $Y_0$:

$$Y_t = Y_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_i$$

Therefore:

$$E[Y_t] = Y_0, \qquad \mathrm{Var}(Y_t) = t\sigma^2 \to \infty$$

The variance grows **linearly with time** and is unbounded. This is a unit-root, non-stationary process; the only stationary transformation is the first difference:

$$\Delta Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1} = \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma^2)$$

#### 2.2.2 Random Walk with Drift

A more realistic extension introduces a constant drift parameter $\delta$:

$$Y_t = \delta + Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma^2)$$

Equivalently:

$$Y_t = Y_0 + \delta t + \sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_i$$

Here $\delta$ looks superficially like the slope of a deterministic linear trend, **but its statistical behaviour is fundamentally different**:

- $E[Y_t] = Y_0 + \delta t$ (linear in $t$, same as deterministic trend)
- $\mathrm{Var}(Y_t) = t\sigma^2 \to \infty$ (grows without bound, unlike deterministic trend)
- The standard error of the drift estimate is $\sigma/\sqrt{T}$ (OLS valid), but the forecast standard error is $\sigma \sqrt{h}$, growing with horizon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** A random walk with drift *looks* like a deterministic linear trend in expectation, but the *variance* of the trend itself is random and unbounded. A single large shock permanently alters the path. This is the central idea behind the **unit root** literature (Nelson & Plosser, 1982).

### 2.3 Comparison of Forecast Variances

For an $h$-step-ahead forecast from horizon $T$:

| Model | Point Forecast $\hat{Y}_{T+h}$ | Forecast Variance |
|---|---|---|
| Deterministic: $Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \varepsilon_t$ | $\alpha + \beta(T+h)$ | $\sigma^2$ (constant) |
| Random walk: $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | $Y_T$ | $h\sigma^2$ (linear in $h$) |
| RW with drift: $Y_t = \delta + Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | $Y_T + \delta h$ | $h\sigma^2$ (linear in $h$) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Property | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Deterministic linear trend | $Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \varepsilon_t$ | OLS valid; stationary around $f(t)$ |
| 2 | Random walk | $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | Unit root; $\Delta Y_t \sim WN$ |
| 3 | RW with drift | $Y_t = \delta + Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | Mean linear; var grows with $t$ |
| 4 | Mean of RW | $E[Y_t] = Y_0$ | Constant |
| 5 | Mean of RW with drift | $E[Y_t] = Y_0 + \delta t$ | Linear |
| 6 | Variance of RW | $\mathrm{Var}(Y_t) = t\sigma^2$ | Unbounded |
| 7 | Forecast variance (RW) | $h\sigma^2$ | Grows with horizon $h$ |
| 8 | DF test regression (no const) | $\Delta Y_t = \rho Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | $H_0: \rho = 0$ (unit root) |
| 9 | DF test regression (with const) | $\Delta Y_t = \alpha + \rho Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | Standard form |
| 10 | DF test regression (with const+trend) | $\Delta Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \rho Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | For trended series |
| 11 | KPSS statistic | $LM = \sum_{t} \frac{S_t^2}{\hat{\sigma}^2}$ | $H_0$: stationary |
| 12 | ADF correction | Add $\sum_{j=1}^{p} \gamma_j \Delta Y_{t-j}$ to DF regression | Handles serial correlation |
| 13 | Spurious $R^2$ | OLS on two I(1) series $\to R^2 \to$ non-zero | Always check stationarity first |
| 14 | Order of integration | $Y_t \sim I(d)$ means $\Delta^d Y_t$ stationary | $I(1)$ = unit root, $I(0)$ = stationary |
| 15 | Removing stochastic trend | First-difference: $\Delta Y_t$ | Removes unit root |
| 16 | Removing deterministic trend | Detrend: $Y_t - \hat\alpha - \hat\beta t$ | OLS detrending |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever you see "$\mid x \mid$" or "$\vert x \vert$" in time series, use `\vert` or `\mid` inside LaTeX. In KTU answer sheets, use simple vertical bars — they will not break a printed answer.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydrology:** Reservoir inflow series exhibit stochastic trends driven by accumulated rainfall shocks. Deterministic detrending would mis-estimate the long-run mean.
- **Aerospace telemetry:** Sensor drift can be modeled as RW + drift; OLS detrending without differencing is statistically invalid.
- **Civil infrastructure:** Bridge deflection under cyclic loading is a deterministic trend superimposed on stochastic vibration noise — combined models are required.
- **Energy load forecasting:** Power consumption integrates random-walk demand with deterministic seasonal + linear trend components. SARIMA models (which you will study in Module 5) formalize this.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Variance of the Random Walk

**Goal:** Show that $\mathrm{Var}(Y_t) = t \sigma^2$ for the random walk $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$.

**Step 1 — Write the recursion explicitly.**

The defining recursion of a random walk is:

$$Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$$

**Step 2 — Unroll the recursion iteratively.**

Starting from an initial value $Y_0$ (treated as a constant), we expand:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_1 &= Y_0 + \varepsilon_1 \\
Y_2 &= Y_1 + \varepsilon_2 = Y_0 + \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 \\
Y_3 &= Y_2 + \varepsilon_3 = Y_0 + \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
Y_t &= Y_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the expected value.**

Using the linearity of expectation and the zero-mean property of white noise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[Y_t] &= E\!\left[Y_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_i\right] \\
       &= E[Y_0] + \sum_{i=1}^{t} E[\varepsilon_i] \\
       &= Y_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{t} 0 \\
       &= Y_0
\end{aligned}
$$

So the mean is **constant**, independent of $t$.

**Step 4 — Compute the variance.**

Since $Y_0$ is constant (variance zero) and $\{\varepsilon_i\}$ are mutually uncorrelated white-noise shocks with $\mathrm{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Var}(Y_t) &= \mathrm{Var}\!\left(Y_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_i\right) \\
                  &= \mathrm{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_i\right) \\
                  &= \sum_{i=1}^{t} \mathrm{Var}(\varepsilon_i) \quad \text{(uncorrelated shocks)} \\
                  &= \sum_{i=1}^{t} \sigma^2 \\
                  &= t \sigma^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

$$\boxed{\;\mathrm{Var}(Y_t) = t \sigma^2 \;\longrightarrow\; \infty \text{ as } t \to \infty\;}$$

This divergence is the **mathematical fingerprint of a stochastic trend**. The trend's own variance grows with time, making the path increasingly uncertain.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Forecast Variance of Random Walk with Drift

**Model:** $Y_t = \delta + Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, with $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$.

**Step 1 — Unroll the recursion from horizon $T$.**

For an $h$-step-ahead forecast, write the future values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{T+1} &= \delta + Y_T + \varepsilon_{T+1} \\
Y_{T+2} &= 2\delta + Y_T + \varepsilon_{T+1} + \varepsilon_{T+2} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
Y_{T+h} &= h\delta + Y_T + \sum_{j=1}^{h} \varepsilon_{T+j}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the point forecast (conditional expectation).**

Conditional on the observed data up to $T$, future shocks $\varepsilon_{T+1}, \ldots, \varepsilon_{T+h}$ are unobservable and have conditional mean zero. So:

$$E[Y_{T+h} \mid Y_T] = h\delta + Y_T + \sum_{j=1}^{h} E[\varepsilon_{T+j}] = h\delta + Y_T$$

**Step 3 — Compute the forecast error.**

The forecast error is the sum of future unobservable shocks:

$$e_{T+h} = Y_{T+h} - \hat{Y}_{T+h} = \sum_{j=1}^{h} \varepsilon_{T+j}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the forecast variance.**

Because the future shocks are uncorrelated with zero mean and common variance $\sigma^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Var}(e_{T+h}) &= \mathrm{Var}\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{h} \varepsilon_{T+j}\right) \\
                      &= \sum_{j=1}^{h} \mathrm{Var}(\varepsilon_{T+j}) \\
                      &= h \sigma^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Construct the 95% forecast interval.**

Assuming approximate normality of the sum (CLT for many shocks), the 95% interval is:

$$Y_{T+h} \in \left[\,\hat{Y}_{T+h} \pm 1.96 \sigma \sqrt{h}\,\right]$$

The interval **widens** with horizon $h$, unlike a deterministic trend where the interval remains $\pm 1.96 \sigma$.

$$\boxed{\;\text{Forecast variance of RW with drift at horizon } h: \;h \sigma^2\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Autocovariance of a Random Walk

**Goal:** Show that the autocovariance function of a random walk does not decay with lag.

**Step 1 — Write $Y_t$ and $Y_{t+k}$ in expanded form.**

For $k \geq 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_t     &= Y_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{t}   \varepsilon_i \\
Y_{t+k} &= Y_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{t+k} \varepsilon_i = Y_t + \sum_{j=1}^{k} \varepsilon_{t+j}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathrm{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t+k})$.**

Using $\mathrm{Cov}(aX+b, Y) = a\,\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y)$ and bilinearity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t+k}) &= \mathrm{Cov}\!\left(Y_t,\; Y_t + \sum_{j=1}^{k} \varepsilon_{t+j}\right) \\
                          &= \mathrm{Cov}(Y_t, Y_t) + \sum_{j=1}^{k} \mathrm{Cov}(Y_t, \varepsilon_{t+j}) \\
                          &= \mathrm{Var}(Y_t) + 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross-covariances vanish because $Y_t$ depends only on $\{\varepsilon_1, \ldots, \varepsilon_t\}$, which are uncorrelated with future shocks $\{\varepsilon_{t+1}, \ldots, \varepsilon_{t+k}\}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute the variance.**

$$\mathrm{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t+k}) = t \sigma^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the autocorrelation function.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_k &= \frac{\mathrm{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t+k})}{\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}(Y_t)\,\mathrm{Var}(Y_{t+k})}} \\
       &= \frac{t\sigma^2}{\sqrt{(t\sigma^2)(t+k)\sigma^2}} \\
       &= \frac{t}{\sqrt{t(t+k)}} = \sqrt{\frac{t}{t+k}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the limit.**

As $k \to \infty$ at fixed $t$, $\rho_k \to 0$. But for fixed $k$ as $t \to \infty$, $\rho_k \to 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence:** Sample ACF of a random walk decays very slowly. A correlogram that does not cut off is a **diagnostic signature of a unit root**, used by the KPSS/ADF tests in the next module.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Why Spurious Regression Happens (Two Independent Random Walks)

Let $X_t$ and $Y_t$ be **independent** random walks, each with variance growing as $t\sigma^2$.

**Step 1 — Define the OLS estimator.**

Regressing $Y_t$ on $X_t$ (with intercept) gives OLS slope estimator $\hat\beta$ and $R^2$.

**Step 2 — State the spurious regression result (Phillips, 1986).**

For two independent $I(1)$ series, the OLS estimator satisfies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat\beta &\xrightarrow{d} \text{non-degenerate random variable (NOT zero)} \\
T \cdot \hat\beta &\xrightarrow{d} \text{a non-degenerate mixture distribution} \\
R^2 &\xrightarrow{p} \text{non-zero limit} \\
t\text{-stat} &\xrightarrow{d} \text{non-standard distribution, no unit normal limit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Intuitive explanation.**

Both $X_t$ and $Y_t$ contain a common factor: **time**. As $t$ grows, both series wander further from their starting values, so any OLS regression finds a "fit" even though no genuine relationship exists. The deterministic-time index is the omitted common cause.

**Step 4 — Implication for engineering practice.**

Always check the order of integration (ADF / KPSS) **before** running OLS. If both series are $I(1)$, perform **cointegration analysis** (covered later in the syllabus) or difference the series.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Deterministic Trend | Stochastic Trend |
|---|---|---|
| Trend function $f(t)$ | Non-random | Random, $f(t) = f(t-1) + \eta_t$ |
| Trend persistence after shock | None | Permanent |
| Decomposition | $Y_t = f(t) + \varepsilon_t$ | $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ |
| Variance of $Y_t$ | $\sigma^2$ (bounded) | $t\sigma^2$ (unbounded) |
| Forecast variance (h steps) | $\sigma^2$ | $h\sigma^2$ |
| ACF behaviour | Decays (stationary residuals) | Decays extremely slowly |
| Valid inference | OLS standard errors | Differencing or ARIMA needed |
| Differencing removes trend? | No (would overdifference) | Yes |
| Detrending (subtract OLS fit) removes trend? | Yes | No (leaves residual trend) |
| Test of choice | Deterministic-trend DF test | ADF with appropriate lags |
| Example | Annual mean temperature rise (climate debate) | Stock market index, exchange rate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Trend Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed Time Series Y_t] --> B{Is ACF slow-decaying?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Does variance grow with t?}
    B -- No --> D[Stationary Series: I 0]
    C -- Yes --> E[Run ADF Test H0 unit root]
    C -- No --> F[Stationary heteroscedastic]
    E -- Reject H0 --> G[Stationary I 0 use OLS]
    E -- Fail to Reject --> H[Stochastic Trend I 1]
    H --> I[Apply First Differencing Delta Y_t]
    I --> J[Re-test with ADF on Delta Y_t]
    J --> K[Build ARIMA 0 1 0 or higher]
    A --> L{Trend visually obvious?}
    L -- Yes, smooth --> M[Fit OLS with time regressor]
    M --> N[Residual test for unit root]
    N -- Residual stationary --> O[Deterministic Trend]
    N -- Residual has unit root --> P[Mixed: Use KPSS or detrend then ADF]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Forecast Variance Growth Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Deterministic Trend Y t equals alpha plus beta t plus epsilon] --> B[Forecast variance equals sigma squared]
    B --> C[Interval constant across horizon h]
    D[Random Walk Y t equals Y t-1 plus epsilon] --> E[Forecast variance equals h times sigma squared]
    E --> F[Interval widens as sqrt h]
    G[Random Walk with Drift Y t equals delta plus Y t-1 plus epsilon] --> H[Forecast variance equals h times sigma squared]
    H --> I[Interval widens as sqrt h, mean shifts by delta h]
```

### 4.3 Concept Map — Trend Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Time Series Trend] --> B[Deterministic]
    A --> C[Stochastic]
    B --> B1[Linear alpha plus beta t]
    B --> B2[Quadratic with t squared]
    B --> B3[Exponential log linear]
    C --> C1[Random Walk ARIMA 0 1 0]
    C --> C2[RW with Drift]
    C --> C3[ARIMA p d q with d equals 1]
    C1 --> D1[Variance t sigma squared]
    C2 --> D2[Mean Y0 plus delta t]
    C3 --> D3[Differencing d times]
    B1 --> E1[Subtract OLS fit]
    B2 --> E2[Subtract quadratic fit]
    B3 --> E3[Take log then subtract]
    E1 --> F[Stationary residual]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    D1 --> G[Stationary after differencing]
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Effect of a Single Shock on Each Trend Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Single Shock at time t0] --> B[Hits Deterministic Trend]
    A --> C[Hits Stochastic Trend]
    B --> B1[Series briefly perturbed]
    B1 --> B2[Returns to prescribed line alpha plus beta t]
    C --> C1[Permanent level shift]
    C1 --> C2[Future path is old path plus shock]
    C2 --> C3[Shock never decays]
    B2 --> D[Memory: zero]
    C3 --> D2[Memory: infinite]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual mnemonic:** In a deterministic-trend world, a shock is a temporary detour. In a stochastic-trend world, a shock is a permanent relocation. This is the engineering intuition behind why **differencing** (which strips out accumulated shocks) is the right tool for the latter, while **detrending** (which strips out a deterministic curve) is the right tool for the former.
